Excellent: xxx
Very good: xx
Worth a visit: x
Not worth a visit: o

Headline: Ghana: Compost to Reduce Waste - EPA
Body_Text:
Accra — The Deputy Director of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Mr. Louis Atsiatorme has asked households to practise composting to reduce their volume of waste.
Opening a one-week exhibition on recycled items by some junior secondary school (JSS) students in Accra yesterday, he said most of the waste generated from food such as cassava, plantain, cocoyam and yam peels could be composted to fertilise the soil.
The exhibition dubbed, "the West African Clean Development Project", is being organized by the Swedish Martha Association in Finland in collaboration with the Metro Public Health and Education Department of the AMA. The products exhibited include a straw house, paper building, slippers and made from lorry tyres.
Mr. Atsiatorme said there has been an upsurge in indiscriminate disposal of waste and filth "all leading to the spread of diseases which affect public health".
He said it is essential to train the youth to develop sound environmental practices. "It is therefore relevant to help them develop attitudes that will not destroy the environment, especially attitudes concerning domestic waste management."
He said the EPA in order to curb further deterioration of the environment has adopted guidelines for the development and management of landfills and healthcare waste among others.
The Programme Cordinator of the association, Mrs. Susan Manteie told the ADM that the project is aimed at helping students acquire theological knowledge about making compost and recycling organic waste at the home.
Ms. Hawsatu Ziblilah, 12, of the Boundary Road JSS was given a mountain bike valued at ¢420,000 for excelling in an essay competition on waste management organised by the association, while Faustina Tabuaa, JSS 3, of the Ringway East JSS received the same prize for topping the Art category. Master Augustine Ayivor of Kotobabi "15" JSS received notebooks for making a slippers (Afro Moses) out of a used tyre.

Headline: Nigeria: Vision for a New Kwara
Body_Text:
Lagos — For those of us who knew what Kwara was, you will agree with me that the urgent challenge of our time is to rebuild our state because our beloved State is now in ruins.
Kwara before now was a State of industry and great agricultural enterprise. From the flourishing paper mill at Jebba that employed over 40,000 people at its peak, to the sugar company in Bacita that was home to over 4,000 workers. From Matchco industry that employed a great number of people to Tate and Lyle, Philip Morris, Pepsi Plant at Ilorin. From the budding steel works at Ajase-Ipo to the Singer Plant at Sanmora, our State boasts of a sizeable industrial economy.
Today Kwara is a radical departure from its industrial past. To make matters worse, the agricultural credit schemes, extension services, Commodity Boards that acted as catalyst to the agrarian economy have long disappeared courtesy of the social economic experimentation of the Federal government in the past 15years.
Little wonder therefore that we are saddled with massive unemployment, poverty due to lack of viable means of livelihood, low income and the attendant crises of insecurity, violence and incessant conflict at the slightest provocation. Young ones and even the older hands that are able (those in the productive segment of the society) have long migrated to different cities all over the country running around in traffic to eke out a living under sub-human conditions or searching for non existent jobs.
While previous administrations in the State may not be solely responsible for creating the environment for the disappearance of industries and agricultural enterprises in the State, the truth of it is that they could have arrested the process of de-industrialisation with the resources at their disposal.
It will be stating the obvious to say that development stopped in Kwara State after two great leaders that we have had-General David Bamigboye and late General George Innih. We can therefore consider the past three decades after their administration in Kwara as the years of decadence and waste.
Talking about rebuilding Kwara therefore is talking re- industrialisation, as there is no way to alleviate poverty and rebuild the state except by promoting rapid industrialization and agricultural development. People can earn money from gainful and sustainable employment from which they can overcome want and squalor. When people earn enough to support their lives they can pay taxes that will be used to build roads, education, water, electricity and other social amenities.
In the challenge of building a State the role of who is Governor is fundamental. For me, I look at this one as the one to be the torchbearer of our people's hope. The one who if he must succeed must be the Chief Messenger, imbued with a lot of fear of God, who has unshakable belief in equity and justice, a vision and focus that is clear and sharp and above all an unquestionable integrity.
I have searched my mind and prayed to God almighty, and I am convinced that I am eminently qualified to take up this task.
I have already communicated this decision to my ward LGA members, senatorial district and the entire people of Kwara State. More importantly, I have already obtained the expression of interest form no. 0060052 to concretise my intention. I also deem it fit that I should share my thoughts with those of my colleagues from the fourth estate of the realm.
Our mission God willing is to build a new Kwara by creating an economic base through rapid industrialization and agricultural development programme. We shall promote 1000 private small- scale industries and 50 medium scale ones in four years. Our government shall promote a cooperative movement of a target 4000 farming families whose income will be raised to about him per family per annum. The immediate effect of all these will be an estimated 40,000 jobs directly. Our industrialisation programme shall be powered by an independent power plant that we shall establish through a joint venture. Through these programmes we shall arrest poverty and replace it with prosperity by creating sustainable employment.
A massive social development programme, which we see as the minimum, will complement the agricultural and industrial development programme, which is our core programme, and basic programme expected of all governments. Its components are: rapid expansion in road network, rural electrification, provision of clean water, educational reform and award of bursaries to students after the first year in office, low-income housing on owner-occupier basis, promotion of basic health and preventive medicine etc.
For anyone who hears a Nigerian politician talk of promoting 1000 enterprises and raising the income of over 4000 small scale farmers to N1,000,000 annually per farming family, the temptation is to dismiss it as one of those campaign promises, the other temptation is to doubt if the fund for the project will be available.
With all seriousness, a review of the earnings of Kwara State Government from the federation account in the past three years shows that this is possible. As at May 2002 Kwara has earned about N24.5billion paying only 13billion in salaries and other emoluments that frees about 12 billion for economic and social development. Even if state earnings drops by 20%. The state will still be able to afford over 10billion for economic and social development over a period of four years.
Our estimated target is to promote 1000 enterprises that will process available agricultural raw material like groundnuts, cassava, yam, etc. The funding portfolio is to offer N4, 000,000 credits each at 3% interest rate over a period of four years totalling N4 billion to 1000 enterprises, whose qualification shall be entrepreneurial experience and counterpart funding of N1, 000,000 each. Our estimated target is to kick start agriculture by making available N2 billion revolving credit at 2% to farming cooperative over a period of four year supported by a farmers guaranteed price policy. The estimated 4 billion should cover infrastructural expansion and general administration expenses, which will be kept at the barest minimum.
Our strategy towards funding the social development prograrnme will be to reduce waste in the contract award by utilizing the service of a Joint Venture State Engineering Construction Company to be incorporated for medium scale road construction, electrification and water projects. The Joint Venture Company will also bid contracts in other States to generate revenue. Local contractors will however not be left out in patronage.
Our administration if elected will pay a high premium on the welfare of civil servants, pensioners, women, handicap and the youth. A housing programme on owner-occupier basis and a comprehensive health insurance policy will be promoted. We are committed to rapid economic and political empowerment of women by ensuring that women occupy over 30% of all political appointments.
Compatriots for over three decades after the creation of Kwara State it acquired a reputation for harmony and peace, our people were outstanding for their peaceful conduct and the milk, which our land produced, became a symbol of our people's conduct. Recently and unfortunately, Kwara has acquired notoriety for violence, political conflicts and squabbles. Age old animosities and divisions are being awakened to serve political ends. News, rumour and threat of death of political opponents are fast becoming tools in politicking.
We shall when elected pursue a comprehensive initiative to heal our land and restore peace. We are best positioned for this, as we are not a product of the past political divides, we are neither pro nor anti anyone; we are only pro Kwara and pro development. We shall pursue without relent a policy of inclusiveness and political accommodation of all political tendencies in the State who share our vision for rapid development.
It is our desire that as we go out we shall be able to make a difference, to let it be clear that we have come out not just to be a contestant but to contest to win and God willing, we shall win.
We do hope our competitors will participate in the campaign with decorum and concentrate attention on issues that will help us to rebuild our state as this is the singular reason why we are out.
We are out to shift political discourse from persons and personalities to ideas and issues, to move politicking from the inane and the mundane to the fundamental and the substantial.
Our intervention is informed by the earnest desire to rise above the manipulation of ethnic and other primordial sentiments, which has kept our people down for a long time, and focus on the urgent question of how to create jobs; make houses available at affordable cost, kick start the state economy and lift our people above poverty through hard work.
Olawepo, former Deputy National Publicity Secretary of Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), is a gubernatorial aspirant in Kwara State.

Headline: Nigeria: The Senate, Their Senate
Body_Text:
Nigeria is a great country. Nigeria, apart from its numerous God-given material resources, has in its human resource fold great minds like Wole Soyinka, Chinua Achebe, Balarabe Musa, Bala Usman, Gani Fawehinmi and Rev. Father Matthew Kukah. Aside excelling in their respective fields of endeavour, these Nigerians and many more like them have over the years acquired a reputation for not suffering fools gladly or stomaching nonsense. They have come to represent the courageous moral face of a society known more for its degeneracy. One is therefore surprised at the deafening silence of this group of Nigerians in what seems to be the growing trademark of our legislature. Of particular concern to us here is what has been happening in the upper chamber from inception till date. Why do we continue to remain silent even in the face of monumental pilferage and profligacy? Should we allow our collective heritage to be pillaged by a few politicians who appear unconcerned that the country is suffering from the dishonour of being the second most corrupt nation in the world?
When will our leaders who have come to be considered the conscience of our collective existence frontally confront the irresponsible and reprehensible attitude of most of our present crop of politicians?
It is indeed sad that our Senate operates as if it is oblivious of the unprecedented suffering in the land; as if it is business as usual even in the face of horrendous scandals that have rocked it from day one. First, it was a scandal of monumental fraud levied against its first president. A committee appointed by the Senate investigated and indicted him. The second president did not fair any better as he was equally booted out by his colleagues for reasons that were not too different from that of the first except that in his own case, he had the misfortune of adding (or subtracting) the letter "s" to his first name at a certain stage in his life.
Not satisfied that the scandal about their furniture and constituency allowances have been forgotten by Nigerians (in their usual mass amnesiac manner) and always not lacking in finding fresh ingenuous ways of lining their pockets, they thought out an impeachment project which we understand was because the President was beginning to tighten the financial noose around their necks without regard for the fact that they are getting ready for their second terms (a luxury our legislative despots would not allow the executive enjoy!). But even then, they want to run with the hare and hunt with the hound; some evil geniuses will sponsor them to cause such confusion, yet they will take any sum that comes from the presidency's Messrs Fix-It under the pretext of discontinuing an exercise that was made, we hear, to be a gold mine in the first instance.
This brings us to the various serious allegations and counter allegations presently flying in the senate, thanks to this entire impeachment saga. Instead of our so-called distinguished senators getting to the root of these allegations and counter allegations, they resort to most unusual and unconstitutional means such as indefinite suspension, possibly expulsion etc.
Of course by now, Nigerians are used to the situation whereby the Senate depending on its whims also acts the Judiciary or the Executive. Most times they perceive themselves as being in competition with the Executive; they have a penchant for not doing things right or even doing the right things.
In spite of the present mood and condition of Nigerians what bothers them is whether or not the senators should use sirens, the position senators should occupy in the Order of Protocol, immunity for senators for prosecution, etc. Meanwhile, the primary responsibility of making laws for the good governance of the nation takes a back seat.
There is much unconcern, so much greed, so much vendetta, so much hatred for transparency and accountability in this chamber of "distinguished" Nigerians that make us lack the audacity to defend ourselves when others around the world call us the second most corrupt nation known to man today. While our senators take pleasure in querying ministers and a few of their own that disagree with some of their unwholesome tendencies; while they are quick to chronicle the sins of Mr. President, these Lords of the Manor believe they are infallible. If they ever accept being so, even if such wrong borders on criminality, they use the divine rights they have arrogated themselves to pardon each other "for the sake of reconciliation".
So, pray, what else is totalitarian if not such a situation where a single institution of government arrogates to itself Legislative, Judicial and Executive powers? But then who rescues us from this monster that is threatening to devour us? Where are our intellectuals, our statesmen, our opnion moulders, our men of conscience? Why can't they stand out and be counted by condemning the very unsavoury tendencies of this crop of politicians since their voices are more likely to make a difference? Need we call any louder?

The need to improve Kwara South, by Arc. Ashiru
By Olasunkanmi Akoni
HE is a man known for candour, humility and simplicity; a resilient man committed to the improvement of his community and the advancement of his people. A prudent and transparent man to the core in all his dealings with both the low and mighty of the society.
The man we are talking about is no other person than Architect Lola Ashiru, a senatorial aspirant on the platform of Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Kwara South.
In a recent chat at his Norman William Street, South West Ikoyi office, Ashiru told Vanguard that his planned adventure to the Senate under the auspices of PDP in Kwara South was basically to bring back the lost commercial glory for which his people were well known.
He blamed the present crops of politicians in authority for their penchant to enrich themselves and not to sense the interest of the majority Nigerians who put them in the office.
According to him, the Igbomina and the Ibolo people of Kwara South are highly industrious and dominate the commercial activities of the years past.
He lamented that the commercial activities have now been taken over from them when there was no committed leader to facilitate the progress of the commercial activities.
Arch Ashiru pointed out that the future of young graduates is being threatened by unemployment which could invariably lead them to social vices among other criminal acts.
Regrettably, Arch Ashiru stated that his constituency lacks a credible leader that could adequately represent their interest to bring back the lost glory in commerce, and ensure better future for the younger generation and development for the region.
An accomplished Architect of 23 years, he reiterated that his senatorial ambition was not to enrich or accumulate wealth or money, but rather he sees the task ahead as purely a call to service to his community and humanity as a whole.
To achieve this goal, Arch Ashiru stated that he hopes to approach it from governmental, non-govern-mental and international points.
Already, he said discussion has commenced with some non-governmental and international agencies to embark on some development project in Kwara south. This he stated would soon materialise.
He also blamed the low development performance of the present leadership to lack of preparedness for this task noting that the challenge in 1999 was just to get the military out of the power at all cost without adequate plan for the democratic task ahead.
Arch Ashiru, thus, opined that, now that people are conscious and aware of democracy, and more importantly when all the contenders for the senatorial seat are highly educated professionals with years of proven experience, the atmosphere he said, would be different in all spheres, come 2003 elections.
The emergence of many candidates for the senatorial seat, is not a threat to Ashiru. To him it is a good thing for democracy. Commenting on critics of politicians, he said that not all politicians are out to deceive the electorates especially nowadays when electorates are fully aware of their political rights and power to choose the candidate of their choice into elective offices, without fear of any intimidation. While agreeing with the fact that some politicians have done well, even where many have failed to deliver, Arch Ashiru enjoined politicians to eschew political violence and bitterness in the next dispensation for democracy to thrive in the country.
He decried the spate of political associations in the country and called on all politicians to imbibe the spirit of accepting defeat in good fate and not a politics of do or die affair.
He therefore assured the people of Kwara South of his journey to the senate to represent their utmost interest and bring back to them 100 per cent of their approved national cake without pocketing any of it before it gets to them.
Arch Ashiru, also took time to comment briefly on the face-off between senator Olusola Saraki and Governor Lawal, describing it as unfortunate. He advocated tolerance and peace to enhance development in Kwara State.
Also taking a careful assessment of the present political activities in the state, Arch Ashiru expressed confidence that the on-going merger of All Peoples party (APP) and PDP in the state spear-headed by Senator Saraki is a sure victory for PDP in the state. Undeterred by the happenings on the political landscape in the country, Arch Ashiru expressed belief that there is light at the end of the tunnel for the nations body polity.
Africa's almighty muddle
In recent times and on an annual basis, The Economist, a London Magazine of some 159 years of publishing, engages a futuristic endeavour by projecting into the in-coming year. This year, it has come out with its special publication, "THE WORLD IN 2003", wherein Nigeria got a mention. This is a re-production of what the magazine forecasts for Nigeria.
TWO large mineral-rich countries -Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo- will dominate African events in 2003. Both have an abundance of resources the rich world wants. Nigeria's oil production will top 3m barrels a day by the end of 2003; Congo has half the world's stock of rare minerals, such as germanium, that are vital in high-technology production. Nigeria is slipping towards catastrophe; Congo is peddling away from it.
Nigeria's 130m people are waiting for presidential and parliamentary elections in June against a backdrop of a failing economy and religious violence. Voters share a common disdain for the president, General Olusegun Obasanjo, and for his mostly opportunistic opponents. Waiting not so patiently is Nigeria's military. Until the elections, General Obasanjo holds the trumps; control of the purse strings and western backing. But if the elections are massively rigged and violent, expect the military's return. The question then will be whether the military can again hold the country together at gun point. Baleful Nigerians warn of their country becoming Africa's Yugoslavia.
This will add to West Africa's woes. Cote d'Ivoire's president, Laurent Gbagbo, will battle to contain a northern rebellion and may be consumed in the process. To the west, Sierra Leone struggles with post-war reconstruction while warlord President Charles Taylor's regime in neighbouring Liberia faces a rebellion. Constitutional and multi-party Ghana, Mali and Senegal stand out like beacons of stability in the region.
One of West Africa's worst presidents, Togo's General Gnassigbe Eyadema, may finally leave in 2003, as the constitution demands. Having shot his way to power in 1966, General Eyadema is Africa's longest -standing leader and one of its most brutal and corrupt. True to form he is grooming his son, Ernest, for succession. The other big succession will be in Kenya where outgoing President Daniel Arap Moi will attempt to anoint Uhuru Kenyatta, son of the country's first president, and may split the ruling party in the process.
Central Africa starts 2003 more hopeful. If the cajoling by regional and western powers works, Rwanda, Uganda and Zimbabwe will withdraw all their troops from war-ravaged Congo, perhaps allowing the Congolese to decide how their country, two-thirds the size of Western Europe, should be run. The World Bank and the European Union are offering to lend more than $2 billion for reconstruction while writing off most of the loans they made to the former despot, Mobutu Sese Seko.
Grand projects to renovate the railway from mineral rich-Katanga to Angola's Benguela port and to harness the country's huge hydro-electricity potential are being considered. To the east, Rwanda's president, Paul Kagame, is due to stand in promised multi-party elections in mid-2003, the first since the genocide in 1994.
It will be General Kagame's toughest year, with 120,000 prisoners accused of genocide due for release and the return of more than 20,000 soldiers from Congo to a struggling economy. Neighbouring Burundi faces its own test when Tutsi president, Pierre Buyoya, is due to hand over power to his Hutu deputy, Domitien Ndayizeye.
South Africa will keep its peacekeepers in Congo, help shore up its ally, General Kagame in Rwanda and step up military co-operation with Nigeria. President Thabo Mbeki's biggest foreign-policy crisis will be Zimbabwe, again. Expect him to nudge President Robert Mugabe into retirement, a move negotiable more with his political associates than his opponents. The associates will lobby for protection of their ill-gotten gains and no roll-back on land resettlement to be written into the deal. South Africa knows how important a settlement is to prevent chaos heading south.
South African companies will further entrench their grip on Africa's consumer markets. Africa's cell-phone market is projected to increase in size by 35% in 2003, the fastest in the world; most of the companies winning the contracts will be South African -owned or managed.
Diplomatic competition between South Africa and Libya will intensify. Mr Mbeki has outflanked attempts by Colonel Muammar Qaddafi to control the new African Union. But Colonel Qaddafi's street credibility will remain high in Africa. Libyan troops help out with security in at least five African states, and Libyan oil dollars pay the dues of ten more to the African Union.
But Libya attempts to help mediate peace in the Sudan Islamist regime in Khartoum. Indeed, none of those tryced. That will not stop the United States and the European Union from trying; oil and the probing of Khartoum's Islamist, about his links to al-Qaeda will keep Sudan on the watch list.
Much is expected of Angola now that it has ended its 30-year war. President Jose Eduardo do Santos has pledged to retire. GDP growth is likely to outstrip the IMF's projected average of 4.8% for sub-Sahara Africa for the year.
Looking to the continent as a whole, stronger coffee, cotton and metal prices will boost exports. And looking to the future, any progress in cutting rich-world subsidies and tariffs on food products at the World Trade Organisation talks will benefit Africa too.
The Intelligence Unit of the Economist, also presented Nigeria in figures projecting some indices for the coming year 2003.
Nigeria
GDP: $44.9bn
GDP per head:$360
Population: 124.8m
GDP growth: 4.3%
Inflation: 13.5%
Presidential, legislative and state elections will be held in April 2003. Nigeria has never had a successful transfer of power from one civilian government to another. It could happen this time, as the president, Olusegun Obasanjo, is seeking a second term, but military intervention cannot be ruled out.
The common growth rate will pick up in 2003, driven by higher oil production and investment in offshore oilfields and new gas projects. Few of the gains will trickle down to the poor, however. Loose fiscal and monetary policy and a weakening exchange rate will keep inflation in double digits.
To Watch
Sharia Law. The fault lines between Christian and Muslims have widened; sharia justice, which includes amputation and death by stoning, is perhaps the best example of this. Now enforced in 12 northern states, many Christians see this as an attempt to make Nigeria Islamic. As elections approach, sharia law will be fertile ground for conflict.

Original_Search_Title: Adefarati's Ondo State: What Manner of Development?
Headline: Nigeria: Adefarati's Ondo State: What Manner of Development?
Body_Text:
WHAT do the following names have in common - Arogbo, Mahintedo, Ugbonla, Mahin, Asewele, Uba and Simerin?
They are rural, riverine communities in Ondo State, which, prior to the Adebayo Adefarati administration, had never experienced the phenomenon of electricity in their citizens' lives.
Enter the colossus - cue in Chief Adebayo Adefarati and behold, those communities situated in the creeks and waters of Ondo State now enjoy continuous flow of light in their island communities.
In fact, the commissioning of the Arogbo-Ijaw electricity project way back 2000 marked the very first time in the history of Ondo State that "light would shine" on and in any Ijaw community and on Water, anywhere in the state, perhaps in Nigeria. Some breakthrough!
The visionary leadership of Chief Adefarati has continued to spread democracy dividends amongst the oil-rich peoples of the state by making the development (human and infrastructural) of the petroleum-producing region of the state, the centrepiece of the Rural Transformation Project (RTP) of his government.
And he is progressing "exceedingly at pace" to ensure that the "goose that lays the golden egg" i.e. the riverine people of the state, profit more than most from their contribution to the portals of the government revenue.
In fact, the current administration has put in place an agency, the Ondo State Oil Producing Areas Development Commission (OSOPADEC) which ensures that 70 per cent of the derivation proceeds accruing to Ondo State is spent within the oil producing communities which would translate into the complete rebirth of those communities by the end of Chief Adefarati's second tenure in 2007.
From the rural resurgence visible in the past three years, the people are united in the telling the governor to renew his mandate with the people for another term in 2003 so that the ongoing excellent implementation of the rural agenda would be fully actualised as planned by 207.
Ondo State farmers have been firmly in the vanguard of feeding the nation (growing cassava, yam, plantain, fruits, etc) and also earning foreign exchange by growing cash crops (cocoa and oil palm).
An integrated road network is a strategic imperative for the millions of farmers in the state who need to transport their crops to the urban cities and towns where they are required for consumption and export.
Since 1999, Governor Adefarati has matched and surpassed the expectation of the farmers and the general citizenry by embarking on the most comprehensive and far-reaching road construction network since the creation of the state in 1976.
From the 14 key inter city roads ranging from Akure-Alade-Idanre, Ondo-Laje and Ifira-Ipesi-Idogun in the Akoko hinterland, Governor Adefarati is opening up the rural areas for the farmers and their goods, to the urban centres and major cities for easy and accessible transportation of produce to sustain and enhance their livelihood.
Apart from this obvious benefit to farmers, this colossal network has generated invaluable and unquantifiable economic dividends and prosperity for the people, with spin-offs in the areas of:
* Cottage industries have continued to spring up all over the state
* Enhance transportation business with new commercial vehicles dotted and strewn over the entire state landscape and skyline.
* Unprecedented boom in the advent of new private car owners in the state, as evidenced by the 1000 per cent increase in the revenue generated from the registration of new vehicles since 2000 by the government.
* New shops and warehouses springing up even in remote areas recently opened up via the new road network which has unleashed a new breed of rural and urban entrepreneurs in the land.
The fall-outs are clearly visible in every part of the state which even Governor Adefarati's staunchest critics would grudgingly accede to.
Suffice to add that all the intra city roads in all the key cities are currently undergoing massive rehabilitation - Akure, Ondo, Owo, Ikare, Okitipupa, to mention a few - Ondo State has never, ever seen anything or experienced any phenomenon close to what Chief Adefarati's government is "planting" on Ondo soil.
When the word "Rangers" is mentioned in the Nigerian parlance, the first connection that readily comes to mind is Enugu and football - Enugu Rangers.
But in Ondo State, the word Rangers has a brand new meaning to the populace - it is the Health Rangers Scheme (HRS), which is the kernel of the free Health Delivery Scheme, designed to take medical care to the doorsteps of the people in every local government area in the state.
The scheme is uniquely novel and hinged on simplicity itself:
* Every week a team of medical personnel including Doctors, Pharmacists, Nurses and paramedical personnel visit a particular local government area attending to the health needs of the people, visiting their houses and their community centres.
* Those requiring secondary attention are referred and taken to the nearest state hospital for free treatment and attention.
* No area is left out - these are 16 new vehicles and two speed boats exclusively purchased and maintained for this scheme - the first of its kind by any state government in Nigeria.
Governor Adefarati has ensured that nobody in Ondo pays for health care treatment and delivery in the Sunshine State as dressings, drugs and treatments are offered free of charge.
The massive rehabilitation of all hospitals has been virtually completed to bring them in line with the optimal standards set by the World Health Organisation (WHO), which is the benchmark the government has set for itself to ensure that the governor's favourite cliche, "Health is Wealth" is being realised and actualised amongst the populace.
Ondo State is perhaps the only state in the federation where all the recommended drugs in the Essentials Drugs List (EDL) of the Federal Government are regularly available round the clock everyday and all year, on recommendation.
Since February 21, 2000, when this government launched the Free Health Care Delivery Scheme, nobody in Ondo State has been turned back from receiving drugs or treatment on account of the drugs not being available.
The "No Drug" syndrome has been expunged from the Ondo State medical vocabulary since the beginning of the new millennium.
As a corollary to this, every local government area, by the end of Governor Adefarati's first term would have received the dividend of a General Hospital i.e. for those areas that were without any prior to his becoming governor.
What the citizenry is witnessing in Ondo State is an outpouring of affection and affinity for the colossal achievements of Governor Adefarati and his collaborators in literarily transforming sunshine into the lives and hearts of his people.
The bonding and symbiotic relationship that exists between the governor and the people would manifest in a massive approval of the extension of his mandate for a fresh term in 2003 to enable Chief Adefarati etch a lasting legacy of rural upliftment, economic emancipation and a healthy and wealthy citizenry in Ondo State.
That is the wish of the people - maybe the good Lord grant the desires and expectations of the people in the Sunshine State.

Original_Search_Title: Back to Basics in Cross River
Headline: Nigeria: Back to Basics in Cross River
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor Donald Duke shows how poverty can be truly eliminated
through agricultural revolution.
Linus Anoyom said he is completely overwhelmed by his sudden rise in
society. Anoyom, as the chairman of Uyanga Pineapple Growers Multipurpose
Co-operative Society, Cross River State, recently led his members to meet
with Mr. Donald Duke, the Governor of Cross River State, in Government
House, Calabar, on a 'thank you visit for changing our lives.' To show
Governor Duke that the dividends of his administration's agricultural
policy, especially, the large-scale farming of pineapples, has actually
alleviated their poverty, the Society's members literally invaded Government
House with their new cars bought with the bountiful gains they had been
reaping from the sale of pineapples harvested from their farms.
Anoyom, like many Cross Riverians, as they fondly call themselves,
embraced "agri-revolution" which has created jobs and made many big-time
businessmen.
According to Duke, the vision behind his agricultural policy is to
re-orientate the people. Until the advent of his administration, he said,
the people of the state usually sought employment mainly in the public
service. "Agriculture has the potential to employ the bulk of our people
within a very short time. For instance, we have the largest palm nursery in
the world. What we have decided is to grow the oil palm and give it out as
seedling at little or no cost to encourage our people to grow them," Duke
explained.
The governor disclosed that he ventured into the growing of the crop not
because it is a traditional crop but because the conditions for growing it
in the state are favourable. He stated that it is one of those fruits that
have an increasingly large market and are of industrial application. "We can
make juice out of it, and it matures within a year and is easy to
harvest. We also found out that with the project still at the pilot stage,
most of our farmers are already building houses and buying cars. I am amazed
!" Duke explained that he is pleasantly surprised that even when there was
an initial pioneering problem of not having enough suckers and the
government was forced to import suckers from Ivory Coast, Ghana and
Cameroun, the dividends are already there.
Chris N. Aigbe, Commissioner for Agriculture, told TheNEWS in Calabar
that to a large extent, the Duke' s administration has had resounding
success in achieving major production, distribution, storage and marketing
of agricultural products in the state . He said that over 70 percent of the
vision of alleviating poverty of rural dwellers through the policy had been
achieved.
The Duke policy is novel in nature. The target of the administration was
to develop at least 10,000 hectares of oil palm within four years. He said
on the face of it, it might look small, but when compared with the fact that
since independence the state only had 13,000 hectares, it is a remarkable
feat to achieve 10,000 hectares within four years. Beyond that, the state
government also produced oil palm seedlings for 20,000 hectares which are
distributed to farmers free of charge.
Aigbe stressed that pineapple farming had impacted almost instantly on
the lives of the people because of the short gestation period. He said
members of the Pineapple Growers Multipurpose Co-operative Society who
embraced the policy have been empowered. " They have bought new cars, some
of them have married new wives and others are building houses of their own,
thus alleviating the poverty of our people." Beyond the traditional cassava,
rice and yam, the administration has ventured full scale into the
cultivation of cocoa, cashew, castor and oil palm.
The Duke's administration reasoned that as a way of alleviating the
poverty of his people, it divided the new estate of cashew into four
hectares each and allocated them to rural farmers. The same was done for oil
palm estates. Each four hectares were allocated to beneficiaries of the
landlord communities.

Original_Search_Title: Opportunities for Women in Nigeria's SMIS
Headline: Nigeria: Opportunities for Women in Nigeria's SMIS
Body_Text:
Nigeria is endowed with abundant human and natural resources not fully exploited. With a population of over 120 million people, Nigeria is potentially the largest single market in Africa. In natural resources, the country is rich in agricultural produce and livestock, such as cocoa, rubber, timber, groundnut, cotton, palm produce, cereals, citrus, soya beans, cassava, yam, sheep, goat and cattle. The country also has vast deposits of oil, gas and solid minerals, including iron ore, coal, lead and zinc, tin and columbite, kaolin, gypsum, baryte, phosphate, bitumen and gold, among others. With these resources, enormous opportunities exist for women to actualise their potentials. However, despite the availability of opportunities and resources, Nigerian women are yet to fully utilise them, leading to low productivity amongst women. I agree that inadequate flow of information on available opportunities and absence of guidance to would-be women investors are some of the obstacles that militate against women in small-scale business. It is believed that conscious efforts by women to participate in the production system will enhance our productivity, employment and prosperity.
What are SMIs?
There is hardly any unique, universally accepted definition of SMIs, because the classification of business into small/medium and large scale is a subjective and qualitative judgement. Rather, each country tends to define this category of industries based usually on the peculiar needs of public policy. Even within a country, the definition changes over time, depending on the circumstances and specific objectives of the institutions. There are, however some common indicators in most definitions, namely: The size of capital investment (fixed assets), annual turnover of the three indicators derives from the ease of measurement.
New definitions have been adopted by the Federal Ministry of Industry for micro/cottage, small and medium scale industries in the country as follows:-
Micro/cottage industry: An industry with a total capital employed of not more than N1.5 million, including working capital but excluding cost of land and a workforce of not more than 10 workers.
Small scale industry: An industry which total cost is over N1.5 million but not moe thatn N50 million, including working capital but excluding cost of land, and a labour size of 11 to 100 workers.
Medium scale industry: An industry which total cost is over N50 million but not more than N200 million, including working capital but excluding cost of land and labour size 101-300 workers.
These new definitions are informed by the realisation that they require varying support services and modified strategies, facilities and intervention instruments in order to effectively address their structural peculiarities and varying technical, economic and socio-cultural characteristics and constraints. The place of SMIs in any economy cannot be under-estimated, as the sub-sector is the mainstay of the economy and the bedrock of industrialisation the world over. The current high unemployment and under-employment can only be alleviated by women empowerment and the development of micro and small scale enterprises.
Most women in business are hindered by a number of factors; such as limited entrepreneurial experience and expertise, lack of capital and access to cheap credit, out-dated technology, poor working conditions and the absence of effective support services.
Others are: inadequate technical/infrastructural support; inadequate protection of SMI sub-sector in the macro-economic environment; inadequacy of instrument for promoting SMI development; limited access to institutional credit; poor management capability; poor record keeping of their activities; tendency of diverting loans to other non-business activities; deliberate attempt to default in loan repayment; prevalence of combination of business and personal finances; high failure rate of small businesses; high cost of source funds by banks to SMIs; sources of banks' loan able funds, inability to provide acceptable collateral to secure their borrowing; multiplicity of SMIs co-ordinating institutions, and unstable value of the naira.
The need for women to productively and profitably engage in SMIs is an urgent, and important one because they are at the receiving end of poverty.
Potential Gains of SMIs
There is no doubt that the SMIs sub-sector has a big potential in contributing to the national development of any given nation. Some of the potentials include: employment generation, entrepreneurship development, industrial dispersal, technological adaptation, utilisation of local raw materials, forward and backward linkages, rural industialisation, and greater share of ownership which make the business to be in the country permanently.
Past Efforts
In the recent past, we have witnessed the implementation of several efforts to boost SMIs in Nigeria. Notable among which are the disbursement of N13.5 billion to about 250 industries by the National Economic Recovery Fund (NERFUND) now defunct; giving of N250,000 loan to every approved SMI by the former Nigerian Bank for Commerce and Industry (NBCI) and the establishment of the Industrial Training Fund (ITF) to facilitate the development of Entrepreneurship skills.
During my tenure as the Minister of Industry, concerted efforts were made to revitalise the Industrial Development Centres (IDCs) and industrial estates all over the country and to make them functional for SMIs.
Present situation and New Initiatives
The Federal Government is making effort to improve the economy and return it towards the path of growth. I sincerely desire that women entrepreneurs would do everything to maximise the opportunities inherent in the new initiatives and policy thrust. Some of which include:
* Utilisation of existing industrial Development Centres (IDCs). Specifically, the services being rendered by the Industrial Development Centres (IDCs) are management services; technical services;
* Establishment of Small and Medium Industries Development Agency (SMIDA);
* Establishment of Bank of Industry Limited in 2001, in order to address the problem of access to long-term finance Institution (DFI) with an authorised share capital of N50 billion. The bank is expected to assist the resuscitating ailing, small and medium and large-scale industries as well as promoting new ones in all the six geopolitical zones in the country.
* Establishment of Small and Medium Industries Equity Investment Scheme (SMIEIS): The Bankers Committee established a SMIEIS by setting aside 10 per cent of their pre-tax profit commencing from last year for equity participation in SMIEIs. About N10 billion has been pooled into the fund.
The following conditions are necessary for any SMI seeking funds form SMIEIS: enterprises should have a maximum asset base of N200 million excluding land and working capital; enterprises should not have les than 10 or more than 300 employees; SMIs should be registered with Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) and in compliance with Companies and Allied Matters Acts (1990) such as filing of annual returns and audited financial cable tax laws and regulations as well as render regular returns to the appropriate authorities; those prepared to ensure prudent utilisation of funds granted under SMIEIS; enterprises prepared to make the company's books, appropriate authorities (This include SMIEIS granting bank and the CBN) when required; and SMIs who are prepared to comply with the guidelines of the scheme even after funds hav been disbursed; and SMEs committed to keeping up-to-date records on the company's activities under the scheme.
* Standardisation and Quality Control: Quality Assurance Programme, which is for quality system certification (ISO 9000 and ISO 14000) has been expanded to cover small and medium industries (SMIs) with a view to making them competitive in the globalised world economy.
* Incentives to Invest in SMIs: There are numerous incentives applicable to the sub-sector. These incentives are varied, including tax and tariff concessions, exemption, and relief such as pioneer status, local raw materials utilisation, investment in economically disadvantage areas, export oriented industries located in various Export Processing Zones (EPZ) in the country.
* Marketing of SMIs Product: In order to encourage SMIs, the Federal Government has directed that locally made goods should be patronised. Nigeria with a population of over 120 million people, is a potential market for finished products. Moreover, our favourable geographical location makes the country accessible either by air, land or sea and in consequence, puts any industrialist whether small, mediume or large at a strategic location to explore the entire ECOWAS sub-region, African Continent and world at large;
* Promotion of SMIs Cluster and Networks: For enhancement of their competitiveness and access to technology and global market;
* Development of Infrastructural facilities: So as to improve the investment climate and reduce the sot of doing business. This is being improved upon through deregulation and privatisation.
Strategies for Development of Women for Economic Empowerment.
Women should come together and mobilise funds for investment in SMIs from the sources earlier mentioned; and women should participate more in Organised Private Sector (OPS).

Original_Search_Title: Consumer Price Index Drops in September
Headline: Nigeria: Consumer Price Index Drops in September
Body_Text:
Composite consumer price index (CCPI) a change in prices paid by consumers living in both Urban and Rural areas stood at 4967.9 in September, 2002 from 4985.6 in August, 2002 which represents a drop in the indices by 0.4. The decline according to Federal Office of Statistics (FOS) was largely attributed to decreases in food items, drinks and personal household goods. However, the prices of clothing materials and cost of medicaments received a boost in many centres, this reveals that the urban indices rose by 4.4 percent, while the rural index fell by 1.3 percent respectively.
Consequently, the average consumer price level in the 12 months to September, 2002 took an upward trend with 10.0 percent. This in effect was lower then 12.3 percent observed in August, 2002, while making similar comparison.
In this regard, the urban and rural indices increased by 7.7and 10.5 percent over the same period. The percentage in the average CPI for the 12 months ending September, 2002 over the average CPI for the 12 months attracted 14.8 percent.
This invariably means that a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services bought with N100.00 during the 12 months period ending September, 2001 sold for N114.8 on the average for the corresponding period ending September, 2002.
It also on this premise, indicates a decline when compared to 15.6 percent obtained by making same comparison in August, 2002.
The Composite Food Index (CFI) went down in September, 2002 by 1.5 percent. This downward trend was occassioned by decreases in prices of yam tuber, plantain and sweet potato.
FOS reported that the level of CFI was higher in the corresponding year by 5.7percent.The average annual rate of rise of the index was 19.4 percent for the 12 months period ending September, 2002.
The All item index was less than farm produce which excludes prices of agricultural product and went up by 1.6 percent in September, 2002.
In furtherance to this, FOS reported that in the 12 months to September, 2002 the index increased by 16.2 percent and the average annual rate of rise of the index was 7.6 percent for the 12 months to September, 2002 as well.
On the other hand, the All items index rose by 3.8 percent in the third quarter of 2002, if compared with 3.0 percent increase noticed in the second quarter, the change over the first quarter was an increase of 5.5 percent.
Comparably, in August, 2002 the CPI witnessed 0.8 percent decrease as a result of low prices in stables. In effect this translates to mean that goods and services bought with N100.00 sold for N115.6 on the average for the period ending August,2002.
The August report went on to say that the CfI also dropped by 2.6 percent, the decline then according to the FOS was due to decreases in prices of yam tubers, maize, vegetables and fruits.
Continuing, the report stated that the level CFI was higher in that of the corresponding year ending July, 2001 by 10.5 percent and the average annual rate of rise of the index was 22.2 percent for the 12months period ending August, 2002.

Original_Search_Title: Buhari And the Politics of Napoleon
Headline: Nigeria: Buhari And the Politics of Napoleon
Body_Text:
In this piece, I do not speak of Napoleon Bonaparte of France. I speak of Napoleon the wily pig in George Orwell's Animal Farm. Napoleon was a pig that enjoyed prominence in the politics of Animal Farm alongside Snowball, another pig of political clout. While Snowball was a pig of towering intelligence and resourcefulness, Napoleon was a dim-witted pig that relied more on cunning and brute force to achieve his goals. Like Koranche in Ayi Kwei Armah's Two Thousand Seasons whose vapid spirit could not direct his hands to bring out any beauty, any truth locked in the woods, whom he and his age mates were carving into masks, Napoleon's spirit was incapable of directing his mind to ideals that would uplift the Farm. Like Koranche, he believed in burning the beautiful masks carved by his age mates out of envy and fear of the consequences of the contrast between his ugly log of wood and the beautiful mask carved by other children. So the beautiful plans that Snowball sweated day and night to draw up, Napoleon lifted his leg and urinated over. But that was not enough. Since he could not match Snowball eloquence for eloquence, idea for idea, vision for vision, and virtue for virtue, he resorted to the cheapest and oldest trick of those who cannot counter performance with performance: treachery and betrayal of the common good. Under the cover of secrecy, he raised puppies into hounds that chased Snowball out of the farm so that Sir Thomas Gresham's law by which good currencies were driven out of circulation by bad ones could flourish.
In Nigeria today, the politics of Napoleon is alive and flourishing. There are many politicians today to whom the fear of Buhari is the beginning of wisdom. His vision, performance and integrity, they cannot counter by a better vision, integrity and performance. So they have taken to raising puppies into hounds that should chase him away. Nothing more than the recent The News magazine's brazen publication titled: 'Buhari's N25 billion fraud, depicts this. After going through the said publication with an attentive mind, I asked myself what was left of professional responsibility in a noble profession. For there was nothing in the publication fortifying the paper to sling mud on the reputation of a man who symbolises probity and accountability in our national life. There is no evidence provided by the magazine on how Buhari as a person defrauded the country of the staggering sum of N25 billion while he was in PTF. Rather, the magazine's report was built on allegations contained in the report of the Interim Management Committee (IMC) that was constituted to wind up the PTF. The report of this committee according to the magazine found out among other things that PTF under Buhari: made an excessive order of HIV/AlDs kits, grossly inflated purchase prices causing the country a loss of N579 million, allowed Afri Projects Consortium to overcharge it. That the Fund went beyond its intervention areas to residential estate development etc, etc. All these allegations are according to The News raised by a committee chaired by Haroun Adamu, a man said by the paper to be of doubtful integrity. Though Haroun Adamu is a man of little credit, it served the convenience of the paper's task to invest credit in the product of a man without credit. It is in order. A man who will worship a sheep must first of all discount its stupidity and invest it with the qualities of a god. That such a man without credit is one vulnerable to the temptation of taking to his credit that which stands in the credit of others is not a consideration that exercises prudence in a mind given to vicious propensities. But to a discerning and objective mind, it understands why a bankrupt is in law disentitled to the management of the affairs of other men. The fear is a real and present one that he can appropriate to the satisfaction of his own bankruptcy the property of others, especially if such a man is one with a king size grudge against the property owner as in this case.
But let the above be with reason as it ought to be. To show how bad an influence self-interest is on intellect, the paper sees the allegation it says are contained in the IMC report as good as the judgement of a court which under S.137 (d) of the 1999 constitution will bar Buhari from contesting the 2003 presidential election because some people regard the IMC as a 'Competent Authority.' Haba! Since when have the sentiments of a man on the street been making courts out of committees? The faux pas is truly embarrassing .
What most spectacularly gave away The News as either being a hound in the hands of Napoleon or a child-chief priest that does not appreciate the gravity of his office and the oracle he represents was if summoned in a court of law to, on the basis of its publication, prove its case against the former head of state, it will most certainly say: 'We are sorry; we were only paid to do the job. We did not know it will come to this.' It is sad. The Buhari the paper said has stolen N25 billion, in the same breath, it wondered how a man with little financial means like him can ever hope to contest and win an election against moneybags like Umaru Shinkafi, Babangida, Atiku, etc. One would have thought that with such looting as ascribed to him by the paper, he will be astride a mountain of dineros. Petty contradictions such as this are the parodies a man exposes himself to when in favour of the fleshpots of Egypt, he abandons the path of honour.
To further reveal its hands as a hound in the hands of Napoleon, or indeed given the ethnic posturing of this magazine, as Napoleon itself, the paper then went on to hold Buhari to derision as a religious fundamentalist hated by Christians and animists of the Middle Belt, South-East, South-West and South-South. To strike a humbling contrast between what Buhari preaches and is known for and what the paper alleges he is, the general's indictment of the present government in a paper he delivered in the University of Jos recently was thus quoted with relish by the paper: 'Corruption which back on 30th May 1999, they promised to eliminate is nowadays enjoying unprecedented prosperity.' This quotation instead of achieving its intended effect, however, backfired and glanced disparagingly upon its user. For only in a country where corruption enjoys unprecedented prosperity does one find sensational, uninvestigative and sponsored publications such as the one under reference. A government that confuses blackmail with anti-corruption, shoots innocent people and provides cover for rascals and so corruption prospers without check under such a government.
Gentlemen of the press, yours is another noble profession. One under the right professional climate, I will not mind exchanging my wig with in this information age in which the man in charge of information holds the yam and the knife. Gentlemen, the nobility of your calling flows from the responsibility of your office that seeks to provide information to the populace on matters affecting their interests so as to engender understanding between them and authorities or personalities with potentials of positively and negatively affecting them. The character of your office has without consultation with you placed you above politics and partisanship. Yours increasingly appears to suffer fraternity with the office of a judge in a courtroom required by the nobility of position to hold the scales of justice even between the disputing parties and not himself jump into the arena.
So therefore in this season of politics in our polity, your neutrality and evenhandedness are on 24 hours call duty. If in exchange for honour, you opt for the role of Napoleonic dogs, no one needs tell you that you will relate more with the toes of men than with their hands, which by the present conduct of some of you is what you desire most. I cannot remember that Napoleon after consolidating his hold on power conferred chieftancy titles on his hounds. What I can remember was that he alone became Animal Hero First Class; a closet terror whom all the other animals including the dogs that chased out Snowball had to be ululating. 'Comrade Napoleon is always right,' even when he was placing severe rations on their feeding and killing them under treacherous circumstances. Indeed, in this country, if we still have what can be called a memory, in 1984 when Decree 4 the Public Officers Protection Against False Publication was passed and the media went into a 'righteous' rage and conspired to chase out the government that enacted that law, what happened to the media after that? Another government came to power and a great man of the pen called Dele Giwa received a parcelled bomb. Kayode Oni, Onome Osifo Whiskey, Kolawole Ilori and Ayodele Akinkurtu were arrested and detained for offensive publication. Journalists, to use the metaphysical words of Ferdinand Oyono in the Old Man and the Medal, became invisible like ghosts and disappeared like coins: things that did not and wouldn't have happened to them under the reign of Snowball. The News enlisted today by another Napoleon was proscribed and thousands of copies of its publication seized by the then Napoleon. The point is, most worms have hooks buried deep inside them, hence the need for wary nibbling by many a fish. It is eternally always better to deal with a customer that tells you your wares are bad when they are than with one that praises your wares in your presence while working behind you to severe your credit lines.
A. K. Usman wrote in from faculty of law, ABU, Zaria.

Original_Search_Title: American Notes (8)- Chief Matthias Iwuora Akukwe
Headline: Nigeria: American Notes (8)- Chief Matthias Iwuora Akukwe
Body_Text:
The conspiracy of my friends was total. Frank Aigbogun, Amuzie Akpaka,
Ikeddy Isiguzo, Ely Obasi, Taiwo Obe and others. They set upon me with this
diktat: Get married! It was 1983. I was 32. We were all on the staff of the
newly established newspaper, The Guardian, and we lived, bachelors all, in
this old one-storey building in Shomolu, Lagos that belonged to the Ibru
family.
I pleaded with my friends to show some understanding, pointing out that
32 years was not dismal for one to still remain unmarried. But they
disagreed. "Get married!" My problem was compounded by the fact that my
friends had a formidable ally in my mother. She let whoever paid any
attention know her attitude in the matter. Had a prophet prophesied that her
own son would be without a wife at 30 years, she would have retorted by
branding the fellow a charlatan and advising them to swap jobs. But, now,
her worst fears had come to pass! And the problem itself was incapable of
explanation, seeing that there were all over the place eligible spinsters
waiting to be carried across the threshold! In the last analysis, did it
indicate that she would not have the joyous privilege of carrying in her
arms a grandchild? When I asked whether the brood of Nnenna, my elder
sister, did not count as grandchildren, she put me in my place: Let's hear
word!
It was in this state of two-sided pressure that, during 1984, I
accompanied a friend to an address in Surulere, Lagos. She had a message for
a Chief Akukwe. When we got to the place, a lady in her mid-20s served us
drinks and engaged in small talk before disappearing. She was Chief Akukwe's
daughter. In my eyes, she did not look bad. Later I sent word to my
mother. I had freshly looked at all sides of everything on the marriage
spectrum, I told her. And it now seemed to me that she had a point all
along. Would it not make some sense if we asked a few questions on the
Akukwe lady? People around when my mother got the message later revealed
that she immediately invited neighbours over for instant celebration. My
mother knew the home of the Akukwes very well. Even though it is in another
town, it cost you only 16 minutes or so to get there from my own father's
compound.
She found out the next time Chief Akukwe was expected from Lagos and
paid a visit to the man of presence and stately bearing. And she decided,
the Matriach, that we were on. The man's daughter would be a knockout in her
own right. Ultimately, we tied the knot and I signed on the dotted line!
Chinweizu was there, his views on marriage notwithstanding. That was how I
got into the world of Chief Matthias Iwuora Akukwe. His Surulere residence
is a stone's throw from Nduka Obaigbena's ThisWeek magazine. And I often
took my friends from The Guardian and ThisWeek to visit my father-in-law, a
man who told beautiful stories connected to the time Dr. Azikiwe and others
fought for independence wielding nothing but oratorical quintessence. The
late Ely Obasi would often ask that we paid yet another visit. What
heightened our encounters with the man was the bacchanal ecstasies that
framed them, for Chief Akukwe did not hate a tipple!
Often our visits coincided with those of patriarchs like my
father-in-law. He would often let them into the secret of what a
distinguished writer I was, you know, the young boy that "decisioned" the
white man in his own tongue! Sometimes he added that I was level headed, so
well behaved. Needless to say that I enjoyed all the flattery. But it was at
a stage in my life when I did not believe that hot water could kill the
tortoise, and I often wondered whether Chief Akukwe really knew his
son-in-law.
Time passed. On August 22, I went to see him. He was sitting in his
usual position in front of the house, carrying in his arms one of the
grandchildren. I was traveling to the United States the following day. I sat
next to him and we talked some. He was pleased to learn that Ijeoma, my
daughter, was on her way to medical school. "The offspring of the snake
cannot but be long", he observed. "You will have a safe flight." When I rose
to go, he said, "You will see Godwin, I am sure. Tell him we are fine."
Godwin is his eldest son. As my mother-in-law saw me to my jalopy, I turned
to wave. Instead he asked me back. "Tell Godwin that we are fine", he said
again.
Radcliff, Kentucky. On Wednesday October 16, I returned from Cincinnati,
to warm up for Los Angeles. Godwin had developed an idea. He had been having
this feeling impossible to place. He thought his family in Lagos should get
together for thanksgiving at the end of November. He had $500 to burn. All
the Akukwes in Lagos would go to their local church, St. Anthony's Gbaja on
a Sunday morning for a high mass offered on their behalf. Then they would
retire to the father's house for feasting. How did we get the money through?
I telephoned my brother Charles, hitting his GSM. I asked him to leave the
Naira equivalence of $500 with Chief Akukwe. The details of what the money
was for would be sent by e-mail. Charles was visiting Onitsha but would
return to Lagos the weekend and go straight to Surulere to deliver Godwin's
message.
On the morning of Saturday October 19, Godwin had a story. He hadn't
been fair to me, he said. Since I was visiting, he should have given me a
treat well before now. He knew this place in Lexington. They offered choice
food and drinks in assorted variety. We would make the hour-long journey on
the night so he could give me "a real treat". I didn't see why I should not
support his scheme. After launch that day, he had to go to Elizabethtown,
ten minutes away. In his absence, I accessed my e-mails. And there it stood
starkly before me, the news I knew would come before long but which we
thought would tarry. "Dad", wrote my daughter Ijeoma. "Papa is dead. He died
last night in his sleep. Uncle Benjamin came this morning to tell Mummy."
The message was copied to Godwin's e-mail address.
I telephoned Lagos immediately and confirmed the terrible news. Part of
the message I left with Chief Akukwe household on August 22 was that Godwin
would be home in time for Christmas. It meant that the father could not
wait. When Godwin came back, he said he required some sleep. When he woke,
we would set out for Lexington. I remembered that when I first arrived, he
had looked me in the face and asked, "How is my father?" I had replied that
the man was fine but that the trot downhill had become all descent. For some
time, it had of course been clear to many that Chief Akukwe was on the way
to joining his ancestors. He had slowed down considerably; in fact he was
frail. I allowed Godwin a few minutes rest and requested to have a word. He
joined me in the living room and turned down the TV, which was saying
something about the Beltway sniper.
"I have news from Lagos", I said. "And t is not the best."
"What is it?
"Don't be alarmed", I told him. "But your father is dead." Godwin felt
like someone pole axed. He is a spitting image of his father, and they were
close. He placed his right palm on his temple, closed his eyes and reclined
on the chair. Two minutes later he sat up. "How did you get the news?"
The money sent through my brother became handy for organising the first
things about the Chief Akukwe's last rites. Godwin's anticipated
thanksgiving will take place, after all, from November 27. For if a Nigerian
lived to be 86, it called for thanks and praises to the One with the knife
and the yam. We, kindred spirits, would no longer sit in his porch and
sip. But the memory of our encounters that lasted for nearly 20 years would
linger. My one regret is that my hands would not be among those to carry his
casket through those elaborately carved gates that gave into his compound,
the ancestral home of his father Chief Ngwuakpu, who was the traditional
ruler of Eziowelle. But my mother would be there, 84 and vibrant. Do not be
surprised if she raised an arm of salutation and the place resonated with a
ditty to the deceased

Original_Search_Title: When Widowhood Tries to Rob You of Property
Headline: Uganda: When Widowhood Tries to Rob You of Property
Body_Text:
Kampala — Amina Awaazi is an unhappy woman. The death of her husband not only made her a widow, it also brought forth a new war.
Awaazi has to fight with some of her in laws for her marital property. It is a war that has been going on for the last eight years.
"I married my husband in 1981. It was a legal marriage in the local mosque," she says. He passed away in 1994. They had six children. Later on her husband Awaazi Gulume married two other women, with whom he had four children. A polygamous marriage was no problem for Amina, because the Islamic faith permits it.
Amina did not have any problem until the death of her husband. "I began cultivating yams and maize at the far end of the land to get food and money for my children. None of my in-laws was willing to help us out," she says.
Soon, the in-laws, led by a fiery sister Madina Namugwanyi ganged up against her. They told her to leave the land, because she did not have any right to it. The two other co-wives gave up immediately and went away, because they could not stand up against these in-laws. One of them has since died.
"Every day they come here and molest us. They threatened to put a padlock on the door to my house if I did not leave," Amina says. They accused her of stealing their brother's land. They also accused her of bringing other men into their brother's house. She denies all these allegations.
"I have never stolen any of this land, neither do I have any men, even if it is eight years since the death of my husband," Amina says.
She prepared herself for a fierce fight. On her side is the clan head. Several clan meetings to try and solve the problem have been held, but all of them are overridden by Namugwanyi. Namugwanyi and a brother-in-law threatened to beat her up if they ever saw her near her farm. "She stops all the other clan members from talking to me. Whoever supports me becomes her enemy," says the widow. "The clan head is no longer liked by the other group, because he supports me." When her husband died, he left her and the children in an incomplete house. He left some cattle and money in the bank, but all this she never saw. "We only saw this on paper, but it was taken by Madina and her group," Amina says.
In 1999, she decided to take her case to the Iganga district RDC, Mrs. Albina Opio. On October 15, same year, the RDC wrote a letter to the family members asking them to leave Amina alone. This was after the RDC invited Namugwanyi for arbitration, but she failed to show up.
"The letter from the RDC was passed on to every clan member. We thought the problem had been solved, but that was not the case. Madina still wanted to chase me away," Amina says. She kept on taunting her everyday. Meanwhile Amina fought back, not letting go of her rights.
At the beginning of this year, she brought a surveyor, who demarcated the land into six plots. She subsequently sold one of them to pay school fees for her children and complete the falling house. This drew more wrath from Namugwanyi.
"She immediately went to the DISO and told him that I was trying to steal their land. She even lied to him that my late husband was a soldier, and that I had never married him legally," Amina says.
When arbitration time came however, Amina Awaazi pulled out old photographs of their wedding. Madina was baffled. The DISO ruled in Amina's favour.
Awaazi has completed the house. Her children went back to school. But her in-laws are not happy. "They are asking me why I completed the house without permission from them," Amina says.
During the course of the construction of the house, Namugwanyi used several officials from the town council to molest her. These included the Rates officer Dan Waiswa, Land Patrol officer Robert Mwanda, Land supervisor Abu Muyinda and Engineer Robert Mwembe. When The New Vision contacted the council, the officer refused to comment on the issue.
Samson Muzeeyi, the clan head, said he has done everything to help the widow. "I think she would have been away by now, if it was not for my support."
Muzeeyi pointed out that there is absolutely no reason stopping Amina from selling part of the land, or developing it in any other way, since the land belonged to her husband. "As far as lam concerned, this is not clan or family land," he says.
The heir Abdalla Gulume is now 20 years and able to decide for himself. He said he supports his mother.
If it was clan or family land, Amina would have no right to sell part of it without consulting the clan or family. " This land was given to these children by their father. Each of them was given his or her area," Muzeeyi said. Adding, "There is no reason for any of the other children to come out and claim the widow's land, since she is keeping it in trustee for her children," he says.
Apparently, the clan leaders vote is only one, against the rest of the clan. "Madina manages to silence them because she has some little money," Amina says. Together with the clan head, the entire LC 1 council supports the widow. But as the case is, this is a clan affair, to which they can only offer arbitration. "We have talked and talked to Madina, but she has failed to listen," Khasifa Lwaga, secretary at the LC 1 council says.
Amina's children also support her. "We are very sick of our aunt's behaviour. We just call upon her to listen to our cries and keep away from our mother," Abdalla Guleme said. Jaliya, another of the children said, "None of those people can give you money when you go to them. Our mother has suffered a lot to see that we look like other children since our father died. Let them leave us alone."

Original_Search_Title: How This Teenager Escaped From a Kidnapper
Headline: Nigeria: How This Teenager Escaped From a Kidnapper
Body_Text:
A 13-year-old boy who lives with his elder cousin at Asaba, the Delta State capital, escaped from a man who had earlier on kidnapped him at gun point. Ugwoke and his aunt narrated their experiences to Okey Ndiribe who was at Asaba recently.
Okwudili Ugwoke had just walked down Celestial Church Street, Asaba, to buy some chewing gum. Afterwards, he decided to take a walk to the intersection of a major road in that area of the city. As he was walking along the road, a Peugeot 504 station wagon car with tinted glasses suddenly stopped beside him. The man behind the wheel instantly pointed a gun at Okwudili and ordered him to get into the car or get shot. The youngster froze. His eyes darted to the left and right looking for an escape route. But the kidnapper who had a fierce glint in his eyes, guessed what was going on in Okuwdili's mind and repeated his order in a very harsh voice. His legs wobbling, Okwudili climbed into the car and the kidnapper sped off with him.
Speaking to our reporter about his experience, Okwudili said the kidnapper held the gun with his right hand while he held the steering with the left. He continued: "As the man (his captor) drove the car towards the River Niger bridge, it had a fault. He then drove into a mechanic's workshop to repair the car."
Okwudili summoned a rare courage for a child of his age after he got over the initial shock of being kidnapped in broad daylight. He began to plot his escape. Said he: "While he was repairing the car, he had an argument with the mechanics. It was then that I quietly opened the car door, sneaked out from the car and ran away." And after his miraculous escape at the mechanic's workshop, the teenager ran as fast as his legs could carry him. "As I was running away, I saw some yam sellers near the bridge. As I was there, I saw the kidnapper again when he drove past towards Onitsha at top speed. I shouted that this was the man who attempted to take me and that he's carrying a gun."
Still terrified, the yam sellers then told the young Okwudili to stay with the night guards in that area for the night. They also told him that in the morning, he would be taken away from there. That night, he slept at a yam seller's shed. According to him, "they fed me and also rubbed a local medicine on me. They told me that it was to prevent anybody from kidnapping me again."
The following morning, the yam sellers made good their promise and took Okwudili to Onitsha. According to the teenager, "in the morning, they came and took me in a car to Onitsha. I met somebody in the car who asked me where I hailed from. I told him that I am a native of Nsukka. The man asked me to describe the section of Nsukka I came from. I did. He told me that he knew that area but that he wasn't going to Nsukka at that time."
But the man promised to take him to the studio of Anambra Broadcasting Service (ABS) at Onitsha. "This man and the first person who accompanied me from the yam sellers area at Asaba, took me to the studio of ABS," he said. When this young kidnap victim got to ABS and on enquiry, established his identity, he also disclosed the profession of his elder cousin and guardian. "I told them that he works in a bank. They asked me the name of the bank. I could not remember the name of the bank, but I told them what I knew is that, he works in a bank whose vehicles are painted in red stripes. The man said that he knew the bank."
After this initial interrogation, Okwudili was then taken into another section of the studio where he was shown to the viewing public on the screen. Radio announcements were also broadcast. And shortly after the broadcast, his elder cousin arrived the studio and took him away. "I was very happy to meet my people again. I thank God for saving me from that man who wanted to take me away by force. I don't know what he would have done to me and that's why I thank God for saving me."
For Okwudili's auntie, Mrs Chinweuba Ugwoke, it was a traumatic experience while Okwudili's sudden disappearance lasted. When the young boy didn't return home the night he was kidnapped, his aunt initially thought that it was a bad dream that would soon be over. She had heard stories of how children were kidnapped but never imagined she would face a similar situation. So, when she came back from her office to charge her car battery at a nearby battery charger's place, and didn't meet her young brother-in-law, she thought that he may have gone to play in the neighbourhood.
