And so I began to ponder over the assignment: to cover a war? What if the unfortunate happens? God forbid, I quickly reflected in my mind, apparently trying to appease myself.
So my thoughts continued to wander for the next eight days, until I eventually set out to travel on Friday, October 4, 2002. This state of mind was not because this was to be my first time of covering a crisis situation. No. At least, I had covered a few potentially dangerous crises locally, particularly those that have to do with religious diversities between Muslims and Christians. These include the Salka religious disturbances in Niger State in 2000, the trouble over the beheading of the Chief of Azara in Nasarawa State last year, and the recent ethno-religious conflicts in Shendam Local Government of Plateau State.
One particular source of concern for me, however, was that while my previous experiences of covering war situations were local or restricted to Nigeria, this time around it involved some foreign land where I particularly envisaged confronting a problem of communication with the people, since I never had course to learn French for even one day. Again, I pondered seriously over the mode of transportation - by road to Ouagadougou and back. Indeed, I had cause to ask myself if this was going to be an odyssey for me.
But here too I was enthused by some words of the Editor-in-Chief exactly one week before the trip. He said to me: "It will really turn out to be an interesting experience for you." This brief comment was to be my major source of strength and encouragement even where I encountered a number of difficulties in the course of my assignment. This was particularly more so when I met a few hostile people, and when I realised that I did not speak with a single key government functionary to strengthen my report, and I was the running short of money.
Indeed, it was quite exciting as the Editor-in-Chief said, particularly while conversing with a people who do not understand one's language, and at the same time one does not understand theirs.
And so the trauma began when I got to a village called Kalgo, about 11 kilometres before Birnin Kebbi in Kebbi State. There was no vehicle to proceed to Kamba, the border town between Nigeria and Niger, and there was no hotel to lodge in. Of course, the next logical conclusion was to go to the nearest Mosque, as a Muslim, and stay there. But what if some robbers or thieves steal in and cart away my bag with all the money inside? But God came to the rescue.
Then, I made a very silly mistake! Forgetting to change the Naira to CFA at either Jega or Kamba until at Melbou, a village in Niger where I wanted to buy tea. Well, I had to pay a price for that by changing the currency at N10 more than the normal rate. Meanwhile, there was a small encounter with a Nigerien Immigration official at Kamba, who nearly insulted me for not being prepared to do away with some N40 as every other passenger in the vehicle. He was, however, quick to apologise when he realised I was a journalist who had all his travel documents.
But I was soon to realise that the Nigerien frontier Gendarmes and their own police, are probably as corrupt as our own here.
On reaching Niamey, the capital of Niger, I realised there was no vehicle going direct to Ouagadougou. And none was going to Kantchari, a border town mid-way into the journey either. So, another 24 hours was spent. Our bus to Ouagadougou took off at about 3:30 p.m. on Sunday, October 6. Inspite of my initial resolve not to dish out a dime as tip-off to any government official on the road, I was made to part with CFA 1000 at an unscrupulous Niger frontier at Makalondi, Niger's part of the border between it and Burkina Faso.
Suddenly, however, I realised I was amidst the 'honest people' (Burkinabes) when we got to Kantchari, Burkina Faso's part of the border with Niger. After spending another night at Kantchari, we arrived at Ouagadougou at about 3 p.m. on Monday, October 5, 2002.
An almost flat country, Burkina Faso is sandwiched between the humid southern countries (Cote d'Ivoire, Ghana, Togo and Benin) and the Sahelo-Saharan countries in the north (Niger and Mali). Located in the heart of West Africa, the country is inhabited by over sixty different ethnic groups including the Mossi, Fulani, Gourmantche, Bobo, Gouronugi, Lobi, Senoufo and Samo. It however adopted only three, Dioula, Maore and Fulani as its national spoken languages apart from French, which serves as the official language of communication.
Although Burkina Faso is a landlocked country, its nomendature is still favourable for growing several crops like guinea corn, maize, yam, rice and several cash crops.
However, its economy depends largely on exports and imports to and from Europe, Cote d'Ivoire, Ghana, Togo and Benin republic. This probably explains why the country is also severely affected by the current crisis in Cote d'Ivoire, which has inhibited imports from, and exports to that country, from Burkina Faso.
As for the people, like Sankara named them, they exhibit a reasonable level of honesty and modesty in their dealings. In the towns, villages and at all levels of the social hierarchy, relations between the Burkinabe and foreigners are most courteous and without affection. In the restaurants, markets and corridors of the ministries, visitors are warmly welcomed
In fact, one writer argued that: "One would be hard pushed to find a kinder group of people in Africa than the affable, smiling and extremely obliging people of Burkina Faso." This could have been exaggerated to some extent. But it still paints a good picture of what a visitor should expect in that country. The people are also of very proud of their country, probably because of the relative sincerity of their leaders.
It is noteworthy that inspite of the people's pride in their country, they hold certain countries in Africa, most especially Nigeria, in high esteem. Thus, wherever I introduce myself as a Nigerian, the quick reaction is: Oh, Nigerian? Or, you are from Nigeria? If I nod, then at each point they will say Bonjour.
Burkina Faso is not just a land of honest people, but also of arts and craft. From the buildings, they are mostly beautifully designed as in the case of the University of Ouagadougou hostels, the people's shrines etc, etc. It also has remarkable highlands such as the Falaise de Gobnangou (Gobnangou Escarpment), Falaise de Bonfora, and numerous others.
The unique arts and craft of the people is also displayed through their modes of dressing, their hairdo, and their women's handbags, etc, etc.
It may also interest many readers to know that as a very modest country, there is no clear-cut distinction between the upper, middle and lower classes in that society, as virtually everybody owns at least a motor-cycle or bicycle that conveys him to where he earns a living. Indeed, there is no such craze to acquire wealth (lawfully or unlawfully) as in some countries. Almost everybody's ambition is to either educate himself/herself or do anything that would promote the overall growth and development of that country.
Also, the modesty of the people reflects in their style of living, as even ministers in that country move about in single cars without pilot cars.
However, although there are many Muslims in Burkina Faso, there is no concerted effort to uphold the doctrines of Sharia like you find in say Nigeria. This, perhaps, stems from the fact that there appears to be many nominal Muslims in the country. This probably explains why both male and female students live in the same hostel, share the same bathrooms and toilets, but with different rooms.
In the final analysis however, Burkina Faso is a model of a fine African country with a rich cultural heritage.

Headline: Nigeria: The Richness of Potatoes
Body_Text:
Lagos — A significant observation at the markets visited last weekend showed
the abundance of potatoes, an edible starchy tuber.
Potatoes produced by certain plants of the genus solanum, with its
entrance into the market, brings some respite to consumers who prefer
it to yam because it packs a lot of nutrients.
Potatoes come in different species like the white potato, the common
potato, the Irish potatoes and the sweet potato. All these species
are highly digestible, they supply amino acids, protein, thiamin and
nicotinic acid.
According to Mr. Adedamola Bello, a food technologist, potatoes, if
cooked in their skins contains and give one third more fibre than
peeled potatoes.
According to him, cooking potatoes with their skin preserves the
nutrients found just under the skin.
Potatoes, according to a cook, can be utilised and prepared in
several ways. It can be boiled, baked, roasted and grilled among
others.
According to Mrs. Joe Siju, a trained nutritionist, who works as a
cook with a fast food company"warm salad can be made out of
potato.Others are potato chips, baked potato, salamander, potato
pottage, potato fish soup and so on".
Mrs. Joe explained how potatoes make a big difference in terms of
what it contribute to consumers' diet. For instance, potato fish soup
can be perfectly achieved by peeling, washing and boiling for 10
minutes in 3 cups of water, before draining and grating.
Boil 3 cups of water, add smoked fish, onions, dried pepper and salt
to taste, stir and allow to boil for 10 minutes. Reduce heat and add
the ground crayfish, grated potato, efirin leaves and allow to simmer
for 5 minutes until it thickens a little bit.
Mrs. Joe advises consumers to serve the potato fish soup hot for
delightful taste.

Headline: Ghana: Yam Export Business Under Threat
Body_Text:
...due to activities of cocaine smugglers
COCAINE EXPORTERS who smuggle the illicit drug among boxes of yam, virtually abandon the thousands of tons of yam when they arrive in London in the United Kingdom, content with the huge sums of money they make from selling the stuff.
This is threatening the yam export business, one of the major items on Ghana's non-traditional exports list and worried dealers say government must act now. Should the yam export trade collapse, the producers, largely peasants in the north and the Afram Plains, will be impoverished under slumping producer price, it was further learnt.
A research Chronicle conducted, following a tip-off, indicated that the popular "pona" yams cost more at the main yam market in Accra, the Konkomba Market, than it does in London. A hundred (100) tubers of that yam species cost ¢600,000 at Konkomba. That means 10 of the tubers cost approximately £5.00 while a single tuber costs 50 pence there.
Various costs of adding value and handling push the cost of yams being exported to London up. Clearing, wrapping and packaging into cartons, binding, weighing, loading, transportation to the port and off-loading are among 10 processes that increase the cost of every carton of the tubers by £1.00. And the empty carton costs £1.15.
A yam exporter pays about £2.00 as the freight cost (FOB) of one carton of yam which contains 10 tubers from Tema to London. These add to make the value of one carton of yams £8.00 and, assuming the exporter adds a margin 25 per cent on it, the value of the carton should be at least £12.00.
The price of one carton of 10 tubers of "pona" today on the market of London is £5.00, £1.00 less than what it is really worth.
The confounding reality is that far from discouraging the yam exporters from shipping more yams to the UK and other European markets, the glut actually seems to be encouraging some of them to send more yams.
Chronicle learnt that those mindless of the colossal losses incurred are those who excavate hollows in the tubers and push rolls of cocaine inside.
It was learnt that among the about 1,000 cartons of yam that each of such criminal exporters posts, only two to four of the boxes may be containing the powdered substance. But if they are able to pull those few boxes out and land them successfully in the hands of their drug clients in London, the worth of the cocaine far exceeds the value of the rest of the yam.
"In some cases, they literally abandon the whole pack of yams to rot in the markets, content with their drug booty," one frustrated yam dealer added.
Chronicle search for the root cause of the problem established that for some time now the export of some non-traditional crops, particularly yams, has been too closely co-ordinated and monitored.
Anybody at all can jump into the trade, virtually uninhibited and whether they really export yams or something else no one checks seriously," was another exporter's lamentation.

Original_Search_Title: Tinubu to Inaugurate Justice Group Soon
Headline: Nigeria: Tinubu to Inaugurate Justice Group Soon
Body_Text:
Lagos — Lagos State Governor, Bola Ahmed Tinubu, said that his government has concluded plans to inaugurate Justice Group among the major ethnic groups in the state.
Tinubu disclosed this over the weekend through the Secretary to the State Government, Alhaji Aro Lambo, who represented the governor at this year's annual Igbo New Yam Festival organised by the Eze Igbo of Lagos State, Eze H. N. Ohazurike, held at the State Grammar School, at Ijaiye area of the state.
The essence of inaugurating the Justice Group, Tinubu said, was to explore opportunities of interactions with the ethnic groups to forestall peace, unity and progress, adding that the opportunity of meeting with the Igbo "today" had been a much needed opportunity he has long been nurtured. He described Eze Ohazurike's address which centred on peace and justice among one another as timely.
He also informed Ndigbo in the state that the suspended breakfast meeting would soon resume with the governor at Marina Lagos.
He promised that there would be more progress come next year in the country and urged all Nigerians to denounce violence at all levels.
The festival was used to confer chieftaincy titles on 12 deserving Igbo women and six deserving men, with Chief M.C.K. Ajuluchukwu and Dr Ozuo Okoye, the traditional prime minister of Igboland, who represented Chief Odimegwu Ojukwu.

Headline: Nigeria: Tinubu Reassures Non-Indigenes
Body_Text:
LAGOS State Government has restated its resolve to co-operate with non-indigenes to foster unity. Governor Bola Tinubu, reiterated this at the weekend during the Iriji (New Yam) festival put together by Ndi-Igbo resident in the state.
Represented by the Secretary to the Government (SSG), Omotilewa Aro-Lambo, the governor implored Ndi-Igbo to always pursue the path of justice, saying it is the thinking of veritable leaders.
Gov. Tinubu said a platform for the coalition of stakeholders called the Justice Group was in the making, and pledged that the three major tribes in the country would be represented in it.
The relationship, he said, was to ensure that the tribes were fully represented in the scheme of things.
Speaking on the occasion, the Eze Ndigbo of Lagos, Eze Nwabueze Ohazulike, recounted the significance of the festival, saying it is a celebration of bounteous planting and harvesting season.
He added that the event provided Ndi-Igbo the opportunity to promote and acquaint themselves with their culture as well as for the protection of their language.
Chief Ohazulike who expressed dismay that some Igbo sons and daughters could not speak the Igbo language, pointed out that a people without a culture were akin to a dead race.
Eze Ohazulike who performed the purity and love rites (Ika Nzu) along with other traditional rulers at the state High School, Oke Oba, Ifako-Ijaiye, a Lagos suburb equally prayed for the peace, stability and togetherness, not just for the Igbo but for the nation.
He challenged the citizenry to respect constituted authorities, just as he enjoined politicians to make justice, transparency and equity their watchwords.
Earlier, Eze Ohazulike had bestowed traditional titles on some Igbo sons and daughters among them six men and 12 women.
Eminent personalities partook in the celebration. They included a representative of the Ikemba of Nnewi, Dim Chukwuemeka Odumegwu-Ojukwu, Onowu Ozua Okoye, frontline politician, Chief M.C.K. Ajuluchukwu, Managing Director of Ausken Royal Furniture Limited, Chief Austen Okolie and the Ezeike iv of Nibo, Anambra State, Eze (Dr) M.C. Ngene, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Akumazi And the Season of Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Akumazi And the Season of Yam
Body_Text:
Lagos — Among the Igbo, yam holds a pride of place in the midst all other crops. It is the king of crops and it is celebrated as such. The importance attached to yam can be appreciated when one considers the fact that in Igbo cosmology, a deity is dedicated to yam. To some Igbo groups, this god of yam is called Njoku and in some other places it is known as Ajoku. Yet others call it Fejioku or Ahiajoku. They all refer to one and the same thing. The difference can be understood in the context of different dialects that inundate Igboland.
After harvest every year, ceremonies are held to thank the god of yam for bountiful harvest and to pray for good harvest the following year.
In recent years, the new yam festival, as the celebration is called, has been taken beyond Igboland. The perspective has equally changed. Beyond the aspect of ritual and tradition, new yam festivals these days are now used as opportunity to bring communities together and forge relationships among people. It also serves as a forum to launch self-help development projects.
That is precisely what the Akumazi community in Lagos will be doing on Saturday at the premises of NTA Channel 10, Surulere, Lagos. Akumazi Umuocha is in the Ika Northeast Local Government Area of Delta State.
In an interview with THISLIFE in Lagos, Dr Kester Nwadiani, president of Akumazi Progressive Union (APU), Lagos branch, said the new yam festival to be held by the association in Lagos on Saturday is a celebration of life rather than death as is always the case.
Traditionally, he noted that new yam festival is celebrating bountiful harvest every year.
According to him, those who are titled in the traditonal sense don't need to start eating new yam until the festival is held. For others too, he said, it is a time to offer sacrifices to the gods.
The main objective for the celebration, Nwadiani said, was for APU to give awards to members of the association who had served the Lagos branch as presidents of either the men's wing or the women's wing. In addition, some other individuals who had helped the association in some other ways would be recognised.
On the origin of the association in Lagos, Nwadiani said it was founded in 1925. He said APU, Lagos branch was in the process of acquiring land to build is permanent meeting place in Lagos. The association, he said, is open to all indigenes of Akumazi in Lagos who are 18 years old and above.
The ceremony, according to him, would begin at 10 am.

Headline: Uganda: Food Prices Push Up Inflation
Body_Text:
Kampala — The prices of most food items locally, have shot up due to the prolonged dry spell experienced this year.
Matooke prices have risen from sh2500 to sh4000 and sh5000 for a medium sized bunch.
Prices for cassava, Irish potatoes, sweet potatoes, and yams have also gone up by sh500 from sh1000 a heap and passion fruits and peas have gone up by sh1000 per kilogramme. Meat and fish prices were the first to be hiked.
Scovia Nakatte, a vendor at Nakawa market attributed the increases to the dry spell that has delayed harvesting.
"The general explanation is that when it normally gets to the dry spell, food supplies decline and the prices go up. We are competing with exporters who offer better rates and always go for the best," she said.
The hike is also attributed to increased fresh produce exports and cross border trade.
This recent increase in food prices has for the first time pushed Uganda's inflation level above the one percent mark. Formerly cheap food kept the inflation rate down.

Headline: Nigeria: The Comeback King
Body_Text:
Everyone in England is thrilled by the return to form of Arsenal and
Super Eagles striker, Nwankwo Kanu. Except, of course, opposing
defences and goalkeepers.
Arsene Wenger, coach of Arsenal F.C. in the English Premiership, is
having a swell time. So is Nwankwo Kanu, his rangy striker, whose
five goals have contributed in no small way to Arsenal's unbeaten run
in the current season.
Wenger, Kanu and Arsenal have good reasons to feel good. They have
been winning matches and in style. The result is the pole position in
the league and superlative performances on the European circuit.
Kanu is the one hugging the headlines, and justifiably so.
The leggy forward appears to be enjoying a new lease of life at his
London club. Rising from the substitute bench to emerge as one of the
leading goal scorers at Highbury, he has once again defied the odds
against him.
At the beginning of the 2002/2003 season, manager, Arsene Wenger,
wanted to off-load him to any willing club; the English tabloid
labelled him a player collecting $40,000 weekly to sit on the bench.
But, despite having an indifferent year in Arsenal's domination of
the soccer scene last season, Kanu took everything in his stride
saying, "When I want to leave, I'll tell everyone that it's time to
go, but I still want to win the champions league with the team."
Kanu got his chance in the match against Jay Jay Okocha's Bolton
Wanderers where he scored not only his first goal of the season, but
the all important winning goal on away soil.
He followed this feat with a brace, yet another 4-2 away victory
against Leeds United. He was on song for both the first and last
goals.
And just like last week where he featured prominently in Arsenal's
1-0 away victory over Auxerre in the prestigious UEFA Champions
League, the two-time African Footballer of the Year was in action
throughout the match in which French international, Patrick Viera,
drew the last blood.
The gangling striker, who has been consistently linked with a move to
several European clubsides, the most recent being the Kevin
Keegan-tutored Manchester City, over the weekend, was on song twice
under ten minutes in a 3-1 whitewash of shapeless Sunderland.
Perhaps Kanu's strikes against the relegation-threatened side played
a part in the club's management's decision to sack its coach, Peter
Reid.
His goals not only took Arsenal's unbeaten run to 48 consecutive
games, but, his second strike was adjudged as Goal of the Week by the
British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC).
According to the BBC, "Kanu's goal was quite simple in its execution,
but the making of the goal, Kanu's fifth in five games, made it the
standout strike of the week." The radio station described it as a
'golden goal' in a special piece entitled 22 Steps to Heaven.
In a volte-face, Arsene Wenger, who had wanted to off-load the
gazelle-limbed Nigerian export, has been pouring encomiums on him for
taking his chance.
"The most important thing is that we have 20 quality players, it
doesn't matter who comes in. He has always shown a great attitude and
I have got a lot of respect for that. It's not been easy for him as
he's got a world-class player fighting for his position."
Kanu's footsteps had always been dodged with career-threatening
issues. In 1996, he was diagnosed as having a faulty valve in his
heart, went through surgery and came back to hit the headlines as one
of Africa's soccer greats.
His misfortune then, he turned into a fortune for other Nigerian kids
suffering heart defects with the establishment of the Kanu Heart
Foundation.
The foundation has catered for scores of Nigerian youths, who have
been flown to Israel for corrective surgery.
Though Kanu is a big hit in Europe, his biggest achievements so far
in Nigeria are his gold medals of Japan '93 U-17 championship and the
gold medal run at the 1996 Olympic Games in Atlanta, before the heart
problem was discovered.
However, Arsenal's 3-1 win over Sunderland at Highbury was the team's
30th consecutive Premiership result without a loss, a record
previously set at 29 by Manchester United.
Now that they have the Premiership record, the Gunners, with Kanu in
tow, are eyeing the all-time top flight run of 42 games held by Brian
Clough's legendary Forest side of the late 70s.
Arsenal's unbeaten record, according to Arsene Wenger, is a pointer
to the fact that the team is consistent and wants to win every game.
"The record we have broken is a big one and we are proud of that. We
gave the right ambition and spirit. We'll try to keep the run going
and do what we want to do," Wenger disclosed.
Interestingly, Dennis Bergkham, whose injury gave Kanu the needed
opportunity to showcase his potentiality after being written off,
might have to sit it out on the sideline until he is fully fit to
break into the team with Kanu's graceful run and record of notching
up five goals in five matches.
The smile is off the face of Austin Otoku, former skipper of the
national soccer team and coach of Union Bank FC of Lagos, as he
wastes away from an attack of stroke and without help from his former
employers. BABS OYETORO met him
The smile is off the face of Austin Otoku, former skipper of the
national soccer team and coach of Union Bank FC of Lagos, as he
wastes away from an attack of stroke and without help from his former
employers. BABS OYETORO met him
y problem started on 6 December 2001. Before this problem began, I
was looking ahead to the new season with a great expectation. It was
this problem that shattered my dreams for that year.
As the season was about to start then, I decided that my boys should
play a pre-season friendly match against a Lagos-based team - the
Athletico F.C. - as a tune-up.
The match started on a high tempo and before we could settle down,
the Athletico boys had scored. We were one goal down.
Not quite long after, a through pass was floated into our vital area.
As my goal keeper went for the ball in the air - all this while I was
on my feet watching the proceedings from the bench - all I saw around
our goal post was sun and a big yam.
The next thing that happened was that the ball sailed into our net
for the second goal. Thereafter, my legs started wobbling. I fell
down and I became unconscious. My players on the field came to my aid
and I was lifted up and taken to the hospital.
I was the only one that saw these objects. The whole thing was like a
spiritual attack directed at only me.
I didn't suspect that anybody was behind this attack. I have always
been a Christian. I don't have to suspect anybody. And I strongly
believe that my God will deliver me.
At the beginning of the problem, I was taken to Kent Hospital in
Keffi, Lagos, where I was on admission for four days. I was later
discharged from that hospital. When my condition did not improve,
Union Bank took me to another hospital - Obalen Aje Hospital,
Obanikoro. I was finally discharged on 19th March, 2002. While I was
in that hospital, my wife signed three different bills given to me by
the hospital management before they were later sent to my employers.
The first bill was for N57,000 and the second bill was N58,000 while
the third bill was for N38,400. At the first hospital we attended in
Keffi, my wife was not given any bill to sign, it was only my former
assistant coach, Banwo (now the substantive coach) that showed the
bill of N40,000 to my wife. Surprisingly, I got to know through one
of the staff that Union Bank had expended the sum of N1m on my
treatment.
Immediately I was discharged from the second hospital, Union Bank
withdrew completely from my treatment. It was through the former
coach that we got to know that my appointment was terminated and that
my salary had been stopped since December last year.
From the period I returned from the hospital, the Union Bank FC
management has deliberately kept me at an arm's length. My wife had
made frantic efforts to reach the management but the security men at
the gate would not allow her. I am sure these people are acting on
instructions.
It is painful to see myself in this condition, having spent some
months in the hospital. I had a relapse because I could not continue
with the treatment, since I don't have the money.
It's my Church, Shepherd Hill Baptist Church, Obanikoro, that has
taken over the responsibility for my medical treatment and even
feeding. They took me to Gbagada General Hospital for treatment. I
was only discharged on 17 June.
My problem with the management, according to the team manager, Mr.
Tunde Popoola, arose from the fact that I informed the Lagos State
Football Association and the Nigeria Football Association of my
predicament. But let me put the record straight. I did not report
Union Bank to any of these bodies. The Lagos State FA officials got
to know about this by chance. And it was through them that the NFA
got to know about it too.
I doubt if there is anything wrong about it if these people or bodies
get to know about my problems. I have served this nation in the past.
Unknown to many people, I captained the Nigerian Olympics football
team to Mexico 68. I was in the team that played a 3-3 draw with
Brazil that year. I was the captain of the national team that played
Ethiopia at the Liberty Stadium, Ibadan. The likes of the late
Oppone, Tony Igwe and Peter Aneike were my team mates. So, if these
people get to know about my predicament, what is the big deal about
that? Should anybody use that against me when I am already dying? I
think what is important now is my survival.
Honestly, I don't deserve what I am getting at the moment, having
served this nation and Union Bank FC faithfully. Look, I brought
Union Bank from the amateur division to the professional cadre. I won
the Lagos State Challenge Cup for them in 2000. I won the Central
Bank Cup for them in 1999.
When I was with the Stationery Stores FC of Lagos, I won the national
Challenge Cup for them in 1950. Ike Shorunmu was my goalkeeper. We
lost the Mandela Cup at the semi-final to Zamalek of Egypt in Lagos.
I have paid my dues as far as football is concerned. But I don't pray
I end my life this way.
I have only two children. The two of them are boys. They are both
graduates but are currently jobless. The small salary their mother is
getting from her place of work is what we're depending on. And this
meagre income cannot sustain all of us.
In fact, I am using this medium to appeal to the Union Bank to look
away from whatever "sin" they claimed I have committed and come to my
aid. I also appeal to well-meaning Nigerians to come to my
assistance. They should not allow me to waste away like this."

Headline: Uganda: Urban Farm Becomes Resource Center
Body_Text:
Kampala — FRANCIS Gonahasa owns a three- and-a-quarter-acre piece of land in Busega, near Kampala. With it, he and his wife have managed to practise small-scale farming.
Whereas land in Kampala is scarce, Gonahasa revealed that he has managed to plant over 20 different crops on his small piece of land. "Many people in the city think they can not carry out farming because they lack land. Our home has now become a resource centre to train women in agricultural skills," he said.
Women together for Development Uganda (WOTODEV) a Non-Governmental Organisation train from Gonahasa's farm.
On October 4, Zoe Bakoko Bakoru, gender, labour and social development toured Gonahasa's farm before she gave out awards to WOTODEV women from Kampala and Wakiso districts.
On top of growing crops, Gonahasa rears animals. The crops grown include cabbage, cassava, matooke, spinach, yams, oranges, passion fruit, among others. Despite the dry season, all the crops The New Vision saw looked healthy.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria Must Revamp Its Real Sector to Survive - Jamodu
Headline: Nigeria Must Revamp Its Real Sector to Survive - Jamodu
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Minister of Industry, Chief Jamodu has said that for Nigeria to survive economically it imperative that she must revamp her industrial sector which is presently in shatters.
Jamodu raised this point at the National Symposium on Revamping Industrialisation of the Nigerian Economy, organised by Standards Organisation of Nigeria (SON) in consonance with the 2002 Nigeria Quality Week and World Standards Day Celebration.
Speaking at the occasion held at the National Art Theatre over the weekend, the minister who was represented by Mr. Damian Agbanelo, Head, Quality Control, Ministry of Industry, maintained that by resurrecting its industrial power Nigeria can absolve the army of the unemployed in her streets.
In his paper entitled: "Power Supply and Industrialisation in Nigeria, Which Way Forward," Engr. J. Mokoju, Managing Director of NEPA, posited that power supply is critical to industrial development and that his organisation is primed to rise to the challenge of improving the power situation to enhance industrial growth.
However, he stressed that the power sector is very capital intensive, arguing that this mandates the urgent need to increase private participation in the sector to ensure success.
According to him, "this will serve two important purposes. First, the government will be able to free up funds for investments in sectors such as health, education, security.
Director-General of Raw Materials Research and Development Council, Alhaji Abubakar Abdullahi in his paper, "The Strategic Race of Raw Materials Development in Industrial Growth, The Nigerian Experience," pointed out that Nigeria is adequately blessed to attain industrial mastery and economic buoyancy but that poor management has been the bane of the country.
For instance, he said despite being the world's largest producer of yam and cassava the nation has never thought to enhance its capacity in these products in order to generate export market and its attendant foreign revenue.
Abdullahi called for government support of processing of local food condiments and fruits such as tomato, mango, orange, saying that this would save the nation million of dollars in foods/fruit that go waste as a result of lack of processing equipment.
He praised efforts by government to save local industries through import restrictions, saying this has helped many industries, although some loopholes such as the porous borders and unwholesome practices need to be properly addressed.

Headline: Nigeria: Jos Market Traders Face Hardship
Body_Text:
Jos — Traders at the Jos ultra modern market gutted by fire about eight months ago have continued to suffer untold hardship.
An investigation carried out by Daily Trust revealed that most traders who usually display their wares in the middle of the road are either stampeded by the heavy traffic flow or have lost most of the their customers who no longer know their location.
They also complained that heavy downpour in the metropolis has constituted another great source of concern to them. This is because they no longer have a place to keep their goods, which are mostly perishable.
The development has also affected the prices of goods in the market as the prices of goods have continued to soar high. A survey revealed that prices of commodities like gari, local rice, potatoes, coco yam etc have sky-rocketed.

Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Thousands Flee Bouake
Body_Text:
Tiebessou — The number of people fleeing the town of Bouake in central Cote d'Ivoire rose from 100 a day on Wednesday to 2,500 on Friday and 5,000 per day on Saturday and Sunday, according to French army officers in Tiebissou, about 100 km south of Bouake. There were
also reports that other people had fled to the nearby town of Didievi.
However, the total number of people displaced in central Cote d'Ivoire since September, when insurgents occupied Bouake and towns farther north, was still unclear. A United Nations inter-agency mission which travelled on Thursday from Abidjan to the capital, Yamoussoukro, and then proceeded to Bouake saw
hundreds of people walking or boarding the few vehicles available in small towns along the way.
Most of the displaced were Ivorians although some 156 people from Burkina Faso sought shelter at the Catholic mission in Tiebissou. They said they had been displaced from their homes in villages around the town.
Sources in towns located between Yamoussoukro and Bouake provided figures on displaced persons they had managed to register, noting that most of the displaced were in transit. "The majority of them stop by for up to three
days and then move on," one such source told IRIN.
In Brobo, about 35 km from Bouake, the French military said some 10,000 people had arrived in the town on Friday. Up to 10 October, the Catholic church in Brobo was assisting 2,000 displaced persons, the parish priest said. Thousands of others sat by the roadside and any available open space as they waited to move on. The number of people passing through Brobo each day between 26 September and 10 October averaged about 4,000 to 5,000, sources said.
M'bahiakro, about 90 km from Bouake, received between 2,000 and 2,500 people on 11 October. Officials at the Catholic mission in the town told IRIN they had assisted some 4,739 people between 28 September and 10 October.
In all the transit points the most pressing needs were food, health care, protection, water and sanitation.
Armed men - who, according to government sources included mutinous soldiers and mercenaries - occupied Bouake after an aborted coup attempt in Abidjan on 19 September. All businesses and offices in Bouake have been closed since then. The market was virtually empty with only a few people selling yams and onions.
"We cannot buy anything because we have no money," a resident told IRIN."Most of us are just thinking of how to leave this place because life is becoming impossible."
The central hospital was operating on skeleton staff and there was no food for the patients. "We were forced to start cooking only for the staff of 80 but we will close the kitchen this evening [Saturday] because we have run out of stock," said a staff member.
Humanitarian agencies were responding by providing medicines and food. The World Food Programme (WFP) said on Sunday it had airlifted a tonne of high-protein biscuits to Yamoussoukro in a bid to bring urgently needed food rations to the displaced. The supplies, WFP said, came from humanitarian stocks in Guinea. It said the biscuits were essential during the first days of displacement to avoid malnutrition among women and children in
particular.
The agency planned to establish a logistics base in Yamoussoukro so as to better respond to the "unfolding crisis in the northern areas of the country".
WFP also said more than 2,000 displaced persons in Yamoussoukro and areas around Bouake had received "urgently needed" food rations and that the International Committee of the Red Cross had begun distributing WFP food aid to vulnerable groups in Bouake itself on Sunday.

Headline: Nigeria: Dialogue On $1.5 Billion Loot
Body_Text:
Lagos — "Did you hear the amount of dollars government is expecting to recover from the Abacha family in the money- for-freedom settlement?" "Men! $1.5 billion cool! I tried inputting that figure into my calculator and the poor machine almost crashed".
"That means your calculator is using acid or kerosene instead of battery?" "Why blame my calculator? Can't you see that the money is huge? Have you attempted converting the funds into Naira? I bet, your computer will surely hang up if you attempt it."
"It looks to me like a fairy tale. How did Abacha steal so much money? This is in spite of the money already recovered by General Abubakar's regime, and the ones yet unknown." "I hear the total amount stolen from the nations coffers is about $4 billion. The $1.5 billion is from the funds traced to bank accounts in Europe minus $100 million settlement for the Abacha family."
"So, that man who was arresting and locking up 419 people and bank directors for several years was himself helping himself with the nation's treasury? What an irony of fate!" "$ 1.5 billion! The Naira equivalent is 187.5 billion at the rate of N125 to a dollar! " " Impossible, N187.5 billion! What was he going to do with all that money?" "If you distribute that money to the 88 million Nigerians, each person will be smiling to the bank with about N2,130."
"Do you know the level of economic activity that money would have generated in Nigeria if it was made available to the common folks? Each person receiving N2,130 will turn our economy into another Japan. That is the fastest way to eradicate poverty in Nigeria forever!" "And also eradicate armed robbery and all the other vices plaguing our country today. In fact, the man's Family Economic Advancement Programme, FEAP, would have been most effective if he had supported the families by sharing part of his loot with them."
"At a minimum annual interest rate of 10%, that N187.5 billion will be generating about N19 billion every year for the family. So even if the Abachas spend N1 million daily for their needs, they will only spend N365 million in a year, which is not even up to half a billion. That means it will take about 51 years to finish the first year's interest earned on the loot. In the meantime, interest is accumulating on the principal for the 51 years at the rate of N19 billion per annum, i.e. N969 billion. "
"But the interest will also earn interest in the usual banking fashion?"
"Yes. It means that the whole generation of Abachas, to the one millionth generation, will not be able to finish the interest earned on their forebear's loot in their life time." So when will they start dealing with the principal amount itself?"
"My brother, only God knows. Can't you see they will never touch the principal amount forever? If the money does not earn any interest at all, which is most unlikely, it will take 514 years of spending N1 million daily for the Abacha generations to finish their great grandfather's N187.5 billion loot."
"So what did he really need that kind of money for?" "It is sheer greed, wickedness and gluttony, nothing else."
"That is what you call it. But I call it Life Family Support Programme (LFSP). He has secured the life of all the unborn Abachas." "And government wants to leave $100 million to the family? I think that is being over magnanimous."
"Ah! You have not even gotten the $1.5 billion and you are talking of being over magnanimous. If $100m will help release $1.5 billion don't you think it is an award - winning negotiated deal?"
"What of the interest the money has earned all these years since 1998 when Abacha senior died? At a conservative rate of 4% per annum interest on $1.5b comes to $60 million. That means for four years the accumulated interest amounts to about $240 million alone, not to talk of the interest on the interest!"
"You are just wasting your time. Stay there and be hallucinating over figures. The man's son said he is not aware of any deal with the Federal Government. He is not even ready to give out part of the life family support his father took five years to plan and execute. And here you are arguing whether he deserves the $ 100 million offer from the government."
"Call it what you like, I still maintain the family is not entitled to it. Let them go and enjoy the interest that accumulated on their loot while Mohammed was in jail. They cannot even finish it in their lifetime and in the life of their unborn generations."
"I hope you are not getting me wrong. I am simply saying that it is a display of good negotiation skill if all it takes for you to obtain a release of $1.5 billion is only $100 million. Look at it from that angle. Negotiation is all about Win: Win. You win and I win, no loser! After all, you should commend the skill of the man who was able to 'obtain' that amount of money from the public coffers in the glare of all regulatory and supervising bodies in the country. You also forget that the Abachas hold the yam and the knife - both the $1.5b and $100 million are still in their clutches."
" I see the point you are making, but I think it is only fair for you to appreciate my own point of view. This money we are talking about was not stolen yesterday. It has been there since 1998 and even beyond. Government only recently managed to identify the overseas accounts where they were stashed away. We have not even discovered all of the loot. I am saying we should collect everything from them and let them live on the over $240 million interest the loot would have yielded over time, and on the other money not yet identified."
"My worry really is the tribal colourations the issue of Mohammed's arrest, release, rearrest and re- release has taken. All of a sudden, the northern politicians see the issue of recovering our money from the Abachas as victimisation. The gang up to ensure his release worries me sick."
"When General Abubakar recovered over $600 million from the Abachas it wasn't the victimisation of a northern son."
"It is terrible!"
"Oh! Now you see my point. The whole thing is assuming a completely different posture. Now they see it as victimising their own son, and not recovering stolen money from a treasury looter. Look at the calibre of personalities that visited Mr. President to demand Mohammed's release. They must have intimidated the President into submission."
"My brother, any other person would have been intimidated. At a point I really wondered whether it was the same issue we were talking about, or may be there were other matters unknown to lesser mortals like us." "I hear Mr. President's travails at the National Assembly and his impeachment saga are not unconnected with the continued detention of Mohammed Abacha. If there is any element of truth in that rumour, then I am afraid there is no hope for Nigeria."
"It's all politics, or rather, politricks. They say in politics there is no permanent enemy but only permanent interests. The permanent interest of the North is control of political power. Every other thing is a means to achieving that end."
"But Mohammed has twice denied striking any deal with the Federal Government to repay $1.5 billion. In fact, after his release he still maintained that his release was unconditional and has nothing to do with the much touted money-for freedom deal." "Is he saying our President is Iying then?" "I don't know who is Iying! Na you talkam o! I don't know what to believe anymore." "If there was an agreement to which all those big men from Kano State signed as witnesses, then let the President publish it in the papers, or let him re-arrest Mohammed. That will give credibility to the government's side of the story. Right now people are beginning to doubt the whole gist."
"I think I agree with you on that point. If nothing is done now, sooner or later the whole thing pales into insignificance and Nigerians will forget about it. And life goes on as usual." "I can bet the photocopy of my degree certificate, if Obasanjo does not recover that money, forget it. It is gone." "Why are you not betting the original copy?"
"Ah! My brother, this is Nigeria. Everything is possible. If I bet the original I will be on my own o!"
"I won't even be surprised if Mohammed Abacha declares to contest the 2003 presidential election. He will rather use the $1.5 billion to buy votes than release it for President Obasanjo's 2002 Budget." "I think you are trivializing this matter. What do you mean?"
"So you didn't hear that ANPP visited the Abacha family recently to woo him into their party? What do you think will be the outcome of those manoeuvers?"
"God forbid bad thing! If Mohammed becomes the President of this country, he will steal all of us alive in broad daylight, not just our money." "Well, you had better prepare yourself for that possibility."

Headline: Uganda: Hyacinth Back With Vengeance
Body_Text:
Kampala — MASSIVE: The hyacinth clogging the mouth of Murchison Bay on Lake Victoria, Port bell
The empty-handed fishermen were not singing the usual songs of hefty catches.
In a small dusty landing site at Murchison Bay, Port bell, a sombre mood envelops the fisher folk. As the women get out of their crumbling shacks, there is no cause to smile. They curse the gods of the lake whom they believe sent the water-weed that has blocked the landing site.
"This is one of those days, I feel like quitting fishing. If I had something else to survive on I would give up and avoid the lake," Ali Wanyama, a fisherman says.
Wanyama's back is against the wall. The weed has destroyed what has been earning him his daily bread. He says that the water hyacinth would probably stay for weeks. "When the wind blows it away, it will break up into fragments. "We wont go fishing until it goes back into the middle of the lake," adds Wanyama.
The evils of the water-weed are well known.
The deadly weed entered the lake in the early 1980s, according to the head of the hyacinth control unit, Omar Wadda. At its height, the fast hyacinth covered large areas of the lake hindering fishing there by affecting the welfare of the fishing communities, he says.
Wadda contends that the hyacinth has been dealt devastating blows, but it is still a force to reckon with. "It has seeds, which can stay dormant for years and germinate after 10 years. So the struggle against it is far from over," he says.
Three methods were used to bring the weed under control. "We relied on manual, mechanical harvesters and biological methods to bring it under control," he says.
While this has worked in most parts of the lake, there are limitations at Murchison bay. They want the weed to thrive. As a result, the weed permanently covers a portion of the lake close to the mouth of the bay. This is where water from the Nakivubo channel enters the lake.
Wadda says that the Nakivubo swamp, which used to naturally filter the wastes from the Nakivubo channel before passing on the water to Murchison bay has been reclaimed by yam growers and developers.
Wadda says that they keep applying weevils on the fragments that break from the main bulk of the weed at the bay. he is sure the information the department has will help keep it under control.
Published on: Saturday, 12th October, 2002

Headline: Nigeria: Brutal Militia Terrorises Nigerians
Body_Text:
Lagos — Onitsha, Nigeria: Even by the standards of this most violent of cities, the killings at a busy junction one recent evening got people shouting.
Armed men waiting at the junction cornered Barnabas and Amaka Blessing Igwe, a couple who were prominent lawyers in this part of Nigeria. They pulled them out of their Mercedes, witnessess said. One of the attackers cut off the wife's left leg with a machete. He hacked at her back so that her body folded. She breathed her last right there.
The men fired into the air to frighten away passers-by, though the witnesses, people living in nearby building, were able to tell the police later what they had seen. The killers pumped bullets into the husband's body. They got into the Mercedes and ran over him. But he was still alive and was taken to a nearby hospital. His brother, Vincent Igwe, arrived in time to see him die.
"He recognised me," Igwe, 45, recalled. "He was in great pain. He said, 'What killed me was government.' I shouted, 'Why is the government killing people?'
"He asked me to pray for him," Igwe continued. "I asked God to save him, but then he gave up."
As chairman of the Nigerian Bar Association for the state of Anambra, Barnabas Igwe, 43, had been one of the fiercest critics of the state government and the governor, Chinwoke Mbadinuju. The lawyers in Onitsha accused the Anambra government of being behind the couple's killing. Mbadinuju denied any inovlvement.
In the year leading up to the attack on the Igwes, Anambra had become the most gravely mismanaged of Nigeria's 36 states. Unpaid teachers were on strike for the full school year, few have gone back to work. All state civil servants and court workers have been on strike for many months.
The state's bar association accused state leaders of pocketing the money meant to pay the striking workers and, just before Barnabas Igwe was killed, demanded that the governor resign within seven days. With elections coming up next year, political killings have been on the rise across this West African country, whose military rulers ceded power to civilians only three years ago. In several states, politicians have supported ethnic militias that have been used to intimidate rivals and, according to rights organisations, have sometimes killed them.
"Our great fear is that politicians who must win at all costs will force their way through by using ethnic militias," Abel Guobadia, chairman of the Indepen-dent National Electoral Commission, said in an interview in the capital, Abuja.
Here in the state of Anambra, one of the country's most notorious militias, known as the Bakassi Boys, was legalised by the state government in August 2000, and the salaries of its members have been paid from public money. The Bakassi Boys, whose official name is the Anambra State Vigilante Services and whose members belong to the main ethnic group of this region, the Ibos, have an office in the governor's headquarters in the state capital, Awka.
Onitsha's main market is one of Africa's largest, selling everything from scrap metal and used clothes to bathroom scales and yams. Because of the strike by state workers, the city is littered with mountains of uncollected garbage, some of them sporadically on fire.
Trucks and cars, spewing thick exhaust fumes that sometimes make it impossible to see up a road, try to skirt potholes up to two-thirds of a meter, or two feet, deep. With the gridlock, armies of men make a living by pushing wheelbarrows around congested streets.
The chaos of this huge market has contributed to Onitsha's reputation as one of Nigeria's most violent cities, where criminals and traders fight each other for supremacy on the streets. In the main market on a recent afternoon, a car carrying Bakassi Boys bulldozed its way through the crowds, its siren screaming. Three of the men leaned out the windows, holding shotguns. The militia has been credited with reducing crime in killing criminals and leaving their headless bodies on the streets as warnings. Since they were formed three years ago, they have summarily executed more than 1,000 people in Anambra, according to Amnesty International.
But according to Nigerian and international rights groups, including Human Rights Watch, criminals are not the militia's only targets. The rights group have documented its killing of critics or opponents of the state government.
"I can't say it was the Bakassi Boys who killed the Igwes," said Pete Obiorah, secretary of the state bar association. "But the manner of the killings, which was by matchete cuts, is the same style used by the Bakassi Boys in their own executions." Obiorah also noted that the Igwes had not been robbed in the attack.
The state government denied that the Bakassi Boys had been involved.
"It couldn't have been them," said Victor Ositadinma Ezenwa, secretary to the government and one of the governor's top aides. "The head was not chopped off. Instead, after he was shot, they ran over his body with a car."
