Headline: Ghana: Mystery Death
Body_Text:
The mystery surrounding the death of four-year old John Adiko, a pupil, remains unravelled almost a week after his body was found about a mile from Rainbow Motors, near the Spintex road in Accra
. Family sources said Ms. Sekyibea, a cousin of Adiko's father, took the little boy to his school, Learning Tree Academy, on the Spintex road on Wednesday, October 3, this year, and left him to enter when they reached the gates. That was the last time the family saw him alive.
Enquiries revealed that a woman selling yams took the boy away not quite long after Sekyibea had left him in the school.
A former teacher of the boy had seen the yam seller holding the hands of the little boy who was crying and the teacher supposed he was missing home and that the yam seller was giving him assistance.
In fact, on inquiring the teacher was reportedly told by the woman that she wanted to send her children to the same school Adiko was attending and would be glad to be assisted by the teacher.
The teacher said he would make a call first before joining the yam seller and the boy to the Learning Tree. But before she returned from the communication center the woman had bolted with the boy.
Mr. Robert Adiko, John's father, said on the same day around 2:00 p.m. he heard that the boy was lying dead about a mile away from Rainbow Motors.
When Mrs. Adiko, who is six months pregnant, heard the news she fainted out of shock and could not be revived until several minutes later in their Okponglo home, near the Spintex road.
Police Inspector Wilson of the Kpeshie Divisional headquarters, handling the case, said a caller had told them on phone that the body of the boy was floating in a pond and when they rushed there he was actually lying beside the pond.
An autopsy was being carried out on the body, while investigations into the act continued, he assured.

Headline: Nigeria: Strangulation Taken Too Far
Body_Text:
Lagos — Surviving the traumatic 30-month civil war was a terrible ordeal. But living with a deliberate state policy of curtailment is a nightmare. Although the tendency has been to shy away from the fact, the truth remains that Ndigbo are the worse victims of the perpetually marginalizing schemes of the nation's power blocs.
To benefit from federal projects, Ndigbo have had to double their persuasive skills, far and above those of similarly large ethnic groups in the country. Until and unless they take their case to the public domain, their case is as good as lost.
Yet, the impression is given of the Ndigbo as full and equal partakers in the nation's socio-political process. Quite obviously, successive governments have successfully implemented such schemes that ensured that almost 32 years after the civil war, Ndigbo are very much on the fringes of things. Merely canvassing the idea of a President of Nigeria of Igbo extraction is regarded as an affront. Mentioning it practically draws the ire of the typical Igbo haters; especially those who still believe the Ndigbo are yet to serve out the full punishment for their "rebellious act." To this moment, it is evident that all manners of designs are continually plotted to achieve the neutralization of the Igbo political interest. Twice under the present dispensation, unwarranted changes were effected to the Senate leadership. Unfortunately, the wrong reasons were bandied to rationalise the action.
Funnily, there were those who argued that the changes, unjust as they were, were nonetheless facilitated by Ndigbo's love for money. But they simply missed the point. The same people who sold the nonsense and those who imbibed it belong to that specie who always credit Ndigbo with less political astuteness than they clearly deserve.
The truth of the matter is that Ndigbo is constantly on guard and ever so conscious of the designs to relegate them to the nation's political backwaters. So, each time they had consented to finding a replacement for the position of the Senate President, they had done so conscious of the necessity to resist the temptation to fall prey to the grand design of those who are constantly on the look out for the slighted excuse to deny the Southeast that position. It is not a hidden fact that even the present federal government or its agents are sparing no effort to stagnate Igbo areas, socio-economically. This point is beyond debate. Why? Because three and half years into the life of the administration, Ndigbo are still crying cap in hand demanding their fair share of federal projects for the Southeast.
Does anyone require a special probe's findings to believe that a state policy of non-investment in Igboland exists and is being brazenly implemented? Can anyone in government, including the Ojo Maduekwes and Kema Chikwes of this world, who sadly have become government's megaphones on Igbo affairs on account of a small pot of yam portage, proudly beat his or her chest and point to a single federal government project executed since the present Federal Government assumed office.
Renowned novelist, Prof. Chinua Achebe, highlighted this obnoxious policy many years back when he stated, inter alia: "Many have tried but nobody has succeeded in explaining away the citing of five steel mills ...only in the north and west of the country." Taking cue from Prof. Achebe, it is pertinent to also ask why no one has successfully explained the absence of an international airport in the Southeast. Nobody has equally explained the criminal neglect of major federal highways in the southeast.
Ironically, it took the visit of Vice President Abubakar Atiku recently to the Southeast for a belated realisation of the extent of the deplorable state of roads in the zone. Are we then to believe that the Minister of Works and Housing's inspection tour of federal highways on assumption of office was simply a merry-go-round trip? Or doesn't the ministry have field officers who report to Abuja the state of roads around the country?
Only last week, the Imo State Governor, Chief Achike Udenwa, drew attention to awful state of roads in his domain, pointing out that unless urgent actions are taken the state will soon be physically cut off from neighbouring states. It is shocking to contemplate this situation when some states in the country have virtually all their inter-state roads fully rehabilitated, with construction commencing on new ones.
So bad are the roads in the Southeast that any one embarking on a trip to the zone and some parts of the South-south is simply on a suicide mission. It is bad enough that it now takes more than six hours to get to Benin, three hour in excess of the normal travel time, then making a trip to Igbo heartland and states beyond it is almost a journey to eternity. Yet, in a government that prides itself with financial prudence, some one considered it expedient to expend 12billion naira to construct a by-pass that leads to nowhere in particular.
Now, some people are at it again. With barely six months to the general election officials of government are bandying figures on the proposed rehabilitation of roads in the zone. Why now? Why make a political capital of an issue that other zones have massively benefited from, without even a whisper? Is this not another electoral bait? Must Ndigbo be put through this nasty prank again? What happened to the promises made in 1998 and 1999?
Landlocked and highly dependent on road transportation for its inherently commerce-centred economy, the southeast is definitely one zone in the country where the absence of motorable roads is tantamount to strangulation. Yet, for obvious motives, it is the only part of the country saddled with the worst network of roads. Such is the grave deprivation and callous act, which has remained the lot of the Ndigbo.
Consistently advanced as reason for this unbridled deprivation against Ndigbo is what other groups have termed the "Igbo Problem." Proponents of this problem define it as the inability of Ndigbo to speak politically with one voice. But must the Ndigbo speak with one voice to deserve what is duly theirs? Did the Southwest speak with one voice before roads in the zone were massively rehabilitated by the present administration? Or, did the Northeast act in similar fashion before the network of roads in the zone received extensive facelift? Beyond the Shagari era, roads in the Southeast have existed at their own peril.
As is quite obvious, the so-called Igbo problem is clearly, a case of misperception. Short on substance as the claim is, Ndigbo cannot make any apology for their republican values.
As Arthur Nwankwo pointed out "the Igbo culture, being (highly) receptive to change, individualistic and highly competitive," gives "the Igbo man an unquestioned advantage" as well as credentials to pursue both individual or communal advancement over other groups hampered by traditional and feudal hierarchies.
For those to whom Ndigbo have remained a permanent fixation, harping on the perceived Igbo problem is simply a disingenuous way of finding excuses for perpetuating such unthinkable schemes, which we have witnessed over time. Whatever is the problem of the Ndigbo exists within the Nigerian political context. Because as Prof. Achebe espoused on this issue: "Every ethnic group is, of course, something of a problem for Nigeria's easy achievement of cohesive nationhood."
So within this holistic context, Ndigbo are not different. Indeed, more than many other ethnic groups, Ndigbo have worked and pursued with greater vigour the crusade for the unity of the country, relative to the politico-economic gains that have accrued to them there-from. This is pay back time. The dividend of democracy or whatever tag it goes by must not be denied Ndigbo, neither should such goodies be the exclusive preserve of any zone on account of who the President is or where the vice President comes from. Or who the minister of works is.
Ndigbo must put up a bold fight against the recurrent policy of official strangulation against them. It is obnoxious, inhuman and so totally unacceptable.
Iwob writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Pandemonium As Randy Security Personnel Rape Housewife
Headline: Nigeria: Pandemonium As Randy Security Personnel Rape Housewife
Body_Text:
Lagos — Fear gripped residence of Ajegunle, Apapa, Lagos, as the Hausa community in the area took to the street early Friday following sexual assault on two of their daughters and a housewife by three randy soldiers.
The soldiers, who were identified as Corporal Sunday Simeon, Lance Corporal Amos Tafawa Balewa both attached to the Ajegunle Police Station on a joint security patrol and Lance Corporal Christopher Uche, aka Ecomog, 79 NA 14231, who is supposed to be serving at the 72 Air Borne Paramilitary Battalion, Benue State, but was aaleged to be always visible at the station.
They were accused of forcefully having carnal knowledge of a 16 years old virgin, living with her aunt in Ajegunle, and a 26 year old lady who was said to be a yam seller at the Alayabiagba market.
The Hausas, whose first pot of call was the Ajegunle Police Station, also accused the security operatives of extorting N15,000 from one of them, whose name was not given .
Spokesman of the Hausa community, simply known as Hassan, said trouble started when a trailer load of yam arrived at the popular yam Market, on Thursday night, and as usual, "intended buyers rushed to the place to get good yams" .
He said the girls and some other Hausa men were in the place when some soldiers suddenly stopped their vehicles and ordered their arrest.
"We (the men) were later allowed to go, after we had parted with N15,000. It was later in the night that we had that the two girls were driven to a bush, where they were raped by the three soldiers. The most painful aspect of it all is that an innocent girl of sixteen years of age was rudely disvirgined in a horrendous manner", exclaimed the aggrieved Hassan.
But for the quick intervention of the Ajegunle Divisional Officer (DPO), Mr. Felix Vwamhi, (Chief Superintendent of Police), the aggrieved Hausa men would have taken law into their hands, as they vowed to kill any man seen in a military uniform.
The DPO, expressed disappointment over the incident, and promised to get to the root of the case and insisted that the culprits would not go unpunished.
"Just yesterday (Thursday), I called Ecomog and warned him that I don't want to see him again in this station because I have been hearing series of negative report on his activities. Afterall he is not supposed to be here."
Vwamhi stated further that the molested virgin would be taken care of by his office, as deliberate step to pacify the Hausas and also the other lady would be given maximum security physically and psychologically.
Speaking during interrogation, one of accuse, Uche, who also identified by one of the victims, denied being part of the action, as he claimed to be relaxing after intoxication from several shots of locally made gin otherwise known as Ogogoro.
Although, he confessed that he was in the Nigerian Port Authority patrol vehicle when the people were arrested but declined being part of the exercise.
He said he did not know when the girls were raped and got to know of it when he was arrested by the police. He also said he did not know the names of his friends, who brought the vehicle but confessed that they both worked with NPA as a driver and clerk.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Grains, Food Stuff Drop in Bauchi
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Grains, Food Stuff Drop in Bauchi
Body_Text:
The commencement of the harvest season has forced the prices of grains and other food stuff down to between 20 per cent and 100 per cent in the Azare area of Bauchi State.
A measure (mudu) of millet, which was sold for N130 last month now goes for only N60, while the same measure of groundnut now sells for N140 instead of N180.
The price of a measure of guinea corn has also dropped from the previous N130 to N80, a measure of old and new beans dropped from N130 and N120 to N70 and N65 respectively.
A measure of old maize, which was sold for N120, now sells for N110, while the same measure of new maize sells for N80.
The price of an average measure of potato has also dropped from N250 to N100, while four pieces of yam previously sold for N800 have dropped to N4,000 to about N2,000 and a small sack of onion dropped from an average price of N4,000 to N3,000.
The price of locally produced rice, however, remained stable as a measure of rice goes for N170, while imported rice recorded marginal rise in price as a measure rose from between N180 and N200 to between N210 and N220.
A dealer in Azare market, Malam Wada Mohammed, told NAN that the drop in the price of grains was as a result of the commencement of the harvest season and the influx of new grains into the market.
Mohammed said the price drop, which began at the beginning of this month would continue for long and that the prices of maize and millet would drop further at the end of the month when farmers in neighbouring towns and villages were expected to harvest their crops.

Headline: Nigeria: Security for the Belly
Body_Text:
Lagos — Food, hunger, nutrition and malnutrition are inter-related in one way or another. Food contains nutrients. Unlike hunger, which is the absence of food, malnutrition explains the bad medical conditions arising from insufficient or excessive consumption of nutrients by the human body. Nutrition studies not only food but also, its effects on the body, while it deals with the body's needs in terms of specific nutrients, ways and means of supplying them through diets and the effects of failure to meet the needs.
It is also not arguable that good food, that is, food eaten in the right quality and quantity promotes good health. This fact is explanatory in the processes food passes through. These are ingestion, digestion, absorption, metabolism and excretion. If something goes wrong with any of these processes owing to abnormalities either before or at birth, or due to the consumption of poor food, the body ultimately suffers ill-health, leading to one form of sickness or another.
People who eat correctly are usually healthy and built with the energy required for optimal productivity. How? Since they usually have sound physical, psychological, mental and emotional health. On the other hand, malnourished people display the reverse character traits. These include: large stomachs, flat bottoms, thin arms, thin legs; brown hair, skin sores, x-shaped and kshaped legs. Furthermore, low intelligence, aggression, wrinkles, sunken temples, deep cheeks, dull eyes, dry skin, lethargy and apathy are also known traits of bad feeding. However, the truth is this: factors that are responsible for malnutrition also highlight the imperatives of food security.
The unavailability and unaffordability of food is assuming a widening dimension in the country. Hardly surprising than ironical in a nation of about 120 million people, occupying a land mass of 923, 768 kilometre, spread over northern Savannah belt that is ideal for food crops such as yams, vegetables and cereals and the rearing of livestock and southern rain forest that is ideal for cash crops like cocoa; oil palms, plantains and cassava. Food has indeed become scarce in spite of several warnings by experts. The problem has reached a level that "people now eat for survival rather than for nourishment," observed Mr. Samuel Kayode, a Lagos-based social analyst.
There is little hope yet for a quick way out. In a recent interview, Mr. Ademola Tunji, Director of National Strategic Food Reserve (NSFR) alerted that the nation's food reserve was below 10,000 tonnes. While noting the implications on food production, he called for more government actions in the area of agriculture.
While the importation of food had been an easy way out of the grim realities of a food crisis, the immediate effects have been colossal loss of revenue, depletion of Nigeria's foreign reserves and dumping of low quality food products in the country. It was announced after a recent Federal Executive Council (FEC) meeting that Nigeria's foreign reserves dropped during the first six months of this year by $1.7 billion mainly due to food importation.
How can food security be assured in Nigeria? Food security, is the availability and affordability of quality and quantity foods for the masses. It is a cardinal focus of the plan of action of the World Food Programme (WFP), intended to reduce global poverty by 2015 by reducing to half, cases of the over 800 million people currently suffering from hunger, out of which 24,000 die daily, according to Mr. Kofi Annan, Secretary-General of the United Nations Organisation.
If the government of Nigeria must provide sufficient food for the people, the first measure to be put in place is an effective food and nutrition policy, which should define in clear terms the nutrition focus of the country. A national policy on food and nutrition is a major factor of national security.
In the opinion of experts, for any national policy to respond adequately to Nigeria's current food demand, it must be based on scientific evidence. Adewale Omololu, a retired Professor of Nutrition at the University of Ibadan and Professor Tola Atinmo of the same institution stressed this in recent interview. But before Omololu spoke. Professor Wahlqvist of the Department of Medicine, Monash University, Australia and President, International Union of Nutritional Sciences had stated: " Any evidence- based nutrition policy must be scientifically rationalised to domains such as consensus, implementation, success rates, knowledge and effects on the health of the population." He also stated that it must be subjected to scientific paradigms or hypothesis, specific evidence, field studies, analysis and integrative science, amongst other yardsticks.
Ordinarily, a national policy should include all the recognised measures of food security. The development of a system of better agricultural methods and implements is very paramount in any plan to secure food. In this regard therefore, President Obasanjo's initiative to return agriculture to the forefront of national economy through the creation of food production committees, subsidised sales of mass production farming implements and invitation of experts from agriculturally developed countries such as China is commendable. That the government must have recognised the enormity of work to be done is undeniable. This recognition, Professor Ango Abdullahi, Special Adviser to the President on Food Security admitted in a recent lecture on "The role of agriculture in Nigeria's poverty reduction strategy, delivered at the Federal University of Technology, Minna, Niger State, according to him, Nigeria has been struggling with inadequate food, safe water, and health care in the last two year because of the weakness of the agriculture and manufacturing sectors of economy. He said it has become so bad that the world's seventh crude oil producing country is now one of the 20 poorest countries of the world.
Notwithstanding its recognition, government must also take responsibility for the obvious poor dividends from the agrarian economy until now. In the opinion of Professor Jude Njoku, Vice- Chancellor, Federal University of Technology (FUT), Owerri, Imo State, the government made a blunder when it failed to make agriculture the central focus of its poverty alleviation (and later eradication) programme. In a lecture on the changing roles of agriculture in the Nigerian economy, delivered at the institution's 2002 Agric Week, he stated: " The surest and most enduring way to alleviate poverty would have been through agricultural development programmes." Professor Njoku accused the government of paying lip service to the creation of clear-cut development programmes until now because of its belief that crude oil income will sustain the nation's economy forever.
The same issue of over-reliance on crude oil revenue instead of embarking on an effective land reform, among other issues were earlier identified as factors militating against the availability and affordability of foods by a former Deputy Vice-Chancellor of the University of Lagos, Professor Isaac Adalemo. He spoke at lecture delivered at the Centre for Development and Democratic Studies, Lagos State University (LASU), Adalemo made the following recommendations.
The re-allocation of control over agricultural resources to local, self-provisioning units;
_ Dependence on mass initiative rather than government directives; Freedom from dependence on trade; Re-uniting agriculture and nutrition; Understanding that agriculture is an end, not just a means to an end; . Ensuring that industry serves agriculture, and
_ Recognising the need for co-ordinated social planning.
The third measure that must be put in place to ensure lasting security of food is the employment of effective strategies for the control of food prices. It must be asserted that Nigeria is not yet ripe for the kind of free market enterprise that leaves the supply and distribution of farm products entirely in the hands of middlemen. It is also instructive, at this juncture, to laud the FG for the proposed multi- commodity development and marketing companies being planned to take-off next January. Such establishments and virile consumer associations are the means of bridging the price gaps in democratic societies. The active take-off of these organisations are being awaited with great expectations especially with the upward spiralling movement of the prices of foods. For example, investigations showed that a loaf of family size bread now sells for N120. The price is expected to go further up when millers eventually roll-out micronutrient enriched flours. A bag of yellow garri sells for between N2,800 and N3,200 while the small bag of white garri sells for N2,000 as against the big bag, which sells for N3,200 indicating a marginal decrease for the latter. It is the same situation for beans, which finest brand, drum base or golden beans sells for N8,550 per bag. In the case of rice, a price have moved up marginally to between N3, 200 and N3, 500 in the same way the ban on imported frozen chickens has pushed the prices of chicken up from N350.00 to between N700.00 and N850.00.
The fact that most of the gains from the economic culture of exploitation goes to the middleman necessitated the call by Prof. Njoku for an active programme on the marketing and pricing of agricultural commodities, Prof. Adalemo recommended " the Chinese policy of relatively increasing outputs and slowly increasing prices for agricultural commodities along with low prices for farm supplies (inputs)."
All agencies of the law must be empowered to enact laws on food fortification and prosecute offenders and saboteurs of the food chains. For instance, Alhaji Aliyu Babangida, an Adamawa State-based farmer, who spoke on this matter, wants government to prosecute those that profit from the diversion of fertilisers to black markets at the cost of N1, 800 instead of the official cost of N1,200 per bag, which led to the alarm sounded recently by the state farmers about an impending low crop harvest.
Also, there is the need for more nutrition education programmes that will educate people on the effects and control of hunger and malnutrition, dangers of low priority on nutrition, food storage processes, food combination benefits and dangerous food habits. People want to know about the control of malnutrition and its control through fortification of foods, vitamin and mineral supplementation and dietary diversification. As Caroline Uddoh observes, the average Nigerian farmer works in very difficult condition and often eats only once in a day. The effect of this situation is constant sickness, which reduces his productivity through diminished capacity for work. Education or enlightenment can correct this situation.
Nutrition enlightenment programmes must have clear objectives, in languages spoken and understood by the people. It must be planned with emphasis on nutrients, its values to the body, its sources, composition and forms; breast-feeding, child care, signs and symptoms of the various forms of malnutrition; effects of infectious diseases on the nutritional state of the child and gastro-enteritis; hygiene and hygienic methods of food preparation; the effect of bad cooking habits on the nutritional value of foods; advantages of budgeting and bulk buying of certain foods, special nutritional needs of certain people such as pregnant women, babies, adolescents, adults, aged and the sick; importance of regular weight records for children and cooking demonstrations.
The various forms of government can undertake the promotion of nutrition education and enlightenment programmes. Non- governmental organisations (NGOs), corporate bodies and individuals have their respective roles to play as well. For instance, the high importance that is attached to food and nutrition in the United States of America began with resolution by the Congress and Senate, which declared the right to food as National Policy. Another example is that of the Mazahua Indians, some 200,000 communal people in a village stretching within mountain forests, 100 miles west of Mexico City, who now live better lives in the Monarch Butterfly Biosphere Reserve, courtesy of Alternaire, an NGO working in eight different poor, rural communities around the butterfly's roosting ground. This organisation has worked for the past five years to equip villagers with important knowledge such as how to build better houses, how to farm without using chemical fertilisers and how to make land productive etc. Today, many organisations including the US Fish and Wildlife Services now aid the NGO's effort with the needed financing.
Corporate bodies still have a long way to go, especially, as it regards the greedy nature of many even in the face of huge profits. Though a few corporate organisations really spend money to make profit. On the other hand, the role of the individual in the promotion of good health is similar to that of the media.
It is not enough to be critical of rural-to-urban cities migration of employable youths, in search of white-collar jobs, without embarking on the development of rural communities. Social amenities, credit facilities industrial abilities and government patronage must always be facilitated for the rural dwellers. The empowerment of all rural dwellers is imperative if the foods Nigerians need to eat to fight hunger and malnutrition are to be produced by farmers. This is the easiest means to harvest good food and good health for all.

Headline: Nigeria: IOD And the North
Body_Text:
All regimes in Nigeria, present and past, have made allusions to the fact that the private sector is the engine that powers the nation's economy. The current administration of President Olusegun Obasanjo has in fact declared that the plank of its economic policy is private sector driven. Yet, the private sector in Nigeria has depended largely on government for its survival as a human depends on oxygen. Governments at all levels seem to be in a kind of double talk regarding the place of the private sector on the nation's priority list. While government spin-doctors profess with their mouths that the sector is top on the list, there is nothing in their checklist that proves this. Consequently, the private sector in Nigeria has been left in a rudderless state aided only by the wind.
But the place of the private sector in any nation's journey towards self-sustenance and development cannot be discounted. In the last three years of our return to democracy, things seem to have become worse for the sector. Professionals in the private sector and even the self-employed now apply to government agencies for employment. The nation's crude oil resources, which have proved to be more of a liability than munificence from God, are dwindling in spite of the continuous favourable international prices of the products. OPEC quota allocations have continued to be unfavourable to us and we have been told by those who have personal interests in the cartel that it will continue to remain in our interest to remain within. The oil sector to which we almost solely depend on for survival is poised for a crisis as the United States braces itself up to declare the Gulf of Guinea a region of "American national interest" (whatever that means) and other dependent countries of Europe spruce up to call the bluff of OPEC. While the nation suffers the consequences of these effects, it is the North that bears the most brunt.
In the last three years, over 90 percent of the factories in the 19 states of the north have shut down. Kano that was once the industrial pride and beehive of the north is now a vast rural state of beggars and the ever-growing army of the unemployed. A graveyard silence has overtaken the industrial sites that used to straddle Kaduna South, and the modern industrial layout of Minna that was constructed in the late 80s and early 90s have since found other uses. Ilorin that is a bridge between the North and the South and once enjoyed the consideration of several entrepreneurs as a result, is now a place renown only for the " James Bond" battles between Olusola Saraki and Governor Mohammed Lawal. In Jos, only a few like the resilient NASCO group that is determined to weather all the storms still remains in business and even that, capacity utilisation remains at embarrassing levels. It is for these reasons and many more that the Institute of Directors (IoD) Kaduna branch, of which I am the secretary, is organising a member's evening which is expected to take place on Saturday, 28th September 2002 in Kaduna. The IoD Kaduna superintends the activities of the institute in the 19 northern states including Abuja.
The IoD was formed in London in 1903. The IoD (U.K) still remains the international headquarters. Its Nigerian branch was cobbled through the dogged instrumentality of the late Alhaji Ali Baba in 1983 when he was still a minister in the Second Republic government of Alhaji Shehu Shagari. At different times, Chief Anofi Guobadia, Chief (Mrs.) Olutoyin Olakunrin, Alhaji A. O. G. Otiti, Oba Oladele Olashore and Mr. Emmanuel Ijewere have been presidents of the governing council. Alhaji Umar Abba-Gana, the former managing director of Africa Petroleum (AP) is the seventh and current president of the governing council. Nigeria is only one of four countries in Africa that has IoD branches - the other three being South Africa, Zimbabwe and Ghana.
The IoD stands at the apex of leadership of the organised private sector. It is in the forefront in the affairs of the national economy and promotes the enabling environment for businesses to flourish. It was brought to the north also by the late Ali Baba in 1997. The first chairman of the Kaduna branch was Alhaji Falalu Bello, the chairman of Intercity Bank Limited and it was fostered by Mrs. Edna Iyorshe, publicity shy but probably the most powerful corporate Amazon in northern Nigeria who was its first secretary. Mrs. Iyorshe has for long been a general manager in the octopoid NNDC in Kaduna and has chaired the boards of directors of probably a dozen companies and served on scores of others. She also had my friend, Engr. Ahmed Ibrahim, the smart and hardworking managing director of NNIL and chairman of Drake and Scull Ltd. assist her as the co-secretary. Together they had the task of conferring the kind of personality that was required of a lofty organisation like the IoD. They nurtured it to the respectable level that was desideratum before its formal inauguration on 15th September 2000 in which Alhaji M. I. Yahaya emerged as the chairman. Alhaji Yahaya, a doyen of the nation's private sector has served as managing director of NNIL, Savannah Bank and Union Bank at different times. He has also served on the boards of over 20 companies and is currently the chairman, chief executive and some would say "owner" of the not so new and fast growing NUB International Bank. The articulate and high profile management consultant of international repute, Amina J. Ibrahim is the vice chairman and yours sincerely serves as the secretary. Mr. Frederick Durlong, an ultra-elite of the NNDC who has also served in various board positions over the years is the treasurer and Lawal Kankia Ibrahim, the managing director of Arewa Hotels Limited and board member of several other companies of the NNDC group is the publicity secretary. It is this team that is in the forefront of the efforts to renew the private sector in the north via the IoD.
In the stark reality of the current situation, the north and indeed the nation must reexamine the state of its economic well being. Last week, in this column, I wrote eerily of the economy in the alarming title: "Nigeria is broke". But Nigeria has no right to be broke. It is potentially one of the richest nations in the world if we know the right things to do and get serious about the cankerworm called corruption. The nation is blessed with vast human and natural resources and we are not known to be in the league of nations that play host to such natural scourges as earthquakes, volcanoes, tornadoes and the sort. We have favourable weather conditions and our soil is one of the most fecund. It is so much so, that there is a joke that if one inserts a finger in our soil and keeps it there long enough, it will germinate. The only scourge in Nigeria is leadership. It is the leadership that has failed to harness the organised restlessness of the Nigerian people positively towards development. It is the leadership that sets standards for the followership to steal.
What moral suasion would the head of a government who is desperate for a second term and therefore corruptly enriches himself have in reprimanding the petty thief or pickpocket that works for him. The rich economies of South Korea, Singapore, Malaysia and Indonesia were built by the private sector and some of these happened during dictatorships. Can the informal economy in Nigeria continue to thrive in spite of the government's obtuseness? Oil producing states in Nigeria have lately become obstreperous over resource control (which by the way has some merits), - even though much of the quarrel is really about the control of these resources into the pockets of some of the governors. Have the non-oil producing states considered the prognosis of these agitations? My Tiv friends have threatened to ensure that one tuber of yam sells for $20 in line with the international market price of oil if the oil producing states get their way.. Please don't laugh, there are serious. Nigeria can exist as a wealthy nation even without crude oil. The nation is blessed with more precious metals (apart from diamonds) than South Africa, which is now one of the richest nations in the world and by far the richest in Africa, on account of these same metals. And 80 percent of these metals are found within the northern states including may like Tantalite which at a time was almost exclusive to Nigeria.. Plateau, Nasarawa Kogi and Kaduna states can exist as rich nations on their own on this score. Too, almost every crop grows in Nigeria. Crops that were once thought to be exclusively temperate, we just recently found out, flourish in the north. Sundry berries such as strawberries, blackberries etc, grapes, passion fruits, apples, sweet melons etc grow in various parts of the north. How have we appropriated some of these comparative advantages to improve our export earnings? Nigeria is the world's largest producer of cassava, yet ours is the only nation among the major producers of this root crop that does not earn foreign exchange from the commodity, in spite of the fact that cassava constitutes the staple animal feed in Europe. Countries like Thailand Malaysia, Singapore and Indonesia, which are even farther from Europe than we are and therefore pay more freight charges, earn stupendous amounts of foreign exchange exporting this commodity to Europe.
The Obasanjo government has killed the idea of an Abuja stock exchange. In its stead, the government deceives everyone that there would be a commodity exchange, which itself should be a great idea since most of the commodities are produced in the north. But if Obasanjo deceives the people about the establishment of a commodity exchange, why shouldn't the meeting of the 19 northern state governors pick up the gauntlet? What should be the northern position on the ongoing privatisation? The 19 state governors still don't know what to do, so why shouldn't the private sector lead the way? By the way, the presidency through the Bureau of Public Enterprises (BPE) has mandated the IoD (Nigeria) to draft a code of corporate governance for the newly privatised corporations. The IoD was advised by the BPE to "borrow ideas from codes already done in other countries such as the "King Code" produced by IoD South Africa. NEPA concentrates mainly in Lagos. Why shouldn't some investors and consultants in the north consider partnership with Independent Power Producers (IPP)? I note the efforts of my friend, Bilya Bala, of Renascent Consult, a consulting company in the power sector in this regard. Ostrich farming which South African white farmers pioneered is now one of the biggest businesses in the world. In Nigeria these large birds thrive only in the north. How many businessmen have explored this virgin area, which currently earns billions of dollars for many southern African farmers in Zimbabwe, Namibia and South Africa? A few years ago, the governors of the 19 northern states invited Vice Admiral Murtala Nyako (rtd) and Audu Ogbeh, two northern large-scale farmers to submit papers on how to improve agricultural productivity in the North. The governors simply took the papers and "kept them in view". None of them has revisited the issue since. They have been more interested in awarding contracts. But an adroit Donald Duke, the governor of Cross River State invited the same Audu Ogbeh (that was long before he accepted the thankless job of chairman of the PDP) to submit a blueprint for boosting the remunerative value of agriculture in his state. The governor implemented the recommendations he proffered and today, several farmers in Cross Rivers State earn foreign exchange from the cultivation of pineapples.
There is this story that made the rounds some time ago, though its origin is apocryphal, it is instructive howbeit. It was said that a few months ago, the Minister of Environment submitted a proposal to the president on the need to secure the northernmost borders of the country against desert encroachment by planting economic trees across the shelterbelt that extends from Kebbi to Yobe States. It will involve the purchase of high breed seedlings of some high-yield economic trees such as gum Arabic, eucalyptus and several others and planting them across vast areas of land. That would be killing several birds with one stone. Apart from taming desertification, in some 10-20 years from now, the economic gain that would be reaped from this common sensewould give those states their own "resource control". The president was said to have been initially excited by the idea but the excitement was short-lived. He has since abandoned the project. But why wouldn't the governors of these states pool their resources to do this for their own people? Why must they wait for the president who obviously does not think he has any stakes in the matter? Governor Saminu Turaki of Jigawa State should know the significance of this project. He owns plantations in Cross River State for the export of wood. For the same reasons and perhaps more, the northern states governors (and possibly the eastern states) should have a far greater stake in the dredging of the River Niger, a project which was commenced by the PTF but now "safely" abandoned by the Obasanjo administration. The Internet is the next biggest business. The prospects are far greater than crude oil and diamonds put together. We should consider designating either Kaduna or Jos the Internet hub of Nigeria at once and then work towards it, sparing no expense. Many Asian countries including India are getting out of the woods via this route.
In developed countries of the West, the IoD has been in partnership with governments in the unceasing improvement of models of economic development. The IoD in Kaduna is poised to accept this challenge in the northern states. That is why I am inviting all company directors and stakeholders in the organised private sector in the north to the colloquium that would hold at No: 13 Inuwa Wada Road, Kaduna by 8:00 pm on that day.
Professor Sheikh Abdullahi, the director of Institute of Administration, Ahmadu Bello University Zaria who is at home with concepts of the administration of enterprises in the north as he is with the praxis, will present the lead paper. It promises to be a stimulating evening.
Contact sam@nigeriaunlimited.com
Warped logic on Dr. Lukman and the oil sector
Nigeria is broke, the title of Sam Nda-Isaiah's Monday column of September 16, 2002 attracted my attention particularly the bit that had to do with OPEC's quota and Dr. Rilwanu Lukman's position as presidential adviser on petroleum and energy, as well as OPEC conference president.
In Sam Nda-Isaiah's warped thinking, Dr. Lukman, has proved unable to defend our strategic interests and therefore, the president should appoint a minister of petroleum. What logic, as if that will automatically confer on the person so appointed, some magic wand to shore-up Nigeria's quota in OPEC, as well as earn us more revenue from petroleum.
I am constrained to believe, that Sam Nda-Isaiah, was simply up to pure mischief otherwise, he will appreciate that Dr. Lukman, a modest, humble, debonair and unassuming personality of no mean capabilities, has over the years, deftly deployed his meticulous and suave negotiating skills, to steer the Nigerian oil industry, OPEC and other oil producing nations, through and out of difficult if not turbulent situations, that have characterised, the international oil market, as a result of western manipulations.
Perhaps, Sam Nda-Isaiah, needs to be educated on the fact, that the whole idea of allocating production quotas, is to ensure that prices do not at anytime plummet so low, as to exacerbate the socio-economic problems of both OPEC and non-OPEC producers, where oil revenue accounts for the sizeable chunk of their foreign revenue, as a result of glut in the oil market. At the same time, prices must be maintained at such reasonable levels in order not to harm the economies of consumer nations, who may be tempted to evolve all manner of jackboot methods, to destabilise OPEC and its other allies.
Which in Sam Nda-Isaiah's estimation will be better? Allow Nigeria and other OPEC member countries, to raise their production quota output to any level desirable and earn five USD per barrel of oil and less revenue, or apply deft negotiating skills and tact as Dr. Lukman, has been doing, to maintain reasonable production quota ratios and earn between 25-30 USD per barrel of oil, as has been the case for nearly one year now, since he became OPEC conference president, thereby ensuring more revenue for oil producing countries like Nigeria?
One hopes Sam Nda-Isaiah, does not belong to the class of those, who believe in throwing away the bath water with the baby, just to enjoy temporary reprieve from a problem whose resolution requires careful and painstaking planning? Or perhaps, is it just a case of wanting to score cheap political points, on the pages of newspapers?
Sam Nda-Isaiah will do well, not to occasionally allow sentiments becloud his better sense of judgment. He should equally learn, to acknowledge persons, with impeccable credentials and undisputable achievements, in their chosen career and profession, which Dr. Lukman is, and refrain from casting unwarranted aspersions on them, for want of any tangible arguments to advance, in his sometimes respectable Monday editorial column.
Hopefully, Sam Nda-Isaiah, will help in promoting the cause of objective media ethics, by publishing this rejoinder.
E. N. Agbegir
AD Press & Public Relations
For: Presidential Adviser on Petroleum and Energy

Original_Search_Title: What's So Terribly Wrong With GM Food?
Headline: Kenya: What's So Terribly Wrong With GM Food?
Body_Text:
The greatest brake on a people's progress and health is food security and production. Without these, any society lives a nasty struggle between starvation and hunger.
There is also no time to develop specialised groups like writers, philosophers, doctors, scientists and a whole range of sophisticated craft workers and trades. The progress of hungry a society becomes stunted, simple and delayed. Yet genetically modified crops and foodstuffs have potential to make many poor areas of the world, including Africa, self-sufficient in food production.
Africa has always been the continent least able to produce surplus food. The fault is not in African farmers but soil and climate inputs. The average soil depth is just 45 centimetres - the world's thinnest. Three fifths of Africa is unsuitable for sustained yield from food crops. The same area is infested with tsetse flies and locust plagues. Africa's food production truly illustrates the saying that "geography is destiny".
Imported crops are now the overwhelming choice of African farmers. These include the banana, soybean and white rice whose origin was Asia, sugar cane and yams (New Guinea), maize, potato, tomato, squash, red beans and peanuts (Americas), the wheat-barley-oats group (Middle East) and lentils (India).
Many of these food crops did not reach Africa until 500 years ago with the growth of international sea-trade, explaining Africa's past low population relative to Eurasia - despite it's huge landmass.
The amazing variety of indigenous African wildlife was untameable. Despite success with North African wild cattle, Africa was unable to milk wildebeest, herd elephants, use Egyptian geese for poultry or warthogs for ham. Hence, it imported sheep, goats, camels, milk cows, pigs and chickens from Asia, Europe and Indo-China. Food shortage has been a perennial African problem, whether of vegetal or animal nature.
Africa began to catch-up with Eurasia in population only after the introduction of exotic genetic imports around 1400 AD onwards. However, the explosion from some 30 million people two centuries ago to 700 million today has brought Africa once more to a tough reality. It is a continent with harsh geography - at least in farming terms.
GM crops are a potentially wonderful emancipation of African agriculture that should normally be welcomed and researched by those of goodwill. To free Africa of food want would be a noble, humanising achievement. What "miracle rice" did for Asia, GM food might do for Somalia or Sudan.
Africa needs all the food advances it can get - and fast. However, not so for a loose association of anti-GM activists from Europe. These in turn have their own allies among Africa's well fed intelligentsia.
The more extreme of them demand a ban on any research into GM crops - so much for the march of science and an open mind. On the other hand, there are those who feel ill, but not at the idea of a GM meal. It is rather the sight of overfed white people lecturing starving black peasants about the need for 'proper' farming.
GM seed producers put "terminator genes" into seeds to ensure farmers buy new seed stock from multinationals. This prevents the spread of "rogue" seeds that anti-GM protesters presumably desire. Yet, farmers have been using F1 and F2 hybrid seeds for decades, also requiring new seed stock to be bought annually. This has raised no protest. GM seed is merely a logical step in plant breeding.
What does it matter if a poor farmer gets a double yield from GM seed and is "forced" to buy more seed from a company, as anti-GM activists oddly complain of? If he does not like this economic arrangement, he can return to his traditional seeds. He is, after all, free to choose.
Over five million farmers worldwide in the US, Argentina, Canada and China now grow GM crops on 50 million hectares. That's an area the size of France. Moreover, with big countries like Indonesia about to join in, the "GM club" is set to increase rapidly.
The EU activists cannot force such large nations into anti-GM stances but are happy to do so to hapless Uganda or Botswana. The height of absurdity (or hypocrisy) was reached when EU nations in August informed starving Southern Africans it was all right to eat GM maize from the USA, but they must not plant any in future for post-famine food production!
Dazzling the ignorant with pseudo-science and false fears is the way many anti-GM activists proceed. Africans should be left to research and choose their own approach to GM foods without coercion by Europe's agricultural lobbies and bourgeois food faddists.
Mr Covington is a tourism professional in Nairobi. Comments\Views about this article

Original_Search_Title: Police Uncovers Plan to Disrupt Voters' Registration Exercise
Headline: Nigeria: Police Uncovers Plan to Disrupt Voters' Registration Exercise
Body_Text:
Owerri — IMO State Police Command says it has received intelligence report that some people who are opposed to the on-going review of voters' register, have perfected plans to disrupt the exercise in some parts of the state.
"To actualise this nefarious enterprise, the group has chosen this period of the voters' registration exercise to celebrate new yam festivals, wrestling competitions and compulsory community meetings," Mr. Oko stated.
He described the act as "a deliberate attempt to scuttle a worthy Federal Government programme," as well as to disenfranchise millions of citizens of the state.
The command warned that it would not sit back and watch what it called 'few elements in the state, derail the nation's nascent democracy."
Continuing, the police warned that in view of the foregoing, "it would not tolerate any act capable of causing breach of the peace in the state or such acts that will portray the state as a lawless.
The Police command also promised to "apply minimum force where necessary to disperse any group or group of persons found to be disrupting in any manner, the voters' registration exercise."
The PPRO appealed to all law abiding citizens to go to the registration centres nearest to them, to get registered, and assured that adequate security arrangement have been put in place to ensure a hitch free registration exercise.
"Parents and guardians are hereby warned to advise their children and wards appropriately, to avoid unpleasant consequences, pleas," Mr. Oko stated.
Meanwhile, a gubernatorial aspirant on the platform of All Nigeria People Party (ANPP), Imo State, Chief Mike Ahamba (SAN), has appealed to the citizenry to 'go out and register."
Chief Ahamba made the appeal while addressing members of Saint Mulumba's Catholic Parish, Owerri, after participating in a pontifical song mass on Sunday.
"To register as a voter is a civic right. The peoples' vote is their power. You cannot vote in any person of your choice or vote out those who have failed to deliver to our expectations if we do not register now," Ahamba said.
Speaking also on the voters' registration exercise, during an exclusive interview with Vanguard in Owerri, the state chairman of National Democratic Party (NDP), Mr. Bom Ohiri, appealed to officials of Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to be fair to all the parties.
"If INEC is fair to all the political parties, there won't be any problem but if they decide to be funny, we will resist it," Engr Ohiri said.
He disclosed that his party is interested in this voters' registration exercise, adding that 'anybody who fails to work towards the success of this programme is an enemy of Nigeria and Nigerians."
Although Ohiri did not want to be drawn into giving his views on the content of the police bulletin, he however said that 'they may have their facts which I do I may not comment on it."

Original_Search_Title: A Load of Cash for a Loaf of Bread
Headline: Nigeria: A Load of Cash for a Loaf of Bread
Body_Text:
Bread is fast disappearing from breakfast tables as price hikes take it out of reach for most Nigerians
Bread, a staple food in   Nigeria   has disappeared from the dining tables of low-income consumers as a result of the increase in its price by 20 percent. A family-size loaf of bread that   sold at N100 in July  now goes for N120.  Newswatch gathered that this was necessitated by  the increase in the price of flour which  led to the strike action by   bakers in   Lagos  , August 6.
Matthew Ogunyanwo, national president of the master bakers and caterers of   Nigeria  , indicted the flour millers and the government for being responsible for the crisis that rocked the confectionery sector. He said the pains of prohibitive cost of materials and ingredients in the daily production of bread and confectionaries had made its production very uninteresting, discouraging and boring. This, he said, compelled bakers to increase the cost of bread.
Newswatch  investigation further revealed that since the strike ended, the size and quality of bread had deteriorated. Most families now have either reduced the consumption of bread or opted for other alternatives such as yam and bean cakes for breakfast.
Newswatch also learnt that apart from bread, the prices of other consumer items such as fish, turkey, chicken, gari, yam, rice, beans  had all gone beyond the reach of the average Nigerians.
Benedicta Openiyi, a retired banker and now proprietor of Tinuope Enterprises based in Iyana-Ipaja, who sells foodstuff and provision told Newswatch that apart from beverages, prices of food items go up on a daily basis. According to her, the purchasing power of most Nigerians  has gone down due to the devaluation of the naira, and non-payment of salaries as most workers are being owed several months of salaries. "Business is dull," she said.
Evangeline Ekpei, a dealer in frozen food, said a carton of chicken that sold for N2,100 about a month ago now sells for N2,800 while turkey that sold for  N2,400 now sells for between N3,200 and N3,800. She attributed the increase in the price of turkey to the ban placed on the importation of turkey by government and the recent seizure of turkey at the Idiroko  Border.
The destruction of the seized turkey by Customs, according to Ekpei, led to shifting  of consumption from turkey to fish and meat which led to the increase in their prices.
She said people  were scared of buying turkey from dealers because they felt the turkey in circulation was  part of the consignment the Customs officials used chemicals to destroy at the border recently. "We are now getting our daily bread from the sale of fish," she told Newswatch.
The high inflationary trend in the country is reflected in the consumer price index released by the Federal Office of Statistics, FOS. As at April 2002, composite consumer price index which measures changes in the average level of retail prices paid by consumers in both the urban and rural areas of the country was 4744. This represents a 2.2 percent increase above 4644.1 recorded in March 2002. The rise in the index, according to the FOS, was due to increases in the prices of food items and kerosine. According to FOS, the average consumer price level in April 2002 was higher than the corresponding level a year ago by 12.8 percent. This was substantially lower than 17.4 percent in March 2002.
The composite food index rose by 1.9 percent in April, 2002 due largely to the increase in the prices of staples as gari, yam, and plantain.
The monthly average charge in composite consumer price index for the four years 1999 to 2002 shows fluctuation in price level digits from a single digit to two digits level.  For example, in April 1999 the inflation rate was 9.9 percent and in April 2000 it fell to 1.8  percent but rose to 13.9 percent in 2001. As at April 2002 it had risen to 17.9 percent. This is contrary to government promise of bringing the inflation rate to a single digit.
Newswatch Volume 36 No 12, September 23, 2002

Original_Search_Title: C-River Gov't Urges Parents to Introduce Wards to Farming
Headline: Nigeria: C-River Gov't Urges Parents to Introduce Wards to Farming
Body_Text:
THE Cross River State government has advised parents in the state to introduce their children to farming with the same zeal which they would encourage them to go to school.
Deputy Governor of the state, Chief John Okpa, observed recently in Ikom Local Government that Nigeria was a proud agricultural nation before the advent of crude oil.
He said that before oil became the nation's numebr one foreign exchange earner, the West could boast of cocoa, the North, groundnut pyramids, the Eastern Region had palm oil amongst other cash crops from each of the old regions.
Okpa who was at Ikom to join the people in celebrating the new yam festival of the area praised the people for forging cultural affinity which engenders the spirit of co-operation and unity among them.
Although cocoa is produced in large quantity in the area "which is a good export product," he said the people there also harvest yam and cassava in large quantity and for export too.
He appealed to them and those of Akparabong not to relent in the cultivation of cocoa, reminding them that the old and dying trees of the commodity were pointers to the fact that efforts have dwindled in that sector.
Okpa then told them that as they celebrate the harvest of new yam festival they should give the glory to God "for His abundance gift of agriculture," adding that, he was proud to identify with a people who are conscious of their history and identity.
On his part, Chairman, Akparabong Development Association, Chief Odey Obim, said the visit of the deputy governor to the area was an indication that he cared for the people.
Obim said the visit was historic "and the home-coming of a worthy son who has always identified with the aspirations and problems of the people," appealing to God to see him through in all his endeavours.

Original_Search_Title: Price Increases On Gari, Yam Raise Food Index
Headline: Nigeria: Price Increases On Gari, Yam Raise Food Index
Body_Text:
Lagos — Federal Office of Statistics (FOS) yesterday in Abuja said rise in the prices of Gari, plantain and yam had contributed to the average rise in prices of other foodstuffs in June.
The FOS, in its food price index made available to the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) at the weekend, said that Composite Price Index rose by 1.2 per cent.
It said that the price of vegetables fell in many centres, but the level of the composite food index of 10.3 per cent was higher than the corresponding level a year ago.
It pointed out that the average annual rise for the 12-months period ending in June 2002 stood at 25.9 per cent.
The FOS further stated that "all items index less farm produce, excluding prices of agricultural produce, increased by 2.2 per cent in June 2002.
It said that within the period, the index rose by 19.7 per cent while average annual rate increase stood at 3.7 per cent for the same period.
