come here promising an economic heaven when, in fact, they will end up
putting us in hell. Whatever agreements government enters into with such
people or companies should be mutually beneficial.
The kind of agreements that allowed the Malaysians to take over our
telecommunications system, our drydocks and our film industry should not be
repeated. Apart from strict honesty and integrity on the part of government,
we should also expect government to do more than provide the so-called
enabling environment for economic growth.
I know that official circles spout the line that it is not the business
of government to be in business and that the private sector should be seen
as "the engine of growth." I agree that those Ghanaians who have the
resources and the will to establish industries to provide goods and services
should be encouraged.
They should be helped in the acquisition of land, for example, loans on
favourable terms and, possibly, reasonable tax breaks. But government should
not adopt a totally hands-off policy ­that leaves everything in the hands of
the private sector.
Centuries ago, Western governments and their people acquired the
enormous wealth they enjoy today through colonial and imperial exploitation,
pillage, piracy, burning, killing and maiming of subject peoples. Spain,
Portugal, Belgium, France and Britain were the most guilty in this
respect.
Old-style colonialism is dead but new ways have been found by the West
to continue with the exploitation. The World Bank and the International
Monetary Fund (IMF) are two very useful and devastating tools in the hands
of the West.
The West preaches economic liberalism for the rest of us while it
imposes all kinds of barriers to stop manufactured goods from the rest of us
from entering Western countries.
Of course, it is obvious that the Kufuor government cannot assemble
sailing ships and pirates and brigands to do what the West did centuries
ago. But the government should be more active in helping local business
people by taking another look at trade liberalisation. I am not asking for
an outright ban on all products. That is obviously unrealistic.
Still, something ought to be done to check the influx of many goods of
dubious quality that stifle the efforts of local entrepreneurs. Again, a
careful look should be taken about divestiture. Not all state enterprises
are loss-making. To divest them merely because the West says so could do
harm to our well-being.
Foreign investment, yes. But, government should also look inwards to our
people. Government can also be in business.

Headline: Nigeria: 'My Critics Are Mischievious'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Benue State Governor, George Akume, recently addressed some newsmen over the crises rocking the state chapter of the People's Democratic Party (PDP), Kola Ologbondiyan was at the event. Excerpts
How has it been administering Benue State in the last three years?
Governance is full of excitement and challenges. It is about making lives meaningful to the people. You know before you aspine for an office, you need to have a programme you intend to implement to the fullest. You are in position to manage the resources of the people in a meaningful and realistic manner and because the programme may sometimes be hampered in terms of the finance of the project.
But I want to say that by and large that it has been very exciting. I am working with the people, helping to solve some of their problems and above all providing the infrastructure for the benefit of all.
Although we are constrained because of inadequate resources to put necessary infrastructure on the ground.
But this administration is believe to have made voter available to states them previous administrations?
That is not true with due respect to Mr. President. It is true that he has tried to block some loopholes and that brought about marginal increase in the resources accruing to the state. The basic fact is that in 1999 when we took over my wage bill was N148 million. This was essentially because the past military government in Benue state did not start the implementation of General Abdulsalami's minimum wage. So when we started the actual implementation the wage bill jerked from N148million to N276million and we have to grapple with this problem. We inherited onstanding payment to public servants to the tune of over N886 million in terms of their salaries and allowance. I have spread the thing out for a period of time and by the end of 1999 I had levelled up.
By 2002 we started major drive, but what did we find? A new minimum wage which came on stream without consultation with the governors and this almost affected our wage bill. We pay over N500million as salary.
When you talk of more resources coming you should pay cognisance of the balance sheet, what we take in and what we pay out and remember, we are talking about a democratic government with a House of Assembly and the complement staff: we are talking about the executive governor, the deputy governor, and the complement of staff as provided for in the constitution. So we have more expenses now than what used to happen under the military. Therefore, it is not correct to say that more resources are coming to state governments. We have made this point clear and remember when president Shagari came up with a new minimum wage he also adjusted the revenues allocation to ensure that states have more funds to enable them pay and also to execute programmes that is what we have been saying. This has not been case under the present arrangement.
This is not really the problem of Benue State, it is the problem of states that do not have oil but again even within the context of states that don't have oil, you must look at their programme in terms of age of the state.
For example, Oyo, Lagos, Kaduna, Enugu, Edo states and hosts of others are older than Benue state.
Lagos started as the capital city with full infrastructure provided by the government, therefore Lagos is in a better position to generate more revenue than Benue state. Enugu, and Edo states are also placed in different perceptives with Benue State and of course closely because these were the original capital States and therefore, they have a minimum infrastructure, development efforts that is second to none and because of this so many people have been attracted to these places. Industries has also followed ----- so they are in a better position to generate more funds that Benue state After that you have another segment. This state that came on board before Benue and I want to look at development in form of the contribution of government towards development and infrastructure towards advancing the course of industrialization and so on.
Because in the final analysis what is there by way of internally generated revenues not necessarily the input of the present government. It is a historic effort which cannot be ever looked.
Having said that, let me also quickly add that the whole country is in problem. we have to go back to Agriculture, we have to go back to the fundamental of our own base existence and that is agriculture.
We are doing very well without oil, because of oil which is a major factor in industrialization, government tended to have parried other areas of meaningful development such as agriculture. Today oil is no longer able to meet the challenges of the development of this country. So we must go back to the basics.
In Benue state that is one of the cardinal thing we are trying to do. we are now given more attention to agriculture but remember agriculture in the first instance is also capital intensive.
If in some segments of this country the federal government has been able to put in place certain infrastructure that support massive agricultural development. We have dam irrigation in the far north because of the special environmental problems the desertification, ecological problems and so on. Today, those of us who never thought we could be part of that problems have become a part of it.
Therefore, we also require assistance from the Federal Government. Inspite of the fact that assistance is not forthcoming. we are not just sitting down, we have gone back into massive development of one key agricultural produce seeds, soya beans, yam, we are also the leader. We believe we can make a breakt hrough different produce and that is why we have decided to reactivate the Taraku Mills because it is a veritable market for farmers in Benue state. in the past because of the closure of Taraku Mills, farmers are not sure of where to sell their produce but as at today Taruku Mills is under rehabilitation. So you talk of processes in other sectors like sucrose, we are handicapped in this area because of special experience we had with the project in Kastina Ala but was sorted out and we are now at the threshold of moving Taraku mills has started textile production so we are very positive that in the next few months there will be a positive development in Benue state.
Secondly, we are looking at something bigger than that. We are looking at processing of our own yam in to yam flour and cassava into starch for process of local consumption and also international consumption. The market is immerse, we have received very encouraging reports that America and western Europe. So we are gearing up towards a major take-of to agriculture in Benue State. it is all embracing, it includes fishing, poultry and so on. But frankly we are handicap and that is why we have decided to go to the capital market to source for some of the funds, and we are also going to discuss with some of our foreign partners. we would have started very beautifully in that area but because of the September 11, 2002 bombing in the united state of America.
We have received very encouraging offers. Some of these people were ready to make huge commitment in the sphere of our economic development. Although a few of them have come , so I want to say that inspite of all these problems that I have enumerated what we are getting today on a monthly basis by way of locally generated revenue is 100 percent more than what we inherited when we took over in 1999.
I'll like to say the federal government needs to intervene in a major way in agricultural sector in the interest of this country's economy. Other countries have done it and they have continue to do it. There is no way we can take off very meaningfully without the intervention of the Federal Government. It is done all over the world. so Nigeria cannot be an exception.
And I think that the request of Mr. President that we should bring crops which we think if cultivated we could excel is for improving the lot of our people and also enhance our own internal revenue base. Here in Benue, we gave beans seed and soyabeans so partly the ball is in the court of the federal government because they have been trying to do something but they haven't done it but we are not just sitting down watching as we are producing in the area of agriculture.
What is your internally based revenue eneration now?
When we took over at a point it was N9 million in a month. But now we are generating between N60 m to N70 m but even that is not enough. Our target is N150 m but quite frankly we inherited a lot of problems.
Your party, the People's Democratic Party (PDP) is having some problem in the state. What accounts for this?
First, I will like to say that politics is a game at a higher level. where you have an organization as large as a political party, you will definitely have some little frictions here and there. For instance, if you have a large family, there is no way you can avoid occasional misunderstandings but these should not be so strong as to destroy the foundation of either the organisation or the family. I want to look at your question within that context. You have a sitting government and some other people went to believe that this government must take directives from them which is totally unacceptable. The issue is if you want to rule, you must subject yourself to the determination of the people. I want to say with utmost modesty that we don't have a real problem in Benue state. Agreed there are some people who for obvious reasons have decided to fight the People's Democratic Party (PDP) but they are marginally relevant to us and then you don't have to force people to remain in the same political camp with you. If they choose to leave. They free to do so. What has happened in real sense is that a few people who feel they can not control me have decided to vacate which is okay as far as I am concerned. It happens in every democracy. In Britain, the Labour party at a point lost some of its members who called themselves Liberal Democrats and that has not in any way tampered with the success of the party in that country. Here too, we are on a solid ground so it doesn't bother me that certain people are leaving the party. If a senator, who at the national level has contributed nothing on the floor of the senate never made any contribution when his people were directly invaded by the military, never moved any motion condemning the action of the military says he wants to be governor, let him come and contest because we are in a democracy. Don't forget that this is Nigeria where people don't even obey the law as such they are free to insult the governor because they want to run for the office. I wish them luck but I am not in a hurry I believe that I am in a better position than these people because I have mastered the road, I have traveled the road and I have known the corners the dangerous bend etc. but they haven't got that advantage. Some believe that if they can not make it in the Senate, they would make it in the state.
I have heard so many stories but I am not bothered at all because in the final analysis the people will determine and the people's verdict is supreme and I believe it will also be the verdict of history so we have no problem. Chief (Barnabas) Gemade has declared his interest to contest the presidential election, I will say good luck to him. But I don't know whether he is in PDP because a little band of supporters he controls has moved into what do you call it, UNPP on his directive but he says he will run on the platform of UNPP, I don't know where he really belongs. Remember that this is Nigeria.
In Britain, a very strong Labour man was suspended from the party for attending the function of another party. Here one leg could be here while another would be there and where you think is safe you eventually join one leg with the other. That is our brand of politics but PDP is in control of Benue state and by the grace of God it would continue to control the state. We are very solid. During our primaries the turn-out was very massive. It was as if we were holding an election and during my tour of the local government tour, the turn-out was massive where ever I went. So these are some of the parameters though which you measure your party strong but hey don't know. That is why sometimes they talk of coalition against the governor the existence of coalition means you have a believe that you cannot do it alone you have therefore worked out a desperate move to impossibly sink your differences and fight the governor but you can't achieve that. So you depend on your access to the media to say whatever but the truth is that at the fullness of time, the people will determined. Benue is firmly PDP.
There is a group called the Benue Proactive Coalition. Recently they placed a caveat at emptor in a newspaper against government and before now there were several publications in the same direction. How potent do you consider their fight against your government?
Really when I spoke about coalition. I was not referring to the group that calls itself Proactive Coalition because most of them can't define the word proactive and I'm not mincing words. You need to know most of the people calling themselves proactive. A majority of them are illiterates and I'm not kidding. Some of them were dismissed from service, some collapsed the Lobi Bank some didn't finish school some went to Custom and were dismissed, some are wanted for the refund of N40 million to Benue state government over JS Tarka Foundation they are just floating, some are not important to us at all yet, they say they are based in Kaduna but they want a change in government of Benue State. All of them can not win a ward in local government elections. We look at their profiles and we laugh. Honestly, let me give you their names and on your own find out whom they are, whether they possess any educational preparedness to effect any meaningful change and their antecedents whenever they have worked. Frankly, we are not disturbed by the proactive because they are some people who believe that if you want to make some notice-me-noise you go to the press. There was an instance when Mr. President received them in audience and when I saw him, he was talking about them and I was just laughing and I told Mr. president that you started it because they are your people who came to visit you. How can they support you because at the time they said the President he did not know the people he was dealing with. They came brought pictures they snapped with the president. But armed robbers can also snap photograph with me if I don't know them.
Although the President was shocked over their calibre and I know he would have learnt one or two lessons from the act attitude of these people. These are the people that have decamped to the UNPP and are going to Abuja to pledge their loyalty to the President. In any case even if they were in the PDP, they have no electoral value. they didn't play politics with us and we won all our elective positions. So what is there contribution to the success of the president nothing. Absolutely nothing, but they said they were disappointed when the president failed to give them something after their meeting.

Original_Search_Title: Learning From Sydney, Korea And Manchester
Headline: Nigeria: Learning From Sydney, Korea And Manchester
Body_Text:
Television has introduced new pressures on whole publics in countries
which witness the rituals and organisational techniques used by those who
are hosting international sports fiestas. Each spectacle becomes a challenge
or rebuke to others.
The Australians used the Sydney 2000 Olympic Games to tell the visual
history of their ethnological growth and highest achievement to date, as
well as their unfinished search for racial integration. The Koreans used the
construction of stadiums for the 2002 World Cup to integrate their
ecological sensibilities into their architectural designs; with the swans'
wings in flight being one of the designs much emphasised.
Their most visual message to television viewers was their communal
spirit which was spectacularly expressed in whole peoples in stadia wearing
the same red dresses. Their unspoken national income was in their team doing
better than Japan; a much resented, if not hated, former colonial power. Out
of Manchester (or "Madchester") 2002 Commonwealth Games came England's
ingenuity in exploring the frontiers of light as a structural element in
architecture; a celebration of their achievements in the racial
incorporation of her colonial grandchildren; and a rare explosive
appreciation of talent and excellence by athletes other than their own
except when beaten by the St. Kitts and Nevice in 100 metres.
They could not be blamed for a Queen who made them momentarily miss that
human electricity in Princess Diana, particularly at that moment in the
beginning of the games when the little girl with severe heart problem
brought the Jubilee baton containing the Beckam provided sensitive
support. The Queen might have done a lot for the spirit of her people and
the world if she had touched Kristy Howard, that brave little light of
England. That royal chill was a lesson of sorts.
The central message that runs through the three arenas of collective
sporting is the integration of a people's productive genius and spirit, as
well as what economists call "the multiplier effect", into the event. In
Australia you can see that every weaver of every type of ancient costumes
and tools had earned money from the thousands of units needed; painters,
musicians, choreographers, architects, engineers, industries had made money
from meeting the demands of the opening ceremony along.
In Korea and Manchester the organisers had made orders for shirts,
flags, balloons. Manchester added even brooms and paint and a canvas in
their closing ceremony. Each event pushed fireworks by computer designing to
new mountaintops.
Nigeria will be hosting the 2003 All Africa Games. The government has
probably made one false start already by building an "enclave" and not a
national stadium in Abuja.
The spirit of the nation's ancestors and heroes past might have been
better pleased with a stadium built by the brawn of Nigerians and not German
and Chinese technology power. The military corps of engineers working with
hundreds of thousands of unemployed youth and workers draw from a "federal
character" pool might have taken longer, but as the Egyptians say: go slowly
to get there faster. Instead, Nigeria will "showcase" a Julius Berger-China
made enclave stadium, and pass it off as a national achievement.
This is a far cry from the achievements of Australia, Korea and
Manchester. Apart from the malnutrition which the people's history and
spirit are being subjected to, the billions of naira (now speculated to rise
beyond to 90 billion) will not touch Nigeria's food producers; textile
weavers (if the workers were required to wear work clothes woven in their
home areas); fabricators of wheelbarrows and buckets for carrying soil and
concrete, brewers of burukutu and palm wine, pepper-soup, amala, pounded yam
and miyan kuka, kilishi and suya sellers etc., etc.
The 'multiplier effect" factor will not propel the local construction
engineering and manufacturing sector. The Australians told a narrative which
avoided this economic cardinal sin.
The enclave structures fall apart from neglect and pilfering after their
construction. Blame is put on "lack of maintenance culture". It is like
asking a stepmother to care for an orphan the same way a natural mother
would. The economic umbilical chord must enter Nigeria's sports
planning.

Headline: Nigeria: Maiduguri Faces One-Week Blackout
Body_Text:
Maiduguri — Government, business and domestic activities in Maiduguri are gradually grinding to a halt as electricity supply will be cut off for not less than one week: Already the city is in its 3rd day, blackout.
According to Maiduguri, NEPA district manager, Mr. Johnson Bola Adeyemi one of the three high tension policies used to divert power from the three pulled-down towers got broken and caught fire over the weekend.
Maiduguri metropolis and environs were earlier subjected to a four days blackout last month. Adeyemi explained that the power outage which began in July 2 was caused by a powerful windstorm which pulled down the high tension towers on the Maiduguri-Gombe 132KV line along Maiduguri-Damboa road, about 70km from the city.
He further explained that the Authority's engineers subsequently erected three temporary wooden poles to divert electricity from the broken steel towers. The option was taken as a permanent solution would have cut off the city and three towns from power supply for upwards of four weeks. It is one of the three women poles which fell apart and caught fire.
Adeyemi blamed windstorms for the incident. "The wind-storm has caused a lot of damages to our system by pulling down three-high tension towers and burnt one wooden pole which was used to supply electricity to the metropolis and its environs," he said.
He also said that contract for its re-construction has been awarded on the FG. According to him, best krypton, the contractors handling the project has mobilised material and men to site and assured that electricity supply will be restored immediately the pole is replaced a process that will take not less than five days.
One of the effects of the power outage is hiking of the price of water by its vendors, popularly called "Yam Moya" in the local parlance. Already a 25-litres jerry can of water has jumped from the old price of N5 - N10 to N25.
Also affected is the world bank assisted water scheme (WBAS) located near the University of Maiduguri which has ceased to function as there are no funds to buy diesel to power the standby generators.
Small businesses like hair-dressing saloons, business centres and barber-shops would also witness a down-turn in business activities owing to the power outage and high cost of buying fuel for their generators.
"However, our belief is that before this week runs out, the government, whatever the case may be, will release all our salary arrear."
THISDAY investigation however revealed that from all indication, the judicial crisis in the state is far from been resolved as the workers expect more than was paid.
Consequently, cases awaiting hearings in courts and the acquisition of legal documents through the courts for various reasons might still remain unrealistic dreams for the people of the state, should the workers decide to resume their strike action again for nonpayment of their salary arrears.

Headline: Nigeria: Adieu, Jolly Good Fellow
Body_Text:
Lagos — There was no foreboding of danger on the fateful day. Three friends, united by the journalism profession over the years, took time off their busy schedule to unwind. The triumviate had been reporting crime for their respective newspapers for a number of years. In a profession that is no respecter of weekends or public holidays, they decided to make the most of the little time on their hands, after a hectic Saturday work. En-route the fun cum-professional ride, from the blues, danger knocked. When the dusts raised by the unfortunate fatal auto crash cleared, one layed dead and the two others hospitalised following injuries received in the accident.
Succinctly put, this is the story of how Sisaa Agbo of The Post Express and Emma Nnadozie of Vanguard, and the late Ben Akparanta of The Guardian met their fate penultimate Saturday on the Third Mainland Bridge in Lagos.
Speaking to THISDAY shortly after his discharge from the hospital, Agbo, still wearing stitches on his head, said the trio had no premonition that danger laid ahead on the day in question. He said Ben and Emma had called him from a location in Ikeja and invited him over for lunch. Due to certain pressing matters in his own newsroom however, he told them on phone to go ahead with the lunch, but they agreed that they should meet him at The Post Express in Apapa for a certain assignment on the Lagos Island.
The duo came to his office as promised after a trip to Bariga and waited on him to tidy up before they moved over to Lagos in Ben's car. With their Lagos assignment over, Agbo led his two other friends to a joint at Bonny Camp where they sell his Tiv delicacy of pounded yam.
He recounted what happened shortly before they embarked on the ill-fated trip: "After my meal, Ben's brother, Richy, a staff of Hallmark Bank, called him to say he was waiting for Ben at Ojota at a place called Ngozi Hot Spot. Ben told him he was with Emma and me and the guy asked him to come with us because we all know one another. He said since we haven't seen for a long time, we should come for us to chat over a few bottles of beer. One of my naval officer friends wanted us to stay back and have drinks with him, but I said no, this man who called us now is our friend whom we had not seen for a long time. We then left Bonny Camp at about 8.00pm".
He said that when they hit the road, he sat on the passenger seat in front while Emma, who sat at the back tried to caution Ben to take it easy since they were no longer under deadline to keep the appointment. Agbo said all of them laughed and Ben who used to drive fast, slowed down considerably to the extent that they even poked jokes at him again why he was so slow. When they had advanced considerably on the bridge between 80 and 90 kph, he saidthey noticed a luxury bus in front of them, which Ben tried to overtake. With the poor visibility, he suddenly noticed there was a broken truck ahead of him and because the bus was by the side, he tried to apply the brakes and the next moment, the car somersaulted.
After it came to a halt, Agbo said Ben was the first to come out followed by Emma while he was trapped inside the car until eyewitnesses came to his rescue by pulling him out. "Ben and Emma were shouting, 'we are journalists, please help' and passers-by stopped to render help. People were now making calls to the police and the ambulance services notifying them of the accident", he said.
He stated that with blood streaming down their bodies, a good Nigerian among the people offered to take them to the Gbagada General Hospital, but the road network was so bad they could not make it there. They were taken to Folabi Medical Centre at Oworonsoki, where he said the doctor was very co-operative. After a check of their heartbeats, he concluded that Ben was the most critical and should be taken to the Lagos University Teaching Hospital (LUTH), while the two of them remained there. He said the same good Nigerian took Ben to LUTH.
At that point he said Richy called to find out why they hadn't arrived. When he broke the news of the accident to him, he said Richy told him he would go over to LUTH to see Ben immediately. But a distraught Richy called back to say he had combed everywhere in LUTH without the trace of Ben. The brother headed to the Ikeja General Hospital once more without luck.
By 11.30pm, the man who took Ben to LUTH came back to say he was eventually taken to the Gbagada General Hospital and probably because of their state, he did not want to reveal to them that Ben had passed on. He said the man could not go home that night because he was heard saying they had locked all the gates leading out and that he was going to sleep at the reception. "I asked him to leave his address for us but he never did when I asked the next day. So I don't even know the name of this good Nigerian", he said of the man who rescued them from the claws of death.
At six the next morning, he said Richy called to say he could not locate Ben whereupon he told him to go to Gbagada where he was eventually taken. Thereafter, Agbo made another frantic call to Ben's wife correcting the earlier information that her husband was in LUTH. Moments later, Richy called and in a very sober voice, said contrary to the information earlier that Ben was responding to treatment, that he was brought in there dead. Both of them were obviously shattered by the news on their hospital beds. They quickly made contact with Ben's wife again and as a decoy, told her to stay back and prepare something so that Richy and herself could take to Gbagada as a decoy.
A complete gentleman and a detribalised Nigerian in the true sense of the word, the late Ben, THISDAY learnt had always been a very careful driver who never allowed any fault to manifest in his car. He said Ben, who hailed from River State was married to a lady from Cross River State. When his wife had their first child about five months ago, he christened him Gbolahan. To him, he married while in Lagos and his fortunes changed dramatically. Therefore, Gbolahan, which means wealth in Yoruba, brought him wealth. A simple man by all standards, Agbo described the departed journalist as one who could hardly hurt a fly. "If you had any disagreement with Ben and you try to behave funny the next time you meet him, he would smile and walk up to you to shake your hands. Thereafter, he would plead with you to forget whatever transpired the previous day", Agbo added.
He described the parlous state of affairs in the country, which have brought this sorrow of losing Ben. "If the street lights were working, Ben wouldn't have been dead today. The road was so dark, you could not see far enough", he said. He also cited the attitude of the LUTH medical staff, whose non-challant attitude necessitated going back to Gbagada that night. On the night of the accident, there was no ambulance on sight on the Third Mainland Bridge to take them to the hospital despite of the claims of government officials of a 24-hour service on the long stretch of the bridge.
Also speaking with THISDAY from his hospital bed, head crime beat of Vanguard, Nnadozie, the third occupant of the car said: "We were to meet our colleague, Sisaa Agbo of Post Express. We all had an appointment, but before we headed for the Apapa office of The Post Express, I left with Ben to see somebody on Toyin Street, we used his red starlet tudox. By the time we left Toyin Street, we remembered we had an interview appointment with the Oba-elect of Bariga, Oba Timson, we raced to Bariga through Ogudu.
"On the way Nnadozie saw an obituary poster of a police officer and called the name out: 'Adegbite'. Akparanta applied his brakes, rushed down from the car and went to see the poster to confirm the name, then he rushed to the house which was close to the place and banged at the door to condole with the family. He enquired about the burial, he was told Adegbite had been buried about three days earlier and he said: 'Oh God, what is all this".
When they came out and saw the poster again, the late journalist was said to have lamented the death of the police officer, whom he called his friend. He did that for a long time. Nnadozie explained that it took them about an hour to get to the Oba's house due to a traffic jam, but unfortunately they did not meet the king at home.
Asked whether they used seat belts while they drove around town that day, Nnadozie replied: "Nobody thought of that. Before we entered the Third Mainland bridge, I remember I told my friends, gentlemen, let's take things easy . There is always this rush on the part of motorists, especially at that hour because of the fear of bandits. The place was not lit at all as it was in total darkness. We had the option of plunging into the river, into the lorry or allowing the car to be carried by the double decker bus beside us. I just heard the crash and that was all".
Nnadozie said he regained consciousness immediately: "I thought I was using my hand to break the glass, I did not know I used my arm. The car had turned sideways, I brought out my head, sympathizers came in and got me out of the vehicle. Then, I can't say what actually happened, it was as if Ben was crawling out of the vehicle, but Sisaa was trapped.
"I don't know how I managed to come out with my handset. While we were still looking for someone to convey us to the hospital my phone rang, it was my editor, Gbenga. I said Oga, I'm dying, he said 'Emma where are you, what happened?' I said I don't know where we were, somebody out of the crowd was explaining where we were". The sympathiser who took them to the hospital simply said he hails from Enugu State without mentioning his name.
Nnadozie, who had a broken hand and collar, is responding to treatment. He said a thorough x-ray had been carried out on him and no internal injury was found.
"We were all bleeding as soon as we got to the hospital. Ben was shouting, 'I'm bleeding inside'. I was wondering whether somebody can know if he was bleeding inside. I checked my own body to know whether I was bleeding inside".
Nnadozie stated that the Lagos State Commissioner of Police was alerted by their colleagues who got the information about what happened and he sent his men to go and check the vehicle. "Could you believe that before they got to the spot where the accident occurred the lorry driver had sneaked in to tow the lorry out of the place as if he deliberately packed the tipper there to cause the carnage, kill people and then realise it was time to take it after a life has been lost". Nnadozie queried bitterly.
Even as friends and relations continue to mourn Ben, Nnadozie asked: "The biggest question is how many people will they kill? What is the essence of having a bridge like Third Mainland Bridge without street lights? Why is the bridge not policed? Where were the Lagos State Traffic Management Agency(LASTMA) officials, who used to tow people's vehicles indiscriminately? Where were the Lagos State Emergency buses? He expressed disappointment at the way the dead are treated at the Gbagada General Hospital. He alleged the they dumped Ben's body amidst decomposing unidentified bodies in the hospital. "Before 12.00 noon on Sunday, Ben's body had swollen. It was difficult to identify him. It took the timely intervention of the Commissioner of Police on the orders of the Inspector-General , the Oba-elect and The Guardian management for them to retrieve Ben's body, confirm the autopsy and do the embalming".
Looking back at the accident, Nnadozie felt that the late Akparanta instinctively wanted to save the two others: "If he had allowed the vehicle to move the way we saw the lorry, we might have plunged into the lagoon. He tried. Everything happened so suddenly". He advised Nigerians to learn how to stop at accident scenes, because the victims might be somebody dear to them.

Headline: Nigeria: When Women Held Chevron Hostage
Body_Text:
Lagos — For weeks last month, women in the riverine area of Delta State took on Chevron Nigeria Limited, which they accused of neglect and exploitation. Sefy Megafu, who visited the area, writes on her experience
Ugborodo is a small town of five small communities in the riverine area of Warri, Delta State. In spite of the fact that five communities make Ugborodo, you can trek the whole town in about thirty minutes. But indigenes say that this did not use to be the case. They claimed that these communities were a wide expanse of land
This land is however, being submerged by the ocean. And the way the inhabitants live qualifies it for no other expression than a slum. Erosion is fast eating away the soil and what is left of the land is pitiable. Houses, built with bamboo and corrugated iron sheets are clustered together. And mosquitoes feed big on the inhabitants every night.
In this place there are no schools. The only private school has no teachers even as the people lamented that the state of the only Grammar School in Ogidigben, one of the communities in the area cannot be compared to a primary school in Warri.
The five communities namely Ode Ugborodo, Ogidigben, Mandangho, Ajudaibo and Ijaghala (which was sacked in one of Warri's crises all originated from a common ancestory and lacks basic social amenities. They have little or no contact with their neighbours in mainland, Warri. Shopping or trading is done on specific days. Sometimes through barter. The indigenes are forced to exchange their wares usually fish, crayfish, prawns for other commodities like garri, yam and pepper from the mainland.
A journey to the mainland could last as long as three hours on sea in a speed boat with hazards of high tides, strong currents and ocean waves. As a result of the foregoing, the indigenes have now become hostile. Any new face in town is perceived as a threat and regarded with suspicion, a stranger must be accompanied by natives to reduce the chances of being kidnapped, abused or taken hostage.
"If you go to that place, you will be sorry that you are from the community. No good house, no good roads, no good water and light. What are we saying? Afterall, we are not beasts. We are human beings. Even the indigenes have nothing to live on because Chevron's oil has driven away the fish, crayfish and the prawns", said an Ugborodo woman activist who spoke to THISDAY.
But on the other side, seperated by a small canal, stands Chevron Nigeria Limited, an American oil firm in grandeur. There are all necessary social amenities, communication system and easy means of transportation.
This contradiction is agonising for the people who live on the other side of the canal and for years, they have shown their discontent. A unique method of protest occured when some women stormed the Escarvos Tank Farm.
Escarvos Tank farm is Nigeria's third largest oil export terminal. Perhaps, this explains why about 150 Itsekiri women invaded the company's Escarvos Tank farm and laid siege on the crude oil terminal in utter disregard to entreaties from the oil firm to vacate the storage and export facilities.
The women who said that they did not destroy the firm's property, disclosed that they slept on the floor for ten days. They also noted that some slept in the boat and others in the waiting room at the airport. "All we wanted was to tell them our grievances". But the first two days, they admitted would have been rough if not that they cautioned the security guards, saying, "We are not for you. We have not come here because of protocols. We are not armed. Put yourself aside. We are for Chevron. We want our demands from Chevron. If you meet our demands, we are leaving. If you do not meet them, we are sitting down. This is our home".
The women's intervention could have been informed by the fact that previous resistances of oppression by the men - folk were often marked with violence.
To them, they have been exploited. Chevron has taken their means of livelihood. Mrs Dorathy Urowoh Ejuwa, known as the 'Iron Lady' who participated in the siege told THISDAY: "In my childhood days from Ogidigben we walked for miles before we could get to the sea shore. But today, it is not so. My grandmother's house is right in the middle of the sea. Most of the houses are right in the centre of the sea. Erosion has taken half of Urgborodo up to the sea shore. If the tide is low, you see the sticks of the houses about an inch or two".
Chevron is perceived as having a hidden agenda in which it wants to oust the indigenes from their ancestral home so that their land would be acquired.
They lamented that the place is being submerged gradually despite Chevron's capacity to sand fill. "May be they plan to do it when they buy it from us. At the moment, they want to write it off. They want to declare it irrelevant. It is being washed away by the ocean waves. During high tides, the whole place is flooded", the indigenes observed.
This suspicion heightened when Chevron insisted that the people had to leave their ancestral home to relocate to the new town that would be built across the Escravos river by the company.
"But we don't want to leave that place for them. Let them fill that place so that it will not be washed away by erosion. Urgborodo today, is the heart of this country. That is what is sustaining this country, the 95 per cent oil from Itsekiri land", she said.
Explaining further, she insisted that while Chevron's land is not affected by the sea, the indigenes suffer during high tides. Most times they have to climb on top of platforms (woods) to get into their houses. The oil firm, the indigenes believe is exploiting the community without a plough - back. They alleged that Urgborodo was doing well before the presence of Chevron. "Now the place is disorganised. The old women are dying of hunger because they cannot fish". Ejuwa maintained that Chevron must pay N50,000 per month to each elderly person from 60 years old for sustainance.
Fishing which was a means of livelihood is currently disturbed by the crude oil that flows into the water. Recalling the past, she stressed that iced fish was never seen in Escarvos. "My grandmother never knew what is called iced fish but now, Escarvos has turned to a land that eats iced fish". She stated further that the people could not even farm because of the gas line. "There is so much destruction in the community", she claimed.
The protesting communities said they were most annoyed that Chevron never showed any commitment to social responsibility towards host communities since its thirty years of production at the place. They likened the company's attitude to that of the colonial masters or what could be found in the apartheid regime. While condemning Chevron's relationship with the communities, they asserted that the US oil firm must do something tangible for the indigenes.
In a statement made available to THISDAY, it was stated that no amount of intimidation and resort to military occupation would dampen the spirit of the people in the fight for their rights.
This was why women felt the need to protest and take some demands to the oil firm. Among other things, they want their children to be employed by the oil firm without undergoing aptitude tests. The women of the communities, they emphasised, must be sustained with jobs in the Chevron yard. They insisted that Urgborodo community must have its quota in the firm's employment system.
Beside these, their land must be maintained to a reasonable standard as Chevron discharges its social responsibilities to its hosts. "We told them they have to do this very fast. When we say very fast, they should understand that language. We are not minicing words. They should immediately do something about the agreement. The employment must start right from the day of the agreement", Ejuwa stressed.
Although Urgborodo has been able to reach some agreement with Chevron especially in the area of employment not all the demands have been aceeded to. "We agreed on some things but not all because the demands were lenghty. For instance, we do not want our indigenes to be called contract staff. That is dehumanising. All indigenes working as contract staff are to be automatically converted to Chevron staff. We created some employment opportunities", explained a notable spokesman for the community.
The general notion, THISDAY gathered was that the multinationals in the Niger Delta float registered corporate business concerns that front for them through which they award contracts to themselves with the use of outside labour. Another issue that broke the camel's back is the fact that Chevron was said to have recently recruited fifty new people from Lagos. Sources revealed that the recruits are currently undertaking training in the US.
But Urgborodo Community Trust which has the official mandate to run the affairs of the community threatened that the new recruits would never work on the farm unless the indigenes were recruited as well. "It is impossible for them to work here. We will endanger their lives. They are certainly not going to work here", THISDAY was told.
Furthermore, the communities demanded that Chevron must inform the indigenes to prepare their people anytime the company intends to recruit workers.
Meanwhile, the oil firm has promised to give the local people 60 per cent of its employment quota according to an official who spoke on behalf of the secretary of Urgborodo Community Trust, Hon. Elliot Kperegbeyi.
The development is based on the realisation that Chevron had failed to implement the 60 per cent quota system for the indigenes and 40 per cent for the catchment areas.
Chevron will also empower the indigenes with community skills and allow the communities to tap energy (electricity) from the Tank plant. Confidently, the official assured that the situation would warrant the community not being taken for granted any longer. He described the dialogue as partially successful in the sense that they would like to see how far the oil firm would implement the agreement.
Problem emanated when Chevron failed to honour the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) that was signed with the community. The agreement stated that Chevron would construct a new town for Urgborodo people. The new town was tied to the first phase of the company's projects. But at the completion of projects Chevron tried to embark on its Liquid Gas Project. Needless to say that the indigenes believed that Chevron allegedly refused to develop their land because of what they assumed to be a sinister move by it to occupy their land. "That is why they do not want to develop Urgborodo at all. Ask them to do something, they say no afterall , we want to build a new town but the new town is not forthcoming".
Certainly, the Itsekiri are not alone in their demands. Just after their dialogue ended, Ijaw women took up the mantle and launched an eleven-day siege on Chevron's four oil flow stations. The siege came amidst Ijaw rulers call for compensation for ecological damages in the community.
Consequently, in an MOU signed by Chevron and the representatives of Ijaw women at Abiteye, the company promised to strenghten its relationship with the host communities, maintain continous dialogue and consultations with them while supporting sustainable community development, local business development and training and skill acquisition to enhance development in the community. It further conceeded to a deliberately fair and equittable representation of qualified indigenes from the communities in the company's work force.
Chevron will also build a cottage hospital at Oporoza and employment positions by the firm shall be shared equally among the ten villages that make up the community. Additionally, it would pay N200 million as compensation for damages in the community. Still, some representatives of the Ijaw community seem not satisfied with the proposal and claimed that they had not been given conscession in the agreement.
In the same vein, Ekpan community that threatened to disrupt the production of the oil firm if not attended to has succeeded in reaching an accord with Chevron.
A letter in which they expressed disappointment and distaste over the contemptous and provocative manner in which they are treated as regards employments and contracts stated: "Be guided that the parcel of land housing your establishment is a bonafide property of Ekpan community. We are entitled to a reasonable per cent of employment, development and contract quota for Ekpan community in accordance with government's 60 per cent statutory policy on employment and contracts for catchment areas/host community, which have conspicously eluded us. We wish to therefore, vehemently protest Chevron (Nig) Ltd's non - compliance in the areas of employment, servicing companies and contracts".
The statement, which was signed by the Ekpan woman leader, Chief Bridget Erigha, noted that though Chevron had always complained of lack of vacancy, those at the helm of affairs employed members of their families and divert opportunities meant for the community in disregard of government's statutory laws.
As at the time THISDAY visited, the disruption had cost the NNPC/Chevron Joint Venture 110,000 barrels per day of oil. Another 300,000 barrels of crude oil were lost to an inferno that engulfed one of the crude oil storage tanks at the terminal. The total loss was estimated at $7.8 million.
As dialogue continued between Chevron and the host communities in the Niger Delta, the Federal Ministry of Environment warned oil multinationals, construction firms and manufacturing companies operating in the region to adhere strictly to environmental laws or face the full weight of the law.
Similarly, the European Commissioner for Development, Co-operation and Humanitarian Aid, Mr. Poul Nielson while on a three - day visit to Nigeria to inspect some micro projects and areas of environmental damage in Port Harcour, asked the oil companies to clean up major oil spills that occurred over thirty years as well as the most recent spill which he said are polluting the environment.
Recent findings by environmental experts posit that the restiveness in the Niger Delta has to do with increasing noise level in the zone and the flaring of gas 24 hours a day. Studies have also linked such atmosphere to increasing risk of stress, high blood pressure, madness and other diseases. "The environment is so hostile that at the slightest provocation, the people become restive and ready to fight. People are just damaging the environment and the health of the people without regard to safety standards", said an environmentalist, Prof. Lou Obispo.
With the stance of the Governor, James Ibori, the communities are perceived as having government's backing. Recently, he stated, "I believe that these issues can be discussed and resolved amicably. The best way to deal with it is through dialogue. I deeply sympathise with them. I am from this community so I feel what they feel". Apparently, he believed that the local people were being marginalised and deserved a better deal and insisted on a need for a change of policy.
Hon. Baro Henry, former leader, Legislative Arm, Uwie Local Government Council in Effurun, in his comments, noted that such an admission by the government portends something good. He explained that if the communities had been having a little backing from government, they would not have been contemptously treated. He called on the government to ensure that companies operating in the area live up to their social responsibilities.
Still, in many quarters it is believed that the problems in the Niger Delta bother on the restructuring of the country. It is assumed that anything less than that will continue to make the oil companies to marginalise the region.
According to Mr. Samuel Jimoh Eshenake, Manager, United Community Bank Ltd, Ekpan, the federal system is dominated by the majority ethnic groups. He advocated that Nigerians need to go back to the roundtable and stressed, "We must begin to talk about resource control. I want us to define it beyond derivation in such a way that we must be a major stakeholder in the industry. We must come back, we must talk".

Original_Search_Title: Stories About the Rand Are World's Apart
Headline: South Africa: Stories About the Rand Are World's Apart
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — As expected, Christine Qunta's minority report on the rand's 37%
depreciation last year did not provide any fireworks. It merely called for
more investigation, a call that government is unlikely to heed.
Qunta failed to come up with new evidence or starkly different
conclusions to those reached by the other commissioners, advocate John
Myburgh and Development Bank of Southern Africa CE Mandla Gantsho.
Although most of the differences related to the use of language, or what
Justice Minister Penuell Maduna refers to as nuance, the reports did give
important insight into broader debates about how SA should handle its
growing integration into the global economy.
It can be assumed that the authors of the majority report are more
comfortable with SA's integration into the global economy, despite the risks
it poses for the country's small and relatively open economy. This is in
line with the thinking in the presidency and the national treasury, which
sees globalisation and the fuller integration in the global economy as not
just inevitable, but desirable.
On the basis of her report, Qunta might plausibly be classified as an
economic nationalist, who views big corporations with suspicion and probably
sees greater regulation and not less as the solution to the country's
vulnerability.
The report almost takes one back to Trevor Manuel's early days as
finance minister, when he used to rail against the disproportionate
influence of "amorphous markets" on people's lives.
This is most evident when Qunta touches on the role of speculators
citing the example of the east Asian crisis of 1997 and her defence of the
Reserve Bank's administration of exchange controls.
In her report, Qunta draws widely on the experiences of the socalled
Asian tigers, and largely accepts the view that currency crises tend to have
more to do with outsiders' perceptions and actions than domestic
policy.
At face value, the Malaysian case would seem to support the
argument. Its currency depreciated 40% in six months during 1997 despite
posting economic growth of 8,7%, having low inflation of 3,8%, a 2,5%
unemployment rate and an external debt equivalent to 42% of gross domestic
product (GDP).
Qunta also quoted Joseph Yam, CE of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority,
who in 1999, blamed the Asian financial crisis on unmanaged and unregulated
capital outflows.
The trigger was the collapse of the Thai baht after investors, spooked
by a sharp drop in the country's exports, sold assets in anticipation of a
devaluation of a currency they felt was over-valued, but ironically given a
superficial boost by capital inflows.
It quickly spread through the whole region, affecting seemingly healthy
economies from Malaysia to Hong Kong.
The Malaysians took it particularly hard, with President Mahathir
Mohamad changing overnight from one of the darlings of the Washington
consensus to one of its loudest critics. Statements that crony capitalism
was largely to blame were particularly hurtful because of the suggestion of
racism in the analysis.
But what was clear was that the punishment meted out to east Asia was
disproportionate to the crime.
Mahathir blamed the crisis on greedy speculators and reacted by
reintroducing selected exchange controls, including putting a stop to the
trade in the currency and fixing its rate to the US dollar.
Those days seem a distant memory now as the economy recovers from the
bruising it took.
Those who said Mahathir's rejection of orthodoxy would lead the economy
into a path of destruction have been forced to eat their words. For their
part, the Malaysians seem oblivious to the fact that their neighbours, who
did not reintroduce exchange controls, have also since recovered.
Qunta, though, is likely to have taken expert testimony by
representatives from Malaysia's central bank to heart, and probably saw it
as confirmation of the inherent dangers of opening up one's economy to the
vultures sitting in financial institutions' trading rooms.
The theme is also evident in her defence of the Bank's actions and her
findings regarding Deutsche Bank's activities.
She does not make conclusive findings about the legality of Deutsche's
asset swap deals, except to call for an investigation by the office of the
director of prosecutions the main difference between her and the other
commissioners.
Another source of difference is that she absolves the Bank's October 14
statement, which told banks to observe existing exchange control
regulations, from blame.
She stops short of accusing private banks' CEs of lying or misleading
the public, but dismisses their claims that the October statement had been
misinterpreted, noting they had been fully briefed before it was made
public.
Her distrust of private sector players and preference for more rigorous
regulation also becomes obvious here.
She notes that for exchange control regulation to be effective, there
has to be an element of trust between the regulators and the regulated. She
notes that most big corporations view exchange control in a negative light,
the implication being that they cannot be trusted to honour their
responsibilities.
Qunta is also critical of the fact that the commission never had the
scope to investigate the activities of authorised dealers, including their
loss or profit positions during the period under review.
With their inconclusive findings, the most that can be said of the
reports is that they probably shed more light on the debate about the
economic direction SA should take.
There are two streams of thought. On one side there are those willing to
play the global game within the existing framework. Qunta represents those
in favour of a more forceful approach, which is not shy of increased
regulation.

Original_Search_Title: Yam Traders Want Security Assistance to Retrieve Trapped Goods
Headline: Nigeria: Yam Traders Want Security Assistance to Retrieve Trapped Goods
Body_Text:
Lagos — Yam dealers in Plateau State have sent a Save Our Souls (SOS) plea to the state government to assist to retrieve their trapped goods, particularly yams, in the troubled spots of the state.
A spokesman for the dealers, Mallam Salisu Nayalwa, told journalists in Jos,the Plateau State capital, that their yams and other commodities worth over N20 million have remained trapped in Shendam, Longtang North, South and Klasa council areas.
He explained that they fled for their lives, living behind their properties and goods when hostilities broke out in villages they went to purchase goods.
Nayalwa said they need the state government assistance to retrieve the goods because it is unsafe for them to go back to the villages with security escorts with killings still going on in some of the places.
He named villages still involved in fighting as Mabudi, Wadata, Dadin Kowa and Gwiwar Kogi. He said while normalcy seemed to have returned to the headquarters of the councils areas, some of the towns were still being ravaged by Fulani and Taroh militias.He appealed to security agents to assist them, pointing out that their goods were being destroyed by the warring tribesmen.

Headline: Nigeria: Governor Akume's Wife Speaks
Body_Text:
Mrs. Regina Akume, the Wife of Benue State Governor, Chief George Akume,
Sheds Light On How And Why She Chose Him As a Life Partner.
From all indications, you are an ardent Roman Catholic. What shaped
your choice of religion?
I was born into the Catholic faith. I grew up as a Catholic child.
My parents were Catholic and therefore bred me in Catholic ways. I have held
on to it because it is what keeps me going.
I can see too, with the number of people waiting to see you, that you
are a very busy person. How do you cope being a housewife and the First Lady
of the state?
I am busy doing those things I am supposed to do. I have helps, both
office and personal ones. They are very efficient. I do not do everything by
myself, but I try to see often times what they do. I try to know what my
husband is going to eat and all that.
Do you still cook for your husband?
I do not. I have many able cooks. But what I do is to know what my
husband wants to eat and arrange the food for preparation. Thank God, my
husband is a very good person. He is not picky and what I put on the table,
which I feel is best for him, he eats. From time to time, I ask him
specifically what he wants to eat. Sometimes, he gives orders and at other
times, he asks me to cook whatever I desire.
I know your people love pounded yam, do you also love it?
I am not a lover of pounded yam. It may interest you to know that I
love garri (eba) and a lot of vegetable. The other one is tuwo (guinea
corn), and my mother still makes amala for me. I also eat rice a lot with
vegetable.
How do you start your day, I mean, right from the morning rituals?
I have a calendar of days for prayers. Mondays are for the twenty-one
decades of Rosary, the family wakes about quarter to five o'clock to say
this. On Tuesdays we say five decades of Rosary.  Wednesdays the fifteen
decades and Thursdays are for the family altar, where we read the Bible
together and also preach to the children. At times, we wake up 4.30 a.m. to
do this.
Fridays, we say the Holy Wounds of our Lord Jesus Christ and the
teaching of the Cross and Sorrowful Mystery. Saturdays are for the prayers
of St. Michael, the archangel and Sundays are free days, for Church and to
go to places you may want to go. In the evenings too, we come together to
say the prayers just before going to bed. I have been doing this for the
past fifteen years and it has become a part of me.
You know, I am Mariam's daughter.
How is your typical day like?
After prayers, I go to the office to receive visitors. At times, more
than a hundred of them. And I love listening to people's problems. I may not
have the answer but my prayer is that Lord make me able to listen to them as
a mother and those I can help, Lord, assist me to help them, if I cannot,
they should at least leave my office with smiles on their faces.
Anything in your upbringing that has prepared you for this role?
My father was a very compassionate person and I am my father's
daughter. My mother is a very disciplined person. She is still alive while
my father died a couple of years back. The fact is that, I love reaching out
to people. I operate better when I am in the midst of lower cadre (people)
who are prepared to give you their hearts. I do not feel too good in the
midst of the rich, i.e. the famous. Perhaps, because I am from a poor
parental background, I do not mind sitting with them all day just listening
to their problems. You know too that my father had twelve wives and we, the
children, are over forty in number. I may have picked this from there. And I
try to reach out to all of them. At primary school, I was the school's head
girl. I was really tough and stubborn. My husband was also in my class.
Will it be correct to say that the relationship between you and your
husband started while you were in primary school?
We started the boyfriend and girlfriend stuff in 1965. Then, we only
wrote letters like: You are the 99 per cent of my heart; you are the only
mosquito in my net; you are the only sugar in my tea. So, we always wrote
letters, exchanging gifts like handkerchiefs; I would knit one and send it
to him, and we took pictures together. My husband sat directly behind me in
class. He was very supportive of me right from then. Any time I made a
noise, as the class monitor, he would write down my name. When the teacher
asked him to give a knock, he would just do it so gently that it could not
even hurt a fly.  Somebody else would then be asked to do it. And if that
person gave me a hard knock, he would continue to write the person's name as
a noise maker for sometime.
Do you still have that time now to do the things you used to do while
growing up with him?
I am still energetic. I run around a lot and I am still strong.
Thank God I still do the things I used to do.
As a busy couple, do you really have time for your bedroom duties?
I do not want to talk about that here (laughs). My husband is a strong
man but I do not want to talk on that. I am raising children in the
religious way. Not that it is bad to talk sex, but not in the media. Just
take it like that, my husband is still very strong.
How and what sweet words did your husband say to you that made you
fall in love with him as a husband?
(Laughs). My husband is a very shy person. It took him a very long
time to say I love you. He is not an exhibitionist. He is an introvert. I
knew what I wanted and I let them (parents) know that.
What really made you fall in love with him?
At that time, we could not really talk much. Your days are a lot
better than our days. I think because he was very kind to me, he accepted
me, even as I was a difficult girl. Being a tough girl, nobody wanted to get
close to me. You know, I was in the class of only boys and I was behaving as
if I were a boy. And he could see me as a woman when others were running
away, saying I was too tough. He came close and tried to understand
me. Until now, he still displays those qualities.
How many children do you have for him?
I have three children.
Did the two of you attend the same higher school?
No. I did my first degree in the United States where I read Home
Economics and Nutrition. I did my Masters in Public Administration (MPA) in
Benue State University, Makurdi.
What are those things that turn you on?
Well, inwardly, when I reach out to the less privileged. When I go to
villages, reaching out and touching the lives of these rural dwellers. When
I watch them receive these gifts, they roll on the floor in deep
appreciation. I feel very happy when I see them do this.
Any phobia?
Yes. I do not climb steps or planes and look down.
It scares me. I am allergic to people who keep malice because, I have a
tremendous capacity to forgive. And I cannot take people who destroy others
because they want to get something from you. I hate people who do not like
progress. I do not get angry with them, but I persuade them to change.
How do you relax?
I read, especially the Bible and religious books and sometimes, I
watch Christian movies. Thank you.

Original_Search_Title: The Masquerades Come Dancing in Akoko
Headline: Nigeria: The Masquerades Come Dancing in Akoko
Body_Text:
Lagos — The new yams are here. And it is the season of festivals in the farming communities across the country, especially in the southern region.
For the Akoko people in Ondo State, this is an important festival that draws the sons and daughters of the community even those residing outside. From Akoko North-East to Akoko South-West, from Akoko North-West to Akoko South-East Local Government Areas, the various communities began the yearly celebration to welcome the new yam in turn early this month at Ikare Akoko. However, the celebration train moves to a community in Akoko South West- Okaodo-Iwaro- renowned for its rich Odun Ijesu (new yam festival) tradition dating back to primordial times.
In contemporary Nigeria, very few communities, especially in the Western part of the country, still celebrate the new yam festival the traditional way. Nowadays, most communities are fast discarding the age-long tradition labeling it, 'barbaric and primitive,' while some modify and re-christene it, 'Day' using the name of the community.
Yet, some communities, especially in the Eastern part of the country, still keep the tradition as passed on by the ancestors. In contemporary Enugu State for instance, the new yam festival is still being traditionally celebrated, at least, in eleven parts of the State. Each community still retains the name by which its ancestry found the festival.
Like their counterparts in the East, the Okaodo-Iwaro community, though have made some modifications in the manner they celebrate the festival, it has tried to hold on to tradition as passed down by their forefathers. They have suppressed all attempts at making them abandon the tradition.
While announcing the programme for this year's celebrations last week, the traditional ruler of the community, Oba Lawrence B. Omorinbola explained that the community does not fancy re-christening its new yam festival because "it is a traditional legacy passed down to us by our fathers. We cannot wake up one day and just change it because of our contemporary religious leaning."
Though, himself a staunch Christian, he says he believes as tradition holds it, that the living are not the only ones celebrating the festival. "Our ancestors are among the celebrants. It is not just our festival. It is a festival for both the living and the dead. They do visit us at that time. How would they feel if we have to change the tradition just like that?" he asked.
He argued that "What we are celebrating is Odun Ijesu Titun "arrival and eating of the new yam." The festival marks the end of the farming season indicating a time of rest before the commencement of another farming season. We cannot re-christen it 'Iwaro Day,' because it's not the community's birthday. May be we may have a day set aside for such day in future," he said.
On his part, the President of Okaodo Progressive Union, Lagos Branch, Prince Tony Kayode, expressed that Odun Ijesu Titun is the unifying factor that holds the community together. "It is one festival that our kinsmen and women never want to miss. It is good for the community also, because it always offers us the opportunity to discuss the progress and development of our town. That is the only time that you can see majority of our people visiting home. More than just a festival, Odun Ijesu Titun is a re-union call for sons and daughters of Okaodo-Iwaro community. It is a period when indigenes come together to show love by eating the New Yam from the same bowl and drinking from the same keg of palmwine, " he enthused.
He explained that though, the festival still maintains its rich ancient tradition, "it has also gone through serious modification to reflect contemporary pop culture." Part of populist culture added to the celebrations these days is the invitation extended to popular musicians to entertain the indigenes and guests in live performances during the festival.
"Since 1989 or thereabouts, Chief Elemure Ogunyemi, the Ekiti music exponent has been a regular feature at the festivals. Last year, one of our very own facilitated the participation of one of Fuji music's greatest artistes- Wasiu Alabi, known as Pasuma Wonder. And he has promised to come this year, even if uninvited. "
He said that Okaodo-Iwaro community is a potential tourism enclave if properly harnessed. "Gradually, we are taking the Odun Ijesu Titun to a level that it will assume a cultural carnival with universal appeal". He, like Grisso, an African of the Diaspora, born and raised in Trinidad believes that Africa is the spiritual origin of the various carnivals found in all the countries of the New World where Carnival has become an institution - notably in Rio de Janeiro, New Orleans, and Port of Spain. "We want to show the world that Africa is the origin of carnivals found in cities around the world," he said.
He added that visitors to the festival also have the opportunity of visiting Oke Maria, on the mount Gboli range about 100 metres away East of Okenode. Oke Maria is an ancient Catholic pilgrimage spot for Christians the world over. Church history holds that a popular Reverend Father saw the apparition of the Virgin Mary on the spot early 19th century.
The Okaodo-Iwaro Odun Ijesu Titun is a celebration of the peoples' rich culture and tradition. A week-long affair, the festival features cultural displays by masquerades, hunters Ijala, Oton Dance by Orisha Oko worshippers, maiden dance by worshippers and maidens of Umote goddess, among others.
The festival normally begins with the clearing of all the roads leading in and out of the community by appointed age grades. As it is believed in most communities celebrating the new yam across the country, the people of Iwaro Akoko also believe that the ancestral spirits who will visit the town during the festival will have to use the roads. More importantly, the road leading to Okun Ore, (under the Ore tree) on mount Gboli, in Okenode, about 800 metres in the outskirts of the town.
Okenode was where the people first settled after leaving Ile-Ife in the wake of the spread of the Yoruba Kingdom after the demise of Oduduwa, progenitor of the Yorubas. One of the things that attracts tourists to visit Okenode is its surviving 16th and 17th century architecture. All the houses have collapsed but the palace built by Asin Dawodu I (Olugbangada) with a string of stream behind it remains in tact.
Though the community had since resettled in Iwaro, Okun Ore, the ancestral royal shrine remains paramount in the life of the people. That is where all Asins are crowned till today. Hence, the yearly pilgrimage to Okun Ore where sacrifices are offered to the ancestors and the gods of harvest and for peace of the town and prayers for bountiful harvest in the approaching farming season during Odun Ijesu Titun.
The Okaodo-Iwaro Odun Ijesu Titun has a long and intriguing history. Legend has it that it evolved about 300 or 400 years ago during the reign of Asin Olugbangada. A terrible famine ravaged the entire kingdom threatening the population of the tribe. Olugbangada, a king with great magical powers, was said to have entered and spent three months inside the Olugbangada stream. At the end of the third month (towards the end of July), he was said to have returned with assorted food crops and fruits including the yam which he introduced to the farming community for the first time. He then charged the community to set aside a day to celebrate the arrival and eating of the new food crop, yam.
"Since then till today and I hope forever, the community has been celebrating the arrival of the new yam. And yam has grown to assume a prime position in the diet of the community," Oba Omorinbola stated.

Original_Search_Title: Proposed New Kaduna 'Ll Generate N20b Annually, Says Committee
Headline: Nigeria: Proposed New Kaduna 'Ll Generate N20b Annually, Says Committee
Body_Text:
THE proposed New Kaduna State if created, would generate about N20 billion annually from local endowments, the chairman of the committee for the creation of the state, Col. Elias Nyan has said.
He noted at the inauguration of the 15-member state creation committee in Kaduna that the area with a population of about 2.5 million, was blessed, in commercial quantities, with ginger, cassava, yam, soya beans, solid minerals, fisheries and livestock.
According to him, "we have the best all year round grazing reserves that can be tsetsefly-free with sufficient water for livestock development for export.
"We already know that we are endowed with sufficient skill to develop and train manpower for export to other states and neghbouring countries.
"All we need is freedom for self determination which the creation of a new state will offer," he said.Col. Nyan justified the yearning for the creation of New Kaduna State from the existing one, saying that the agitation was brought about by the search for justice."We want our state because we want justice. It is the lack of justice that forces our people in a crisis situation to put themselves on the line of fire to be shot and killed in the protection of their dignity."It is lack of justice that makes it possible that our own clap and jump for joy at the demise of ours.
"It is that lack of justice, met lack of peace, that ----- our people to be level that they think it is normal to deny their own and adopt an adversary in a contest that is otherwise within their own reach to win.
"Nobody can teach us anything about peace and nobody can deny us justice forever.
"Intellectual as the battle for the creation would be, we shall demise all democratic means to ensure our total self-determination," the chairman stated.He urged the people of the South part of Kaduna which makes up the proposed state to have faith in themselves and the ability of the committee to press for the relisation of their dream for an own state.Col. Nyan therefore called for their cooperation and support for the onerous task ahead.

Headline: Nigeria: JAMB As the Model
Body_Text:
Is the Joint Admission and Matriculations Board just astoundingly abreast of our hi-tech world, or did the sanitization process which had begun under the Abacha administration actually borne fruit? Perhaps a bit of both, but the fact is, JAMB has proved itself to be the comeback kid. An organization that has been so fraught with fraudulent activity, a sad state of internal administration and the inability to quite gain or earn the confidence of the people (including those who depended on it as the only route to university education, and later to other tertiary institutions of learning in the country) is suddenly an organization to watch.
To suggest that all the corrupt people have been flushed out of JAMB is to underestimate the power and the proliferation of corruption in this country. Corrupt people remain in their multitude, sadly, particularly in our civil service. It would not require any stretch of the imagination to figure out this includes JAMB.
Yet, JAMB has somehow made itself a model for all parastatals by employing the only means by which an organization can function properly. Structuring. Jamb has managed to put in place structures which will live after the people who created them, which will prevent massive corruption of the scale to which we are used, which will continue to ensure administrative efficiency long after its creator is gone unless there is some kind of sabotage. JAMB has demonstrated beyond reasonable doubt its belief in the theory that no one person's interest can supersede the national interest, and that national interest can be crowned by structures.
First, by its new 'computerized' system, JAMB deletes automatically a multitude of ills. The most obvious of these is the problem of crowds which have traditionally assailed the organization's offices from time immemorial.
