The number of students who have to see a JAMB official for their own peculiar problem will have to personally and physically see them, but a student who just wants to get the same information which thousands of others want to get will simply visit a website. Of course, this would come with its own problems, but progress always does.
Naturally, the less the access is to the officials, the less the access will be to the corrupt official, but the most important aspect of JAMB' hi-tech move is that it proves that accountability and openness makes a huge difference not only to the presence of the organization but to its very future.Our democracy has a lot to learn from this.
Yet JAMB has not only raised to international standards the level of the expertise of its individual employees, it has created huge revenue and a constant source of that to the private computer practitioners. It has raised the infotech awareness of the over a million students who took the examination this year, and there is no way this will not have its effect on the standards of graduates we turn out from Nigerian universities from now on.
Sycophancy and the abuse of democracy
The last time Mr. Annan visited Nigeria, he left us to face our national bereavement alone at the death (by tea partying, if it must be put in lay terms) of Nigeria's president-elect who was never to be, the late Bashorun M.K.O. Abiola. Abiola was hell bent on eradicating poverty, and most believed he would have. His death therefore had been like putting a steaming bowl of gourmet prepared pounded yam and edikaikong in front of a famished man for long enough to have him inhale the maddening aroma, and then taking it away from in front of him just as he is about to get at it.
It has taken Mr. Annan a little while to pay us a condolence visit, but he could not have made his short visit any more memorable, especially judging by the words by which he addressed us. Without as much as accusing us or pointing fingers, he had spoken about the abuse of democracy, a major disease and scourge ravaging our nation today.
If the fear of God is the beginning of wisdom, the fear of any lesser man or entity than God must, by the same reasoning, be the beginning of folly. The total disregard for truth, justice, equity and fair play which has become so pervasive in our society is by no means exclusive to the political scene. Unfortunately, with a new and delicate democracy to nurture, the environment in the nation right now makes it appear more prevalent in this department.
Political prostitution is not a new phenomenon in Nigeria, and we saw enough during the tenures of the last two dictators. There were enough people who literally put their mouths where their pockets were, so much so that we even had a million man march to prove popular support for the election of the late General Abacha as a civilian President. Against great hope and the goodwill of loyal Nigerians, things have not changed. If anything, the trend has become even more dangerous. It has become infinitely easier to exploit and hide behind the freedoms and liberties available with civilian governance with the often realized aim of propagating deceit for material gain.
If this is so because of the extreme poverty in the land, it is no wonder and certainly no coincidence that the same leaders who have understandable need for political prostitutes are the same ones who tend to keep their led in perpetual poverty, often while wasting money on exorbitant PR measures to somehow convince them that though their standards of living are dropping, they are, by some magic, somehow better off. It is no wonder either that Abiola did not have enough sycophants to ensure he got Nigeria's ultimate power.
The devil, the military political class and the deep blue sea
Right now, one thing is clear within our highly charged and still charging political atmosphere, the result of preparations for 2002 (August) and 2003. We are in dire want of credible presidential candidates. Most states have the same problem on the gubernatorial level, but Nigeria is so structured as to place far more importance on the presidency as the actually unfortunate man on whose shoulder the huge load of power at the center rests. We are in dire shortage of credible candidates, and the fear that 2003 might end up as business as usual is becoming more real and more justifiable by the second. Of course this could have been helped and can still be, if we wake up from this national slumber and realize that we can do something about the continued exclusivity and the unlevel playing field created by INEC of the Nigerian political scene specifically for 2003. There has been talk of massive boycott of registration, but that, judging by the general attitude, seems hardly likely.
The question of who a credible candidate is however getting more difficult to define. Is he the one who has an impeccable manifesto? Events in the more recent past have flawed that as a means by which a 'credible candidate' might be identified. There is now emerging a new political class in Nigeria_ the one of the politicians who have had enough exposure to be able to figure out the problems of Nigeria, and have enough education and intelligence to know exactly what to do to fix it, but alas, they do not have the integrity, and that is hardly their aim for seeking power. This leaves us with the present leadership, the determination of the Igbos to get the presidency this time around and those who have served under military administrations as the groups which we have to chose from.
Many fear for an igbo presidency, which might be unrealizable within the context of the money politics which we have accepted, as the Igbos are not very famous for diverting funds to Switzerland.The fear in ethnic quarter's that if the clamour for an Igbo presidency arises purely from a perception by that group that it has been marginalized, it is likely that the other ethnic groups might suffer the same fate as they had. This might not be a clever way of thinking, but politics is not about brains, it is about public perceptions.
But it is those who have served under the military who will have the hardest time convincing their followers, and this is in spite of the fact that notable Nigerians of integrity have served Nigeria under the worst military dictatorships (these include Tai Solarin, Wole Soyinka, Lateef Jakande, and others). Putting in mind however that it is between the devil and the deep blue sea, how can we run away from those who have served under dictatorships and embrace those who had been the dictatorships themselves. The whole thing makes little sense.

Headline: Nigeria: Scarcity of Yams Hit Niger
Body_Text:
Despite its position as one of the major yam producing states in the country, acute scarcity of the commodity has forced many families in Niger State to suspend it from their menu, the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) has reported.
Yam, a staple food in many homes, and grown in large quantity in Paiko, Kuta, Gwada, Beiji and Garattu areas of the state, has now become scarce and expensive.
Minna, the state capital is the worse hit by the scarcity, as prices of the commodity had sky-rocketed beyond the reach of an average income earner, while people at the suburbs and rural communities have resorted to premature harvesting of the commodity for consumption.
Hajiya Amina Kuta, a yam seller, told NAN that it was only when yams are in season that they are cheap and available, but that whenever they are not enough, they become expensive and scarce.
At the popular Gwari market in the heart of Minna, prices of the commodity had gone up by more than 150 per cent against its cost at the beginning of the year.
The price for a park of 10 big tubers which sold for N650 in January, now go for N1,400, while the average sized yams which were sold for N450, are now sold for N1,000.
Between December and January when farmers harvest their produce, five tubers of yam were sold at about N150, but the same now goes for N350.
A yam seller in the Gwari market said, "although this is the period when yam is generally scarce and expensive, it has never been this costly, and we are not happy too because the purchase has dropped drastically.
"We hope that by August-September, things will improve as farmers would have started harvesting," he added.
Meanwhile, the usual hustle and bustle in the Gwari yam market which during the harvest period used to attract buyers across the country, had faded, leaving only scores of retailers to market the little they could buy from villagers.
Prices of other food items like beans, maize, cassava, gari and corn that used to be cheap at harvest period have increased between 100 per cent and 150 per cent.

Headline: Nigeria: New Yam Offers Respite
Body_Text:
Lagos — A significant observation at markets visited last weekend showed the
entrance of the eagerly awaited new yam.
Though the new yam is not yet in abundance, but with the old ones still
in the market, the prices were quite affordable for lovers of yam. This
brings temporary relief to consumers who had, before now, cancelled pounded
yam from their family menu list due to the high cost of the old yam.
According to the trader, a sizeable tuber of the new yam now sells for
N100, while a standard tuber sells for N150.
Yam is harvested between seven to nine months after planting. It comes
in species, but the common and important yam varieties cultivated in Nigeria
are white yam, water yam, yellow yam, trifoliate yam and aerial yam.
One of the most widely distributed of the world's food crops, yam
constitutes a major and relatively cheap source of carbohydrates. It serves
as a source of energy to man.
Yam can be utilized in various ways. It can be roasted, boiled or eaten
on its own or in combination with other foods like beans and plantain among
others.
Yam is usually boiled and pounded to achieve pounded yam, a popular
delicacy among Nigerians.
This food crop may be used as flour. Yam tubers are sliced, parboiled
and sun-dried. The dry chips are ground or milled into flour.
The yam flour is used to prepare 'amala' which is particularly popular
amongst the Yorubas .

Headline: Nigeria: Battle for Abiola's Inheritance Continues
Body_Text:
The drama is still playing itself out in London .  But what have the
Emir of Zaria , Shehu Idris and business whiz-kid - Onwuka Kalu got to do
with the tussle?  A lot
This is not pretty.  It seems the family of the late business mogul,
M.K.O. Abiola, whose political triumph was cut short by the Babangida
administration in 1993, is still at odds on how to distribute his wealth
among his survivors.  Four years after his death in incarceration, the
acrimonious battle for the control of the purse strings inherited from the
super-rich man, reputed to be worth tens of billions of Naira continues and
it is drawing more and more people into the line of fire as they try to help
the family sort out the mess.
The main bone of contention is number of Wills that Abiola made before
his death.  How many are they?  The answer though complicated may help break
the logjam the family has found itself.  The truth, however, is, no one
really knows how many there are, although there is little doubt that there
may be at least two signed Wills - the 1989 and 1994 Wills.  Last year at a
trial contesting the 1989 Will in London , it was admitted in court that
there may be an unsigned 1993 Will.  Zeinab Duke, one of Abiola's wives had
sought to declare the 1989 Will a forgery when the revelation was made.  The
1989 Will gives substantial advantage to Kola, Samsudeen, Olalekan and
Monsuru, all Abiola's sons from his first wife, Simbiat, as they were
exempted from the blood test all other Abiola's sons and daughters were to
take to prove their paternity.  The four were also the proposed
administrators of the Will.
It was Duke-Abiola's contention in her court papers that the Will had no
original copy and that the bank - NatWest Bank, Pentonville Road , London ,
which was said to be holding the Will in its vault had disclaimed any
knowledge of it.  As she told Newswatch then, "they formulated the gossips
and produced it in paper form and called it a Will.  It has now backfired
because they were supposed to carry it out in England ."  Using her own
writing experts to declare the Will a forgery, she asked the court to throw
it out as a fake.  She lost the case early last year as the court held the
Will authentic.  But it did prescribe that all Abiola's children, without
exemption, take a paternity test.  Thereafter, on August 1, 2001 , a
district judge in the Family Division of the High Court of Justice, in
London , granted an order appointing Adam Broke, Timothy Daniel and Kolawole
Olatokunbo Abiola as "joint administrators with the Will dated 25th October
1989 ."
Just when you thought that the family would finally start putting the
Will palaver to a decent rest, a new revelation threw a spanner in the works
once again.  Apparently, Abiola had indeed put together a "last Will" on
June 7, and 9, 1994, shortly before his arrest and incarceration by Nigeria
's last unrelenting dictator, the late General Sani Abacha.  The new Will
revokes all "testamentary dispositions made by me with the exception of the
Wills I have for any realty or immovable assets I may have in England ,
Wales and Nigeria ."  But it covers all Abiola's liquid and other assets as
well as property in the form of shares, stocks, bonds, bank deposits, safe
deposit boxes, share dividends or in any other form over which I may have
general power of appointment which may at the time of my death be situated
worldwide including Holland , Switzerland , USA , Egypt , Togo , Spain ,
Senegal , South Africa , Guernsey , Jersey - Channel Islands , Canada ,
France , Uganda , Germany and Australia. "
According to papers in Newswatch possession, the joint administrators of
the 1994 Will are His Royal Highness, Shehu Idris, the Emir of Zaria , a
well-known friend of Abiola, and Onwuka Kalu, once regarded as the whiz-kid
of business and owner of the Onwuka Hitek Industries.  In a letter on June
9, 1994 , Abiola told Kalu: "As we have agreed, it is important that I tidy
up my affairs and prepare for the worst especially in view of the continuous
treachery of some of our friends.  Accordingly, I have drawn a Will for some
of my assets in which I have appointed His Royal Highness, the Emir of Zaria
- Alhaji (Dr) Shehu Idris and you as executors.
But the emir told Newswatch in Zaria that he had no knowledge of
M.K.O. Abiola's Will.  He said at no time during and even after Abiola's
lifetime was the issue of the will discussed with him.
"I am surprised that people come to ask me if Abiola gave me the right
to administer his Will.  But each time they come I tell them that I don't
know anything about it.  I wonder where they are getting that information
from.  Abiola never discussed that with me during his lifetime, and the
issue of administering his Will has not come up at least with me even after
his death," Idris said.
He told Newswatch that it is not possible to link him with the issue of
administering Abiola's Will.  This he said is because the concept of a Will
is against the tenets of Islam.  "Even if Abiola had discussed the issue
with me during his life time, I would have declined to administer the Will
because it is against the principles of Islam.  And as a religious leader, I
couldn't have accepted to do that," he told Newswatch.
Idris, however, said that Chief M. K. O. Abiola was a very close friend
of his during the former's life time, and appealed to the feuding members of
the family to eschew bitterness and amicably resolve their differences.  He
said the only way this can be achieved in the family, is through roundtable
dialogue with all members of the family.
He said the earlier this was resolved, the better.  It is only when
there is peace and harmony in the family that the principles, which the late
Abiola stood and fought for, will be better appreciated by Nigerians.
However, Kalu confirmed his own appointment and told Newswatch that the
emir was indeed appointed by Abiola but that because of his tight schedule,
resigned his role and asked Onwuka to take charge of the Will.  Onwuka said
the emir is kept fully briefed about developments concerning the Will.
Nevertheless, Onwuka is finding out that as with the 1989 Will, this new
one too does not sit down well with all of Abiola's family.  Although he
obtained a grant of probate in Amsterdam , the Netherlands , based on the
Will, he has run into problems executing it.  Kola Abiola and the other
joint administrators of the 1989 Will are opposed to the new one, which
gives Kola a lesser role but makes Zeinab Duke, the wife who challenged the
authenticity of the 1989 Will in court, the residual beneficiary.
A letter written to Kalu' s lawyers, Manches Solicitors, on January 4,
this year, by D.J. Freeman Solicitors who represent the court appointed
administrators of the 1989 Will clearly shows what their clients, Kola,
Broke and Daniel, think about the new Will.  According to the letter, a copy
of which is in Newswatch possession, "there is some concern with the family
over the 1994 Will and its authenticity.  I appreciate that your client has
his own views on this.  However, as you will be aware, I represent the court
appointed administrators under an earlier Will and therefore my clients wish
to collect it and administer the assets and therefore have to consider the
position of the 1994 Will very seriously.  For a number of reasons, they are
not prepared to simply step into your client's shoes as administrators under
the 1994 Will unless and until there is evidence that the Will is authentic.
We believe that the matter of the Will's authenticity needs to be dealt with
urgently and the simplest way of doing this is for the two Wills to be
compared so that concerns as to the authenticity of the 1994 Will can be
satisfied."
To this end, D.J. Freeman requested that the 1994 papers be transferred
from the Oxford court where they are lodged to the Principal Registry in
London where the 1989 Will is lodged.  Kalu didn't oppose the move although
believes that rather than resolve issues trying to engage handwriting
experts to test the signatures on the two Wills would further drag the
family's reputation in the mud and that collectively they will continue to
lose a substantial part of their inheritance to claims from the lawyers,
who, as Kalu describes it to Newswatch, "have the yam and the knife to cut
it with."
As Zeinab Duke's challenge to the 1989 Will proved, the conclusion of
handwriting experts may not necessarily be accepted by the court.  Although
the experts she employed cast some doubts on the 1989 Will, the judge still
held it authentic.  As Kalu noted in his letter to his solicitors January
31, 2002 , " I do not really have faith in the handwriting expert they are
going to engage because his work or comparison of the handwriting is going
to be one-sided since the materials for such work is going to be provided
solely by the contestants."
In a letter written by Kalu's solicitors to the administrators on March
26, 2002 , they were asked to provide Kalu with "details of any report you
have received from the expert analysing the handwriting contained in the
relevant documents in this matter.  If the expert's report had been
completed to date, please confirm when I can expect to receive the report."
As at the time of writing this report, Kalu had not received any words back
from the lawyers.
What he would really want is for the family to make some compromises and
resolve the issue to the satisfaction of all members of the Abiola family.
"Compromises must be made for the peace of the family," he told Newswatch.
"They must worry about the cost to lawyers who charge based on hours and
minutes.  The quickest way is for everyone to be ready to negotiate," he
added.
His worries may be justified.  Documents in Newswatch possession show
that on February 25, 2002 , the lawyers sent their bill number 23896 for
£29,439.30 to the administrators of Abiola estate for payment.  Added to
this was also bill 25329 for £22,590.60 for a grant total of £59,029.96.
Newswatch was unable to confirm whether or not the bill was paid, but
suffice it to say that if it had not been paid within 28 days of receipt, it
would have been attracting an "interest rate of two percent per annum over
the lending base rate of the Royale Bank of Scotland ."
William Brouthton, a representative of D.J. Freeman who spoke to
Newswatch on the controversial 1994 Will was unwilling to comment about
charges made by the law firm. "I can't comment on the billing," he
said.
However, Kalu is unlikely to get his wishes that the matter be resolved
by compromise, at least not immediately. Broughthton told Newswatch that the
administrators of the 1989 Will are challenging the authenticity of the new
Will in court.  "We are aware there is another Will," he said, adding "we
are currently dealing with issues regarding it.  In fact, we are challenging
it in court.
It is a scenario that Kalu thinks can drag on for many years if care is
not taken.  He admits that it may be unrealistic to expect those who have
much to gain by the 1989 Will to just give in and accept the new one, "it is
futile to go on like this.  Anyone can find reasons to challenge the Will.
And if you go on like this, the matter may drag on for God forbid, another
10 years.  Everyone must look at the experience of the Okoti-Eboh family and
how long it took to resolve problems associated with the man's estate.  What
is happening is not good for the memory of a great man like Abiola and the
longer the fight goes on, the more the assets are depleted."
What is clear is that the controversy and the court challenges to the
1989 Will have engendered bad blood among key members of the family to the
point where each side may probably not be ready for any compromise at the
moment.  It is hard to see how Kola will accept that Zeinab plays a key role
in the execution of his father's Will.  In fact, during the hearing of the
challenge to the older Will at a London court last year, Gloria, another of
Abiola's many wives was keen to prove to the world that Zeinab was not
married to Abiola, no matter the evidence suggesting to the contrary.  It
was Gloria's impecuniousness, according to a Newswatch source, that led to
the appointment of administrators for the 1989 Will by the court.  The
administrators can disburse money from the estate to aid family members who
are in financial difficulty, although an agreement was reached with Kalu
ensuring that he is kept abreast of such development.
It is also important to note that a wary eye is also cast on Kalu's
role, especially since a member of the family told the man himself that
Abiola is a tribalist who would not make an Igbo man an executor of his Will
or even leave substantial inheritance to Zeinab by making her a residual
beneficiary.  Kalu said he has nothing to gain but has been trying to keep a
promise he made "to a friend."
Nevertheless, the fear here is that no one really knows the value of
Abiola's wealth or what the residual inheritance would amount to for Zeinab.
Even Kalu has been pleading with all and sundry to forward to him a list of
Abiola's holdings they may be aware of with lawyers to Kola and the other
administrators of the 1989 Will to "let me have: (a) full details of all
assets of Chief Abiola which you have located up to date, (b) full details
of those assets which you have distributed, and (c) full details of to whom
those assets have been distributed."
If the 1994 Will survives all challenges, especially the court
challenge, not a few people outside the Abiola family will thump their chest
that it reflects the generosity for which the man was so famously known.
Apart from the generous sums of money bequeathed his children, wives and
extended family, he left substantial money for many causes, charities and
organisations that he supported in his lifetime.
Among the African and other foreign organisations who benefit from this
1994 Will are: the International Red Cross, International Peace Academy,
Martin Luther King Centre in Atlanta, Georgia, USA, Amnesty International,
United Nation's Children's Fund, Greenpeace International, Black
Parliamentary (sic) Caucus, Jimmy Carter Foundation, the Abiola Foundation
for Reparations, and the African National Congress (ANC).  The Imperial
Cancer Research, London and even the Confederation of African Football are
also beneficiaries.
Locally, the Gani Fawehinmi Scholarship Fund benefits from the Will.  So
do Nigeria Labour Congress, Nigerian Union of Journalists, National Union of
Petroleum Engineer (sic), NUPENG, National Association of Nigerian Students
(NANS), Campaign for Democracy, Civil Liberties Organisation, National Union
of Road Transport Workers, Petroleum and Gas Senior Staff Employees of
Nigeria (sic) PENGASSEN (sic), Nadeco, Baptist Boys High School, Abeokuta,
Nigerian Football Association, Zulikha Abiola Memorial Qur'anic Centre,
Nigerian Supreme Council for Islamic Affairs, Christian Association of
Nigeria and the Nigerian Union of Teachers.  Ajoke Murtala Mohammed, wife of
the assassinated Nigerian leader, was left $50,000 in the 1994 Will.
For these so-called beneficiaries, this is a pie in the sky for them as
the authenticity of the Will remains a major headache and it may take
sometime down the road for the controversy to be resolved.  This is why Kalu
told Newswatch that he is trying to encourage the emir of Zaria to call a
meeting of all the stakeholders in this battle for Abiola's wealth to
resolve the issue.  He said he was disappointed that Yoruba elders have not
thought it fit to call members of the family to order to protect Abiola's
good name.  He is equally unhappy that President Olusegun Obasanjo, a man he
claims benefited from Abiola's misfortunes, has not intervened to help the
family sort out its problems.  There had been rumour that the president had
tried to help the family but Newswatch was unable to confirm this.
The safe prediction is that unless there is some "divine" intervention,
the battle over the Will of the late business mogul may rumble on for
sometime to come.  It is indeed a sad prospect.

Headline: Nigeria: The Imminent Voters' Registration Scam
Body_Text:
General Colin Powell, the American Secretary of State whom Newsweek called ". . . the most respected figure in American public life" once said that the majority of Nigerians were scammers. I think that was in 1994 or thereabout after he had retired as the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff of the US armed forces. In the context in which he made the statement, it would appear then that he was referring to Nigerian leaders. Not a few Nigerians were miffed by the description. I remember several Nigerians reacting very angrily to the statement at the time. Those who took umbrage at the characterisation thought that the general was unfair to Nigerians especially as he was admired by a broad cross section of Nigerians. Powell, the blunt-spoken four-star general must have spoken experientially. He must have had a few raw deals with Nigerian leaders. The protest from Nigerians, especially those living overseas, was so vehement that the general was forced to recant and apologise. But he had made his point.
Powell's watershed statement was made about eight years ago, but he could as well have been speaking today about the present regime in Nigeria. Since the last three years of the Obasanjo administration, official scamming has become a part of the nation's democratic folklore. The Electoral Act-Gate, perhaps the mother of all scams, is still every fresh in our minds. It was in that scheme that we saw how those we have elected to be leaders over us from the very top descended to the level of common criminals to forge a law that could have shut the political space. One of them even suggested that they should lie to the Nigerian public and call it printer's devil. In normal climes, all those involved in that disgraceful act would be spending time in the VIP rooms of maximum security prisons and not in the VIP lounges of State Houses as is currently the case. The incidence dealt a serious blow to the heart of our democracy and damaged irretrievably whatever "trust" still remained in the relationship between the people and the elected government. Nigerians could not believe that their leaders, most of whom claim piety and affinity with God, would be caught "pants down" in that act of political adultery. And in politics, especially in a democracy, "trust" is the most important ingredient. Our leaders have lost their people's trust and they know it.
It should therefore not be a surprise that suspicion has trailed every step or anticipated move of President Olusegun Obasanjo. It is in that light that the current hues of blue murder about the impending update of the voters' register, which should normally be a matter of course in a democracy, should be seen. Nigerians, especially Northerners, are screaming foul play and have warned of dire consequences should INEC confirm their fears. And what are those fears? The Arewa Consultative Forum (ACF), the umbrella socio-political organisation of the diverse and disparate peoples of the 19 Northern states and Abuja which make up over 50 percent of the nation's population has raised an alarm that there was evidence aplenty that INEC, which had lately been operating in the image of Obasanjo, has concluded plans to rig the voters' register to give the president a head start in 2003. Ordinarily, such warning would be ignored since it has the appearance of a prototypal product from the Nigerian rumour mill of the usually "reliable source". But because it was coming from the ACF, which has never been known to be flippant, and with the Electoral-Act-Gate still raw in our minds, the whole nation has been on tenterhooks since the break of the "rumour". To worsen matters, the APP (this seems to be the recognised name as INEC has denied knowledge of any party called ANPP), which is the second largest party in the country has associated with the ACF's position with the national secretary, Chief George Moghalu lending his voice to the matter. The newly registered NDP even went a step further. It has dragged INEC before the Federal High Court in Abuja.
The problem with INEC is that even though it makes claims to independence from executive interference, everyone knows that by the very nature of its structure of operations, its umbilical cord is still desperately tied to the apron strings of Aso Rock. And though the letter and spirit in which the body was created meant it to be independent, everyone knows that the "voice is Jacobs' but the hand is very clearly Esau's" (See April 28, 2002 Monday Column, "Should we trust Guobadia?")
Even before the cry by ACF, it had been clear to even the most casual political observer that one of the items on Obasanjo's private agenda was to demographically restructure Nigeria. And the tools for the actualisation of this grand design are the national ID card scheme and the census. But what people could not be certain about was whether it was Obasanjo's own script or a part of a larger irredentist agenda. Nigerians remember very clearly the keep-hate-alive speech of Chief Abraham Adesanya the Yoruba native-in-chief not long ago during an Afenifere convention in which he told the whole world that the North's electoral superiority was because during censuses, the population of goats and cows are added to the actual figures, and that the Yoruba race was poised to reverse that trend. It would seem that either Obasanjo has been co-opted into the grand project as a Yoruba man himself or he just simply picked up the gauntlet. And as if to confirm that the scheme is part of a larger stratagem, the Yoruba Council of Elders (YCE) has turned itself into the public relations unit of INEC. Its assistant secretary-general, Dr. Kunle Olajide told journalists in Ibadan last Friday that the ACF's alarm was hasty, unwarranted and mischievous.
But whichever is the case; people can simply not comprehend the notion of having the national ID card form the basis for the voters' registration. I, for one, fail to see the nexus between the two. While one is a continuous requirement precedent to elections, the other is simply a means of registering and identifying Nigerians and which for all intents and purposes should be a continuous process with no time frame or limitation. In the voters' registration process, which INEC proposes, all prospective voters would be impelled to complete an intricate and cumbersome personal data form. In the form, a sample of which was recently shown to me, every voter, including of course the unlettered that make up over 70 percent of the nation's population, a majority of which reside in the North's rural areas, would be expected to provide personal information details which would include the voter's age, private and official addresses, occupation, place of birth, state of origin, voter's thumbprint as well as passport size photographs. All these would be expected to be conducted within five working days with each day extending to no more than eight hours.
With all these, it is clear that someone is definitely up to some prank if not an outright crime. I saw the form myself and I find it rather burdensome even for an educated person like me. Why should INEC make the national identity card a basis and prerequisite for voters' registration? What is the motive behind this idea by INEC? Why should INEC abandon the prescribed form in the Electoral Act for a new one, which would gather information for both the voters' registration and ID card? The law does not prescribe this form. INEC has a constitutional responsibility clearly spelt out and it has no business with the national identity card scheme which is within the ambit of another completely unrelated organisation. Why should INEC which was set up as an electoral regulating body suddenly get interested in the national ID card? Indeed, why is the president so overtly interested in the national ID card scheme? Late last year when the budget was being prepared, the president voted N10 billion for the national ID card project and a paltry N3 billion for the agricultural sector which employs the teeming majority of Nigerians. I recollect that the National Assembly converted the budgetary allocation for the ID card scheme into more funds for agriculture and yet the president would not shift position. Clearly, there is something in this ID card thing that the president knows and the rest of us don't.
I am not particularly against the idea of a national ID card for the purpose for which it is meant. My only beef is that the government wants to use it as a political tool against those the president perceives to be his enemies and those who he must put in "their correct places once and for all". Somehow, Obasanjo seems to think that all of us must pay for his incarceration by Abacha. All of us are guilty because we didn't carry placards to protest his imprisonment. So it is now his turn to exact his pound of flesh from us. And in this revenge mission, he does not give a hoot if he pulls the entire country down with him.
This is one democratic regime that has no respect for the rule of law. Of what use is a democracy to a nation if the laws of the land will not form the basis for the actions of its leaders? The law requires that there should be at least 30 days' notice to political parties before the commencement of voters' registration. The three new parties were registered only three weeks ago and they have not been given any notice by INEC regarding the voters' registration. The law also stipulates that the register must close 60 days before elections. It is obvious that if this section of the law were followed, the August 10 local government elections are already a nullity. If the government itself breaks the laws of the land as it currently does, how does it expect its citizens to keep them? It is impossible to be law-abiding in a lawless society.
If the people see something suggestive of duplicity in the undue priority the presidency is placing on the national ID card scheme, it is not without reasons. First, apart from the needless rush and hush-hush nature with which the government is carrying on regarding the issue, there are clear indications that the exercise has already begun in a section of the country in order to place it at a clear advantage over the others. And even in areas where it has not already started, there are plans to fill the key strategic positions with senior staff of a particular ethnic group probably in furtherance of the president's agenda. The timing is also decidedly inconvenient for the majority of the people in the North and riverine areas as enunciated by the ACF. There is no how the rural farmer would trade off his farming activity at this time for the distraction, nuisance even, of an ID card registration exercise. The rains at this time will also affect the registration activities in the riverine areas. If the president is sincere about the project, then he should limit it to the original idea. As an advice, he should commence with the easier aspects by starting with recognised Nigerian institutions such as the armed forces, the police, customs, civil servants and similar groups which are more compact and manageable. This will make the entire exercise easier than starting with the sugarcane sellers in some remote village or Tiv yam sellers in a rural hamlet of Benue State who still view anything Obasanjo with suspicion if not contempt. But my conjecture is that Obasanjo is just testing the people's resolve. If he succeeds with this one, then a lot more will follow. The next target could be to rig the national population census results away from what it has been since the colonial days. This may just be an augury of many more things to come. The president's ultimate aim as I have said earlier would be to shift the paradigm permanently as far as elections are concerned in this country. The president has obviously taken Abraham Adesanya's "goats and cows theory" too seriously. If the theory is to be believed, then it must be the same goats and cows that made Obasanjo's 1999 victory at the polls possible because the decisive votes that gave him the presidency came from the North.
One more interesting thing, I remember very clearly that the foreign company that was awarded the contract for the national ID card project is a South African company that has links to the son of Mr. Pieter Botha, the former South African president, a godfather of apartheid South Africa who refused to appear before Archbishop Desmond Tutu's Truth and Reconciliation Commission recently. If that does not seem to be a contradiction to the government, it is probably because this son of the patron of apartheid shares good company (for profit) with the son of Nigeria's leader who in the past, as a military leader, fought apartheid to a standstill. Blood may be thicker than water, but money as we can see in this case has proven to be far thicker than blood.
That is just by the way. One thing is clear though, if Obasanjo insists, as is his wont, he will not get away with this one.

Original_Search_Title: Development Program Woos Federal Capital Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Development Program Woos Federal Capital Farmers
Body_Text:
Farmers in the Federal Capital Territory have been advised to  utilise the services of the Agricultural Development Programme (ADP) in the  effort to increase food production within the Territory.
Speaking with Abuja Trust in Kwali at the weekend, the  Director, Agricultural Development Programme (ADP) in the FCT, Dr. Sunday  Obamiro, said farmers should begin to embrace the ADP's complementary role to  government's provision of facilities.
"If you don't afford yourself the opportunity, if we want to  see you and you run away, you miss our teaching, so they should all now begin to  re-orientate their thinking that whatever government puts down, is like a  facilitator, and they should utilize it to the best possible means, " he  urged."
Dr Obamiro said farmers should use government facilities to  increase production, adding that no matter how cheaply government provides  facilities, "If you don't go and make some efforts to attract that facility, you  will be knocked out."
He said the FCT had been divided into 66 cells (units), each of  which is manned by trained agriculturists who are assigned to no fewer than 1000  farmers to impart innovations in agriculture.
Dr Obaniro said the ADP in the FCT, just like elsewhere in the  country, was very much alive to its responsibility of searching for and  transferring latest discoveries in Agriculture from other parts of the country  to the FCT in accordance with adaptive researches.
He said contrary to beliefs in some quarters within the FCT,  there were so many farmers living in the rural areas of the FCT who he said, had  been fully integrated in the technical assistance scheme of the ADP.
"If you have not gone to the jungle, you will wonder where these farmers are,  but when you are passing by the road, you see people buying yams and carrying  them away, these people in the jungle that you don't see like other people, are  the people actually doing the magic," he said.

Headline: Nigeria: The Imminent Voters' Registration Scam
Body_Text:
General Colin Powell, the American Secretary of State whom Newsweek called ". . . the most respected figure in American public life" once said that the majority of Nigerians were scammers. I think that was in 1994 or thereabout after he had retired as the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff of the US armed forces. In the context in which he made the statement, it would appear then that he was referring to Nigerian leaders. Not a few Nigerians were miffed by the description. I remember several Nigerians reacting very angrily to the statement at the time. Those who took umbrage at the characterisation thought that the general was unfair to Nigerians especially as he was admired by a broad cross section of Nigerians. Powell, the blunt-spoken four-star general must have spoken experientially. He must have had a few raw deals with Nigerian leaders. The protest from Nigerians, especially those living overseas, was so vehement that the general was forced to recant and apologise. But he had made his point.
Powell's watershed statement was made about eight years ago, but he could as well have been speaking today about the present regime in Nigeria. Since the last three years of the Obasanjo administration, official scamming has become a part of the nation's democratic folklore. The Electoral Act-Gate, perhaps the mother of all scams, is still every fresh in our minds. It was in that scheme that we saw how those we have elected to be leaders over us from the very top descended to the level of common criminals to forge a law that could have shut the political space. One of them even suggested that they should lie to the Nigerian public and call it printer's devil. In normal climes, all those involved in that disgraceful act would be spending time in the VIP rooms of maximum security prisons and not in the VIP lounges of State Houses as is currently the case. The incidence dealt a serious blow to the heart of our democracy and damaged irretrievably whatever "trust" still remained in the relationship between the people and the elected government. Nigerians could not believe that their leaders, most of whom claim piety and affinity with God, would be caught "pants down" in that act of political adultery. And in politics, especially in a democracy, "trust" is the most important ingredient. Our leaders have lost their people's trust and they know it.
It should therefore not be a surprise that suspicion has trailed every step or anticipated move of President Olusegun Obasanjo. It is in that light that the current hues of blue murder about the impending update of the voters' register, which should normally be a matter of course in a democracy, should be seen. Nigerians, especially Northerners, are screaming foul play and have warned of dire consequences should INEC confirm their fears. And what are those fears? The Arewa Consultative Forum (ACF), the umbrella socio-political organisation of the diverse and disparate peoples of the 19 Northern states and Abuja which make up over 50 percent of the nation's population has raised an alarm that there was evidence aplenty that INEC, which had lately been operating in the image of Obasanjo, has concluded plans to rig the voters' register to give the president a head start in 2003. Ordinarily, such warning would be ignored since it has the appearance of a prototypal product from the Nigerian rumour mill of the usually "reliable source". But because it was coming from the ACF, which has never been known to be flippant, and with the Electoral-Act-Gate still raw in our minds, the whole nation has been on tenterhooks since the break of the "rumour". To worsen matters, the APP (this seems to be the recognised name as INEC has denied knowledge of any party called ANPP), which is the second largest party in the country has associated with the ACF's position with the national secretary, Chief George Moghalu lending his voice to the matter. The newly registered NDP even went a step further. It has dragged INEC before the Federal High Court in Abuja.
The problem with INEC is that even though it makes claims to independence from executive interference, everyone knows that by the very nature of its structure of operations, its umbilical cord is still desperately tied to the apron strings of Aso Rock. And though the letter and spirit in which the body was created meant it to be independent, everyone knows that the "voice is Jacobs' but the hand is very clearly Esau's" (See April 28, 2002 Monday Column, "Should we trust Guobadia?")
Even before the cry by ACF, it had been clear to even the most casual political observer that one of the items on Obasanjo's private agenda was to demographically restructure Nigeria. And the tools for the actualisation of this grand design are the national ID card scheme and the census. But what people could not be certain about was whether it was Obasanjo's own script or a part of a larger irredentist agenda. Nigerians remember very clearly the keep-hate-alive speech of Chief Abraham Adesanya the Yoruba native-in-chief not long ago during an Afenifere convention in which he told the whole world that the North's electoral superiority was because during censuses, the population of goats and cows are added to the actual figures, and that the Yoruba race was poised to reverse that trend. It would seem that either Obasanjo has been co-opted into the grand project as a Yoruba man himself or he just simply picked up the gauntlet. And as if to confirm that the scheme is part of a larger stratagem, the Yoruba Council of Elders (YCE) has turned itself into the public relations unit of INEC. Its assistant secretary-general, Dr. Kunle Olajide told journalists in Ibadan last Friday that the ACF's alarm was hasty, unwarranted and mischievous.
But whichever is the case; people can simply not comprehend the notion of having the national ID card form the basis for the voters' registration. I, for one, fail to see the nexus between the two. While one is a continuous requirement precedent to elections, the other is simply a means of registering and identifying Nigerians and which for all intents and purposes should be a continuous process with no time frame or limitation. In the voters' registration process, which INEC proposes, all prospective voters would be impelled to complete an intricate and cumbersome personal data form. In the form, a sample of which was recently shown to me, every voter, including of course the unlettered that make up over 70 percent of the nation's population, a majority of which reside in the North's rural areas, would be expected to provide personal information details which would include the voter's age, private and official addresses, occupation, place of birth, state of origin, voter's thumbprint as well as passport size photographs. All these would be expected to be conducted within five working days with each day extending to no more than eight hours.
With all these, it is clear that someone is definitely up to some prank if not an outright crime. I saw the form myself and I find it rather burdensome even for an educated person like me. Why should INEC make the national identity card a basis and prerequisite for voters' registration? What is the motive behind this idea by INEC? Why should INEC abandon the prescribed form in the Electoral Act for a new one, which would gather information for both the voters' registration and ID card? The law does not prescribe this form. INEC has a constitutional responsibility clearly spelt out and it has no business with the national identity card scheme which is within the ambit of another completely unrelated organisation. Why should INEC which was set up as an electoral regulating body suddenly get interested in the national ID card? Indeed, why is the president so overtly interested in the national ID card scheme? Late last year when the budget was being prepared, the president voted N10 billion for the national ID card project and a paltry N3 billion for the agricultural sector which employs the teeming majority of Nigerians. I recollect that the National Assembly converted the budgetary allocation for the ID card scheme into more funds for agriculture and yet the president would not shift position. Clearly, there is something in this ID card thing that the president knows and the rest of us don't.
I am not particularly against the idea of a national ID card for the purpose for which it is meant. My only beef is that the government wants to use it as a political tool against those the president perceives to be his enemies and those who he must put in "their correct places once and for all". Somehow, Obasanjo seems to think that all of us must pay for his incarceration by Abacha. All of us are guilty because we didn't carry placards to protest his imprisonment. So it is now his turn to exact his pound of flesh from us. And in this revenge mission, he does not give a hoot if he pulls the entire country down with him.
This is one democratic regime that has no respect for the rule of law. Of what use is a democracy to a nation if the laws of the land will not form the basis for the actions of its leaders? The law requires that there should be at least 30 days' notice to political parties before the commencement of voters' registration. The three new parties were registered only three weeks ago and they have not been given any notice by INEC regarding the voters' registration. The law also stipulates that the register must close 60 days before elections. It is obvious that if this section of the law were followed, the August 10 local government elections are already a nullity. If the government itself breaks the laws of the land as it currently does, how does it expect its citizens to keep them? It is impossible to be law-abiding in a lawless society.
If the people see something suggestive of duplicity in the undue priority the presidency is placing on the national ID card scheme, it is not without reasons. First, apart from the needless rush and hush-hush nature with which the government is carrying on regarding the issue, there are clear indications that the exercise has already begun in a section of the country in order to place it at a clear advantage over the others. And even in areas where it has not already started, there are plans to fill the key strategic positions with senior staff of a particular ethnic group probably in furtherance of the president's agenda. The timing is also decidedly inconvenient for the majority of the people in the North and riverine areas as enunciated by the ACF. There is no how the rural farmer would trade off his farming activity at this time for the distraction, nuisance even, of an ID card registration exercise. The rains at this time will also affect the registration activities in the riverine areas. If the president is sincere about the project, then he should limit it to the original idea. As an advice, he should commence with the easier aspects by starting with recognised Nigerian institutions such as the armed forces, the police, customs, civil servants and similar groups which are more compact and manageable. This will make the entire exercise easier than starting with the sugarcane sellers in some remote village or Tiv yam sellers in a rural hamlet of Benue State who still view anything Obasanjo with suspicion if not contempt. But my conjecture is that Obasanjo is just testing the people's resolve. If he succeeds with this one, then a lot more will follow. The next target could be to rig the national population census results away from what it has been since the colonial days. This may just be an augury of many more things to come. The president's ultimate aim as I have said earlier would be to shift the paradigm permanently as far as elections are concerned in this country. The president has obviously taken Abraham Adesanya's "goats and cows theory" too seriously. If the theory is to be believed, then it must be the same goats and cows that made Obasanjo's 1999 victory at the polls possible because the decisive votes that gave him the presidency came from the North.
One more interesting thing, I remember very clearly that the foreign company that was awarded the contract for the national ID card project is a South African company that has links to the son of Mr. Pieter Botha, the former South African president, a godfather of apartheid South Africa who refused to appear before Archbishop Desmond Tutu's Truth and Reconciliation Commission recently. If that does not seem to be a contradiction to the government, it is probably because this son of the patron of apartheid shares good company (for profit) with the son of Nigeria's leader who in the past, as a military leader, fought apartheid to a standstill. Blood may be thicker than water, but money as we can see in this case has proven to be far thicker than blood.
That is just by the way. One thing is clear though, if Obasanjo insists, as is his wont, he will not get away with this one.Contact: sam@nigeriaunlimited.com.

Original_Search_Title: Community Calls for Return of Schools to Missionaries
Headline: Nigeria: Community Calls for Return of Schools to Missionaries
Body_Text:
In order to avert the continuous degeneration of decency in the  society, the Ogidi community of Kogi state resident in Abuja, has thrown its  weight behind the call for the return of schools to missionaries.
The community made the call recently, while celebrating this  year's new yam festival at the police barracks in Area 10, Garki, Abuja.
The group lamented the continuous degeneration of decency in  the society leading to prostitution, armed robbery, drug addiction and other  forms of social menace and called on government to return schools to  missionaries, who they described as "The custodians of decency in our  society."
Speaking on the significance of the new yam festival, FCT  chapter president of the Ogidi Development Union (ODU), Mr. Jerome Amedaiyese,  said the festival was geared towards appreciating God's kindness in anticipation  of a good harvest in the year, adding "It also serves as a forum to give food to  the needy and show love to one another."
Mr. Amedaiyese, a police superintendent, said the festival is  an annual event observed every 15th June in Ogidi Ojimu Local Government Area of Kogi state but  due to prevailing circumstances, the Ogidi community in Abuja could not beat the  date. He however said that because of its importance in the minds of the people  of Ogidi resident in Abuja, the festival was to be celebrated even if late.
"The cultural tentacles of a people shall extend to wherever  those that identify with reside. This is exactly what Ogidi people in Abuja have  done today", added Mr Amedaiyese.
Apart from the feast of pounded yam and assorted drinks  supplied to mark the new yam season, there were songs and dances at the occasion  .
Chief G. E. Olupeka, a member of the community said the occasion was a  demonstration and manifestation of culture and tradition.

Original_Search_Title: The North is Still Under Siege
Headline: Sierra Leone: The North is Still Under Siege
Body_Text:
Freetown — There is every justifiable reason for Sierra Leoneans to be happy and
celebrate the end of the war, which officially ended in January this
year.
The rebel war, which started in March 23rd 1991 in the South-Eastern
town of Boumaru, has left most part of Sierra Leone, particularly the
northern region in a deplorable state.
Frankly speaking, the year 2002 can be described as the mother of all
memorable years, since the country attained independence in 1961.
Why? Because, this was the year when peace was finally restored to
Sierra Leone, after a decade of a brutal civil war.
The May 14 elections have come and gone, and active parliamentary
debates are expected to commence later next week, following the
screening of ministers.
In the midst of the heated parliamentary debates that are taking
place in Freetown, thousands of people who have returned to resettle
in the north are grappling with mountainous problems. Towns like ;
Matotoka, Masingbi, Yele, Kamakwie, Binkolo, Tambiama, to name but a
few, are now rising from the ashes of war.
It is a well known fact that during the war years, rebels destroyed
infrastructure including electricity, water supply systems,
hospitals, secondary and tertiary institutions, vocational
institutes, private institutions and incidentally stagnated
development.
On my recent visit to Makeni, Lunsar and Magburaka , I discovered
that the residents in these towns were yet to recover from the trauma
they were subjected by rebels .
Because of numerous constraints, which include lack of adequate
qualified staff and teaching and learning materials, the Magburaka
Government Secondary School for Boys, which has produced prominent
personalities in Sierra Leone, has lost its glory. Paramount chief
Bai Yossoh Kolifa 11 told me in Magburaka that, when the rebels
attacked the town, they vandalized school furniture and conscripted
many students into their movement.
Magburaka hospital, was renown for its efficacious treatment of sick
people from different parts of the country. It was vandalized during
the war. Today, it is in a dilapidated condition. I visited the
hospital and discovered that , it seriously need rehabilitation. The
wards were shabby and lacked adequate beds and bed sheets.
There was a time when Magbass Sugar Factory use to be the pride of
Magburaka. But today, it is in a deplorable state. Rehabilitating it
will run into millions of leones.
Presently, there is nowhere in Sierra Leone where leprosy patients
could be taken. Masanga leprosy control, which was built in the early
sixties by a German Company, was destroyed. The rebels vandalized the
laboratory equipment.
The buildings which use to house an international non-governmental
organization that assisted hundreds of students, women and even
farmers is now occupied by UNAMSIL Nibatt 11.
In Makeni, the Makeni Teachers College is no longer as it use to be.
I discovered that it too, was vandalized. The people of Makeni cannot
even boast of good drainage system. Now that the rainy season has
started; the Sierra Leone Roads Authority (SLRA) is expected to start
rehabilitating all major streets around the township.
During what Sierra Leoneans nostalgically remember as the good old
days, Masingbi use to be a business center. But today, visitors to
the town are greeted by destroyed houses.
Masingbi was the town where Lebanese and Maraka businessmen use to
deal in gold and diamonds. The town was very vital because it shared
boundaries with Bamakonta a famous mining town, that was known for
providing huge gems.
Bumbuna is in a deplorable state. It is still suffering in terms of
development, I learnt that even though the war has ended, the
authorities have not done anything to rehabilitate the town. Bumbuna
cannot even boast of good educational institutions, water,
electricity and adequate food. A bag of rice in the town is sold for
Le38,000 . There are many people that cannot afford to buy it . They
therefore have to go to the jungle to get bush yams and other edible
items.
Port Loko town where the late S.I. Koroma was born cannot boast of
electricity, water and other basic facilities.
The Port Loko government hospital, which was renown for providing
good health facilities, is no longer functioning.
Recently, I was told in Port Loko that the hospital has been
abandoned by the authorities.
In post-war Sierra Leone, NGOs and the government should formulate
plans for the rehabilitation and development of war-affected towns,
especially those in the north, which were occupied by rebels for a
long period towards the end of the war. During that occupation, a lot
of destruction was done.
If the people suffered during the war, they should not be left
suffering in post-war Sierra Leone.

Headline: Nigeria: A National Love for Titles
Body_Text:
Titles imply status, position and prestige in Africa, but in Nigeria this vital aspect of our culture, and related forms of it, have slowly started to suffer abuse of a fundamental nature. Tadaferua Ujorha recently explored this much talked about area.
Titles abound in all countries of the world. Indeed wherever man is found, sooner or later a title or system of titles evolve, as a means of rewarding one form of accomplishment or the other, among those who make up the mobile human community. Even in the insect world, in this case the Bees, there is a category known as the Queen Bee, which denotes a specific and matchless form of activity. This indeed is food for thought! Perhaps one could argue that the presence of titles is a natural feature of the human community. It is an event which easily flowered out of the delicate swing of man's sense of justice which sought to reward an individual, or individuals with rare talent, boundless goodwill and a spirit of self-sacrifice, for instance, who made profound contributions to the society, at one time or the other. Achebe's Things Fall Apart provides insight on the system of titling among Igbo communities about 1850 AD. He writes of Okonkwo, his Central character "when Unoka died he had taken no title at all and he was heavily in debt. Any wonder then that his son Okonkwo was ashamed of him? Fortunately, among these people a man was judged according to his worth, and not according to the worth of his father. Okonkwo was clearly cut out for great things. He was still young, but he had won fame as the greatest wrestler in the nine villages. He was a wealthy farmer and had two barns full of yams, and had just married his third wife. To crown it all he had taken two titles and had shown incredible prowess in two inter-tribal wars. And so although Okonkwo was still young he was already one of the greatest men of his time. Age was respected among his people but achievement was revered."
The present
An increasingly bewildering situation characterises the system today where traditional titles are concerned, and it is not difficult to illustrate this point. Once a powerful political chieftain was given a title by the king of a pre-eminent community. All seemed to go well thereafter, but all of a sudden the true heirs and owners of the title turned up, the political chieftain lost the title, and the king acquiesced. This is a dramatic illustration of the lucid neglect of tradition, values and culture by those in the fine corridors of traditional power, when they wish to satisfy the dreams and hopes of some of their 'Political friends'. On many occasions it is revealed that certain families rise in protest whenever certain chiefs want to give out a particular title or titles.
Sometimes too, an entire community would reject a person who may not have the peculiar qualities or traits which a particular title deserves. In a number of communities too, titles are created or invented to be given amidst pomp and pageantry on specific days. It is true that culture is dynamic, but to what degree do these titles harmonise with the pristine traditions and values of an ancient people. In Africa, titles have always been given to those firm of limb, agile of body, and with a keen wit and high intellect, but this is not always the case today. Presently, even members of some royal families openly boast that with money, anybody can have a title, so we have truly come to a most dismal pass in the life of the nation. A court case was once instituted about the time when the late Chief M. K. O. Abiola was to be conferred with the ancient Are Ona Kakanfo, and this may confirm cracks in the traditional edifice on this matter.
Traditional rulers such as the Emir of Katsina have always striven to emphasise the value and significance of traditional titles. At a recent turbanning in Katsina, Alhaji (Dr.) Muhammadu Kabir Usman stated that the conferment of the titles, was as much informed by merit, as by inheritance, saying that all the recipients have contributed to the development of the Emirate in particular, and then Katsina in general. There is no doubt that holders of traditional titles have a role and function in communities across the nation.
A perspective on titles
Abubakar Aliyu Liman a highly cerebral scholar, lectures in the department of English and Drama, Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria.
He casts a perspective on the matter thus: "In pre-feudal African societies, through dint of hard work, one earned a title. You have to distinguish yourself through hard work or through valour in warfare etc.,
He added that the situation changed in the feudal age when the focus shifted to the issue of inheritance. In his words "Issues of distinction, valour and hardwork, were gradually eroded with the emergence of the feudal social formation. Feudalism goes along with privilege and this explains why individuals struggle and assert themselves to earn a title. This is essentially because of the privilege associated with the title."
He posits however that there is a difference in attitude between Europeans and Africans on the same matter. "Today, neo-Feudalism is in vogue in the context of this craze for titles. In the western world, people are not concerned about titles. They are more concerned with merit and the contributions an individual makes to society. What distinguishes an individual there is the contribution he/she makes to his nation or his society. Instead of people respecting titles, they respect you for what you are. We don't have that here. In Nigeria it is the other way round. If you have a chain of titles, you stand a better chance of succeeding in whatever endeavour you find yourself. People respect these titles, and titles are more or less status symbols, and hence the craze to earn titles."
Continuing on a rather sad note, he added that the people that matter in Nigeria are those who have titles. In his words "even Professors bracket their academic reference and use what is termed a better prefix, which may be Alhaji or Chief, simply because it would give them some form of distinction." He referred to a one time Vice-Chancellor of the Ahmadu Bello University who dramatically de-emphasised his professorship, as soon as he got a traditional title from his people sometime in the early 90s. He also drew attention to the fact that when most Generals retire from the army, in a little while they acquire a chain of titles, and as it were, become upwardly mobile. In his opinion people ascribe power to these titles which acquire a mythos for themselves. He averred that this abundance of titles and the increasing hunger for them, is a sign of underdevelopment, and can only be found in underdeveloped societies.
Another lecturer in the department of Political Science, Ahmadu Bello University has a position akin to those of Abubakar Liman, outlined above. In his view the problems arising from the abuse of titles in the country owe their origins to a basic clash of value systems. According to him these two systems are the worlds of tradition and modernity. In his perspective modernity ascribes position to people with money and vast material means, while tradition ascribes position to persons who have demonstrated an ability to progress and impact upon society, by sheer dint of hard work.
Finally, Abubakar Liman added that people are naturally encouraged to retreat to the traditional institution to get titles, because the institution is very organised and can easily penetrate and manipulate other titled groups in the society, whether these be the military, political or academic elite. He said "when this society transcends this stage, all these would wither away. We need to work for what we earn. When capitalism begins to take root, there would be no place for all this. For now titles are associated with power and privilege."
Forms of titles
The titles emerging from the traditional institution have greater currency and have a pride of place in the country today. Other forms of titles which are steadily becoming popular are, interestingly, academic titles which are sourced either locally or abroad. Sometimes at a fairly elaborate ceremony a representative of a foreign university would appear with the relevant academic robes and transform a citizen into the holder of a university degree (Honouris Causa) for numerous unclear and vague services. Soon the recipient would adjust her complimentary card to read Chief (Mrs.) (Dr.) Engineer Yemi B. Z. Smith. This is quite a mouthful, but it is the bizarre material upon which our civilisation feeds today. Not too long ago a number of scholars in our universities posited that universities sometimes award honorary doctorates in order to encourage the recipients to assist the institution financially, and this naturally leads to a reduction in standards, because a university ought to award honorary doctorates to deserving members of society, and not to one who is just endowed with vast financial and material resources. Soon such persons would begin to weild influence over such universities, and attempt to influence policies and academic decisions. However, in some parts of the world degrees can be withdrawn if the recipient is engaged in unacceptable conduct.
Even while Britain ruled over Nigeria as its colony, colonial officers noted that Nigerians, for one reason or the other simply loved titles, and it seems the British did oblige. This point is made several times in some of the memoirs of the British colonial officers who served in Nigeria. Indeed Britain is another country where titles are often given from time to time, but perhaps not with our frequency, colourful enthusiam and flamboyance. Not too long ago Queen Elizabeth II Knighted Peter Badejo, a Nigerian creative genius who has contributed much to the Arts in Britain.
History has shown that when an idea or structure begins to suffer abuse of one form or the other, then society is witnessing the collapse of such an idea or structure. The simple truth is that whatever is valued or regarded as sacred can never suffer abuse, especially if that high regard still remains. When men reach out for externals or just the peripheral portions of an idea, then it suffers abuse. Traditional titles, like their academic counterparts, are supposed to be an encouragement to individuals in society. Titles can evolve into the true beacons of achievement and talent, which they were meant to be.

Original_Search_Title: I 'Listen' to My Body a Lot - Charles Edosonmwan, Lawyer
Headline: Nigeria: I 'Listen' to My Body a Lot - Charles Edosonmwan, Lawyer
Body_Text:
AS a young Senior Advocate of Nigeria (SAN), Charles Umwese Edosonmwan has a crowded schedule that takes him around Nigeria and beyond almost on permanent basis.
Charles, a martial arts enthusiast, believes his life lies in the hand of God, however, this does not in anyway suggest that he takes the state of his health lightly.
In this brief chat, he speaks on his work and general attitude to his own well-being. Excerpts:
"Everyday, I wake up around 5.00 am after which I say my prayers. This is usually the first thing I do as a Christian. I normally pray in the company of my wife as my children are still quite small, so they cannot participate actively in the prayer sessions.
After prayers, I may remain in bed for another 10 minutes or so just to relax. I do not do anything at this time apart from lying very still. At times, when I feel like it, I go out for a walk. A few people who are quite close to me know I am into the martial arts, so I'm fond of doing my Katas which is actually some kind of body enhancing exercises. After all these, I return to have a quick bath at about 6.00am before dressing up. I take my time doing this. It is also around this time that the kids wake up and I'd love to spend some time with them since the job does not allow one to see much of them during the day.
I leave home early without taking breakfast. My routine does not really allow time for that. However on weekends, when there is time, we take breakfast as a family though it may come as late as 10.30 or 11.00.
On Sundays when we go to church, we often do without it. But I do not joke with lunch and dinner no matter the circumstances. These are my main meals of the day, and when I eat, it is always in the African way. Pounded yam is a favorite especially for dinner if I have to take something solid.
Most days, I head straight to the court from home or drop in for a few minutes at the office. I stay on the mainland and I have to leave home early enough to beat the usual early morning traffic. Court activities come early so all relevant materials have to be prepared at least a day before in order to move to the court on time.
I go back to the office right after court sessions and if there are meetings and law reports to be read, files to be seen or clients to be met, I do that before leaving. The office closes by 6.00 pm although I could remain until I'm through with any outstanding work. There are times I take my work home. To be frank, no serious barrister would say he does not take work home. There are these law reports that are better read and digested at home. Clients who keep coming to see you or telephoning you do not allow enough time and concentration in the office.
Usually, I retire to bed anytime between midnight and 1.00 a.m. If I'm home early enough, I catch up with latest happenings on the news broadcast on TV. You would also find me glued to the set if a football match is being televised. In fact, I bought a new TV set just for the ongoing World Cup. I love the game and I have not missed a single match. I was a keen footballer when I was much younger, and although I'm too old to kick and jump around now, I still do some training moves related to the game.
It's not that I'm getting very old, but the fact is that I cannot do certain things as well as I used to when I was younger. For instance, I know I cannot kick or punch the way I used to but I believe I'm still quite a healthy person. I know my life is in God's hand. Let us not underestimate His mercies in this regard. It is a good thing to remain active and to work out as much as possible.
There are a few people who cannot really differentiate between working out and building muscles. Anyone who needs to work out should see a doctor first. You need to have your physical fitness assessed to estimate just how much rigors your heart can take. You know how common it is for people to collapse and die while jogging on the road. It's a lot more risky than could be imagined.
My involvement in the martial arts has made me more conscious of my health. Now I listen to my body a lot."

Original_Search_Title: Government Urged to Establish Cashew-Marketing Company
Headline: Ghana: Government Urged to Establish Cashew-Marketing Company
Body_Text:
Cashew farmers at Agyina, a farming community in the Kintampo District,
have appealed to the government to establish a marketing company to purchase
their produce to enhance the cultivation of the crop.
Mr Opoku Agyeman, a spokesman for the farmers, made the appeal at a
meeting to discuss how best to address their marketing, credit and other
problems.
He noted that at each harvest season the farmers are faced with a
problem of marketing, adding that some of the farms were burnt during the
last Harmattan season because the farmers were unable to weed them due to
financial problems.
Mr Agyeman urged the banks to provide the farmers with credit to enable
them to maintain their farms and to increase productivity.
He also urged agricultural extension officers to visit the area to give
the farmers technical advice to enhance their activities.
There are about 20 cashew farmers in the area and they have cultivated
more than 100 hectares of the crop.
Meanwhile, groundnut farmers at Yapare, another farming community in the
Atebubu District, have appealed to the district assembly to provide them
with loans under the Emergency Social Relief Fund to enable them to maintain
and expand their farms.
Miss Afia Kruwah, a spokesman, told the Ghana News Agency that the
farmers were finding it difficult to get credit to hire labourers to help
them on their farms.
Women in the area are mainly engaged in groundnut farming, while the men
cultivate yams on a large scale, she said, but added that, "without
labourers we cannot do much".
Miss Kruwah said the farmers had organised themselves into groups to
secure credit from the banks but all their efforts had been
unsuccessful.

Original_Search_Title: Quashigah Fingers Lapses In Farmers's Day Event
Headline: Ghana: Quashigah Fingers Lapses In Farmers's Day Event
Body_Text:
FOOD AND AGRICULTURE MINISTER, Major Courage Quashigah (rtd), has identified some of the lapses that hindered the smooth celebration of last year's National Farmer's Day as the absence of a time-ction plan and assignments that his ministry failed to give specific names for their completion.
According to him, the committee that was set up by the ministry to steer affairs during the celebrations did its best but had some few shortcomings that were so glaring to have escaped criticism both from the public and the press.
Mr. Quashigah, who was briefing pressmen during the inauguration of a 22- member national committee whose duties are to plan effectively for this year's Farmers' Day celebrations, further noted that a post-mortem assessment made by last year's committee revealed that sitting arrangement on the dais was not properly handled, whilst press coverage of the event was poor due to lack of liaison between the committee and the media.
Continuing, the minister pointed out that more harm was done, when the award of certificates to farmers who worked hard during the year was done with a bit of confusion, while guest artistes who were to perform some educative shows on the programme failed to turn up.
Commenting on the positive side of last year's event, the sector minister congratulated the committee on the grounds that they were able to secure scholarships for some major items for the benefit of award winners, which included the donations of a three-bedroom Estate house and one Pick-up for the runners-up, which was proudly sponsored by the Agricultural Development Bank.
