According to him, this year's event would focus on exposing food processing in the country so as to create employment avenues for the youth and wealth for the country.
Explaining further, the minister reiterated that with the procession of food, one particular product could be used for many things and to serve different purposes thereby preventing wastage in the system.
An example, he noted, was that one tuber of yam could well be processed to be used for a longer period of time other than one person buying the same tuber of yam and cooking a small portion and leaving the rest to spoil.
"With the procession of food, any body can walk into a shop and buy whatever he wants to buy and with the exact quantity," he added.
On his part, the chairman of the committee, Dr. Abdel-Majeed Haroun, gave the assurance that the committee would work effectively to ensure that this year's event is made a memorable one for the farmers in particular and the country as whole.

Headline: Nigeria: Oputa's Unfinished Business
Body_Text:
The Human Rights Violations Investigations Commission (HRVIC), otherwise
known as the Oputa Panel submitted its reports to the president recently.
Though the report has not been made public yet and a white paper is still to
be issued, the government has hastily set up an Implementation Committee to
commence the effectuation of the recommendations.
The idea of the Oputa Panel was a great one. It was in sum, to show us how
we mismanaged ourselves as a nation and then find a way through all of it to
first forgive ourselves before reconciliation. There is no doubt that our
nation has been misruled in the past quite the same way as is being done
today. So the notion of such a panel well in the fashion of the South
African Truth and Reconciliation Commission was overdue. But if people
showed cynicism in spite of its timeliness, it was probably because they
thought the president had other motives in its establishment. It was
believed that the original raison d'etre of the commission was to embarrass
a few people. It was also an opportunity for the president, still smarting
from his incarceration for high treason by the late Sani Abacha, to continue
with the message that he was a victim of high-handedness from the top
(apologies to General Diya). Many of the complainants thought it was an
avenue to get even with the ancien noblesse. General Oladipo Diya thought
for instance that it was an opportunity for him to take his pound of flesh
(with blood) from General Abacha, General Bamaiyi and Major Al-Mustapha and
also recover some monies allegedly seized from him by Abacha. These are
funds he could hardly account for. Brigadier General Sabo (rtd) saw it as a
means to appropriate sweet revenge on Generals Abdulsalami and Bamaiyi, his
former bosses, even if that breaches security and section 167 of the
Evidence Act which forbids anyone from giving evidence on security-sensitive
matters. Major Hamza Al-Mustapha also saw an opportunity to smear General
Abdulsalami who put him in his present predicament. But to many Nigerians,
especially those that can't afford cable or satellite television (for their
movie and comedy channels), the Oputa Panel which was telecast live
throughout its sittings on Nigerian Television Authority (NTA), provided an
affordable opportunity to daily watch first-rate true story movies and
comedies free of charge.
Initially, the mandate was limited to cover the military regimes from
General Buhari's tenure but protests from some quarters made the president
extend the scope to cover his own first term as head of state (1976-1979).
The fact that Obasanjo initially excluded his own regime from scrutiny
should have warned Nigerians of a not too healthy motive. Even with that and
in spite of all the successful court injunctions instituted to restrain the
commission, the Oputa Panel and the president, probably working in tandem,
made the appearances and the possible culpability of Generals Buhari,
Babangida and Abubakar, three former heads of state, the chief assignment of
the panel. Even when the three retired generals were appropriately and very
ably represented by their lawyers, Justice Oputa and the president insisted
on their physical appearance to face questioning. The president desperately
wanted them to appear and, to force compliance, he contrived a stage-managed
appearance himself. It was obvious that the Oputa show (similar to the Bill
Cosby show) would be incomplete if the three generals were not placed before
some small boys from Lagos for questioning and ridiculing. The president
forgot that under similar circumstances during the sitting of the Justice
Irikefe panel that investigated the alleged missing N2.8 billion involving
his military regime during the Shagari days, Obasanjo, then a former head of
state himself like the trio, was covered by immunity from physical
appearance as a former head of state based on the submission of his counsel.
He was represented by his lawyers then and that did not affect the outcome
or credibility of the conclusion reached by Justice Irikefe. The logic to
this is that section 183 of the Evidence Act grants immunity to first class
and second-class Emirs and Chiefs from appearances in courts and before
tribunals and provides for their representation by counsel. If even a
second-class chief can be granted this immunity, what more of a former head
of state who sits in council with the president? But the germane question
here should be that if Obasanjo himself refused appearance before the
Irikefe panel which derived legitimacy from the same Tribunal and Inquiry
Act cap 447 of 1966 and also under a democratic regime like this one and was
instead represented by his lawyers, why shouldn't that also be sufficient
for Generals Buhari, Babangida and Abdulsalami?
I agree completely with the recommendations of the panel that further
investigations be made to unravel the circumstances of the death and
possible murder of Dele Giwa, MKO Abiola, Shehu Yar'Adua, Bagauda Kaltho,
Sani Abacha etc. Nobody, no matter how highly placed should have the right
to take another person's life extra-judicially. But I reject the crude and
jungle justice that the president and the commission are already insinuating
to the effect that they already have an idea of the culprits in these murder
cases even before trial. That is the kind of kangaroo justice that still
keeps General Bamaiyi, Major Al-Mustapha, Mohammed Abacha, Colonel Yakassai
and others in perpetual detention and their treatment like already convicted
murderers even before the completion of trial. By all means, murderers
should be apprehended and be made to face the full wrath of the law but no
one should be allowed to suffer innocently. It is still possible that after
the trial of these people, some of them will be found innocent. About two
years ago, while addressing a gathering of medical doctors, the president
made an oblique remark - apparently referring to Colonel (Dr.) Ibrahim
Yakassai - that doctors should not use their profession and skills to murder
people. This he said would be going against the Hippocratic oath which the
doctors swore to. Why has the president who is the chief law making officer
of the nation (apologies to Senator Jonathan Zwingina) turned Nigeria into a
nation of legal travesties by declaring an accused person guilty even before
trial? It is on record that since Colonel Yakassai was arrested, he has not
even been asked to write a statement. It will be important to know whether
somebody like Ibrahim Yakassai is serving a jail term for the murder of
Shehu Musa Yar'Adua, as the president and his principal officers will want
us believe, or is just simply still in detention awaiting the determination
of his case?  What kind of lawless country is this? Where are the human
rights activists? Where is NADECO? Are we really a democracy? It is
precisely that kind of burlesque justice, which holds a person guilty even
before trial, that the president wants to apply to the former heads of
state.
Back to the Oputa report. Has the president forgotten that a Court of Appeal
(Lagos Division) presided over by Justice George Adesola Oguntade has
declared that the establishment of the Oputa Panel itself was procedurally
illegal and unconstitutional? (Togun v Oputa (N0. 2) NWLR (2001) part 740
pg. 597). Obasanjo was too eager to hang the former heads of state to have
any patience for the fine details of law.
From the recommendations of the commission, it is obvious that strenuous
efforts have been made to smear the names of all three former leaders. For
instance, General Abdulsalami is to be further investigated with respect to
the death of MKO Abiola. The commission should have added in its
recommendation that the delegation from the United States comprising Thomas
Pickering and Susan Rice (who was said to have served the tea from a flask)
in whose arms MKO Abiola died, and by extension former President Bill
Clinton who sent them, should be investigated. How ridiculous!! General
Buhari on his own has been asked to apologise to the families of the
executed cocaine pushers because he and members of his Supreme Military
Council infringed on the fundamental rights of the "businessmen" to deal in
the business of cocaine. I will also add here that General Gowon, President
Shagari, and of course Generals Buhari and others should apologise to the
nation for the execution of all the armed robbers during their tenures.
Their execution was an 'infringement of their fundamental rights to engage
in the business of robbery.' Only in Nigeria would such nonsense like an
apology to the families of executed cocaine pushers be seen in official
books. Those cocaine pushers (according to Chief Gani Fawehinmi, SAN) were
executed because they breached a law that was appropriately formulated by
the Supreme Military Council (the legislature at that time). Anybody that
committed the crime of cocaine pushing at that time knew the consequences of
being caught. Kenneth Owoh and others took chances and they paid the supreme
price. No tears should be shed for them. Anyone that has been a victim of
armed robbery in this country and knows the relationship between such
violent crimes and the use of cocaine will not take the recommendation of
the commission on General Buhari seriously. I am one of such people. I have
twice been a victim of armed robbery attacks!
But as the reader can see from the title of this column today, I don't think
that Justice Oputa has finished his job. He should have for instance
recommended that the petition of Dr. Beko Ransome-Kuti be revisited. Beko
charged that his mother, the distinguished Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti was
murdered on the instruction of General Obasanjo when he was head of state in
1977. A commando-style military attack on the premises of Fela's (Beko's
brother) Kalakuta Republic in which his singers and dancers were repeatedly
raped and his mother thrown from the window left her with severe injuries
which she never recovered from. Funmilayo Ransome-Kuti had been active in
the anti-colonial struggle, years before Obasanjo even contemplated joining
the army and had introduced Fela, arguably Africa's greatest dissident then,
to Kwame Nkrumah of Ghana. It should be recalled that Fela presented the
out-going regime of Obasanjo with a replica of his mother's coffin in 1979.
Second, Obasanjo used a retroactive decree in the heat and passion of the
aftermath of General Murtala Mohammed's assassination to execute (or is it
officially murder) people like Joseph Gomwalk and would have also used it on
General Gowon but for the fact that Gowon was far beyond his reach. General
Hassan Usman Katsina was also arrested and was only saved by providence. The
death of Gomwalk should also be re-opened and examined as the commission has
recommended for others. In what could be described as poetic justice,
Obasanjo himself fell foul of the same law under Abacha (but this time not
retrospectively) but the former head of state whom Obasanjo still
characterises as a direct descendant of Lucifer was gracious enough to
listen to calls for the commutation of his death sentence.
Third, the death of Colonel Abdu Wya should also be investigated. From the
revelation we received about a decade ago, the promising colonel should not
have been executed because the crime of participation in the coup was never
proved against him. It was Obasanjo's vote in the Supreme Military Council
meeting that considered the issue that led to his unjustified death. Chief
Allison Ayida told us he was part of the meeting as the then Secretary to
the Federal Military Government and contended that the arguments against the
colonel were never proved beyond all reasonable doubt as was and still is
standard practice. The late colonel now has growing and lovely grand
children who would need to be told that their grandfather was not a
criminal. Also, the circumstance of the death of his Irish wife is still
shrouded in mystery.  Just like the deaths of MKO Abiola and Yar'Adua, these
two should also be revisited.
Fourth, Justice Chukwudifu Oputa should seek the expansion of the scope and
jurisdiction of his panel's mandate to cover the present Obasanjo
government. This should not be difficult since it has been done before. And
in this case, I promise the distinguished jurist that he may even get the
legislative imprimatur that has handicapped his job so far. That will help
the nation to look into the cases of the abuse of fundamental human rights
and the right to life of Odi villagers, Zaki-Biam natives and General Victor
Malu. The president seems to have subscribed to the pedestrian impression
that women, children and the disabled deserved to be killed in Odi and
Zaki-Biam as reprisal for the killing of soldiers. I am worried that our
president's appreciation of justice runs against the grain of civilised
standards and is repugnant to natural justice. If soldiers were killed, then
the government should have deployed all its machinery of coercion to
apprehend the culprits and not take the lives of other people who might not
even have known a thing about the murder of the soldiers in the first place.
General Malu's own village was even far away from where the 19 soldiers were
killed and there is no evidence of his complicity. Soldiers were sent to his
village to bomb his house, kill his 85-year-old blind uncle's wife and the
uncle himself. An innocent farmer returning home with tubers of yams on his
head was shot point-blank and left on the street to rot. If these are not
cases of breaches of fundamental human rights, then nothing will ever be!
Fifth, there will be the need to revisit the OPC killings in Lagos and other
areas of the Southwest. The president should be made to apologize to the
families of the victims of the needless murders. This is more so because
Nigerians believe the president has not used the full weight of his office
to protect them from the bestiality of the OPC. The victims also include DSP
Amao, the murdered DPO of Bariga police station in Lagos who is a Yoruba
from Kwara State. By the way I can't remember the president sending
policemen on reprisal mission after the killing of the DPO. The DPO was also
a law enforcement agent like the 19 soldiers murdered in Tiv land.
Six, there is the urgent need to investigate the rumours that did the rounds
between 1977 and 1979 when Obasanjo was head of state that the then
government had set up detention camps at the crocodile infested Ita Oko
Island in the outskirts of Lagos. Some of the reports were even carried in
the international media of that time.
Last but by no means least, Oputa Panel would need to take a stand on the
case of theft against General Diya and his lying under oath. The case of
perjury is serious under any system. Diya must be made to apologize to the
Nigerian public.
For true reconciliation, Nigerians have to know the whole truth. The
distinguished members of the Oputa Panel obviously meant well in the
discharge of their assignment. My only fear is that the president does not
turn a perfectly good idea into a political weapon against perceived
opponents in 2003 in furtherance of his mission of vendetta in government.
Contact: sam@nigeriaunlimited.com

Original_Search_Title: Show Them Pepper, Obasanjo Tells Eagles On Phone
Headline: Nigeria: Show Them Pepper, Obasanjo Tells Eagles On Phone
Body_Text:
AS Nigeria's Super Eagles confront Sweden today in Kobe, Japan, in their second match in the on-going World Cup competition, President Olusegun Obasanjo spoke with the players and their officials on phone yesterday, asking them to face the match squarely.
The Eagles need an outright win today to remain in contention.
President Obasanjo told them to put behind them Sunday's defeat by Argentina.
He spoke to, one after the other, Sports Minister Steve Akiga, Coach Adegboye Onigbinde, NFA Chairman, Dominic Oneya and six players.
President Obasanjo told Coach Onigbinde in pidgin English: "The soup wey go sweet go need plenty fire," (meaning no success without sweat). Coach Onigbinde assured the President that everybody was in high spirits while team captain, Okocha said, "the boys are fully ready."
The President told Ifeanyi Udeze that the team should show their opponents "pepper."
"If possible, score from your left full back position," the President added, with Udeze assuring him that "African food," such as "pounded yam" was available to them in Japan.
"Pastor" Taribo West said they were also going to pray to which the President replied: "God will answer your prayer." The pastor told the President that the team had prepared "wonderfully well."
Upon being told that the Super Eagles Supporters' Club were already in Kobe, the President said: "Tell the Supporters' Club that they should cheer the boys to victory."
To Goalkeeper Ike Shorunmu, the President said: "Be cool, calm and calculated. Nothing should pass you tomorrow."
When Garba Lawal told the President that they were all ready, the President asked him whether he could tell Nigerians at home that "victory is assured"? Lawal said the President's earlier letter had also been read to them.
"Fight like wounded lions. Don't eat too much. You are Super Eagles. Everything would be super. You dey kampe!" he told the boys.

Headline: Nigeria: Burying Our Mothers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Up to this moment in the one week plus that his mother's corpse laid out, Comrade John Odah, General Secretary of Nigerian Labour Congress, NLC, has been the quintessential traditional gentleman who is not expected to be going about weeping over the death of a loved one. He consoled his juniors, played host to callers and generally prepared to bid the mother final farewell. But this posture became difficult to maintain at 11.29. a.m. this morning April 19,2002. The tears came down as the coffin was laid out preparatory to the homeward journey to Ingle in Edumuga District of Okpokwu local government area, Benue State.
The finality of death is such that the tears do come down at such moments, more so for urban elite who live dangerously most of the time in terms of interaction with parents where the parents are peasants. The process of industrialisation or of modernisation (westernisation in the case of Nigeria) is such that the traditional family is scattered in different direction. Thus those of peasant parentage get separated quite early; either to continue schooling or to search for employment, all of which are in the urban centres. Working life is urban based too. So, the parent- children interrelation is reduced to the occasional home visits or the shallow consultation that precedes important projects such as taking a wife.
There is no doubt about it that the level of industrialisation in the country is not sufficient to completely entrench capitalist modernity. In fact, the reality is what Claude Ake calls the ruralisation of African cities: "African primordial solidaristic ties have shown a remarkable resiliency in the face of the onslaught of capitalism. Many workers remain peasants at heart, keep a foot in the village and regard the village as home. One of the striking features of urbanisation in Africa is the dire creation of the village community in the city as a basis for recreational activities and for economic and social security."
There is, however, also no doubt that the traditional family order has been uprooted and this has occasioned a psycho-dynamic, socio-cultural and interactive gulf between peasant parents and their urban based offsprings. Although peasants in much of Africa have been radicalised, the infrastructural crisis and political de-participation that are the realities of the peasantry in much of Africa means that the difference in their world and that of their urban kids can be very clear. it is such that only experiences such as a disaster or death that brings out this distance or the realisation of it. This is a social problem and has nothing to do with how frequently a particular son or daughter has visited home or how adequately a mother or a father has been materially catered for . Survival in this context necessarily compels a separation or divide of "the modern" children from their parents. That is, those, like us, whose parents are peasants. And we can be found shedding tears out of "wonder" that mother, father, aunt, niece, nephew is dead too soon. It constitutes a dimension of the problem of colonial experience for the African elite.
A debate that usually arises as part of the resolution of this is usually the question of what manner of burial do we give to a parent- low key, high profile or simply a decent burial?
John Odah typically wanted nothing more than quiet, decent burial for his mum, Mrs Agnes Igba Akogwu but things went out of his hands in this regard. The coalition of labour, NGO activists and sundry comrades who converged to sympathise with him translated to a burial with great fanfare in comparative terms.
A convoy of about 30 vehicles escorted the corpse home to Ingle village in Edumogu District of Okpokwu local government area, certainly the first much of such "foreign" population to this arch type Nigerian rural community. But the Comrades did not bother about this. They blended beautifully with nature, feeding on huge balls of pounded yam whether at midnight or early morning. No wonder, the pounded yam "Directorate" at this burial under the supervision of Ogodo Odah, John's wife could be observed to be at work 24 hours throughout.
At the internment proper of Mrs. Akogwu on April 20, 2002 leaders of women groups, human rights organisations, labour and other NGOs in which John is active gave graveside tributes. The conclusion was the Mrs. Akogwu must have been a good nature woman since John, her son, is good nature and actively involved in patriotic organisations with express commitment to several changes in favour of the masses when he could equally have been mindlessly stealing public funds. This message ran through the statement of Y.Z Yau, the president of Community Action for Popular Participation, CAPP, Cecelia Abashi, the voice of women on the occasion, Barrister Titus Mann, Deputy President of the Civil Liberties Organisation, CLO, Chris Egbe representing Edema Youths as well as the two labour leaders who spoke for the Nigeria Labour Congress, NLC, Daniel Adekola, Congress Vice President and Buga Abuul, Chairman, Benue State Council of NLC.
The death of Mrs. Akogwu and the subsequent trip to Ingle opened the eyes of the leaders of the Nigerian NGO community to the reality that they have so much to do and that 90 per cent of that ought to be in the rural areas, not in the town and state capitals.
If only for this realisation, the trip to Ingle was more than worth it for the activists. Of course, all those who made the trip were happy to be part of a decent burial for the woman who nursed the Chief Bureaucrat of Nigerian Labour at the moment. The fanfare that eventually attended the burial should hopefully instruct our rural youngsters and their parents to seek education in spite of all hardships. For, the alternative is life in the army of social adventurers engaged in banditry, alcoholism, and sundry lumpiness.
In a society where redistribution of social wealth is not based on a systemic, rational model, the acquisition of education and skills is the only step to self-actualisation.
Although our educational systems produces elite who are mercenary in orientation, the balance of forces in the society is such that education can still be a tool for individual and social liberation.
The burial honour given to John Odah's mother and to John by implication, by comrades testified to this reality that some elite are fully aware of the squalor of the lives of our peasantry.
Fare thee well, Igba Akogwu, from this world which you departed on Thursday, April 11, 2002 at Abuja where you were an outpatient a the National Hospital, for the preceding three months.

Original_Search_Title: Seeking a Political Solution After the Supreme Court Ruling
Headline: Nigeria: Seeking a Political Solution After the Supreme Court Ruling
Body_Text:
Lagos — SEBASTINE HON explores the political option to Resource Control
The full court of the Supreme Court of Nigeria had, in a landmark judgment delivered on 3rd April, 2002, fundamentally effected changes in the revenue sharing formula of the federation. Of importance is the fact that the onshore -offshore dichotomy, which had been abolished since 1991, was again introduced by the Supreme Court decision. Thus, a state like Akwa Ibom, which used to rely heavily on offshore oil revenue was robbed of the constitutionally-guaranteed 13% derivation fund from mineral resources. Also, the federation account (income and expenditure) was fundamentally restructured.
Before the epochal decision, the Federal Government had, through the Revenue Mobilisation Allocation and Fiscal Commission, submitted a bill proposing a revenue sharing formula to the National Assembly. By this proposal, 46% of the total revenue of the Federation was to go to the Federal Government; 30% to the states; 16% to the local governments, 10% to F. C.T Abuja and 6% as "special fund" But even before this bill could be passed into law by the National Assembly, the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja, was already enjoying its 1% derivation. The Federal Government was also unilaterally drawing large chunks of money from the Federation account for itself, for the funding of the judiciary and the N.N.P.C. and the servicing of external debts. All these practices, the Supreme Court declared, were unconstitutional. The Supreme Court further directed the Federal Government to pay in arrears all derivation funds, which it ought to have started paying from May 29th 1999, when the 1999 constitution came into force. Revenue from gas was also ordered to be part of derivation and the non- payment of derivation on capital gains tax and stamp duties was declared unconstitutional, null and void.
The net result of these fundamental changes in the revenue derivation and sharing formula of the Federation have considerably rubbed on the polity, heating it, as it were, to near explosion. The depleting of monies accruing to the Federal Government has led to three sharp reactions from that end. First, the Revenue Mobilisation Allocation and Fiscal Commission, RMAFC, has withdrawn its bill on revenue sharing from the National Assembly. Secondly, owing to serious financial squeeze at the National Assembly, the Senate, on 16/5/2002, passed a motion calling on the Presidency to approach the Supreme Court once again for a "stay of execution" of its judgment. The propriety of this motion shall be discussed hereinafter. The Federal Government has also ordered for the full commercialisation of the N.N.P.C., which it was hitherto funding.
From the South-South, reactions have been very sharp, with Akwa-Ibom State which is worst hit, taking the front roll. At a recent public gathering christened " Akwa Ibom Consultative Forum", prominent Akwa-Ibom indigenes, led by the Governor, Victor Attah, slammed the Supreme Court judgment and publicly withdrew support for the Obasanjo-led government. Also, legal and non-legal brains from the South-South, like Professor Itse Sagay, the Hon. Justice Atake (rtd.) Senator David Dafione, etc. have strongly criticised the Supreme Court decision, citing foreign decisions and treaties to which Nigeria is a signatory, in support of their contentions. With all these pressures, the Federal Government has announced that it will find a political solution to the outcome of the judgment, for which see the ThisDay of 20th May 2002.- Now, how should the Federal Government go about this?
There is no doubt that the April 5th, 2002 decision of the Supreme Court is a final decision which, by section 235 of the 1999 Constitution is final in the real sense of it.
Thus, fine prose or legal arguments submitted on the pages of newspapers, pointing how wrong the Supreme Court was in its decision, is neither here nor there. In the case of ' Ibero V. Obioha (1994) 1 SCNJ 44, after the Supreme Court had given a final judgment in a suit, the respondent who lost the appeal filed a motion asking the Court to review its said judgment and to "order the hearing of the appeal in this court or the rehearing of the appeal in the Court of Appeal or the retrial of the case itself". It was the contention of the applicant that the Supreme Court had erroneously failed to take notice of the two additional grounds of appeal filed by the respondent in his appeal to the Court of Appeal. Further, that the Supreme Court had acted beyond it powers to pronounce on the correctness vel non of the decision of the Divisional Officer's Court. The Supreme Court was, therefore, called upon to 'correct' those errors and overturn its judgment. The Supreme Court dismissed the application, saying that by section 215 of the 1979 Constitution which is in pari materia with section 235 of the l99 Constitution, its decisions are "final" in the real meaning of the word. Belgore, J.S.C., who read the lead judgment, held at page 53 thus:
"Once the Supreme Court has entered judgment in a case, that decision is final and will remain so forever. The law may in future be amended to affect future issues on same subject, but for the case decided, that is the end of the matter" Emphasis mine.
From the above, the Supreme Court provided a way out of this current legal grid lock, that is, by the authorities concerned amending the law. Thus, instead of the Senate adopting a motion that the Supreme Court be approached for a stay of execution, a bill can emanate from either the National Assembly or the Executive, amending all the relevant sections of the 1999 Constitution and all other relevant laws the Supreme Court considered before reaching that decision, to reflect the mood of the South - South, the National Assembly and the Federal Executive. This is more so because a stay of execution can only be granted when there is a pending appeal in court, for which see Martins V. Nicanner Food Co. Ltd. (1988) 2 NWLR (Pt. 74) 75 and Mobil Oil V. Agadiagho (1988) 4 SCNJ 174. But there being no further right of appeal from a decision of the Supreme Court, it will be otiose to approach that court with just a motion for stay without an appeal.
The second way the government can wriggle itself out of this economic, political and constitutional logjam is by promulgating a law ad hominen-through the National Assembly, nullifying the Supreme Court judgment. Such a law which, should be comprehensive, should therefore, go ahead to define the revenue sharing formula as it affects all the component parts of the federation. Thus, the panicky measures of commercialising the NNPC or withdrawing the revenue sharing formula bill from the National Assembly should not arise. The best thing is to amend the bill in such a way that it will have a nullifying effect on some of the sore points of the Supreme Court decision. This was done in Nigeria before. In Lakanmi V. Anorney - General, Western Region (1970) 6 N. S. C. C. 143, the Supreme Court of Nigeria had held that the 1966 coup de'tat was not a revolution but an illegal hijack of power. This angered the then Federal Military Government, headed by General Yakubu Gowon, which then promulgated Decree No. 28 of 1973. By section 1 (2) thereof, the Decree nullified the Supreme Court judgment in Lakanmi's case. While Decree No. 28 ousted the Courts' jurisdiction of inquiring into why it was promulgated, the present National Assembly does not need to do that, as we are now in a democracy; but the same result would be achieved.
The Supreme Court confirmed, in a recent decision, the effect of Decree No. 28 on its decision in Lakanmi's case. In Attorney - General of the Federation V. Guardian Newspapers Ltd. & Ors. (1999) 5 SCNJ 324 at 347 - 348, Uwaifo, J. S. C., delivering the lead judgment of the Supreme Court, had this to say:
"The learned Solicitor- General has made copious reference to the decision of this Court in Lakanmi V. Attorney - General (West 1970) 6 N. S. C. C. 143 and Decree No. 28 of 1970 made to nullify it. He has relied on the effect of the said Decree which he says has been enacted in Decree No. 12 of 1994. Chief Williams has also quoted extensively from the decision in Lakanmi case. One cannot run away from the fact that the decision in Lakanmi's case was nullified by Decree No. 28 of 1970". Emphasis mine.
It must be noted that the framers of Decree No. 28 of 1970 were the relevant arm of the military government then, which was the Supreme Military Council. Under a democracy, as we are now, the National Assembly is the law-making organ of the government. Section 4 (2) of the 1999 constitution stipulates thus:
"The National Assembly shall have power to make laws for the peace, order and good government of the Federation or any part thereof with respect to any matter included in the Exclusive Legislative List set out in Part I of the Second Schedule to this Constitution."
The National Assembly can, under items 1 and 39 of Part I of the Schedule and item I of Part II of the Schedule to the 1999 Constitution, make a law ensuring that there is "peace, order and good government" in Akwa-Ibom State or the Niger Delta and even the Federation itself as salaries are presently being owed, oil companies are crying for money, as none is coming from NNPC, etc. (by amending or annulling the offending portions of the Supreme Court judgment). The National Assembly should, as a matter of real national urgency, rely, apart from the decisions of the Supreme Court above, on the other reported case of A. R. C. O. N. V. Fassassi (1987) 3 NWLR (Pt. 59) 42 at 46, where Eso, J. S. C., provided this escape route:
"In the Supreme Court, the decision of that Court, in so far as-that case is concerned is final in all ages...it is final in the sense of real finality. It is final forever. Only a legislation ad hominen can alter it". Emphasis mine.
The third way out is for all the parties to the Supreme Court decision to sit down (as is being proposed by the Federal Government), find a solution thereto and settle amicably. This may sound awkward or even impossible, but then it has the sanction of the law. In Abbey V. Alex (1999) 11 & 12 SCNJ 234 at 246 - 247, Uwaifo, J. S. C., had this to say:
"--The power to settle or compromise 'at any stage of pending proceedings' extends even to that of compromising judgments in certain situations. This is so because under note 1 to para. 383 Halsbury's Laws of England (supra), it is remarked that proceedings remain pending until satisfaction of the judgment. For this opinion, the observation of Jessel M. R in Re Clagett's Estate, Fordham v. Clagett [1882l 20 Ch. D. 637 at 653 is there relied on. There, he stated inter alia:-
'A cause is said to be pending in a court of justice when any proceeding can be taken in it. . . If you can take any proceeding it is pending. 'Pending ' does not mean that it has not been tried. It may have been tried years ago. In fact, in the days of the old Court of Chancery, we were familiar with cases which had been tried fifty or even one hundred years before, and which were still pending Sometimes, no doubt, they require a process which we call reviving... but nevertheless they were pending suits.... "
I think the emphasis should be that if a judgment remains not executed but can still be by the proper procedure being taken, then it might be said to be a step taken at any stage of a pending proceeding. In that sense and with that status, a judgment may be compromised by the parties to it. I can see nothing unusual or unlawful about this. - That was what in effect happened to the judgment in the consolidated Kalabari Native Courts suits No. 321/46 and No. 352/46 when the parties entered a compromise as per exhibit B". (Emphasis mine).
It can safely be concluded, therefore, that while the Supreme Court decision in the famed resource control suit is final, the Federal Government, if at all it is serious about finding a political solution thereto, has several legal and constitutional means of the doing so. And if that is to be done, it should speedily be so done, to avert a looming national calamity of immeasurable proportions. The Federal Executive and the National Assembly have both the knife and the yam; they can cut it anyhow they feel. But let it be fast.
Mr. Hon is a Port Harcourt - based Legal Practitioner and Chairman of the Movement for the Emancipation of the Middle - Belt, (MEMB)

Headline: Malawi: Maize Dominance Worsens Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Lilongwe — Malawi needs to shift its focus from maize to the abundance of "local" foods - many of them growing wild - if it wants to avert future food shortages and malnutrition, a nutritionist based in the country told IRIN.
"It has been ingrained over the last 60 years that maize is food and there is nothing else. People think if they haven't eaten maize, they are 'hungry'," said Stacia Nordin. She hopes to convince Malawians, donors and the government, to re-examine the dominance of this staple food.
"They don't look at anything else like beans or nuts - there is just a big focus on energy from maize and it's killing our land and not meeting nutritional requirements. I'm begging them to wean themselves off it," she said.
Focusing only on processed maize and the energy component of the diet was especially detrimental to people with HIV/AIDS because it doesn't contain all the nutrients the body needs. "If they want to focus on energy they might as well just eat sugar," Nordin said.
Her call comes as President Bakili Muluzi told the opening of Parliament last week that 600,000 mt of maize was needed to avert a food crisis for up to 3.2 million people when food from the current harvest is finished.
Organisations like the World Food Programme (WFP) and Save the Children have already started distributing emergency supplies of maize and the government has called for tenders towards replenishing the stocks that it controversially sold last year.
Nordin slammed media articles that poignantly described people eating wild food out of desperation as she believes that the reintroduction of these very foods is the answer to solving Malawi's food crisis and preventing malnutrition.
She explained that Malawian's ate a variety of "old foods" before the arrival of maize with the Portuguese in the 1790s. Maize was also promoted as a crop by British colonialists and this was perpetuated by previous president Kamuzu Banda, who decided that "maize was the future and everybody had to plant it".
"It was ingrained that other foods were bad and that Malawi was moving ahead by leaving the old foods," she said.
Nordin said constant crops of maize had destroyed the soil's nutrients and this, combined with poor land preservation like not putting organic matter back into the soil, burning and the use of fertilisers, was damaging the soil. Maize is also not resistant to drought.
Nordin wants people to balance their meals by considering the 555 other foods available from local fruits and vegetables and wild food. This would require rediscovering the "old foods" that the "goggos" (grannies) still remember.
Instead of maize people could eat indigenous crops like millet, sorghum, a local rice and a variety of yam and potato-like foods. She said local wild beans called "kakumpanda" and "mikuna" grow quickly and are tasty. Malawi has local plums, berries and figs and every part of the baobab fruit is edible - the seeds, fruits, leaves, flowers and roots. Avocadoes, though also not indigenous, provide a rich food source and even the leaves can be boiled as tea to prevent anaemia. Flour can be made by roasting and pounding the seeds of pumpkins and watermelons.
To help get the message across, she gives groups of up to 20 people a presentation with pictures of the wild and local vegetables available. Much of her lectures are built on information contained in a book called "Useful Plants of Malawi" by botanist Jesse Williamson. Williamson's study was "very scientific so it sat on a shelf".
In Nordin's presentations, she describes the plants and how they contribute to good nutrition. She said the younger generation often don't know the plants "but an older person will then step forward and laugh saying 'I remember that' and take over the presentation and do all the teaching for me".
"I explain to people that they don't need to have money to have a good diet ... This has been one of the better education years because of the hunger. People are realising that the system of just focusing on maize doesn't work," she said.
In the wake of two successive years of poor maize harvests, the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) in conjunction with the government, is now encouraging diversification into drought-resistant cassava. Cassava is traditionally grown along the shores of Lake Malawi on the eastern flank of the country.
FAO official Des Forpes told IRIN that three agricultural research institutions across the country would be used to propagate cassava cuttings, which would then be distributed to farmers. Cassava is a hardy plant, which although unlikely to replace maize, could be grown as an emergency crop providing a rich source of vitamins, minerals and proteins.
He acknowledged that a lot of work would be needed to convince farmers of the benefits of cassava: "They haven't been exposed to it, but with this food crisis they'll be encouraged to grow it."

Original_Search_Title: PDP Women Team Off To India-On Tech Transfer Mission
Headline: Nigeria: PDP Women Team Off To India-On Tech Transfer Mission
Body_Text:
Abuja — AS a 37-member Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) women delegation left for India Saturday, on a technological transfer mission, the party's national secretary, Prince Vincent Ogbulafor, said the visit was not a jamboree.The PDP's chief scribe made this clarification in Abuja at a press conference where he formally announced the party's version of poverty alleviation progamme tagged "PDP's income generating programme".
Prince Ogbulafor said the delegation, which is expected to spend a week in India at the total cost of N12 million, is being headed by the PDP's national women leader, Chief Mrs Josephine Anenih. Other members of the delegation, according to him, are women professionals carefully nominated and selected from each State of the federation and Abuja.
He said the women were going to India to see things for themselves especially small scale industries and to come home with something that would be practiced and passed on to others, adding they are being briefed on tips that they would look for to avoid coming home empty-handed.
India, he said, was chosen because it has an established framework for the training of small scale and micro enterprises as well as other income generating measures.
"The country produces over 2,500 different items from its small scale and micro enterprises. These enterprises account for 40 per cent of value added in manufacturing 35 per cent of national export, seven per cent of the gross domestic product and a sizeable proportion of employment. Small scale enterprises have the greatest potential traditionally for employment generation," he said.
He added that the above constitute the attractions for choosing India for the visit by the women who will play a central role in the execution of the part income generation programme. While PDP State chairman nominated one delegate each, he said, for States with PDP factions or where PDP is not in control, the party ministers were asked to make the nomination.
According to him, the party's income generating programme (IGP) is aimed at complementing government's efforts in poverty eradication through measures at empowering the poor and vulnerable groups, stressing that it will also provide a source of income to the target beneficiaries by equipping them with the necessary means of sustaining themselves.
"We are adopting the doctrine of providing a hoe instead of yam, fishing net as opposed to the provision of fish. By empowering the poor, we are helping to reduce poverty," he said.
While the party has since received about six rice milling machines for onward distribution to the six geo-political zones, he said upon return, the delegation will be assisted by the party to procure small loans from the bank. He further said that the target group of beneficiaries of the IGP are the women and other vulnerable groups, especially the disabled.

Original_Search_Title: Peoples Democratic Party women delegation leaves for India
Headline: Nigeria: Peoples Democratic Party women delegation leaves for India
Body_Text:
THE Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), will today despatch a thirty seven member strong women delegation to India to understudy the Asian country's small and medium scale industry with a view to transferring the skill to the country.
The visit which is the direct result of the recent visit by the party's Income Generating Programme focused on women delegation to India recently, according to the national secretary of the party, Chief Vincent Ogbulafor, is to put women in a position to help themselves and their society.
He said the Income Generating Programme of the party is aimed at complementing government's effort at poverty eradication through measures to empower the poor and vulnerable group. It aims at providing a source of income to the target beneficiaries by equipping them with the necessary means of sustaining themselves.
"We are adopting the doctrine of providing a hoe instead of yam; fishing net as opposed to the provision of fish. By empowering the poor we are helping to reduce poverty," he said.
The PDP secretary said that women are being focused on because of their central role they play in the family and the society. Women bear the bulk of the hardship in the family and any adversity on them affects the family most. Above all they are more vulnerable because of cultural and social limitations on them', he observed.
He explained that India was chosen because of its well established framework for the thriving of small scale industry and micro-enterprises as well as other income generating measures.

Headline: Ghana: Feeding a Growing Population
Body_Text:
By the end of this year, the President's special "cassava initiative" would be yielding results in the form of increased food production for the economy. Processing of cassava other than for food would then be a challenge for the private sector to take advantage of.
My interest in this piece is to continue the crusade on the need to increase or maximise the production, processing and storage of food crops such as plantain, yam and other tubers, as major staple food crops in both southern and northern Ghana.
Majority of countries both in the developed and developing countries for example have attained higher levels in food crop production and this has ensured food security for their citizens through the increase in food crop production, processing and storage of the excess, particularly of cereals such as rice, wheat, barley, corn and potato, just to mention a few.
Apart from wheat, which can only thrive in the temperate zones, most of these food crops in addition to our tropical ones such as plantain, yam, cocoyam, millet and sorghum can thrive and be cultivated on a large scale in Ghana and other parts of Africa. Our continent has the potential and capacity to grow more food and feed its people than is the case today.
A Special Initiative Project for Plantain Cultivation and Processing Not too long ago, plantain became an electoral issue, where the government, then in opposition, often leveled accusation at the NDC administration, now the main opposition in parliament, for not doing enough in the area of food crop cultivation which resulted in the country depending on surplus plantains from La Cote d'Ivoire during certain parts of the year.
The NPP then promised during the electioneering campaign (though I stand to be corrected) to turn the situation around by going into mass cultivation of plantain that would ensure an all year availability of the product for Ghanaians to enjoy their fufu and ampesi without a problem.
We all might be aware that during certain parts of the year the price for a bunch of plantains could be as high as ¢25,000 in the major urban cities of the country. The price of roasted plantain (popularly called "Kofi Brokeman"), the favourite diet of majority of poor workers has now risen from ¢500 to ¢l,000 in big cities such as Accra. A programme to focus on the mass cultivation of plantain, as one of the major diets of Ghanaians must also be given the needed boost as is being done for rice and cassava by the government of President J. A. Kufuor.
The challenge now is how can we ensure availability of plantain as a staple all year round for a growing population so that we would not have to be importing surpluses from La Cote d'Ivoire. In Ghana, six regions out of the ten, namely Western, Central, Eastern, Volta, Ashanti and Brong Ahafo produce plantain. Of this, Eastern Region is the major producer of the crop, producing 855,000 m/tonnes of plantain in 1996 and 817,000 m/tonnes in 1998. Ashanti Region, which in 1996 also produced 435,021 m/tonnes of the crop and increased its yield slightly to 476,355 m/tonnes in 1998, is the second largest producer.
Western Region followed with 279,780 m/tonnes of plantain in 1996 and increased this to 325,370 m/tonnes in 1998, as the third largest producer of plantain in the country. Brong Ahafo, the fourth largest producer in 1996, yielded 173,888 m/tonnes of plantain and increased this to 218,463 m/tonnes in 1998.
The Central and Volta Regions on the other hand are the lowest producers of the crop. Total plantain produced for the years of 1996 and 1998 were 1,823,395 m/tonnes and 1,912,648 m/tonnes respectively. From the data above, it is clear that apart from cassava and yam which is the major food crop produced in the country, plantain is the third largest food crop cultivated, exceeding maize produced in 1998 by almost 897,619 m/tonnes.
This suggests, therefore, that plantain is a major diet for the majority of Ghana's population whose cultivation on a mass scale needs serious attention. In countries such as La Cote d'Ivoire, Barbados, Jamaica and South Africa, plantain, a plant species from the banana family, is cultivated on large plantations.
In the Caribbean, for example, the emphasis is more on the cultivation of banana for exports. Along the banks of the lower Volta in Ghana, irrigations have enabled the cultivation of banana for both export and local consumption in the country, making Ghana one of the few countries in West Africa where bananas on a large scale are exported. Possibly plantain could also be cultivated on a large scale in the country for both local consumption and for export.
Primarily, plantain is best used for the preparation of "fufu" by most communities in southern Ghana where it is boiled and pounded with pestle in a mortar. This process of preparation is labour-intensive and always requires two people, someone to pound and another to stir in its preparation. There have been attempts in the past to introduce technology to reduce the labor involved in the preparation of "fufu," for example, but some of these innovations have not been successful because of the tradition being held by majority of Ghanaians that "fufu" is best eaten when pounded.
To enable us meet the food needs of a growing population we would have to move away from certain traditions that hold us back in terms of conserving time, energy and the drudgery in domestic food preparation that our women go through each day in order to provide food for the family. In the case of "fufu" preparation, for example, plantain, cassava and yam could easily be dehydrated into flour and prepared the way "banku" is done.
In Europe and the United States where laws do not permit noise making, most Ghanaians living in flats have no option than to adapt to the eating of "fufu" prepared with plantain and cassava flour. Interestingly, majority of the men help out in preparing the "fufu" whilst their wives prepare the soup.
However, this is not the case back home. Yet if Ghanaians outside can adapt and switch to "fufu" prepared from plantain and cassava flour why can't we do the same back home? At least we can save our forest from the excessive demand for trees used for pestle and mortar in the pounding of "fufu." At an average of five persons to a pestle used for the preparation of "fufu" annually, Ghana loses in my estimation over 3.7 million pieces of certain species of wood to the enjoyment of "fufu" alone.
The current traditional preparation of "fufu" has a negative impact not only on women, but on the environment and building structures such as flats as well. A call for change in the way "fufu" is prepared is the only way out by which some of these issues could be addressed. Added to the call for an "agriculture revolution" in the country, a special "plantain initiative" is also being proposed to the government for presidential consideration to be debated nationwide as has been done with the cassava project.
In order to be food sufficient, the yield of plantain, for example, must increase from the present levels of 1.9 million m/tonnes to about 3.8 million m/tonnes so that the country would not have to rely on La Cote d'Ivoire for imports. Excess plantain produced could be processed, an opportunity for agro-based industries in rural areas, into plantain flour, which then can be stored and the excess exported to other parts of the world.
Few industries over the years have been involved in processing and pioneering the use of plantain and cassava flour for preparation of "fufu" locally but to date they have not been able to penetrate the Ghanaian market due mainly to the cost of "fufu" flour on the market for the average home.
However, with overproduction and processing of excess plantain into flour that can substitute for fresh plantain in the preparation of "fufu" when the season is gone, the country can then be getting closer to overcoming the perennial food shortage in the country.
Our ability to process and store excess food (a mark for development) would enable us feed a growing population and create wealth for our farmers.
It is only by this that the vision of the President of the Republic expressed in his sessional address to parliament in 2001 as "to eat what we grow and can what we cannot" would be realised.

Original_Search_Title: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Headline: Nigeria: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Body_Text:
Lagos — She may never be able to put behind her for the rest of her life the gruesome death of her 25-year old daughter six years ago from the bullets of a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi (Force No 110279) at their village of Uokha, Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State.
Frail-looking Madam Olohigbe Jegede, 68, who has remained heavily weighed down by that unexpected sad loss of her fashion designer daughter, Miss Blessing Amore, in September 1996, said as she recalled the incident amidst tears to City Diary recently : "Police Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, without cause, shot and killed my daughter in company of three other policemen detailed to our village, Uokha, to investigate and arrest a lad who allegedly beat another to death while they were fighting.
"We were not living in the house where the incident happened. He shot my daughter, killed her, threw her corpse into their vehicle and drove away without even allowing me to see it. Till date, I have not set my eyes on her corpse. We were told that the corpse was deposited at the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, near Ekpoma, we visited Auchi, Afuze and Benin City several times, demanding for her corpse for proper burial, its six years now since she was killed, the police have refused to release her corpse to us. they are not giving any explanations for it either. But we have continued to ask that the police release the corpse to us so that we can give her a proper burial. The corpse is not seen. The policeman involved has long been transferred away from the Afuze Police Division he was when he committed the offence, so is the Divisional Police Officer (DPO) then, Mr. Kenene Onyire, a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP)".
"If only I had known that Blessing would not return home," the aging woman, who said she had separated from the late fashion designer's father, continued: "I would not have allowed her to go and fix her hair that day. We had just finished frying garri and she told me she was going to fix her hair. I told her to return quickly as I was going to put yam on fire for pounded yam.
"The next thing I heard were cries, and shouts of they have killed the girl, they have killed the girl. Of course, I never thought that the person in question was my daughter and I have no reason to think other wise, convinced that she was were she was fixing her hair until somebody ran to me to say that the person he saw on the ground looked like her. I threw away what I was doing, ran to the place but the policemen threw the corpse into their vehicle before I got there. I tried to catch a glimpse of the body but they would not allow me. They drove everybody away from the scene and sped off with the corpse and a bleeding young man they shot along with her.
"Then, we began running around their offices from Afuze to the Area Command Headquarters, Auchi, as well as the State Command Headquarters, Benin City. All through, they did not allow me to see her corpse, I am not even sure they carried out any autopsy on it.
"They also did not take any concrete action aganist the policeman who shot her. Each time we asked them what they were doing about the case, they would tell us that they were handling it their own way. The next thing was that they transferred the man and till date, we never heard anything about the matter again neither do we know where the culprit is or what has happened to him".
As the account had it, sergeant Nnamdi, who was attached to the Afuze Divisional Headquarters of the Edo State Police Command then, was detailed along with three other policemen to effect the arrest of a young boy who had beaten another child to death.
The two kids were said to have fought on September 13, 1996 and one of them collapsed in the course of the fight and eventually died on September 23.
The policemen arrived the town at about 4.00pm on that fateful day, moved to the house of the other kid, who allegedly fought with the dead one to effect his arrest but met the house deserted. They then embarked on indiscriminate arrest of people in the area, relying on what they termed magistrate courts rule 2, which empowers the police to arrest as many suspects as possible to a crime committed to make it possible for the real suspect to be arrested. The entire area was said to have been thown into confusion, with people fleeing in all direction to escape being arrested.
Amidst that confusion, the late Blessing Amore was said to be returning from where she went to fix her hair, unaware of what was going on.
Sergent Nnamdi reportedly halted her, pointed his service pistol at her while ordering her to go into their vehicle. The late Miss Amore was said to have quietly obeyed but the unexpected happened. As she was trying to climb into the police van, Nnamdi's gun went off and hit the lady at her side. The bullet was said to have tore through her, uprooted her breast before hitting and wounding a young man, Friday Ohikhena, who was already in the vehicle. Miss Amore fell and died instantly, soaked in her pool of blood to set the community in uproar. The policemen reportedly fired several shots into the air to scare away the gathering crowd, picked up the body, threw it into the van to join the injured young man and sped away with it.
On the advice of the then DPO of the Afuze Police Divisional Headquarters, Mr. Kanene Onyire, the policemen were said to have taken the the corpse and the injured Ohikhena to the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, for deposit in the mortuary and treatment respectively.

Headline: Ghana: AMA Gives Squatters 2 Weeks
Body_Text:
The Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA) has given squatters living in the Korle Lagoon Project area two weeks to vacate the place, particularly Sodom and Gomorrah, Konkomba Yam Market, Onion Market and Galloway because the Government is losing $74,000 dailyfor not making the area free for the project to continue.
Mr. Solomon Darko, the AMA Chief Executive gave this ultimatum in Accra yesterday. He said the second phase of the project should have continued just after the completion of the first phase but the squatters failure to leave the place has impeded the project and the government is losing huge sums of money due to the contract agreement it has with the donor partner.
Mr. Darko said the project area has become a haven for criminals, unacceptable environmental sanitation practices and the squatters have been removing the stones and gravel from the dykes and drains constructed under the first phase of the project.
He said the lagoon has been subjected to environmental abuse by industries and human activities. "In order to find a solution to the problem, the AMA therefore seeks the co-operation of all squatters in ensuring that the areas mentioned are made free from any human habitation and any other activities likely to obstruct the early completion of all the projects," he said.
Mr. Darko said if the squatters fail to comply with the directive, a special task will come in to demolish the buildings on the project site.
He said an AMA committee negotiated with the squatters who are engaged in petty economic activities to be relocated to another area and those who may want to go back to their various homes would be provided buses for their journey.

Original_Search_Title: 800 Soldiers Keep Peace in Taraba - SSG
Headline: Nigeria: 800 Soldiers Keep Peace in Taraba - SSG
Body_Text:
Lagos — A total of 800 soldiers were deployed to keep the peace in warring communities in Taraba, the Secretary to the State Government (SSG), Alhaji Jika Njobdi, said.
Njobdi told newsmen in Jalingo at the weekend after presenting relief materials to victims of the crises between Shomoh and Jaule communities in Lau local government of Taraba, that the soldiers had, however, started returning to their base.
The Shomoh and Jaule tribes in February this year engaged in a hot fight over the ownership of a fish pond which was the major source of their livelihood during the dry season.
The SSG said that 500 soldiers were deployed to keep the peace during the Tiv-Jukun/Fulani crisis, while another 300 were sent to Sardauna local government following the outbreak of hostilities between farmers and Fulani herdsmen in the Mambilla Plateau on Jan. 1.
He explained that the federal government granted the state government N100 million and relief materials worth N200 million to enable it resettle victims of the crises in the various parts of the state.
He said that the soldiers, who had been in the areas for the past two months were being paid their daily allowances and maintenance of their vehicles from the amount.
According to him, part of the N100 million was used to buy relief materials such as maize and yam seedlings and cassava cuttings to be distributed to the victims to enable them produce food crops.
He, however, said that the state government would run into problems over the soldiers' pay for the next two months, as the remaining money from the N100 million would not be enough to pay them and for maintenance.
He said that the Jaule and Shomoh communities got N1 million worth of relief materials each and urged the communities to live in peace and shun violence.

Original_Search_Title: New Yam Festival Among the Igbo
Headline: Nigeria: New Yam Festival Among the Igbo
Body_Text:
Yam is a crawling plant of the genus discorea family and the tuber is called 'Ji' in Igbo. Yam has at least five species with varying colours; Ji-Aga, Ji-Ocha, Ji-Oyibo, Ji-abana or Ji-Mmiri, Afu Aracha Nma (a variety of Ji-Abiyi).
The majority of the species are indigenous to Black Africa; others are certainly foreign and the Ji-Oyibo is a very good example.
In Igbo land, there is great significance attached to yam to the extent that the coming of, New Yam season is being celebrated in the form of a festival.
Traditionally, the new yam festival is an occasion of merriment to celebrate the beginning of a new life, the resurrection of a crop mutilated initially and buried in the process of which it decomposes or rots, and through a process of metamorphosis springs again to life with no traces of the fortunes inflicted on it at the start.
The second fold is an occasion to express gratitude and thanks, to the goddess of earth in whose bosom, yam sojourns, matures, and reappears as food for consumption. Such reappearance signals in Igbo land an end to hunger, want and even disease.
In recent times, the New Yam festival, like the Christmas festival, has become an annual occasion of home-coming among the Igbos living abroad. They return home not only to celebrate, but also to take part in their town meetings where important decisions are often taken, including projects for the development of their fatherland.
Prof. (Chief) Matiu Nnoruka, the Ezenwenyi of Ihembosi sees the yam festival as an occasion where Igbos are reminded of their roots, their culture and world view. "The Igbo man's respect for the crop is legendary. In the past, wealth was measured by the number of yam tubers a farmer could produce. Rich yam farmers were honoured with befitting titles."
Eze (Dr.) A.D. Okafor, Asiwaju Eze Igbo, of Kwara state traces the concept of the New yam festival to mean a mouth that marks the conquest of hunger with the advent of new yam.
"Yam is highly regarded and revered as the prime crop of the Igboman. It is the promoter of Unity among the community members as well as a wish to share in common, the blessing of the harvest," Okafor said.
He said, "the celebration of our cultural heritage is an imperative necessity because a people that allows its culture to perish would have lost its soul."
In Igboland, communities have various ways of announcing the celebration of new yam. In some areas, it is the task of the custodians of tradition to name the date, preferably, a market day for the celebration.
However in others, the appearance of particular masquerades in a market place is an indication that the feast is at hand.
In Ihembosi, it is the oldest man (Aka-Ji-Ofor) whose duty it is to arrange and announce for all traditional festivals. Nne Orie market day is always the date. Before the new yam festival proper, however, there are two important festivities meant to be its fore-runners: Asara Uhejioku and Ukwu N'Ije
Asara festival is celebrated to honour in general, the planting season, whereas Asara Uhejioku is celebrated to honour the growing yam crop, and to pray to the goddess of earth (Ana) for a bounteous harvest and a hitch-free planting season. A full grown cock, kolanuts and native ground chalk (Nzu), the farmer goes to Ana shrine located in the farm, offers prayers and slaughters the cock whose blood and feathers he sprinkles on his Ofor, and the tender palm leaves used to decorate the Shrine.
New yam festival comes up four days after Ukwu J'Ije festivity. The eve is marked by a general clean-up which is the traditional environmental sanitation exercise. Kitchen utensils are washed, a gesture that symbolises the end of famine (Ugani) which is associated with hunger.
One spectacular event is that like Kolanut, Yam is a sacred crop surrounded by taboos. The origin of such respect is unknown and it is an abomination to steal yam in Igbo land.
According to Eze A.D Okafor, "the person caught doing this act is automatically ostracised until the gods and goddesses are appeased."
In some parts of the land, not every one celebrates the festival on the same day. In Ihembosi, the Ogbus Ehis and Ezenwayi's celebrate theirs some days later.
Young people pay their homage to the elders with tubers of yam and parts of the chicken offered to Ana.

Headline: Ghana: 'Show Love to HIV/Aids Victims'
Body_Text:
FAMILIES OF people living with HIV/AIDS have been urged to show love and care to the patients as this would improve their quality of life.
The appeal was made by the chairperson of the Koforidua Association for Women Empowerment (KAWE) when they made a presentation to an HIV/AIDS patient they adopted at Asamang Tanfoe, last week, in the Atiwa constituency.
Items presented include sugar, rice, cooking oil, tubers of yam, soap, vitamin-enriched drugs and ¢200,000 cash.
Presenting the items to a close family member of the patient, the chairperson of KAWE, Mrs. Joana Asante Ansong, advised the public to join the campaign against HIV/AIDS and help reduce the stigma attached to the disease in the Eastern Region and the country as a whole.
She appealed to churches, traditional authorities and resource persons to assist the association to train more peer educators to improve the lives of HIV/AIDS patients and their immediate dependants.
The family member who received the items on behalf of the patient (name withheld for obvious reasons), thanked the association for the kind gesture and called on other groups to emulate KAWE's example.
The family member who was touched by KAWE's presentation assured all that she would take good care of the patient and asked for God's guidance to do so.

Headline: Nigeria: Egwu Woos Investors to Ebonyi
Body_Text:
Enugu — Committed to create rapid economic development in the state, the Ebonyi State Governor, Dr Sam Egwu, has said that his administration has resolved to be private sector friendly in developing and tapping abundant natural resources for rapid industrial development and economic growth of the young state.
He said the state is endowed with a lot of mineral deposits and good investment climate, adding that the mineral resources available in the state include zinc and lead, brime, limestone, kaolin, gypsum, chalk, false gold, baryte, copper, aluminium, uranium, phosphate, lignite and crude oil.
Speaking during the Ebonyi State Special Day at the just concluded 13th Enugu International Trade Fair, Dr. Egwu announced that it was as a result of aggressive economic development of the state that made the government entered into negotiation with a Chinese firm for the establishment of cement factory at Amofia Ngbo in Ngbo East local government area of the state.
He, therefore, called on genuine and interested partners to approach the state for negotiation in any other area of solid mineral development, while assuring the entire business community of governments' hospitality and conducive business environment.
The governor who was at the trade fair along with his deputy and the state Commissioner for Commerce, highlighted his governmet's interest in the development of agriculture in the state, adding that the state was blessed with fertile soil suitable for all forms of agricultural products.
Agricultural products such as rice, yam, cassava, maize, apple, pepper, okro, tomatoes and vegetable are grown in commercial quantity in the state.
"Our people from the colonial times," Dr Egwu said, "are noted for their commercial rice production and the Abakaliki Rice Mill Industry, is the best and largest in the south east geo-political zone."
The product of the industry, the rice husk, he continued, is another rich raw material for production of ceiling boards, flush doors, fertilisers and papers, saying interested investors in these areas had bright opportunities to make business with the government.
According to the governor, another area Ebonyi State had great prospects for investment was in tourism where good natural endowment for tourism development scattered throughout the state.
He said that the entire part of Ebonyi South, Afikpo North, Afikpo South, Amoha, Ubeyi, Edda and Ivo local government areas were very rich tourism sites, while the Ndibe and Ozziza beaches and the Akpoha structural rock beach at Amoha Local Government were just nature-made scenery.
The governor said that in order to create conducive atmosphere, his government had commenced the construction of a five-star hotel named "The Abakaliki International Hotel Limited" at Mile 50 Abakaliki, which is nearing completion.
In his remarks, the Commissioner of Commerce in the state, Chief S.N. Nwambe called on Chambers of Commerce and the business community to feel free to come to the state and do business as well as invest in the state's abundant mineral and agricultural resources.

Headline: Uganda: Landslides Hit Mbale, Sironko
Body_Text:
Landslides have hit several areas in Mbale and Sironko districts during the Tuesday 10-hour heavy rains, destroying homes, animals and crops.
The downpour, that started at 7:00pm and went on throughout the night, greatly affected Wanale, Busano Bufumbo and Busano sub-counties in Mbale.
In Sironko, Bulungaya, Bumasifa and Zesui sub-counties were also affected.
Wanale LC3 chairman Abdu nabuyobo said carrot, maize, potatoe and bean gardens were swept down the valleys.
"We can now see famine knocking on our doors," Nabuyobo said.
In Bulungaya and Zesui eight houses had their roofs blown off.
In Mbale town, people watching the floods on Nabuyonga bridge, said they saw three goats floating on the river. Several people were seen gathering bananas, yams and other crops that had been brought down from the mountains.
Namakwekwe market was also flooded. Aida nambozo, a trader in the market, said many of their goods were swept away. She blamed the market managers for the poor drainage system. Ends

Headline: Uganda: Gazette Nakivubo Swamp
Body_Text:
THE MINISTER of State for the Environment has warned that continuing encroachment on the Nakivubo wetland is threatening the health of Kampala residents.
The wetland has shrunk from 4.8 square kilometres to 1.3 square kilometres. Yet even this remaining area is under assault from slum dwellings, KCC plot allocations, and the new Nakivubo channel that has brought the point of discharge closer to Lake Victoria.
Matters have been made worse by illegal cultivation of yams as they cannot retain water like papyrus.
The Nakivubo or Bugolobi swamp filters all the sewage from National Water and Sewage Corporation as well as receiving often-polluted drainage from Kampala's industry and road run-off.
The decline of Nakivubo swamp could eventually be very costly to Kampala. Already the NWSC is saying that an additional $10 million per year in chemicals will be needed to purify Kampala's drinking water because of the reduction in size of the swamp.
However all is not lost and it is not too late to rectify matters.
The Ministry of Lands, Water and Environment are making an excellent start by preparing to declare the swamp a protected area. However the legal instrument gazetting the swamp must include all areas of existing illegal encroachment.
The ministry must go one step further and organise for the Nakivubo channel to discharge through multiple reticulated outlets into the swamp. This will reduce the pressure on the swamp and make it easier for the swamp to absorb and retain water. The cost will be minimal compared to the annual extra cost of chemicals for NWSC if the modifications are not made.

Headline: Nigeria: Killing for the Gods
Body_Text:
A community in Igboland still sacrifices human beings to appease the
gods, long years after the tradition was outlawed in Nigeria
Neke in Isiuzo Local Government area of Enugu State is a town caught
inextricably in a time warp. The twilight town crier is still at
work, announcing the Igwe's instructions, the birth of a baby, the
outbreak of an epidemic and the death of an ozo title holder.
Different family compounds are separated by bushes,vegetable farms,
yam plots, oil palms,big trees and ancient groves. On the ancient
trees, the roots of which have been rendered bare by years of sheet
and gully erosions, bats and owls, hideous birds which can turn their
necks 360 degrees, flutter and howl to herald some dangerous
forebodings. In the market square, village elders, commonly referred
to as the Indichies with wrinkled skins, decayed teeth and bald
pates, still gather to deliberate on the goings-on in the
communities. With its history, flora and fauna, Neke strikes a
visitor as the fictional Umofia itself lifting out of Chinua Achebe's
Things Fall Apart.
But there is more to Neke than some romantic reminiscences. The town
has idols notoriously called Ezugu and Odo, the gods of war which are
still appeased by their worshippers, with the blood of human beings.
At the last count, 32 skulls have been recovered by the police. The
discovery was made recently by a group of Christians who also
reported the matter to the police. The idol worshippers were, on 19
December last year, dragged to the Ikem magistrate court, presided
over by Mr. Donatus Alumona.
They were: Odoh Okpara, Patrick Eze, Golden Odoh, Ezekiel Nnamane,
Gilbert Odoh, Matthew Odo, Paul Agbo, Veronica Nnamani, Stephen Odo,
Onyeme Eze, Isingene Nnamani, Catherine Ngwo, Ugwu Agbu, Simon Ogbo,
Daniel Odoh and Robert Agbo. Others were John Odoh, Ede Odoh, Peter
Ogbudo, Odoh Nnaji, Alexander Udonu, James Odoh, Agbo Eze, Christians
Odoh, Amechi Eze, Ezekiel Ugwu, Stephen Ugwu, Erasmus Ogene, Bernard
Okeze, Sunday Nnaji, Ogbodo Odoh, Odoh Agbu Eze and Ogbodo Nwodo. The
Police, charged them with "unlawful possession of fresh human skulls
and thereby committing an offence punishable under section 495 (a) of
the criminal code, cap 36, vol. I, laws of Anambra State of Nigeria
1986 as applicable in Enugu State."
There is, however, a new twist to the entire saga as the same police
force, has charged 21 of the Christians who made the discovery to
court. They are: Reverend Fathers Chibundu Omeje, Fr Christian Eze,
Chijioke Okwor, John Aguma, Chinwenba Okoro, Ebenezer Ugwu, Anselem
Nnamani, Nebechi Ojinko, Ugwumba Celestine, Nnamachi Romanus and Agbo
Valentine Friday. Others were Emilia Agbo, Chime Njeze, Odoh Gabriel,
Nkechi Eze Agbo, Rachael Ogele, Calista Ogene, Melitus Nnaji, Raphael
Odho, Sylvanus Abaru and Nnamani Innocent.
The Police accused them of maliciously damaging the Odo shrine of
Aneke family traditional worship and the residence of Ani Nwogene,
the Onyishi (Chief priest) of Odo shrine, valued at N2.5 million. The
offence, according to the police, is punishable under section 415 (2)
(a), (b) of the criminal code cap 36 vol.1, laws of Anambra State of
Nigeria 1986 as applicable in Enugu State.
