The Christians are also standing trial for allegedly stealing
N50,000, two caps, two native bead ornaments, traditional royal bell,
one smaller meditation bell, tail of a cow, two iron walking sticks,
and two cow skins, valued at N100,000( punishable under section
353(12) of the criminal code). Worse still, the police charged the
Christians for beating up Ani Nwogene, the 80-year old blind priest
of Odo, an offence punishable under section 252 of the criminal code,
cap. 36 vol. 1, laws of Anambra State as applicable in Enugu State.
The last hearing of the case was 11 March 2002. The Christians are
being defended by Barrister F.I.N. Ngwu.
Trouble started last year when Chiefs P.Y.O. Agbo, D.O. Obeta, leader
of the Iwenede Age Grade and regent of Neke, respectively, issued a
public notice that all indigenes and visitors should be aware of the
Mgbamike (Odo) festival, which marks the end of the Odo feast. The
elders warned the people to be aware of 14, 15 and 16 February 2001
and, therefore, should "schedule their activities not to conflict
with these dates."
A reaction however came from the Isi-Uzo local government chairman's
(Mr. Osita Ogene's) office. Ugwu Moses, the local government
secretary, wrote a letter on behalf of the chief executive to the
divisional police officer about the undated public notice. The local
government chairman, according to Ugwu, was unhappy about the threat
to free movement, educational and religious activities for three days
(14 to 16 February 2001) as a result of the festival. "There are no
provisions in the constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria for
any group of people to hold to hostage, the rest of mankind as a
result of its cultural peculiarity," the chairman argued. He
requested that the DPO should ensure that normal social, religious
economic, educational and other life activities are carried on at
Neke community and its environs throughout the period under
reference.
The chairman directed the education secretary, including his heads of
sections and Christian religious leaders, to ensure that their normal
duties are carried out without fear of molestation. Moreover, Ogene
advised the officials and the priests to report without delay to the
police any threat of any sort in the course of discharging their
normal duties.
Emboldened by the chairman's stand, as communicated through the
education secretary at the local level, Mr. Peter Mba, a teacher and
a native of Neke, went to school but met the school deserted. Blank
seats and chalkboards stared him in the face. Everyone, in deference
to the Odo cult, had stayed away. On his way back home, he was
attacked by a group of Odo masquerades and beaten to a pulp. Mr.
Simon Eke of Umu - Egwu village was assaulted by a horde of Ujueme
(junior Odo devotees) extremists who broke into this house and looted
his belongings. Elder Samuel Ugwueze was also attacked by the same
gang at Onueme, mercilessly beaten and his house was also looted. His
offence was that his daughter, a catholic, was among other Christians
who sang that Odo, the Neke god is man-made.
The same marauding band assaulted three Christian girls from
Umu-Egbe, Aneke and Umu-Ona for refusing to bow to the cult. Mr.
Felix Odo of Umu-Ugwu village, a retired University of Nigeria staff
member, was given the same treatment by the same group. The Ujueme
group drove away women (Christians and non-Christians) from their
kitchens and forcefully removed their food and meat at gun-point.
Ujueme age grade even dared the government last year when, because of
threats, the Enugu State Primary Education Board (SPEB) could not
commission the school it rehabilitated at Ugwu-Akparata Neke because
it fell within the festival period .
The bubble burst on 26 May 2001 when, during a crusade in Neke by
Christians, Odo converts confessed that human skulls were still used
for the Odo and Ezugu rituals and initiations. The Christians alerted
the police who swooped on the shrines and recovered 32 skulls. The
Christian leaders who triggered off the discovery were Reverend
Christian Eze of the St. Stephens Anglican Church; Reverend Father
Chibundun Omeje of St. Patrick's Catholic Church and Reverend J.E.
Igwe of the Assemblies of God Church.
The police discovered two fresh skulls in September and two others in
November 2001. A police sergeant, Emma Iko, serving in the local
government council was shocked to the bone marrow. He was among a
team of policemen who recovered the latest skulls (the two decaying
ones). "Nnaa, na waa o, di world sef; dat day, de thing jus dey
smell", the cop lamented, adding, "I no fit chop that day."
Given the new twist in the case, Barrister Ngwu, who defended the
Christians in court, wrote a letter entitled "A call for
Intervention" dated 18 March 2002, to Governor Chimaroke Nnamani of
Enugu State. Ngwu accused the police of double standard, and one John
C. Nnaji, a financier of Odo cult and its atrocities, of making
frantic efforts to kill the case "to ensure that those Christians who
opposed his group were falsely accused and prosecuted." Ngwu
complained to the governor that although Nnaji is the godfather of
the Odo devotees, he was never investigated with his team, "their
team was treated as sacred cows by the police."
Barrister Ugwu, therefore, wanted the governor to intervene by
instituting a commission of inquiry on the activities of Odo and
Ezugu Neke; proscribe all harmful practices of devotees of the
deities, and take whatever steps to protect the lives and property of
Neke indigenes.
In Neke, only strangers are sacrified to the deities. That accounts
for cases of disappearances in the area. For example, Moses Peter, an
indigene of Rivers State, took off from Lagos in January 1998 to Neke
. Peters, 31, had come to visit, for the first time, the family of
his fiancee, Franca, an indigene of Neke community. His mission was
to indicate his interest to Franca's family that he would like to
marry their daughter and to pick a date for other marriage rituals.
Franca and, of course, members of her family waited for Peters who
never turned up.
Back in Lagos, Franca was poised for a showdown with her heart-throb,
but Peters' relations insisted that their brother had travelled down
to Franca's village to see her (Franca). The following day, Franca
dashed back to Neke, but her search for Peters was fruitless. It was
months after that she discovered that Peters, on entering Neke, was
captured by a skull hunter who beheaded and sacrificed his skull to
Ezugu, one of the blood sucking deities in the community .Obviously
knocked out by the incident, heart-rent Franca, today, walks the
streets of Lagos insane.
Stories of this kind have been variously narrated by families of
unfortunate visitors to Neke who never returned till date. The fate
of these victims who, in most cases, have been regarded as missing
persons came to the fore with recent discoveries of human skulls in
shrines in Neke.
Neke, a serene and an agrarian community in the present Enugu State,
had, right from primordial times, been noted for human sacrifice.
This is sequel to the existence of a blood sucking god. The deity,
TheNEWS gathered, is represented by a masquerade called Odo. The Odo
masquerade which appears in public once in two years has several
shrines which include: Amanyi, Ugwu Ota Egbu, Okurenkume Esitowere
and Omego. The second blood sucking deity called Ezugu is said to be
the most dreaded and potent of the affiliate of the Odo. Almost every
male adult of the community is initiated into the masquerade cult.
The Odo deity and its various shrines located in a thick forest in
Neke are a study in mystery. Ghostly and esoteric, the Odo and Ezugu
deities cut the picture of a powerful god bestirring a passive
society, issuing statutes and ordinances to the people. The deities
do not like noise and all interactions are conducted in low voices
and whispers. All structures located around the shrine and deities in
Neke are decorated with human skulls. And, of course worshippers of
the deities and the shrines hardly do anything without reference to
human skulls. From sitting down to dancing and drinking water or palm
wine, especially during the deity festivals, human skulls are used by
the devotees.
Evangelist Joseph Agbo, an indigene of the community, says he cannot
even hazard a guess of the number of human skulls that have, over
time, come into Neke deities and shrines. "It will be like asking me
to count the number of sand by the sea shore", Agbo said. He,
however, asserts that the skulls will run into several thousands.
The people have a dance called Ikpa Ezugu which only indigenes who
have procured human skulls participate in. The Ikpa, a heroic dance,
takes place once every two years during the outing of the Odo
masquerade. During the Odo outing of last year, many thirty-year old
youths and above participated in the Ikpa dance. "This simply means
that they have acquired human skulls and killed people in cold
blood", the Evangelist lamented in an interview with TheNEWS. In
fact, it was learnt that about 17 youths in the mentioned age
brackets participated in the last Ikpa dance. Seventeen human skulls
by implication, would have been used in the process.
No deity or shrine in Neke admits the skull of an indigene of the
community. As a result, aspiring initiates comb neighbouring
communities for human heads. There have been instances whereby such
aspiring indigenes living in the urban area lured unsuspecting
friends to their community only to behead them for the purposes of
procuring their skulls.
A member of the Odo cult is authorised to possess as many human
skulls as he has the ability to procure. In fact, acquisition of
human skulls is a status symbol in Neke. A man's prestige and
reputation are measured by the number of human skulls he has procured
and deposited at the Ezugu or Odo deity. One of the priests of Odo
deity who has now gone blind was said to be in the habit of
sacrificing a human skull to the Ezugu deity each time any of his
numerous wives had a baby. Indeed, procurement of human skulls is big
business in Neke.
According to one of the devotees who pleaded anonymity, human skulls
are, often times, exported to other communities in need by the
custodians of the deity. These skulls are said to be excess skulls
whose owners have died. He further disclosed that a human skull
market once existed in Neke. "The market still exists, but secretly",
he retorts.
Besides this, the people of Neke are widely believed to serve as
mercenaries in procurement of human skulls for communities in need of
them (the skulls) for ritual practices such as burial ceremonies of
prominent indigenes. Such burials in some parts of Igbo land require
human heads, and Neke people, proficient in the art, are reportedly
invited to comb forests for human heads. As the Neke human head
mercenaries work for their clients, they equally use the opportunity
to procure their own human skulls for enhancement of their individual
status in Neke. This practice, according to a one time devotee of Odo
deity, is still on, but with discretion.
It was learnt that this practice of procuring human skulls for status
in Neke dates back about 400 years ago. Neke people were said to be
warriors before colonial rule. They fought a lot of wars with their
neighbours, beheaded their prisoners of war, and sacrificed their
skulls to the gods. During the colonial era, they relished in
beheading white men and women who ventured into their community.
While heads of male victims are deposited at Ezugu, those of female
victims are sacrificed to the Ase shrine.
Non-initiates and women suffer a lot of ordeals during the outing of
the Odo masquerade which is believed to be the most dreaded in
Igboland.
During the period, no woman comes out in the night without a man
accompanying her. For the three days, the outing lasts which also
coincides with a feast of the deity called Mgbadike, no woman,
stranger or child ventures into the streets or goes to market, school
or any social gathering. In fact, all social and religious activities
are outlawed in Neke.
Furthermore, it is a taboo to make any noise during an Odo festival
or switch on security lights. This, it was gathered, is because the
deity does not like both.
The scorpion is considered to be a son of the Odo deity. Any woman
who kills it is made to bury it like a human being to propitiate the
deity. Such burial is performed with a wrapper and a fowl amongst
other things. The woman is, of course, visited with instant death if
she fails to abide by this cultural practice.
The Odo masquerade, believed to be a spirit, is prepared with the
yellowish inner part of palm fronds. Due to the reverence the
masquerade commands, any initiate who discloses any of its secrets,
for instance, saying that it is not a spirit, is killed instantly. On
the other hand, any woman who, advertently or inadvertently, sees the
Odo devotees cutting these palm fronds so as to use it to prepare the
Odo, will be killed by the devotees who will tie the yellow leaf
called "Omu' around her and announce to the community that Odo has
killed her.
Every Odo year, when the masquerade embarks on its routine outing,
especially in February as this magazine gathered, the number of
missing persons in all the nook and cranny of Igboland increases.
Strangers who stray into areas being combed by head hunters from Neke
also swell the list of missing persons.
Osita Ogene, Chairman, Isiuzo Local Government Council, also
introduced another dimension to the cultural crisis by contending
that the skulls could not have been those of humans. "It has not been
proved that those skulls were those of humans", Ogene told TheNEWS in
an interview.
Whose skulls could they have been?
Ogene answers: "... What I am saying is that we need experts to come
into this matter and tell us what it is."
However, Ogene's doubts over the identity of the skulls seem
unfounded as police investigations before the arraignment of culprits
proved that the skulls were those of humans. Moreover, some of the
culprits had reportedly confessed to the police that the skulls
belonged to human beings.
Even an earlier claim by the deity worshippers that the 30 skulls
were those of humans killed by their forefathers in inter tribal wars
during the ancient period was punctured by the police recovery, 7
November, of additional two decaying skulls at the deity. "If the
deity devotees claimed that they inherited the 30 human skulls from
their forefathers, what of the two fresh ones?" Vince Eze, a member
of the community queries.
For the Christians fighting to abolish the use of human skulls for
diety worship in Neke, it has been a tortuous and an uphill task.
According to Evangelist Agbo, many Christians have been killed in the
process while death threats hang on the necks of many. Ngwu, on his
own part, complains of spiritual attacks and threats of his life.
Neither of the two parties is relenting in the war. The situation
seems dicey, as a bloody future seems inevitable for the community.
Can this be averted?"

Headline: Uganda: City Faces Health Hazard - Miyingo
Body_Text:
THE Minister of State for Environment, Dr. Kezimbira Miyingo, has warned that Kampala's population faces health hazards if encroachment on the Nakivubo wetlands is not halted.
Speaking at the official launching of efforts to gazette Nakivubo wetland as a protected area on Friday, Miyingo said the wetland purifies effluents.
Nakivubo treats sewerage from the National Water and Sewerage Corporation and wastes discharged by industries.
But the Nakivubo wetland, which connects Kampala city to the inner Murchison Bay, a water source for Kampala, is threatened by drainage for agriculture, housing and industrialisation.
The wetland was 4.8 square km but has reduced to less than 1.3 square km.
Paul Mafabi, the assistant commissioner of wetlands inspection division, said a legal instrument was being prepared for the wetland to be protected.
"The Minister of Water, Lands and the Environment will declare the wetlands a protected area," he said.
Miyingo was alarmed by the fact that yams, which don't have the capacity to filter and retain the wastes in the water, have replaced large expanses of papyrus, a vegetation characterising many wetlands.

Headline: Nigeria: Food As Democracy's Future
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is believed that a country's strength is measured by the amount and quality of food that is available on citizens' tables. An organisation that aids farmers in Nigeria to further the objective of attaining self-sufficiency in food production alerts the nation on the steps that should be taken for the country to be able to feed her citizens. Godwin Haruna writes
Former Secretary-General of the Commonwealth, Dr. Emeka Anyaoku eloquently put it in proper perspective when he declared that until there was food security, any talk about entrenching an enduring democratic process in Africa nay Nigeria, was not well grounded. The analysis about securing the stomach first before all other things could not have been more apt because of the often repeated aphorism that "a hungry man is an angry man".
At the crucial stages of the Liberian fratricide in the nineties, one of the rebel warlords, who is incumbent president, Charles Taylor, was said to have resorted to "rice bullets" to prosecute the war. He made considerable advances when he used the food weapon to wage his war. There are also several instances in history when food was used to achieve victories in war situations. These numerous historical instances underscore the importance of food in all human endeavours.
Just recently, while declaring open the 13th Enugu International Trade Fair, President Olusegun Obasanjo observed in a speech delivered on his behalf by Senate President, Anyim Pius Anyim: "It is alarming to see that the nation spent a whopping $877.3 million on importation of food items in year 2000". He added to an obviously shocked audience that the country spends as much as $100 million on importation of fruit juice annually. The president bemoaned the situation whereby a country that was so much endowed with good climatic conditions that could supply the food needs of the world engaged in such spend thrift.
However, in the face of this unsavoury situation, an organisation established solely to ensure that the ugly trend is reversed to pave way for food sufficiency in Nigeria, has taken up the gauntlet. Farm Aids Movement of Nigeria (FAMN) came on stream essentially to boost agricultural produce in the country. It's overall aim, according to the promoters, is to ensure that Nigeria, which God has endowed with enormous green vegetation, could fully tap the resources in such a way that hunger would be banished from the land.
Speaking to THISDAY in an interview, the co-ordinator of FANM, Mr. Charles Anyansi, stated that the outfit was an aid organisation in which farmers are assisted with technical and managerial know-how with the ultimate objective of boosting their productivity level. "We in Farm Nigeria came up with the observation that one of the major problems facing the Nigerian farmer, especially the peasant farmer is the problem of ignorance. He is a man limited in knowledge about agriculture. This is a factor of the oil boom which led to the abandonment of agriculture to the extent that the farmer today is handicapped in the knowledge of new trends in the sector", Anyansi added.
He restated President Obasanjo's description of agriculture as the prime resource of the nation to underscore its importance. He quickly added that in spite of this importance, when a farmer is told that he can rear snails, rabbits and mushroom, he would be completely lost. Anyansi declared that even the goat that is reared on a large scale throughout the country has got its advanced technology which the local farmer is not aware of. He said in giving aid to the farmer, one of his organisation's objectives was also to give a voice to the much neglected Nigerian farmer so that he would become an active part of the world community.
Anyansi made a clear distinction between his organisation and the Farmers' Association of Nigeria: "We are not farmers and we are not an association of farmers. Rather, we are a pool of experts including and mostly, agriculturists in various fields. These are people who have volunteered to help the farmer in updating his knowledge, giving him training and upgrading his capacity in order to increase his productivity. Farm Nigeria is to the agricultural sector what Red Cross is to the health sector. Even though we are much more than that, that is the simplest way to put it".
He added that it is in fulfillment of this that the organisation pioneered snail rearing in the country and as a measure of its acceptability among the populace, there is now an association of snail rearers in Nigeria. Anyansi said his group was in touch with various farmers' associations including the umbrella body headed by Admiral Murtala Nyako (rtd) and they have always asked the leadership to suggest areas of assistance that his group could offer.
Anyansi disclosed that as part of the objective of bringing the Nigerian farmer to international limelight and thereby move him closer to new technological trends in the in the sector, his organisation would organise the Nigerian Farmers' Awards later in the year. Also part of the objective, according to the coordinator, is the attempt to enable the Nigerian farmer reclaim his past glory in the concerted to bring his constraints to the public glare. Alluding to the aphorism, "No farmer, no nation", Anyansi posited that no nation was worth its name if it could not feed its citizenry sufficiently. "We want the Nigerian farmer to have his pride of place by calling national attention to their activities. It is the small-scale farmer that is feeding the nation. We think they should be accorded their pride of place. We will use the awards to boost their morale in their chosen profession so that they would have something to look forward to", he said further.
He said inspite of the fact that president Obasanjo came from the farmers' constituency, there was still a lot of ground yet to be covered by the government in order to benefit a majority of the farmers. The coordinator of FAMN said even though he was not a politician, as someone who was in touch with the real farmer, he was in a position to know if the government has improved their productivity level. He said the neglect of agriculture could be easily gleaned from the annual budget projections.
He expressed his concerns thus: "If you can manage to put up polling booths in the smallest farm settlements so that they can vote, why can't you manage to set up a small relevant shop in that community whereby the farmers could reach you?" He advocated the setting up of farm project centres in every local government where the necessary farm inputs are readily available. "You can not plan agriculture in Nigeria in Abuja and hope to succeed. We are saying that government should decentralise agriculture, make it the primary mandate of every local government agricultural development. Now, Benue State has a comparative advantage in yam production, let it face the production squarely with all the necessary assistance...The people on the ground in these areas know what it takes to increase their output, not the man in Abuja", he stated.

Original_Search_Title: Welcome to the 'City of No Regrets'
Headline: Nigeria: Welcome to the 'City of No Regrets'
Body_Text:
Lagos — A first time visitor to Uyo would be forgiven if he develops a claustrophobic feeling because the compact size of the town is built around a five-road axis namely, Wellington Bassey Way, which terminates at the Governor's Mansion; Ikot-Ekpene road, which is the main thoroughfare into the city; Aka road, Oron road and Abak road, which leads the visitor into the hinterland of the state.
Everything in Uyo revolves around Ibom Connection, which is the centre of the city where all the five major road networks take their roots. Uyo which is the heart of Ibibioland literarily means 'bread' but lately, the city used to be known as the "city of no regrets" has been tagged 'Econet town' by inhabitants because of the substantial investment of the Governor Victor Attah administration in the telecoms sub-sector.
Uyo has evolved from a sleepy civil service town to an urban centre that wears the attendant paraphenalia of an economic hub ala increase in population, traffic jam, increase in cost of living and new investments. The present city centre which spans some ten-kilometre radius houses more than a handful of the so-called new generation banks like Zenith, Intercontinental Bank, FSB, Guaranty Trust Bank and Omega Bank, amongst others. The advent of democracy and the recent campaign for resource control led to a rush of people into Uyo. Barrister Kufre Nkeme, a resident of Uyo had this to say of the evolution of Uyo over the past half a decade " Say five years ago, Uyo could be said to be a sleepy town where only retirees live but over time the recent but unsuccessful resource control campaign had brought about an expectation that if Governor Victor Attah had succeeded in his campaign that a lot of money would come into the state. As such there was a rush by businesses to establish a presence in the state hoping to tap from the gains of a successful resource control campaign".
Nkeme said that the increase in the number of banks is a pointer to the fact that the business environment in the city and the state has greatly improved. "Banks emphasise profits, these are people come into business to make profits so if they know they can't make their money here they won't come to Uyo".
For years the city which is home to the University of Uyo had played second fiddle to Eket which is the operational base of oil companies like Exxon-Mobil, TotalFinaElf and Chevron. Ini-odu Akpan , a marketing executive with one of the new generation banks in Uyo, however, said that the image of Uyo as a satellite of Eket, the Mobil Town where things happen has over the years been eroded because of the improved road network and diverse businesses that have come to town. "Uyo used to be dry in terms of business opportunities but I can say that in the past couple of years that has changed. While Eket still has something to offer, Uyo is the place to be for now". Pastor Mbasekei Ayomobi, an indigene of Cross River State, who has been resident in Uyo for the past six years observed that one factor, which points to the evolution of Uyo from a civil service town to an economic hub was the increase in the Igbo population in the city. "The Igbo man comes into your town to do business so the moment you notice a geometric increase in their number the economy of that area is or has been diversified because they come into town in force with almost all sectors of the economy in full flow". A tour of Uyo confirms Ayomobi's assertions as the major supermarkets, the spare parts business, the luxury bus business and other big entreprises are owned by Ndigbo resident in Uyo. Ndigbo in Uyo have become so indigeniseded that the unsuspecting individual would be shocked to learn that the "ibibio" or "annang" man he has just bought a vehicle spare part from is from Nnewi because of their fluent grasp of the mannerisms of the local populace.
Uyo is the origin of the famous motorcycle mode of transport. The mode, which is widely known as 'okada' in Nigeria has its roots in Uyo where it is variously called aka ukeh (where are you going-Ibibio) or ala lok (will you climb-Oron). In Uyo the aka ukeh with their reckless riding habits are notorious for sending a lot of the city's inhabitants to the orthopedic surgeon's table but don't bother to look for a cab ala Lagos style because they are virtually non-existent for intracity transport. The available cabs are found at the intercity car parks so the ala lok continues to reign as the undisputed champion of intracity transport in Uyo inspite of the wails of its numerous victims.
The social divide in Uyo is exemplified in two housing estates in the city. While the state government owned Ewet Housing Estate, which is situated off Oron road, is a haven for the wealthy with its breath-taking mansions, the Federal Housing Estate on Abak road is a definition of the fast fading middle-income earner and the burgeoning army of low-income residents. Ewet is home to top government functionaries and wealthy indigenes of the state like retired Air Vice-Marshall Nsikak Eduok, who is building a luxury resort there while Abak road Housing is largely occupied by junior civil servants and NYSC members deployed to the state as well as retired civil servants.
The pentecostal fire has also caught Uyo in its grip. No more is Uyo a city of staid christian congregations of the Anglican, Lutheran, Catholic and Methodist denominations, the loud and spiritually aggressive pentecostal churches have swept into the city to make it a safe haven for those indigenes who were scared to come home due to the endemic fetish practices that had plagued the area for centuries. While some Lagos churches like Winners Chapel, Redeemed, TREM and Deeper Life have established modest branches there, Uyo has some homegrown pentecostal ministers of the gospel who have carved a niche for themselves in the preaching and miracle business.
Recreation centres were until a few years ago a rare luxury in Uyo. But today, the city boasts of at least two three-star hotels in Amity Hotels and Legacy Hotels where visitors can unwind.
The cell phone craze has also hit Uyo. Everywhere you go to in Uyo someone is clutching a GSM handset, even the ubiquituos aka uke operators are not left out of the JISMmania as Thislife encountered a couple of them making genuine calls on their handsets
In Uyo the fear of fraudsters and touts is the beginning of wisdom. Fraudsters known locally as utohtoh have left many an unwary stranger in tears. The conmen operate in public places, their modus operandi is to converse in loud tones about a non-existent deal while they wait for any likely prey to show interest. Their activities have led to any cunning traits exhibited by any Ibibio man being reffered to as 'Uyo Wayo'. The touts who are reffered to locally as sai-monkey are the scourge of the city, they are Uyo's version of the popular Lagos area boys.
In terms of social activity, Uyo is quiet. Though the city has its share of hang-outs but it can hardly measure up to the standards found in Nigeria's major cities. One particular type of hang-out which has made Uyo popular over the years has been joints where dog meat which is a local delicacy is prepared for consumption. Besides dog meat consumption, Uyo's outlying environs are famous for the bushmeat delicacy. The town is famous for Afang soup, Afia efere (white soup), Edikangikong, Ekpangku Kwo (coco-yam porridge) and Ayan ekpang.
As urban centre, Uyo has its own share of the redlight district. A trip to Ikpa road, which also houses the University of Uyo, throws up the women of easy virtue. Tony Anichebe, a journalist, told Thislife that to woo a lady in Uyo is one of the easiest things to do. According to him, "the average Ibibio girl is friendly, warm and very accommodating. To get a babe here costs one next to nothing compared to Lagos or Port-Harcourt and if you are lucky and you get a career girl she will spoil you completely that's why men find the ibibio woman irresistible". Eno-obong, an office assistant said that the widely acclaimed juju which the Ibibio woman is said to use to hold unto men is unfounded. "The Ibibio woman nay Uyo woman is trained from birth to take care of her man, to pamper and spoil him. I had this boyfriend from one of the northern states who was married and came to Uyo on transfer. As a lady I was particular about everything that concerns him. I would wash his clothes, iron them, mend them and carefully prepare his meals. Mind you I wasn't doing this because I wanted to snatch him from his wife but that's just the way we are brought up and then you add the delicious meals that are indigenious to this area plus the fact that in matters of intimacy the Uyo/Ibibio woman is a natural expert. What else would a man want?"

Headline: Nigeria: Olashore: Reflections On Years Past
Body_Text:
Lagos — He was in the corporate world and it bubbled with his immense influence and tremendous contributions. For years, his name rang a bell in that sphere of our national life. When destiny's itchy fingers snatched him from the boardroom, many thought that was the end of him in national limelight. How mistaken could they be. Even in the hitherto remote village of Iloko-Ijesa, Osun State, where Oba Oladele Olashore, holds forte as monarch of his beloved people, he still remains in constant limelight and for good measure.
Recently, it was exactly five years since the then Prince Olashore left the commanding heights of the banking industry to assume the throne of his forbears as the Ajagbesusi Ekun, Aloko of Iloko-Ijesa. Ever since that momentous occasion of his ascension to the throne, his community have seen a lot of transformation never before witnessed. The community has moved from one development story to the other and the Oba and his people have been basking in the euphoria of such monumental strides in just few years. That is what has made the fifth anniversary celebrations of the Oba tick with many of his subjects applauding.
Iloko is a small town in the Oriade Local Government Area. It is about nine square kilometres north-east of Ijesa. The town was founded about 1113 A.D. by Ajagbusi-Ekun, a prince of Ile-Ife, who was widely known for his great success in warfare. He reportedly travelled far and wide.
The progenitor, Ajagbusi-Ekun and his party traversed the areas now known as the Yoruba empire until they got to Igbadae (now in Atakumosa Local Government Area), where Ajagunba separated from him. He later founded the Ila-Orangun Kingdom. After a short sojourn at Igbadae, Ajagbusi-Ekun set out in search of a permanent abode.
The people of Iloko are very industrious and development conscious. They are predominantly farmers who grow yams, cocoyams and lately, rice. Iloko is also blessed with some prominent and astute businessmen and women who rank amongst the most successful in Nigeria.
The current modernisation being witnessed in the community was started by the immediate past Oba, who is the father of the incumbent. As soon as Oba Samuel Oyerinde Olashore became king in 1952, he mandated parents to send their children to school. The result of the farsighted decision is the emergence today, of a number of Iloko sons and daughters who are prominent in academics and in various professions of endeavour.
The immediate past Oba also attracted to the traditional council highly experienced and younger crop of chiefs who are very educated and widely travelled. This development reportedly accounted for the good governance the area witnessed for the next seven years following the demise of Oba Samuel Olashore before Oba Oladele Olashore was installed.
In the intervening period, the mantle of leadership of the community fell on High Chief Olusola Ogunsanya, a licensed surveyor, the Orisa-Aloko and second-in-command to the Oba who became the regent. He was ably supported by the Odofin, High Chief Olalemi Owolabi, a businessman based in Maiduguri and the Sabo of Iloko, High Chief Ajewole Ifaloye, also a very successful businessman in Oshogbo and a host of other traditional chiefs.
As soon as Oba Olashore ascended the throne, he converted his numerous contacts to the advantage of his people. In the political sphere, he ensured greater unity among the Iloko people. Not only has Iloko been stamped on the political map of Ijeshaland and Osun State, at the national level its impact was beginning to be felt.
In the area of infrastructural development, many roads were given the face lift to enhance the free and unhindered movement of the people. The Iloko Community Town Hall is just awaiting finishing touches, the community maternity and health facility has been resuscitated.
In the educational sector, not only has the Olashore International School continued to thrive and enjoy a pride of place, Oba Olashore spearheaded the establishment of yet another school - the Iloko Community High School. He did this because a majority of Iloko pupils, especially those bred at home could not pass the highly competitive entrance to the Olashore International School. The fees were too high for some categories of students as well, although he took care of this by setting up an educational endowment to cater to the needs of indigent students.
Even as the community school has taken off, the monarch still solicits the support and contributions of friends and well-wishers for its continued development. The school is in dire need of additional classrooms, computer facilities, science laboratories, library, school bus and other necessities in an academic institution.
"We welcome the donation of all or any of the above items from friends and allies and from public-spirited institutions. I assure you of our gratitude and acknowledgment in the annals of the school's history. Indeed, the entire Iloko Community will always remain appreciative of your assistance", Oba Olashore stated in appeal message to Nigerians.
In the economic sphere, he built industries at home and has consistently encouraged indigenes to invest at home. He has also assisted all Iloko indigenes who are qualified to be gainfully employed in reputable institutions across the country.
Since his subjects are predominantly Christians, he built a church for the Anglican Communion in the town while mobilising others to complete the Methodist Church in the ancient town. In five years, the face of the town has evidently been transformed for the better.

Original_Search_Title: I Will Guarantee Greater Ohafia Local Government - Udensi
Headline: Nigeria: I Will Guarantee Greater Ohafia Local Government - Udensi
Body_Text:
He is the people s politician, a grassroots man. His office as Vice
Chairman of the Ohafia Local Government Council, Abia State, is a
beehive of activities. While his counterparts in other LGAs clamour
for responsibilities as vice chairmen, he has so much delegated to
him. He is at peace with his boss. Chief Eric E. Udensi is humble but
visible.
Vanguard encountered this former businessman turned politician for
this interview. He says: I am willing to serve Ohafia people as
Chairman of Council, as the incumbent, says he will not recontest the
position, and believe me I can change things for the better .
Excerpts: From business to politics, how come?
Well, I was in business before I ventured into politics, but that
does not mean I am new in this business. After a stint in big
business as I was Chairman/CEO with Ekojik Nigeria Limited, an
engineering and water drilling outfit, where we did consultancy
services for the Federal Government, I came into politics
representing Okamu Ward in the Ohafia LGA under the platform of the
Social Democratic Party (SDP) in 1991. I was elected councillor into
this Council at that time. I later joined the Grassroots Democratic
Movement (GDM). In the present dispensation, I was a foundation
member of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in this area before I
was elected as Deputy to Elder (Chief) Ufere Ilum Emeh who is
chairman.
Currently, I am vice-president of Ohafia Improvement Union.
You have been here for three years, what new things are you going to
bring to this Council?
The Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria provides for an
executive chairman who is the accounting officer of the council. In
the present dispensation he has the knife and he has the yam,
therefore there is a limit to which even the deputy can do. My
chairman has achieved quite a lot that I would have wholeheartedly
supported his candidacy were he willing to continue. But having
confidently made his position clear to me, I need to bring my own
focus to bear on this Council. Having served as councillor and vice
chairman, I believe I have learnt the ropes enough to make my impact
felt in the Local Government administration.
What new efforts are the people going to expect from you as chairman
of council?
Ohafia is rich in human and natural resources. We are a purely
agrarian economic based as majority of our people are subsistence
farmers, but despite the struggles of the people, government had not
yet provided the basic facilities necessary to encourage the people
to move away from the ancestral farming methods. Ohafia Local
Government can easily move away from the knife and hoe farming
implements we are used to. As chairman, sufficient seedlings would be
provided for oranges, pineapples, cashew, bananas and other cash
crops which are safely grown in our soil here. The government under
my administration will acquire the seedlings and distribute to the
people for free, and I can assure you that within a year or so, there
will be so much for the people that storage could become a problem.
Our people are strong and industrious, all they require is government
assistance. My government will ensure that the PRODA group projects
which is wasting in this Council is rehabilitated. The founding
fathers of this project had anticipated that soaps, vegetable oils,
candles, assorted chalks, cosmetics and other local needs of our
people would be produced here and the surplus sold to interested
parties elsewhere to generate revenue for the council. I will ensure
the rehabilitation and use of this project within three months of
coming into office. I will completely move the council away from the
current Federal Government monthly allocation focus, whereby only
money allocated to the local government is employed to solve the many
commitments of council. Our people must see government and feel the
impact of government at the grassroots.
The Nigerian politician is known to make promises only to renege on
them on assumption of office, why is your own going to be different?
I know the Ohafia Local Government Area, I live here, I interact with
the people. You have been to this office and you can easily see that
the people belong here. The men, women, youths, they are looking for
a visionary leadership for this council and this is what I will
provide. The job is made easier for the people when a purposeful
leadership emerges. What the people here require is for government to
provide the necessary infrastructures and the people will take care
of themselves. It is nothing strange. Ours is an old local
government, infact we are within the first generation local
administrative headquarters in this country. Therefore, our people
should not be suffering. Our businessmen and contractors who work for
government should not go through the pains they do now, if the
government is focused and inward looking. We know that some
contractors borrow money from banks and even from local lenders to
executive projects, so when such people are owed, it affects the way
they look at government. I will ensure that no contractor is owed. We
will not contract out jobs for which funds are not available. Of
course, I am not condemning the present administration, but I am
aware that some local government officials had contributed to the
problems that currently exist here and as chairman I know I can stop
their evil machinations.
How do you rate the Governor of this state, in his bid for a second
term?
Dr. Orji Uzor Kalu is God s gift to our people. There is nobody in
living memory in government who has contributed as much as the young
man has done within three years. I have no doubt that the people will
rise against any forces from wherever who will trample upon his
desire to run for a second term. Infact I want to warn all such
forces to desist because the youths of Abia State are battle ready to
deal a deadly blow to anybody or group who will stand against the
action governor in his quest for a second term in office. We believe
in democracy, the voice of the people are paramount, the views of the
majority of the people must be respected, because afterall, they are
the people who are being served. Therefore, any person from anywhere
who by act of omission or commission tries to impose a leadership in
this state will have himself or themselves to blame. The people love
their governor, because he is performing even beyond their wildest
expectations. Even the Federal Government has acknowledged as much.
The recently concluded Prof. Jerry Gana, Minister of Information and
National Orientation led national tour of projects executed by
governors rated him the best or second best. Therefore, in the
country where many governors are interested in owa-mbe parties, our
governor is delivering and ensuring that the people witness the
dividends of democracy. He is doing well and deserves a second term.
Any right thinking person in this State, without parochial motives
will tell you the same.
You are seeking a grassroots position, how close are you to the party
leadership?
I am on the ground, I have always been on the ground. I am only moved
by desire to serve my people. It is one thing to have a vision, it is
quite another to have the opportunity to showcase it. I have no doubt
that the people who have known me, particularly my disposition will
support me. I am not a hungry politician, out looking for help. But I
believe that things can be done differently with results that will
benefit the masses. Our people have suffered enough. All of our
people cannot be in the city, those who are here should be able to
show through the contributions of government how their own individual
lives have improved. I can make that happen.
How did you see the Supreme Court judgement on the Local Government
tenure?
The Supreme Court is the highest court in the land, made of learned
and able jurists who can stand their own anywhere in the world. Their
judgement must be respected. In this world, you cannot win everyday,
you win some you lose some. The judiciary must be encouraged to live
above board and for the survival of this democracy, the different
tiers of government must respect the verdict of the courts. The local
government chairmen and councillors have accepted the decision of the
apex court and we now await the processes to be put in place by the
Independent National Electoral Commission for elections to be
conducted. The Supreme Court judgement was in the national best
interest.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Second Term, an Injustice - Okonkwo
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Second Term, an Injustice - Okonkwo
Body_Text:
Chief Solomon Okonkwo, the national chairman of Igbo Forum is not known for frivolities. He is also not a man of many words. But when an issue revolves around the Igbo race, Chief Okonkwo doesn't betray his age-long patriotism and commitment as an Igbo leader. This he did as he bares his mind on the thorny issue of Igbo presidency next year. He spoke to EJIKEME OMENAZU. Excerpts:
SIR, can you tell us your political antecedent?
Since after the civil war, while trying to survive, I did choose to devote my time to joint Igbo survival especially in Lagos and the whole nation. After the war, I was the first Igbo man appointed to represent the Igbo in the Landlord Residents Association being held at Baale House in Agege Dopemu. During my tenure, I made it possible that no policeman from Elere police station could arrest an Igbo without referring to me.
Then I became the Igbo leader for Agege-Dopemu. Being an Igbo, I knew we cannot do without God. I founded the only Igbo Anglican Church in the area known then as St. Barnabas Anglican Church, now St. Michael's Anglican Church in Dopemu.
Right from that time, I devoted all my time to the church and Igbo course. After that, I got involved in politics through a friend of mine, the late Alhaji Abdullahi Mai Kano due to my growing popularity. Through Mai Kano, I got in contact with the late General Shehu Yar'Adua. I was among the first set of people who founded the defunct Patriotic Front of Nigeria (PFN) in Yar'Adua's house in Ikoyi, and later Kaduna. I was then appointed the co-ordinator for the Eastern zone. I took Yar'Adua round during his first tour of the East then under PFN which later became PDM now being led by the Vice President, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar.
Among other founding members of PFN at the first meeting were one Alhaji Buhari, my friend Mai Kano, Prof. Ango Abdullahi, Alhaji Kaita, Alhaji Maina Ma'aji Lawan, now Deputy Senate Leader and Prince Yomi Edu. Among the PFN founding fathers then was Vice President Atiku Abubakar. Then from the PFN when there was no more politics, I was elected the President-General of Igbo speaking community in Lagos, a position I held for two years before we formed the Ohanaeze Ndigbo, Lagos chapter. Then I was elected the chairman and I relinquished the position of President-General Igbo speaking community and Deputy-President of Igbo Council of Chiefs which was Eze Igbo Ojukwu's cabinet.
I was the chairman of Ohanaeze Ndigbo in Lagos until Ojukwu, Ralph Uwazuruike and myself set up the position of Eze Igbo in Lagos which brought a lot of crises. Because of this crisis Igbo in Lagos abandoned other units in Lagos and outside Lagos and found the Igbo Forum and I became the national chairman. Igbo Forum since its formation is the umbrella of all Igbo unions especially in Lagos.
You are the chairman of Igbo Forum, what informed the formation of the body?
Due to this crisis, and the need for peace, and to unite all Igbo groups, we decided to revive the defunct Igbo State Union. Since the Igbo State Union was proscribed by the military, we decided to call the new body Igbo Forum. We operate from all the states of the federation.
What are the main objectives of the body?
The main objective of the Forum is for Igbo to have one identity, one voice and one action. By the grace of God, we are moving on the right path.
What is the strength of your body in terms of national spread?
In Lagos alone we have a registered membership of 1.5 million people. In the East we operate on two levels, the Igbo Elders Forum and the Eastern Pioneers which are mainly the youth political pressure group. In all other areas we are known as Igbo Forum but we also have other associate organisations. We have affiliate relationship with town unions and market associations across the nation.
Would you state the major achievements of the Forum?
Our achievements are many. The Igbo are now discovering that they can work with each other, which was not so before. The youth are now discovering new avenues of using their strength and initiative without being purely militant. Besides, the Igbo Forum succeeded in bringing peace between Igbo and Yoruba especially in Lagos. When trouble developed in Alaba area, leaders of Igbo Forum held several meetings with OPC that led to the peace at Alaba and other areas. Since that time, there has been total peace between the Igbo, Yoruba and OPC. Yes, there is now total peace among the people.
Now, we are working on the need to see our people take full active part in the politics of Lagos. I am using this opportunity to call on Igbo to go for registration in any party of their choice. Those who want to contest election in any part of Lagos can contact us to assist or direct them. Igbo should be prepared to vote and be voted for in any election. They should be bold enough to come out.
I want to stress also that Igbo Forum pioneered and joined other groups to host one of our great sons, Senate President, Chief Anyim Pius Anyim. In fact, we wrote the project proposal which the Senate President approved which led to the hosting of Anyim on December 2, 2001; and we took active part in the planning and implementation of the civil reception.
Since then, we have been shopping for the true Igbo persons from which Igbo can pick the next Nigerian president and we are surprised that there are so many people of Igbo extraction who can be president of this nation. What is the relationship of the Forum with Ohanaeze Ndigbo which has also been at the forefront of Igbo cause?
As I said earlier, I was the founding chairman of Ohanaeze Ndigbo in Lagos State. I held that post for four years. Ojukwu and other Igbo leaders installed me here in Lagos. Then we were holding Igbo Day and Iri Ji (New Yam Festival) every October in Lagos which was the greatest attraction of Igbo in Lagos then. Before the advent of Igbo Forum, I was one of the Igbo leaders in Ohanaeze but when the Igbo middle class and masses felt that Ohanaeze then was too meek we felt that there was need for other people that would be more fearless in fighting for the integration of Ndigbo into the mainstream Igbo politics without apologies or fear.
With my position of chairman of Igbo Forum, I am now in full contact with the reformed Ohanaeze especially the political committee which is where we are interested in now. The political right of every Igbo man is our interest now with other groups that are also fighting for Igbo rights and dignity. 

In one of your recent public statements you lamented that the Igbo do not speak with one voice. Why is this so?
It is so because before now, since the end of the civil war, every Igbo man was going about with the defeatist and selfish aura that as a defeated person. He had to find a platform to actualise himself.. While working for himself, he pretended that he was fighting for others. But that is coming to an end as Igbo are learning to overcome fear and come out for the general good of the Igbo. We are now learning how to co-operate with others. With God being on our side, those with defeatist tendency will see that they are in the minority, while those who want to seek the full right of Ndigbo will be in the majority. And that will be Ebe Ano.
Those who still think that they want to be servants should because of their sons and daughters change their mind and guard their utterances and their old ideas making the Igbo followers and not leaders. The tiger has been unchained. I am calling on all Igbo wherever they may be to reject fear, servitude, threats and intimidation of the past and come out now to tell the world that Igbo are part and parcel and full citizens of Nigeria and should have the rights of full citizenship. There are no two ways about that.
Your Forum has also been advocating for Igbo presidency like the other Igbo-based bodies. Why do you think Ndigbo should have a shot at the presidency?
Thank you very much for this question. It is not why do I think but it is as good as accomplished right because it is our right. It was agreed that the presidency should be rotated to the South and Igbo agreed that the Yoruba should go first. In APP, Ogbonnaya Onu left it for Olu Falae of AD. We wanted to show the Yoruba in AD that Igbo can work with them.
In PDP, Igbo worked for Obasanjo against Ekwueme their own son so that Yoruba will have the taste of presidency. The road was then cleared for the Yoruba when Falae contested against Obasanjo, both Yoruba and Obasanjo emerged president. For Obasanjo to want to go for second term will be the worst injustice to Ndigbo and the Igbo masses and youths will not like that.
Those who are telling Obasanjo go for second term are not telling him the whole truth because he will be disgraced. If any voice comes to him as the voice of God telling him to go for second term, he should check that voice because God has also spoken to us that we should not allow this chance to slip from us, because we shall do all within our power to see that Igbo man emerges as president in 2003.
The best thing that will happen to Obasanjo and the best history he can leave for Nigeria is that he should be the person to hand over to an Igbo president in 2003. He would have made the best history for himself and his grandchildren. He should also remember that his dream to go again means that he is indeed seeking his third term having ruled this nation during the military as head of state. This is a great injustice if it is allowed for one man to rule the nation three times in spite of gentleman's agreement to zone the post to the South with Yoruba taking the first shot and then Igbo. Now he wants to perpetuate himself to the detriment of Ndigbo. It is not acceptable to us.
Do you think Igbo presidency is realistic in 2003?
My answer is yes! yes!! yes!!! Therefore, Igbo should come out now and take their right.
You recently named eight Igbo sons who should be considered for presidency. How did you pick them? First, I want to say that these eight people are eminent sons of Igbo and they have performed creditably in private and public lives. From the PDP we shortlisted Chief (Dr.) Emmanuel Iwuanyanwu, Chief Anyim Pius Anyim and Dr. Orji Uzor Kalu.
For APP we shortlisted Dr. Ogbonnaya Onu and Ikemba Ojukwu. Then for AD we shortlisted Rear Admiral Ndubuisi Kanu. Standing by as consensus candidates are Chief Emeka Anyaoku and Chief Sonny Odogwu. We want Ndigbo to seriously consider these names and may be, reach us with their comments and whatever they have to say about them. We need their support because we have started serious consultations among Ndigbo and other Nigerians. We are also in serious talks with other Eastern ethnic groups on this issue.
These eight eminent sons of Igbo to the best of our knowledge can give Nigeria the best president or leader that they will ever have, that will make everybody say "Aha"! We are calling every Nigerian especially Igbo to give us support. What is the Forum doing to convince Ndigbo that these people should be considered for the plum position?
We have had series of consultations, discussions and contacts. We have started to present these names for everybody to join us, shout them, pray for them, those who have money should bring their money, those who have strength should bring their strength, those who have knowledge and wisdom should bring them out. But those who do not have anything, especially knowledge and wisdom should keep quiet to avoid mistakes because Ndigbo will not forgive any person who will contribute to deny the Igbo their right this time.
Have you also been making contacts with the people you shortlisted to convince them to come out for the position in their various parties?
Yes. It is a continuing process. But some of them are afraid of betrayal of their fellow Igbo as happened in the past. That is why I told those who lacked wisdom to keep quiet because Igbo will not condone betrayal this time around.
What do you think Ndigbo should do to avoid their choice candidate being schemed out at the last minute as was the case with Chief Ekwueme in PDP and Dr. Iwuanyanwu in APP in 1999?
That is why we are saying that every Igbo should bring out what he knows best or what he has for the collective good of the rest and that is the collective will of the Igbo race. That is why we have just said that those that lack wisdom should keep quiet.
Those who think they have money to buy the Igbo rights should go and rest and enjoy their money because the Igbo youths will surely react to any unnecessary selfish sale of the Igbo collective right. This is the time for Nigerian president of Igbo origin. Every other thing to the Igbo conscience, Igbo person is immaterial.
What do you consider as the major advantages of Igbo presidency?
First, it is our right as Nigerians to have an Igbo person as president. Knowing what an Igbo can do, any of these eminent men listed or many others not listed yet, will transform this nation to one of the happiest nation in the world because Igbo men thrives on challenges. The Nigerian nation now is a challenge. Put any of these eminent Igbo sons there and you will see that he will turn the nation to a modern wonder of the world in the shortest possible time.
For example, see what the young man, Anyim Pius Anyim is doing in the Senate. He was unknown until he became the Senate President. He was even untested. But he has wonderfully stabilised the Senate and carried everyone along and all the rancour, all fighting and factions are gone and the Senate is now one body moving like one family. The same thing will happen to the Nigerian nation when an Igbo has become the president in 2003. All ethnic militia will go and Nigeria will move like one family and hidden potentials will sprout up and Nigeria will take her position among the comity of nations. Let the sleeping Igbo wake up now.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Traders React to Proposed Modernisation
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Traders React to Proposed Modernisation
Body_Text:
Lagos — Traders in Mile 12 Market have reacted to the proposed modernisation
of the market as hinted recently by the Lagos State Governor, Senator
Bola Ahmed Tinubu.
Malam Abu, one of the key dealers of fresh tomatoes and pepper in the
market explained that if the market is modernised the prices of goods
will automatically increase. The trader fear that the government is
bound to charge them exorbitantly for the new shops as well as the
space to park their vehicles.
Another trader who spoke to MetroBusiness (Mama Kayode), a yam dealer
in the market, noted that the prices of goods are now high but will
even be higher when the market is modernised.
She, however, noted that she would have loved it if the market is
modernised as the present structure that makes up the market is not
conducive for business.
Madam Oshinowo, another prominent fruit dealer pointed out that the
government is not fair to its citizens in terms of welfare. She cited
the poor Mile 12 structure as an example of government careless
attitude to the people. Oshinowo noted that it is good that the
market is modernised to change the face of the filthy market, but
expressed fears that the traders will be made to pay the cost of the
rehabilitation.
"We are not saying that the market should not be modernised at least
the look of the place will change for the better, but the problem is
that we will be made to bear the high cost of the rehabilitation,"
she said.
Another woman who sells tomatoes at Ola market, in Itire-Mushin
dismissed the pronouncement made by the governor as political, adding
that such pronouncement has been made by past governors with the goal
of reaping political gains.
"Don't mind them, they don't want to do any work. We have heard of
such tales before. Tinubu is talking like that because of election.
He wants the peoples votes," she pointed out.
MetroBusiness gathered that the relocation and modernisation of the
market will definitely have some adverse effect on the users as the
taxes/levies charged on the traders may be increased and this will
inadvertently affect price of goods and services. Some also fear that
they could lose their stalls after payment.
"The government is not sincere, how are we sure we will retain our
shops after payment. It has happened in so many markets where traders
paid, yet lost their stalls," one of the traders said.
It would be recalled that Governor Tinubu early this month while
touring Ikorodu LGA announced plans by his administration to commence
work on the relocation and modernisation of the Mile 12 Market.

Headline: Uganda: Mengo Signs Food Deal
Body_Text:
THE Mengo government has signed a memorandum of understanding with the Uganda Bio-diversity Network (UBN) to promote the growing and eating of local foods, vegetables and fruits.
The campaign will begin in schools in Buganda kingdom as a pilot project.
Mr. Kasujja Musoke, the director of UBN, signed on behalf of his organisation while J.C. Muyingo, the Mengo minister for education and sports, signed for the Buganda government.
The UBN co-ordinator, Annette Suubi Kalanzi, said the initiative is aimed at developing a culture among the people of Buganda to eat local foods, vegetables and fruits which are very nutritious.
She said the project would ensure that people have a variety of foods in their homes, which could help them fight hunger and poverty.
Musoke listed the vegetables that will be grown as Nakati, doodo, ebbugga which are excellent for sight and are rich in vitamins.
Others are Katunkuma and entula, which are good for high blood pressure, and yams which are energy giving food.
Among the fruits are entuntunu, wild sweet fruits, mangoes, jackfruit, oranges, avocados, passion fruit and tangarines.
UNB has set up nursery beds in Ngogwe in Mukono district for various crops which will be used as illustration plots.
youth and women groups and individuals will be trained in modern farming.
Muyingo appealed to Ugandans to utilise the programme in order to curb preventable diseases like malnutrition.

Headline: Ghana: Non-Traditional Products: Marketing Nation's Crops
Body_Text:
Ghana now exports yams, cassava, gari, bananas, plantain, pineapples, citrus, coffee, wheat bran, cocoa shell, waste cocoa beans, shea nuts, textiles, ceramics, handicrafts and charcoal under the Non-Traditional Export (NTE) drive that started in 1994.
Figures made available to the Ghana News Agency by Mr. Castro Kumi Adjei-Sam, Export Manager of Ghana Ports and Harbours Authority (GPHA) showed that NTE which was on a small scale started increasing in 1992 and 1993 when 145,288 metric tonnes were exported.
Between 1994 and 1999, a total of 933,309 metric tonnes were exported but revenue generated was not immediately available.
However, in the year 2000, with 4,725 ship trips made at the Tema Port, 250, 268 metric tonnes of products were exported to earn350 million dollars.
Between January and September 2001, the frequency of shipment was 4,741, and 242,866 metric tonnes of the products were expected to yield 250 million dollars.
These non-traditional products were consigned to Belgium, Denmark, India, United Kingdom, United States, Germany, Holland, Greece, Italy, Brazil, Australia, Russia, Singapore, Indonesia and the Netherlands among other places.
From all indications Africans living abroad patronise the foodstuffs since they do not want to be cut off from their roots, or they relish their traditional food.
Ghana's non-traditional products are also exported to a number of African countries including Nigeria, Sierra-Leone, South Africa, Liberia, Benin, Cote d'Ivoire, Morocco, Cameroon, Democratic Republic of Congo, the Gambia and Kenya.
Mr. Adjjei-Sam said 90 per cent of non-traditional products are exported through the Port of Tema and the remaining 10 per cent through the Kotoka International Airport, adding that nationwide, export made in 2000 and 2001 was estimated at 750 million dollars.
Twelve major products are frequently exported - fresh pineapples, frozen fish, timber products, canned tuna, cocoa products, shea nuts and fresh banana.
The rest are cottonseed, fresh yam, raw cotton, aluminum products and raw coffee.
Mr. Adjei-Sam said in 2000, a total of 202,933 metric tonnes of these high patronage products were exported, while 186,368 metric tonnes were sent out in 2001.
To meet the expansion of the NTE, the GPHA in 1994, converted shed nine at the main harbour for non-traditional exports and it has since been named the "Export Shed" which has a storage capacity of between 8,000 and 9,000 metric tonnes.Though the shed is not air-conditioned, the products do not go bad because they are normally brought in to coincide with the arrival of the ships, which turn around soon after lading. As food items are stored in the shed, cleanliness is strictly observed to ensure that nothing gets contaminated before shipment. For this reason the items are parked orderly according to the type of foodstuff or items.
While it is desirable to sustain the NTE because it generates foreign exchange and employment and enables Africans in the Diaspora to maintain their roots, sight should not be lost of the periodic food shortages experienced in the country as result of the export of large consignments of foodstuffs.
The NTE is now complementing cocoa and gold as major export earners and announcing the arrival of Ghana at the market of the "Global Village".

Headline: Zambia: Religion And Secularism in Africa
Body_Text:
IS there any possibility of dialogue between Christianity and the traditional African religions? The well-known theologian Professor John Mbiti spoke on this topic in Lusaka during March.
He began with an overview of Christian attitudes, telling us how 19th Century missionaries took back to Europe gruesome accounts of African religion. Their view was coloured by arrogance, racial prejudice, misunderstanding and unwillingness to learn from others.
In the popular view, he says, things have not changed much. The western view of the religion in the "dark continent" is that Africans worship spirits and are afraid of them. Even today many religionists, especially the evangelicals, say African religion is the work of the devil and must be wiped out.
They aim to silence open discussion on the subject, he says. The view of the missionaries was that "We brought religion where there was none." This view blocks dialogue, says Professor Mbiti.
In fact, he adds, since humans evolved in East Africa, as they spread outwards they carried the religious themes to the rest of the world. "You did not bring God to Africa. God brought you to Africa." This is his message to the missionaries.
"What you brought was the name of Jesus Christ," he continues. However each religion opened up to the other, and brought about an explosion of Christianity not seen elsewhere in the world except in the South Seas (where the population is much smaller anyway).
According to Prof Mbiti, the Bible presents a picture of society which is close to the African tribal setting. There are similar health and social problems, and the parables are similar to the ancient African folk tales. He adds that African religion is unified and it is misleading to speak of African religions.
It is monotheistic and sees God as a saviour. It is hard to accept Prof Mbiti's assessment. He said nothing about pantheism, or ancestor worship, and he does not take account of the hundreds of gods listed in the Encyclopedias (see for example www.altreligion. about.com).
These gods have a variety of characteristics, and relate to a number of phenomena (e.g. creation, wind, fire, forests, rain, sea, sky, earth, trees, thunder, yams, women, beginnings, divination, fertility, family).
These cannot all be perceived as the same god. Nor did Mbiti refer to the rapid expansion of Islam in Africa.
Scholars such as Tanzania's Nsajigwa Mwasokwa see a closer relationship between traditional African culture and Islam, with its patriarchy, polygamy, and community-oriented principles. Mwasokwa is quoted in "Free Inquiry", the quarterly magazine of the US-based Council for Secular Humanism, as saying "the humanist life stance is badly needed to combat dogmas, harmful traditions, irrational fears, authoritarianism and intolerance".
He was speaking at the first international humanist conference in sub-Saharan Africa, held in Nigeria in October 2001. Speaking at the same conference, Sheila Solarin noted that humanity should eliminate the "god of the gaps" to whom is ascribed anything that is not popularly understood.
The gods of rain and thunder became obsolete after we learned what really affects the weather, she said. Though Nigeria is constitutionally a secular nation, conflicts between Christianity (south) and Islam (north) have created serious tensions which the conference highlighted.
Sharia law has been introduced in several northern states. Safiya Hussaini was recently sentenced to be buried up to her neck and then stoned to death for alleged adultery. Following an intense worldwide outcry, an Islamic court ruled that her conviction could not be safely upheld.
Often Sharia law is administered in such a way that the man in such cases goes unpunished, and his word is accepted where that of the woman is not. However if a similar outcry is raised in future cases we may see an end to the extreme punishments which Islam apparently still considers acceptable.
There is obviously a deep culture clash involved here, and it is partly based on religious principles. If Moslems do not see this form of punishment as barbaric, does the fault lie with them, or with us? The humanist view is that the link between Islam and Sharia has done great damage.
Humanity's attempts to progress to more humane, compassionate and tolerant levels are handicapped by the superstitious relics of the past. In our complex modern Africa adultery is generally regarded as a minor offence, and the real concern must be for the welfare of any children that result. That welfare is not sustained if the court rules that the mother should be stoned to death. "Free Inquiry" editor-in-chief Paul Kurtz says:
"Religions often insist upon submission to the 'will of God'; they preach that salvation is in the next life. Africa has suffered at their hands, with poverty and disease (especially AIDS) plaguing these nations."
I agree with Kurtz. While I applaud the efforts of the Catholic Church to alleviate poverty, I think these should be entirely divorced from any thought of duty to God, or of winning souls for Jesus.
And safe sex (not just condoms) is a more realistic solution than abstinence, for most people. Kurtz continues: "Secular humanists believe that reason, science and the virtues of individual liberty, self-help, and self-reliance are pivotal if Africa is to grow and develop. The council has focused on scientific literacy and critical thinking in its educational programs, and it has consistently defended democracy and human rights. This is spelled out clearly in 'Humanist Manifesto 2000', which advocates planetary humanism."
Noted African figures such as Leopold Sedar Senghor and Wole Soyinka are laureates of the International Academy of Humanism. With their example before us, perhaps it will not be too long before the veil is lifted from the eyes of Zambians and they see superstition, evangelical crusades, and religious dogma and intolerance, for what they are.
We might then succeed in getting a humanist group off the ground here, as has happened in Nigeria, Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda and Ethiopia.

Headline: Ghana: End-of-Service Benefits And Labour Productivity
Body_Text:
Man is said to be an animal producing labour implement. The difference between us Homo sapiens and other animals is that they do not produce labour implements.
During the process of production man exerts either his physical force or mental power on materials to change their form to satisfy his biological and social needs. Man also acts upon himself, that is mould himself into a productive tool and also reproduces himself biologically.
Labour, therefore, is the prerequisite for the existence of human society. There will not be society without labour as labour itself was the prerequisite for the ape to become man. Homo sapiens during the process of production comes into what is called production relations.
The production relations are in two forms, one is for the biological reproduction of man himself and material production. Man and woman are destined to marry each other, biologically reproduce themselves and socially mould their offspring to make them productive tools for the society.
Employers and workers or owners of the means of production and labour are also destined to come into production relations as an employer and worker during the process of production even against their wish.
When people enter into production relations, they make a social arrangement to guide the relation between them. That is known as employment relation or employment contract.
One of the points that employers and workers need to look at during the period of contract is that: will the wage or salary or remuneration of the worker enable him to reproduce his labour?
We want to say that is the wage of the worker enough to biologically and physically make him productive the next day.
Apart from air that is free, the worker would have to buy food, water, clothing, pay for accommodation, biologically reproduce himself, enhance his labour capacity or increase his labour productivity and to make his children productive and useful members of society.
While both employers and workers enter into production relations to produce both material and spiritual needs of society, society also has the responsibility to see to it that the employer is able to make profit so that he will be able to also reproduce his labour, buy machinery, raw materials and pay labour.
The worker is paid enough that he can reproduce himself and sustain himself when he grows, becomes weak and is unable to work. Labour on the other hand is to make sure that it processes the raw material or imparts the knowledge and skills needed.
LABOUR PRODUCTIVITY AND REMUNERATION
Labour productivity means the measure of the efficiency of labour or the time it takes labour to produce or process an amount of raw material at a given period or to attain the target set by the company.
Labour productivity is also measured by comparing the time it takes the Ghanaian worker to process a certain quantity of raw materials as compared with workers with high labour productivity.
Man must be supplied with the right tool or machinery, raw materials and the right environment must be created for him. An employer who supplies his labourer with langalanga rather than a scythe should not expect that worker to be productive.
Now let us make some comparisons. An American labourer is paid more than five dollars a day; meanwhile his Ghanaian counterpart is paid less than a dollar a day. Both workers are to feed on rice that is produced in the USA and fish that is caught in Europe.
Both of them are to pay the same amount for other commodities, services and education. Now our educational institutions are charging the same amount of school fees as they are charged in America.
The retailer of food crops such as corn, cassava, yam, etc., also want to retail these food crops as they are sold in the US and Europe.
The utility services want to charge world price for their services, which they call economic rates. As for the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund (IMF) they wish if we could even pay for the air we breath. The Ghanaian worker, according to our former President, lives on his salary for only two weeks; that was 1995, and perhaps one week now. Do Ghanaian workers have the physical and mental capacity to reproduce their labour?
The answer is obvious. If the economical and political system is unable to make the worker productive, is it the fault of the worker? On the other hand the Ghanaian employer pays himself well and is able to provide for himself and his family all the necessities of life. There are some Ghanaian employers who live even better than their counterparts in America.
END-OF-SERVICE BENEFITS
End-of-service benefits are usually cash benefits that are paid to workers by their employers upon their compulsory retirement or when their services are abruptly terminated.
End-of-service benefits or terminal benefits, severance pay, redundancy benefit, end of contract benefit or you name it, are non-contributory by the employee.
In Britain and other places it is paid as gratuity by the employer to his employee. The same employer may decide that the gratuity he paid to his ex-employee is not enough, and may decide to pay an ex-gratia in addition to the gratuity.
It is, therefore, humane and heartwarming if an employee after serving his employer for many years is paid a gratuity by the employer. End-of-service benefit is not only peculiar to Ghana; it is paid to workers in other countries.
