It may interest everyone to note that despite great losses suffered by Jukuns in the crises, the Taraba State government had expressed greater sympathy to the Tiv, since most of them appeared to be heading back to Benue State. In order to assist the displaced Tivs, the government established two refugee camps in Mutum Biyu and Bali. The camps were provided with food, water, beddings and security to make them comfortable. The camps are still operational.
Also, from the outbreak of the hostilities to date, Governor Jolly Nyame has held more than 10 joint and separate meetings with Jukun and Tiv senior civil servants. Similar meetings were held with traditional rulers, chairmen of local governments and opinion leaders in the affected areas. During the last meeting, the governor directed the senior civil servants to go back to their respective areas and gave them one week to convince their people to allow the Tiv people to return. These are the measures that have now facilitated the return of the Tiv to Ibbi Local government area. During a recent visit to a refugee camp in Benue State, Governor Jolly Nyame not only gave them N500,000 for their transport, but also announced a four-week timetable for the full return of the Tiv to Taraba State. Considering the Governor's personal commitment to the restoration of peace to Taraba State, there is a compelling reason to declare that the Tiv are lucky to have Reverend Nyame as the governor of Taraba State. If it were not Governor Nyame at the helm of affairs, the return of the Tiv to Taraba would be unthinkable and impossible due to the level of mistrust displayed by the warring parties. The governor, who is from the northern part of the state has no immediate stake in the crises apart from the fact that as the man divinely bestowed with the responsibility of leading the teeming people of Taraba State, it is his concern that everyone should live in peace with one another, so as to provide a conducive atmosphere for the government to attend to their basic needs.
Based on the foregoing, Governor Nyame and the government of Taraba State have no reason whatsoever to 'connive' with anyone to kill or destroy property belonging to Tiv people. On two occasions the governor drove to Dan Anacha and personally apprehended truckloads of yam belonging to Tiv people. He handed the trucks to the police and directed them to prevent any unauthorised person from harvesting or selling farm produce belonging to displaced persons. If the police failed to live up to expectation, it is left to the public to decide who had not done his job. Certainly, the governor is more interested than anyone to see the end of the hostilities, because the crises has completely drained the resources of the government and slowed down its progress. The government is not interested in diversionary tactics by people who are pushing for personal benefits from the crises. Mr. Alibi and his fellow travellers should look elsewhere for a way to please their godfathers. The government is looking for peacemakers and not faultfinders or hired mourners who have no sympathy with the issue at hand rather than the size of their pockets.

Original_Search_Title: Guber Aspirant Counsels Ekiti On Tax Waivers
Headline: Nigeria: Guber Aspirant Counsels Ekiti On Tax Waivers
Body_Text:
Ado Ekiti — A gubernatorial aspirant of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Ekiti State, Mr. Ayo Fayose, has called on the state government to waive stringent taxes that could discourage investors from the state.
Speaking at the commissioning of an ultra modern Eatery Centre called De-Prince & Princess in Ado-Ekiti over the weekend, Mr. Fayose expressed displeasure over the low level of commercial activities in the state.
He commended the initiator of the new business outfit, Chief Christopher Uzomah, who is a native of Enugu State, while urging the administration in Ekiti to consider appointing an Ibo man as commissioner for Commerce to boost commercial activities in the state.
In his welcome address at the occasion, the initiator of the outfit, Chief Uzomah, who is the chairman and chief executive of Uzotraco Nigeria Limited based in Akure, Ondo State appealed to the people of Ekiti to see the new eatery centre as part of the efforts to develop the state.
Also the Managing Director of the new outfit, Mr. John Orji described the eatery as an idea conceived out of the desire to bring such quality services to Ekiti State, as the outfit's products are prepared under the most hygienic conditions.
Speaking during an interview, a legal practitioner in Ado-Ekiti, Mr. Ray Anyanwun, described the establishment of the eatery as a welcome development, saying it was another opportunity to break away from the traditional and conservative culture of eating pounded yam every afternoon as launch by the people of Ekiti State.
The legal practitioner commended the People of Ekiti for allowing people of other ethnic groups in the country to contribute to the development of the young state without any form of discrimination.

Original_Search_Title: Hausa Community in Rivers Begs Odili for Mosque
Headline: Nigeria: Hausa Community in Rivers Begs Odili for Mosque
Body_Text:
Members of the Hausa community at Slaughter, Trans Amadi Road, Port Harcourt, have called on the Rivers State governor, Dr Peter Odili, to come to their rescue by ensuring that the authority of Obi/Akpon local government area allow them continue with the building of their mosque.
Their appeal was made over the weekend during an interview session with Weekly Trust.
The chairman of the Butchers' Union Association at the main Slaughter, Trans Amadi, Alhaji Ibrahim Maisundu stated that the mosque committee members decided last year to improve on the temporary structure of the mosque which has been on ground since 1979.
"They found the relevant papers and approached the Obi/Akpon local government authorities who gave them approval to embark on the project," he said.
The chairman lamented that after the approval, the building of the mosque project began on July 22, 2001 and his team was asked to stop work one month later by some local government officials.
He said a delegation from the Hausa community went to the local government to find out what prompted the order and they were referred to members of the Oginiba community who refused to grant them audience.
According to him, the delegation went back to the local government chairman to give him feedback and he informed them that the matter was above his power.
Alhaji Maisundu said the Hausa community tried again and this time discussed with some members of the Oginiba community who asked them to bring yams, drinks and a specified amount of money.
His words: "We promised giving them money and after delivering our promise, the community members refused to set a date for a meeting till now."
Maisundu appealed to every Muslim, especially the supreme leader of Muslims in Nigeria to come to their aid.
The patron of the Northern community in Rivers State and chairman of the central mosque committee, Alhaji Uba Sule said the same problem was also affecting the central mosque at another road in Port Harcourt.
He said since the land was acquired honestly, they should be allowed to go on with their building projects.
Alhaji Sule called on the governor of Rivers State, Dr Peter Odili to come to their rescue because since they have been living in the state for the past 30 years, they have been law-abiding and living in harmony with their hosts.

Original_Search_Title: Tap Nation's Endowed Human Resources - Chaplain
Headline: Ghana: Tap Nation's Endowed Human Resources - Chaplain
Body_Text:
Kumasi — MR. P. S. Antwi-Agyei, a former chaplain of Kumasi Academy, has called on the government to utilize the highly endowed human resources to solve the country's developmental needs.
He noted that Ghana is endowed with human resources which hold solution to our problems but are rotting away without any attention Antwi-Agyei, who is also the founder of the International Christian Business Network (ICBBN), raised the issue with the neglect of Ghanaian geniuses at the expense of development.
Besides himself with rage, Antwi-Adjei did not understand why Ghana could not tap the experiences of a young Ghanaian, Dr. Acheampong, who researched and discovered how to treat brain tumour in 1988.
He also cited another young Ghanaian, one Yidana now a nuclear scientist at the Pentagon Headquarters of the US Department of Defence.
Another Ghanaian, Eric Donkor, reportedly left the computer laboratory of Cape Vars and a computer company in the US and, according to Antwi-Agyei, Donkor is now in charge of checking computer viruses at the Pentagon.
There are the Professors Kwesi Andams, Alloteys, Dr. Henry Benjamin Obengs and Col. Kofi Abaka Jacksons, among others, who could help solve our problems, the chaplain disclosed.
Prof. Andam, a former President of the Ghana Association of Civil Engineers (GACE), it was suggested, could be the solution to the Accra floods, having permanently fixed the bridge between Dabala and Anloga on the Keta road, after it had been swept away several times by flood.
Antwi-Agyei mentioned Prof. Allotey and his team at the Atomic Energy Commission (AEC) as a major source of food sufficiency for Ghana.
When the Konkombas lost all their yam seeds, following the Konkomba-Nanumba war, the aging Professor multiplied yam seeds in his laboratory for distribution to farmers, resulting in a glut in the yam market in Europe.
"The whites are not ashamed to name Allotey's scientific theory after him but Ghanaians are shying away from according him the needed push to solve our food situation back home," Antwi-Agyei lamented.
Antwi-Agyei is also not happy about the government not tapping the rich experiences of 73-year-old world acclaimed Ghanaian soil scientist, Dr. Henry Benjamin Obeng.
The former Director of Soil Research Institute (SRI) is said to have found that the sweet berry is more than gold and that two-and-a-half acres of it could give Ghana $15,000 a year.
Dr. Obeng, who has also done some work on sunflower, claims sunflower could help Ghanaians create wealth and jobs.
"What are we waiting for?" the chaplain questioned.
Chaplain Antwi-Agyei is more angered by the fact that Col. Kofi Abaka Jackson's invention in 1987 of a car which does not use petrol could not be given the needed push at the governmental level.
The patent expired without any help coming his way.
The man is said to have invented other energy saving devices but nobody sees the need to help him come out with his products.
Col. Jackson, a former Air Force Commander and one-time Managing Director of Ghana Airways, has mentioned a number of his innovations for which financial support and partners are required for patenting, development and marketing.
These, according to his (Jackson's) book, "When Gun Rules," include a float-charging system for electric vehicles which application for patent lapsed for lack of funding.
The other innovation is a new turbine system, called Flapping Turbine Technology (FTT), for harnessing energy of natural flow of rivers to generate electricity without building dams.
The European Patent Office granted Col. Jackson patent in February 1998 and that was the end - no push.
Col. Jackson has also lamented on an alternative technology for canoe production which invention could help cut down high price of premix fuel and outboard motors.
These and many others have saddened Chaplain Antwi-Agyei, who is informed that a Ghanaian doctor has been granted licence and flying a plane he assembled in the US.
"Why can't this young doctor's expertise be tapped to fix or find a replacement for the Flying Coffin which generated a public debate during the last regime," he asked.
Antwi-Agyei believes that supporting any of these named geniuses could change the destiny of Ghana.
He charged the NPP government to avoid such "a serious crime" of neglecting Ghana's most endowed human resources.
He also appealed to the government to tap the entrepreneurial skills of people like Mr. B. A. Mensah if the "Golden Age of Business" should achieve results.
"We don't have to kill our own entrepreneurs and rely on foreign investors to build the economy," he cautioned.
The Christian Minister suggested that the government take a cue from former US President Clinton who set up an economic team of some of the best brains and created surplus for President.
These Ghanaian geniuses should be encouraged as think-tanks and concentrate on development.
"Ghanaian geniuses should not be made to rot away", he pleaded.

Original_Search_Title: MP Takes Close Look at Harare's Elections
Headline: Ghana: MP Takes Close Look at Harare's Elections
Body_Text:
Hon. John Mahama, MP for Bole and former Communications Minister, is part of an all-inclusive three-member Ghana Observer Mission sent to monitor the Zimbabwean general elections. Mahama, a regular Chronicle subscriber, has been in Harare, the capital, since March 2, together with George Isaac Amoo, MP for Ayawaso West Wuogon and David Kanga, Deputy Electoral Commissioner. We bring you the concluding part of his article in which Mahama shares with our readers insights into the politics of Robert Mugabe's Zimbabwe and factors that are likely to influence the results of the tension-packed elections.
President Mugabe faces his strongest challenge in the 22 years he has served as leader of Zimbabwe.
The election is too close to call.
The youth who are often called "born frees" because they did not experience the hardship of colonialism and the liberation struggle, form a bastion of support for Morgan Tsvangirai.
They have no memory of the liberation struggle and really do not care a hoot about it. MDC has quite massive urban support, while ZANU PF is better grounded in the rural areas and amongst the older generation.
On the final day of campaigning before the elections, we visit Bindura.
It is two hours drive north of Harare.
We are in luck. President Mugabe is holding his last campaign rally here.
While still several kilometers from Bindura, we see obvious signs of the big event.
All manner of vehicles have been mobilized to transport supporters to the big rally.
A helicopter flies overhead.
I guess it is the President on his way to the rally. When we finally arrive in Bindura and find our way to the rally ground, the place is packed.
There are thousands of ZANU PF supporters packed in an open field. We are unable to get close because of the heavy crowd.
I manage to climb a small hillock from where I have a view of the podium and can take scenic photographs of the rally.
President Mugabe looks visibly tired.
He has criss-crossed the length and breadth of the country campaigning, and addressing 47 rallies nationwide.
Nationalistic and Racist Character
Several Government and ZANU PF officials address the packed crowd before the President himself. ZANU PF's campaign has adopted a quite nationalist and racist character. People are reminded of the difficulty of the Liberation War.
All speakers rail against White Zimbabweans and the British. They are blamed for sabotaging the economy and all manner of ills.
Tsvangirai is characterized as a puppet of the whites, to be used as an instrument to re-colonize Zimbabwe. Blair bashing is the order of the day. President Mugabe describes him as an "arrogant little fellow."
President Mugabe predicts the demise of the MDC after a ZANU PF victory in this weekend's election, and wishes that the MDC would rest in hell and not in peace. He urges Zimbabweans not to sell out their birth right to the white colonialists.
Campaigning is significantly different in Zimbabwe than it is in Ghana.
In most places, but for a few scattered posters, it is difficult to tell there is an election on. There is no music and party slogans blaring from party vans.
I asked our driver, Ananalas, why people do not go about in party T-shirts and display party flags all over.
He answers that people are afraid to be identified as belonging to one party or other because of the fear of molestation. On our rounds I ask more than 10 people on the streets who they intend to vote for, only two are willing to respond.
The untiring David Kanga is a storehouse of wisdom as far as electioneering processes are concerned. He gives us a run-down on what things to look out for during our rounds and how to avoid sticky situations.
He lectures us on electoral systems over dinner and points out the weaknesses in Zimbabwe's electoral system that could potentially be exploited if there is an intention to rig.
He regales us with stories of various election observer missions he has undertaken in different parts of the world.
The two most hairy are the Gambian mission where he is almost killed in a motor accident, and the Sierra Leonian one where he and fellow observers are evacuated from their hotel on motorcycles and by helicopter, moments before rebels overrun the hotel.
It is a privilege to work with him on such a mission. Our visit to Zimbabwe has not been all work. We were honoured guests at Ghana's Independence Day reception held at the Sheraton Hotel, Harare.
Our host High Commissioner Baah Boakye proudly introduces us to the gathering as members of the Ghana Election Observer Mission.
We received quite a lot of attention after that, with a lot of questions about our impressions so far.
Mr. Kanga advises us to be non-committal in answering such questions lest we be labelled as being partisan in favour of one group or other.
I discover a very good friend, Mr. Didier Ferrand, former French Ambassador to Ghana. He is currently the French Ambassador to Zimbabwe.
He is very happy to see me and invites the whole observer team to lunch at his residence after the elections. We also attended a reception hosted by the High Commissioner for the Ghanaian Community in Zimbabwe.
There is quite a good number of Ghanaians, mostly professionals, working here in Zimbabwe.
Mr. Osakunor is strong in the Telecom sector here. Dr. Danso is a Specialist Urologist and has a very thriving practice in Harare.
Dr. Theresa Obi is a Consultant Optometrist. There are quite a few young people who were born here in Zimbabwe and are related to the late Sally Mugabe.
They are totally Zimbabwean and most have never been to Ghana before and speak no Ghanaian language.
They, however, remember their Ghanaian roots and are desirous of coming to visit Ghana one day. One of them, a young man of 25, is a very prosperous broker on the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange.
After the junk food of French fries and chicken we have been having in the hotel, it is a welcome change to see a spread of kenkey and fish, fried plantain and beans, gari foto, yam and palava sauce and many other Ghanaian dishes. We dig in. There is more than enough for everyone.
The High Commissioner leads the way in dancing to the highlife tunes and is supported by several of his staff and some of the invited guests. I feel too full to dance and so I relax and watch. These Ghanaians here are quite assimilated.
When the highlife is over and the music of a popular Zimbabwean artiste is put on, the floor is suddenly jam-packed with writhing and wriggling bodies.
All in all, it is an enjoyable evening. A good way to unwind just before the serious business of elections begins.
ELECTION DAY
The first day of elections is generally peaceful. There are only few reports of isolated disturbances in some areas.
The police are able to deal firmly with these.
There is a very strong police presence at all polling stations. There is an average of seven police officers at every polling station.
The Ghana team randomly covers polling stations in about nine constituencies, namely Kuwadzana, Mabvuku, Mbare East, Dzivarasekwa, Highfield 047, Harare South, Harare Central, Glen Nora and Kambuzuma.
In all the stations we visited, polling was orderly, party agents were vigilant and there were no visible signs of any direct attempt to manipulate the elections at the polling station level.
There were, however, technical problems of people unable to find their names in the register.
There are also a few cases of people attempting to vote twice. These are, however, easily discovered.
The Elections Directorate in Zimbabwe uses a fluorescent liquid that voters are required to dip their hands in.
While the liquid appears invisible on the hand, when held under ultra-violet light, it glows visibly.
Each polling station has a small device with ultra-violet light that is battery powered. This easily exposes any person who attempts to vote multiple times.
Perhaps the only real drawback to the electioneering process itself has been the decision to reduce the number of polling stations in the urban centres in order to supplement those in the rural areas.
Whoever took this decision did a great disservice to the transparency of this election.
It is very easy to have foreseen that this decision will result in long queues in the urban centres especially considering the fact that in Harare and Chitungwiza, the elections were being held on a tripartite level; presidential, mayoral, and councillor.
All the polling stations we visited were packed with long queues of voters both on the first and second days of voting.
It is a credit to the determination of Zimbabwean voters, that people persevered in waiting for between eight to 10 hours in the queue to cast their votes.
As at the close of voting on Sunday, in Highfield, Mbare East and Mabvuku, long queues of between 600 to 700 voters remained.
The tragedy of this election is not the people who remained in the queues, but the hundreds or thousands of ordinary voters who probably returned home without casting their votes as a result of the long waiting time required to do so.
To soak up some of the pressure, polling stations were requested by the Elections Directorate to stay open till late in the night on the first day of the poll. Some stations remained open till dawn.
This did not, however, prevent the recurrence of long queues when polling opened at 7am on the second day of the poll.
Tempers were very short, and in a few places we got into uncomfortable scrapes.
In Kawudzana, irate voters complained to us about the slowness of the process when we arrived there.
Hoping that we as foreign observers could do something to speed up the process, they naturally felt disappointed when we were leaving the station without any visible improvement in the situation.
A few young men moved threateningly towards us shouting some unintelligible things in the Shona language. We vamoosed very quickly from the hotspot.
At Mbare East Stadium polling station, police had a hard time preventing clashes between MDC and ZANU PF youth.
Mayoral and Council registers.
The combination of three ballots in one, created problems for some voters.It meant that voters who wished to participate in all three ballots had to vote in their particular ward since their names were not contained in the mayoral and council registers in other wards.
In some polling stations some of such people were turned away, rather than being advised of their right to opt to vote only in the presidential poll.
After queuing for many hours, it is obvious that such people will not bother to attempt to vote at the correct station.
On the final day of voting the MDC filed a suit at the High Court requesting it to compel the Elections Directorate to extend the poll by one day.
The High Court granted the application. Government has complained that it is administratively difficult to comply with this directive nationwide.
The Minister of Justice, however, announced that the poll would remain open in Harare and Chitungwiza on the 11th of March.
By dawn in the morning of day three, there were queues in most high-density areas of the city.
However, as at 12pm no voting was taking place because the polling officials claimed they had not received any directives from the Ministry of Justice to that effect.
This has created quite some confusion and could be a very tempting reason to contest the result of the poll.
The MDC accuses Government of a deliberate attempt to disenfranchise urban voters who are generally believed to be supportive of the party.
We have reconnoitered some counting centres, but have not entered to participate in the count, because of a strange rule that says once you enter a counting centre, you may not leave until the final results are declared.
Certainly we do not want to be imprisoned unduly in any such centre, considering that we are scheduled to leave on the morning of Wednesday the 13th.
There are, however, Commonwealth and SADC observers covering the count at the constituency centre. All the parties also have their agents present at these counting centres.

Headline: Nigeria: A Dialogue With Village Elders
Body_Text:
Some elders at Piwoyi, a settlement along airport road, have expressed fear and frustration over plan to demolish their settlements, high cost of food items, scarcity of fertilizers and sundry matters. Yakubu Ozohu-Suleiman reports
Two Sundays ago, a visit to some village elders in Piwoyi turned out to be intriguing and thought-provoking. The elders, in their characteristic geriatric wisdom were seemingly well-informed on the subject of their conversation which centred on the planned demolition of all the towns and villages located along airport road by the government of the Federal Capital Territory.
The elders seemed embittered by the decision which they simply considered as a cruel onslaught on the indigenes who were settled long before the FCT was born. According to these elders, they did not have a fair relocation consideration similar to that which took the indigenes of Kukwaba from their location of the Abuja national stadium to Jibi. "Such an insincere resettlement scheme only succeed in swelling the bank accounts of contractors and some corrupt individuals," the elders stated, adding, "we are not sure that the present day government knows that there are poor people in existence. They keep inflicting hardship by the day. Electricity is good, but only those living in the inner city enjoy it. Look at water, there is no trace of pipe in this village, just as in many others where the commoners live."
The most striking point of the conversation was when the elders began to analyse the market prices of commodities, especially food items.
Food, they said, stand out among the basic requirements of the living man. The earth has food and other necessities, which could hardly be exhausted. But, "why are we hungry?" the elders asked.
"Back in the olden days," the elders said, "we produced abundance of the food we needed from manual farming. Today, we are told that there is mechanised farming by which surplus harvest can be got. Unfortunately, this is the time we are most confronted with hunger. A man would stay for a whole day without food. At best he eats once in a day. He needs money to buy food. But where is the money? Our children would go out and work. Their employers would not pay them. They say there is no money. We, the parents, remain at home in hunger."
It was at this point that the elders began to examine the various prices of food items in the market. Yam, they said, has become so expensive that the local farmers who produce them cannot eat. One of the elders said that a tuber of yam, on the average, costs N100, "whereas one who held N900, during our time, be a millionaire and would certainly need a pick-up van to convey such yams home."
"The more we try to farm, the more the land loses fertility, and the more we are told that we need fertiliser to boost produce and the more we are starved of the so-called fertilisers. You can't believe that, at best, what we receive in Piwoyi village here is five bags of fertilizers to be shared among almost 200 farmers. We had very poor harvest last year - yams were deplorably small in sizes and few in number.
"A mudu of garri now costs almost N100. This is because farmers can no longer produce cassava in a large quantity that would make such by-product as garri cheap. By the time cassava passed through three stages of supply, consumers would have to pay through their nose to get any bye-product therefrom."
The elders considered such things as television, radio, even fan, let alone cars, as luxuries of life. Maggi, as far as these village people are concerned, is equally luxury in cooking. "We use locust beans, which we are very satisfied with. It means a lot to spend N10 on magi each day one cook.
"This is a long time ago that garri ceased to be a friend of the masses." In fact, among all, the elders said, salt is the only food item whose price has remained consistent. Any part of this country you go, salt is readily cheap. So, apart from the free air the cheapest commodity on earth is most likely salt." The elders said, amidst laughter, that salt should be declared and given the award as the best economic product of life time.
What about rice and beans? Abuja Trust jokingly asked the elders. The elders replied in chorus, "they are not necessary food items. You cannot compare them with salt." The elders added that rice and beans, afterall are equally expensive such that during certain seasons, only a few can afford to buy them. How can we get N140 everyday to buy one mudu of rice which two of us here can finish at a sitting? They rhetorically asked.
There is no food that can be compared with salt in terms of cheapness, consistency of price and presence throughout seasons. This seems to be the position of the elders who also have a case to make with essential commodity such as palm oil. "Palm oil which we need for everyday cooking has also become so expensive that we have to be buying bit by bit. For God sake, where are our children leading us to? They claim to know more than we do but they have failed. They are greedy, they have lost their senses. They have kept us hungry amidst the inexhaustible that God provided for us."

Original_Search_Title: House: Minister Quarters Contract Inflated By N1.4 Bn
Headline: Nigeria: House: Minister Quarters Contract Inflated By N1.4 Bn
Body_Text:
Abuja — Bars Obasanjo's officer from N'Assembly
To examine el-Rufai for contempt
Nigeria paid the sum of N1.4 billion in excess for the acquisition and furnishing of a total of 63 houses for members of the Federal Executive Council and Special Advisers to the President in 1999.
This was the submission of the Ad hoc committee raised by the House of Representatives to investigate the purchase of the 63 houses by the Federal Capital Development Authority (FCDA) yesterday.
This revelation appears to be the first fruit of the resolution of the House three weeks ago to conduct a wide-ranging probe of the income and expenditure of the executive arm of government.
The ad hoc panel submitted the report on the day the House formally declared the Presidential Liaison Officer (PLO), Dr. (Mrs.) Esther Oduehi, a persona non-grata at the National Assembly complex.
While submitting its report to the plenary session of the House, the committee noted that the N3 billion spent on the acquisition and furnishing of the 63 houses was withdrawn from the Federation Account without due approval by the lawmakers.
Said the committee chairman, Hon. Iheanacho Obioma: "From the evidence of the Minister of Finance and Minister of the Federal Capital Territory (FCT), the 63 houses were bought and furnished at a cost of N3,019,153,178.06 instead of N2.74 billion approved by the president on September 10, 1999."
Obioma said that based on the valuation report of two estate agents, the purchase prices and furnishing costs of the houses were grossly inflated by N878.3 million and N552.3 million respectively.
He said that the committee also established that there was no budgetary provisions for the purchase of the houses in the 1999 and 2000 Appropriation Acts as well as in the supplementary appropriation passed by the National Assembly.
Obioma said that request for the purchase and furnishing of the houses was made to Obasanjo on September 2, 1999 while the supplementary bill was passed on Sept. 8, 1999 and contained no provision for the houses.
"It was also established that the furnishing was done arbitrarily ranging from N3.2 million to N12.1 million per house without regard to the relevant public and civil service regulations," he said.
He said that there was gross violation of the 1999 constitution, the Independent Corrupt Practices and other related offences Act and the Finance (Control and Management) Act.
Obioma said that the committee recommended that the president be cautioned to desist from further violations of the constitution and to immediately submit a supplementary bill to cover the purchase of the houses.
He said that the House should urge Obasanjo to set up a judicial commission of enquiry to probe the purchase and furnishing of the houses with a view to recovering the inflated amount and bring the culprits to book.
The raging war of nerves between the House and the Presidency assumed another dimension yesterday as the House barred Dr. Uduehi from entering its chambers or coming near the precinct of the National Assembly, warning that she risks arrest by the Sergeant-at-Arms.
Also, the House directed the Committee on Ethics and Privileges to investigate alleged violation of legislative privileges of the House by the Director General of the Bureau for Public Enterprise (BPE), Malam Nasir el-Rufai.
The PLO on Monday had accused the lawmakers of "insulting" and "blackmailing" the president over his insistence on accountability and transparency in governance.
The members, while deliberating on what they considered scathing comments on the House by the PLO, called on the Sergeant-at-Arms to the House and other security agents attached to the House to arrest Dr. Uduehi if she ventures near the precinct of the National Assembly or enters the chambers of the House of Representatives.
Deputy chairman of the House Committee on Justice, Human Rights and Legal Matters, Celestine Ughanze, who briefed the press after the plenary session, said categorically that nobody is entitled "to come into the chambers of the two houses of the National Assembly unless granted the privilege to enter."
He quoted sections 14 and 18 of the Legislative Houses (Powers and Privileges) Act CAP 208 of 1990 of the Laws of the Federation of Nigeria to support the ban on Uduehi. He explained that already the House had in a letter to President Obasanjo requested for the withdrawal of Uduehi as the PLO to the House.
Similarly, the House passed a motion directing its Committee on Ethics and Privileges to commence investigation on the alleged breach of legislative privileges by Malam el- Rufai.
According to the motion sponsored by John Agoda and 14 others, the House said the address by el-Rufai to the Ad-hoc Committee investigating the sale of NITEL was a "defamatory statement made and published by Mallam Nasir el-Rufai against the Speaker of the House of Representatives, Hon. Ghali Na'Abba, Hon. Nduka Irabor and the entire House of Representatives."
The motion read thus:
The House of Representatives set up an Ad-hoc Committee to investigate the privatization of NITEL;
The Bureau of Public Enterprises of which Mallam Nasir Ahmed El-Rufai is Director General, being a key player in the privatization of NITEL, was invited to answer questions;
The Director-General presented a paper dated March 5, 2002 (which became widely circulated) wherein he, instead of answering questions put to him, displayed utter disrespect, scorn and lack of confidence in the House of Representatives to inquire into the privatization process of NITEL (especially as regards the roles of the Bureau of Public Enterprises).
Agoda states amongst others thus:
The Punch Newspaper of Wednesday, March 6, 2002 on page 8.
"He discounted the opposition of the House of Representatives on the privatization programme, saying that the credibility of the legislators within the country and outside the shores of Nigeria is not the best and highest in the world."
On the paper he presented, he said amongst others:
"For all the reasons above, we want to make it abundantly clear that we do not have much confidence in the outcome of these proceedings. We however have respect for our democracy and its fledging institutions. We are therefore participating in this forum out of a constitutional responsibility, and respect for the House and the integrity of some of its members. We do not in all honesty expect a fair hearing."
"We understand the context in which this investigation is being conducted. We will not allow the privatization process to be held hostage to the personal interests of a few individuals. We realize that there is a lot at stake here, and there is a concerted attempt to stall the process." See pages 4 of the paper.
"Fundamentally, the Honourable Speaker's statement calls into question the whole basis of this investigation for if indeed the House has decided this to be the case, then it is quite clear that we are all wasting our time here as we would merely be props in the conclusion of a pre-determined outcome. It is difficult to conceive a situation where the Speaker having ruled on an issue, the Committee will come out with findings that are contrary to his views. In that case, we would merely be participating in a charade." See page 4 of paper.
"It is however worth mentioning that it is impossible to expect a fair outcome when the accuser is also the chairman of the committee that will establish the veracity or otherwise of the claims. Even in a village square setting, a man accused of stealing yams is not judged by his accusers but by a neutral party. No less a standard should be applicable to the House of Representatives of the Federal Republic, one cannot be a prosecutor, judge and jury".
The statements reproduced above are in their ordinary meaning contemptuous of the House of Representatives, and in violation of Section 24 of the Legislative Houses (Powers and Privileges) Act, Cap. 208, Laws of the Federation of Nigeria, 1990.
WHEREAS this Honourable House resolve as follows:
That the House Committee on Ethics and Privileges by virtue of Order 16 of the House Rules be and is hereby directed to inquire into and report on the extent of Mallam Nasir Ahmed El-Rufai's violation, if any, of the legislative privileges of this Honourable House.
Recommend appropriate measures the Committee deem fit.
In his comment supporting the motion, sponsor of the motion, Agoda quoted section 24 of the legislative Houses (powers and Privileges) act CAP 208 1990 of the laws of the Federation of Nigeria which reads " any person who (a) publishes any statement, whether in writing or otherwise, which falsely or scandalously defames a legislative house or any committee there of ; or (b) publishes any writing reflecting on the character of the President or Speaker, as the case may be, of a legislative house, or chairman of a committee in the conduct of his duty as such President, Speaker or Chairman or (c)publishes any writing containing a gross, wilful or scandalous misrepresentation of the proceedings of a legislative house or of any member in the proceedings of a legislative house, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine of two hundred naira or to imprisonment for twelve months, or to both such fine and imprisonment."
Agoda, who described the utterances of el-Rufai as contemptuous, called on the members to guide the institution of the House jealously as such utterances from the Director General of the BPE was, he said, capable of bringing the integrity of the House to ridicule.
He was supported by the Chairman of the House Committee on Petroleum, West Idahosa, who said that el-Rufai went too far by describing the investigation that was authorised by a resolution of the House as a charade.
He said that it was not only an attempt to deride the House and its resolution, but also an attempt to incite the people against the members of the House.
Hon. Ibrahim Ganyama informed the House of how a Director of NITEL, Mr. Tunji Bello, allegedly insulted another member, Hon. Temi Harriman in the course of her duty.
Thus, the House directed its Committee on Ethics and Privileges to investigate the utterances of el-Rufai and the recalcitrant posture of Mr. Bello, and recommend appropriate actions and measures to be taken against the officers within two weeks.
Uduehi had on Monday at a press conference in Abuja attributed the decision of the House to investigate Obasanjo to selfish motives.
"The President is not being persecuted and insulted by the leadership and some members of the House for non-performance, but for his high level of accountability and transparency in government business which cannot be negotiated no matter the opposition. The genesis of the crisis can be traced to Mr. President's desire to know the wage bill of members of the House," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Gbagyi: Great Farmers of the North!
Headline: Nigeria: Gbagyi: Great Farmers of the North!
Body_Text:
The Gbagyi, popularly referred to us the 'Gwari' are spread across a number of states in the country. They have an interesting culture, which forbids placing have the head. They are famous for investing places with quite poetical names, and they have and they have an interesting culture of courtship and marriage Yakubu Ozohu-Suleiman recently researched into the Gbagyi.
There are many people in Nigeria and even beyond, who are familiar with the name Gwari, and have encountered some of the people so addressed, but have either heard of Gbagyi as a name of a tribe, or do simply believe that Gbagyi is a tribe other than Gwari who live in the Northern part of the country.
These people are most likely to be surprised, if today they are told that there is no tribe in any part of Nigeria known genuinely as Gwari. History indeed has always produced a phenomenal situation that makes the life of man interesting, and the people who are known and addressed as Gwar,i are not indeed Gwari. They are Gbagyi. Gwari is simply the name of a specie of yam cultivated by the Gbagyi people of Northern Nigeria. Ghagyi indeed are known to be great farmers, producing yams, and a host of other agricultural produce. Gwari happens to be one of the best species of yams produced by these persons, which attracted the interest of people from within the north, and other parts of the country Majority of these customers seemed only to know one thing which is the name of the specie of yam they wanted to buy from a people whose tribal name they did not really know. So, over a long period of time, the name of the yam took prominence over that of the tribe, which produces it, thus producing yet another accident of history.
As great farmers and hunters, Gbagyi, who have registered their presence in many parts of the country todays they are believed to have migrated from Zaria in present day Kaduna State where, indeed, their origin is traced. An adventure, which began long before the colonial era, has today earned for the Gbagyi, not only public respect as great farmers of the North, but also as unquestionable status as indigenes of Abuja the present Federal Capital Territory.
Without any recourse to fighting for ownership of land, Chief John B. Garra, a prominent Gbagyi leader felt it was necessary for people to known that Zaria, as it is called, is only a product of an altered nomenclature "The original name given by Gbagyi who settled and named the region centuries ago is Za'ghiya, a Gbagyi word which means "Civilisation."
"Ze la ghiya" in Gbagyi, means, "the eyes have opened." In other words, a person has come to know what he or she was ignorant of. The story goes thus:
"A long time ago when the Gbagyi settled in the virgin land of Zaria, any stranger (individual or group) who came into the place were considered by the Gbagyi as uncivilised. The Gbagyi then, considered themselves as more civilised than anyother persons. So, after spending a period of time with the Gbagyi in the land of Zaria, the persons, eye is considered open to civilisation. Then the Gbagyi would say to him "za la Ghiya", meaning his eyes have opened to civilisation Zaria as it is presently called is a tongue of the modern times" Chief Gatta clarified.
In fact there is a striking coincidence between this name and the present status of Zaria as a prominent educational town in Nigeria. Perhaps this was a divine calling of the region, or a calculated matching of name and status by our heroes past.
Perhaps also, there is a direct correlation between the meaning found in the word "Gbagyi" and the general trait of humility among the Gbagyi race. The word Gbagyi is interpreted to mean "place yourself low before others", in other words "be humble and modest". "Oza" in the Gbagyi have just as in Ebira language means human being or 'somebody', "Gba" means low. Gbagyi, co-ordinated their relationships with other people whenever they find themselves.
The history of migration has always found explanation in either the search by man for a peaceful environment to live in, or such environment that best suits their activity or occupation as a group. Zaria, according to oral accounts was a vast expanse of forest inhabited by such wild life as lions, tigers, leopards, elephant, and a host of others. These animals were said to be a threat to the Gbagyi, some of whom later decided to seek refuge in other places. Faced with this, a civil war and over bearing external aggression, a proportion of Gbagyi population were said to have fled in search of a peaceful environment, convenient enough for their farming occupation. Some moved towards the inner north, some went west, and others southward, and finally settled in places like Niger, Kaduna, Plateau, Nasarawa, Kwara and Kogi States, and what later came to be known as Abuja, the Federal Capital Territory.
With the prevalence of wild animals in Zaria, the Gbagyi had their first major occupation in hunting. Farming in which they have become famous today, was only a secondary occupation among them. Other economic activities in which the Gbagyi are engaged, are mat weaving and firewood gathering. The culture of mat weaving is so cherished by the Gbagyi, such that up till today an elder would not fail to include in his statement of will, that he or she be buried with a mat, and not some length of cloth or a coffin, as is commonly used in modern times.
In the palace of every traditional Gbagyi King, at least a statue of a lion must be found. The Gbagyi believe the lion is the king of animals. This is anchored in their experiences of how a lion used to kill other animals back in Zaria.
In the whole of Nigeria, and probably Africa, the Gbagyi is the only race whose tradition forbids carrying any load on the head. They would rather carry a load on the shoulder. In fact this culture has made the Gbagyi a distinct race among many in Nigeria.
Elder Nuhu, a Gbagyi and an indigene of Abuja, explains this culture thus. "The head accommodates the most important parts of the body system. The nose which we to use breath and sustain life; the mouth through which we eat, they eyes which we use to see; the ear through which we hear, and the brain by which we think, and which co-ordinates every activity of the body. The head is the king of the body, which must not be loaded at all. The shoulder carries the load better. This is our belief."
Another reason for the culture of carrying load on the shoulder, according to Elder Nuhu, is that the early men among the Gbagyi were such people that never liked to expose their armpits. "You know our forefathers did not wear cloth. They used to tie leaves to cover their private parts. The women too do the same. They even cover their breast with leaves. So, the Gbagyi of the early times believe that the armpit also a private part of the body, which should not be exposed. And you know when you carry load on your head, your hands are raised up, thereby exposing your armpits. By putting a load on the shoulder, this s avoided! Your armpit cannot be seen."
Another reason given for the culture of carrying load on the shoulders is that the shoulders are more flexible than the head. First it can tolerate a heavier load. Second, when one is tired, he can move the load from one shoulder to the other. This certainly cannot happen with the head as it is not more than one" elder Nuhu said.
In modern times some Gbagyi have been seen carrying loads on their heads. This is most certainly due to the attention that education and civilisation have brought to body decoration. There is no hard punishment stipulated in the Gbagyi tradition for violators of the culture of carrying load on the shoulders. There is no doubt that it presents a viable and better way of carrying loads over long distances, despite the shoulder scars and alteration of body posture, that have become of the Gbagyi in this respect.
Marriage systems
In the tradition of the Gbagyi, no form of sexual relationship in permitted between two persons of opposite sex before they are legitimately married. Early (teenage) marriage is permitted, however. A young man who picks interest in a girl is equally forbidden from advancing directly to the girl. He is expected to make known his interest through his senior brothers. It is his brothers who now contact the girls relations who may not, at first involve the girl's parents. The girl's consent is sought, and after this point, the message gets to her parents whose consent is also sought.
After the parents' consent is obtained, arrangement is now made as to when the dowry would be paid. Meanwhile the young man and his prospective bride are not permitted to meet in person. Dowry is not paid in the form of money. At the appropriate time, the young man is expected to organise his age group to farm for his prospective in-laws seven times, which may take a period of seven years. The farming is done just one day, each year, from dusk to dawn. If the man is in a hurry to get his wife, he maybe permitted to farm more than once as the case may be, in a year.
However, seven days to the farming exercise, a traditional alcoholic drink known as Burukutu ('oje' in Gbayi) is prepared and delivered to the parents of the girl as a part of the dowry.
Strictly speaking, a decision has not been reached even at this point as to whether the man would finally marry the girl. He is expected to prove himself in the farm. If in the course of making ridges, for instance, one of his age group proves physically stronger than him, the girl would automatically be given to the man. The man who initiated the request would be dropped. The essence of this is to encourage him to work harder, so that his prospective in-laws would be sure that their daughter would not be hungry in his home. The heartbreak and anguish associated with such outcome is lessened by preventing sexual contact between the girl and the man.
The Gbagyi people are believed to be the major indigenes of Abuja, Nigeria's capital city. This is evident in the names of places found in the Federal Capital Territory as explained by Chief John B. Garra and Elder Nuhu: "Utako is a rather wrong pronunciation of the original name given to the area by the Gbagyi. We call it "kuta kwo", meaning a strong stone, 'kuta' in Gbagyi means stone "kwo" means "strong".
"Kukuaba" is a place in Abuja, which is almost forgotten. It is the place where the present Abuja National Stadium is located. The actual name is "Akuku-Nwamba" meaning, 'soft rock', which was the dominant geographical feature in the area, as the Gbagyi came to settle.
Maitama, which is one of the most prominent places in Abuja, has its nomenclature anchored in the experience of the first Gabgyi man who lived in the area. The actual name is 'ma taama" which in Gbagyi means "I refused to leave.
The first Gbagyi settler in Maitama was said to have encountered stiff antagonism from people who later came to settle with him. They did all they could to discourage him so that he would migrate from the place. But he was said to have insisted on living in the area. Hence he says "ma taama" meaning "I would not leave." As more and more people came to settle with him, he decided to name the place "mataama" which was later altered to become Maitama.
'Aleita, in Gbagyi means "domain of locust beans." Lei means locust beans tree, which was very common in the region presently known as as aleita along Airport road. The place was therefore named after this geographical feature.
Lugbe, also located along Airport road, presents another funny nomenclature. In Gbagyi "olu" means 'Birds'. According to a source the first Gbaygi who lived in Lugbe was said to have built a very small hut for himself. The hut was so small that the people used to mock him to the effect that his house resembled a bird's nest, asking whether he was actually building a nest where he would be rearing birds. This was how the place became known as Lugbe.
Garki was completely altered from the name the Gbagyi had given to the place. There used to be a huge mass of black hill in Garki which the Gbagyi's call "okpeyyi." Okpe" in Gabgyi means "hill" "Okpeyyi" unfortunately was lost along the line, as people began to call the place Garki. "So koi yi" as contained in the letter headed paper of the chief of Garki is said to be a way of immortalising the original Garki nomenclature.

Headline: Uganda: Organic Farming is Their Backbone
Body_Text:
Gabula Atudde women group aims at boosting household food production in peri-urban Mukono
At the crack of dawn, Sarah Katende sets off for her field. In the garden, next to her homestead, she hoes out the weeds and Katende mulches her garden. Around 8:00 am, she returns home to feed her cows and do domestic chores. Thereafter, Katende proceeds to her workplace - Mukono Town council - where she works as a town agent and a community development assistant.
The burly forty-year-old farmer lives in Bugujju village in Mukono district. She is a single mother. Katende caters for three children. The oldest is an undergraduate at Makerere University.
Two miles from Katende's garden, 37-year-old Ruth Kafeero is tending her crops too. She is busy digging and weeding in her garden. She has five children. Her husband is a mechanic.
The women are members of Gabula Atudde women group, established in 1992.
Gabula Atudde is a Luganda phrase which implies that if you do not work, you cannot get a better social position or status.
Gabula Atudde mainly aims at boosting household food production in peri-urban areas. The group encourages working together. This has facilitated the transfer of agricultural knowledge and farming skills from one member to another.
Katende talks with enthusiasm about the group: "Over the years, farm output has increased due to our efforts", she discloses with a smile.
Katende attributes their success in achieving self-sufficiency in food production to Peter Lusembo, the centre manager, Mukono Agricultural Research and Development Centre (ARDC).
The centre has designed one-day intensive courses to equip the women with farming knowledge. The courses include crop, animal and soil husbandry.
"We are trained in modern agriculture. The training is paying dividends through high yields," Katende stresses.
The women grow bananas, maize, oranges, yams, beans, cassava, and potatoes.
Mrs. Christine Luyombya, 75, the chairperson of the group says, "our objective among other things is to improve the welfare of women." Luyombya is a widow. She has eight children.
When the programme began, 10 years ago, the women were ignorant of organic gardening. Luyombya remembers the birth of the organisation.
"We began with only 10 members. The members were not fully conversant with organic farming".
Today, the women are armed with knowledge about organic agriculture. "We use cow dung, urine and decaying plants," Katende explains. "We also make compost bags. The bags contain a mixture of decaying plants, cow dung and soil. We live in a peri-urban area. The members have little land. Compost bags enable us to farm even on the veranda".
Organic gardening has proved a valuable contribution to food production. The crops fertilised by natural manure produce high yield and are free from pesticides. Now the women harvest enough food to feed their families. Sometimes they produce surplus food.
"We sell the surplus. The money got is kept in the bank", says Juliet Ochieng, 36. "When a member gets a problem, we provide moral and material support".
The project has enabled the women to improve on their nutrition. It also provides a lifeline for the families.
"We eat a balanced diet. It includes a variety of vegetables and fruits from our gardens," explains Kafeero. "The money I get from the sale of some produce is a welcome addition to the family income."
She talks about another benefit of the project - social solidarity.
"We hold gift days after every three months. Members exchange gifts on this occasion," reveals Kafeero. "This strengthens the bond of mutual affection."
The women contribute sh100 whenever they meet.
"We save the money and use it for buying craft materials," Katende says. "We meet once every week."
The group engages in other income generating activities, besides farming. Kafeero turns to the subject of craft making.
"We meet on Thursdays to learn craftmaking. We make mats, bags, baskets, and bedcovers. We also knit table cloths. We sell our crafts to people who want them."
In 1997, the women set up a bakery.
"We bake cakes and buns", says Luyombya. "They are often bought by the members."
The group also runs a brickmaking project in lower Kauga in Mukono.
Gabula Atudde has enhanced the status of the women. Their major problem, however, is lack of funds to run the organisation efficiently.
"We primarily depend on our savings and members' contributions. Membership fee is sh3,000 yearly," says Katende." Sometimes well-wishers donate money to the organisation. Nonetheless, our revenue is insufficient. There is a small market for our products".
The group liaises closely with other organisations such as Kulika Charitable Trust Horizone T3000 and Young women Christian Association (YWCA).
"YWCA teaches us how to grow vegetables and to cater for our husbands," Kafeero says.
Do their husbands like the group's activities?
"We are taught how to look after our husbands. We are better wives and mothers. Why should they complain?" asks Kafeero.
Her husband fully concurs with her.
"My wife is an excellent housekeeper," he says proudly. "Besides my family has two incomes now." His face shines with profound satisfaction.
In spite of money constraints, the women are resolute in their struggle for self-reliance.
"Our fight against poverty and food insecurity continues. We believe our efforts are paying off," concludes Luyombya optimistically.

Headline: Nigeria: Pen Pusher in America
Body_Text:
In seat 35B, I fastened my seat belt following instruction from the pilot of the Lufthansa airline, the German national carrier. The Boeing 747 was gaining momentum to depart Murtala Muhammed Airport, Lagos on a 6-hour flight to Frankfurt. Seated next to me was Promise Okekwe, head of the public affairs section of Liberty Bank, Nigeria. The two of us, including 10 others scattered in the economy class of the aircraft were billed for a five-week sojourn in the United States of America, as pioneer participants of the Azikiwe Professional Development Programme (APDF). Therefore, Frankfurt, Germany by our schedule is but a stop over.
As the big bird balanced in the skies, and safety formalities over-as amply demonstrated by the air hostesses-I reclined my seat, jet set to first storm an European airport and then Washington DC. My ever reliable wristwatch-its called Olympio-at a glance communicated time and date to me: 11:55 post meridien; September 30, 2000. If you know what I mean that is 19 days after September 11 (much ado about it?) In my shoes you couldn't wish the terrible day away not when endless questions beat about my mind. Would I, I thought, be admitted into the US after the terrorist attacks on New York and Washington, particularly when the name on my passport begins with Muhammad-a Muslim name-and Muslims, though terrorist/fundamentalist Muslims of Middle East stock, had been fingered as culprits for the attacks? Even though I know I was the farthest thing from a terrorist-ideologically and practically-a remote feeling weighed heavy that an over zealous security personnel may want to scrunitise me on the basis of my names more than was necessary. In other to switch to another frame of mind, and perhaps enjoy airborne atmosphere better-like music, like movies and moving trays of coffee and strange cuisine-I therefore decided to adopt a journalistic countenance to whatever lay ahead. I am not on test, I said to myself. It is rather going to be an acid test for the United States. How it is able to receive the likes of me without prejudice would prove whether in their desire which I share, to contain terrorism, security had become frantic and impudent.
From nine options of on-air audio music channels, I deliberately selected a jazz station from where strident horns and strings from maestros-Dizzy Gillespie, Louis Armstrong, John Coltrane, Miles Davis-wafted successively, interspersingly cooling and raising my nerves. Damn! I was in New Orleans already even though it was not on our itenary. The Azikiwe Fellows, according to the programme, would visit sites in Washington DC and Maryland and spend more time in Denton, Texas. As soon as "Night Life," the Crusaders classic twanged a last note, I became aware I was prodding myself to explain how I felt-as I was alas America bound. My first trip across the Atlantic. Instantly, I recalled that Dr Mitch Land, director Mayborn Graduate Institute of Journalism, University of North Texas, had also asked me a similar question during his visit to Kaduna to assess me. But his was: "Rabiu, how do you feel selected as an Azikiwe Fellow, to travel with 11 others on a professional training in the United States?" Pinching from Shakespeare, I remember saying "well, silence is the perfectest way to herald joy." Deep within myself I was nothing but fervent.
We arrived Frankfurt at dawn. The city was still a bit dark, and because I wasn't occupying a window seat all I could make out was distant kleighlights. Nevertheless, I had enough information which highlighted the city's features and its prime place among Europe's leading cities. Since we were connecting another flight to Washington Dulles Airport various notices guided us to a particular lounge we were supposed to wait until our flight at 1:00pm. With time then at about 6:30-7:00pm, four of us Azikiwe Fellows-Rilwan Abdulsalam (librarian, National Universities Commission), Alhaji Nasarawa (director, Kano State Library Board), Alhaji Lawal Usman (director, computer services of the National Assembly, Abuja), and myself performed ablution in the rest room after which we said a congregational prayer, led by Rilwan. Within the six hours we spent at the airport, all Azikiwe fellows fraternised among themselves and shared common hopes and aspirations.
At 1:00pm we were ready for take-off on board a United Airlines flight, an American carrier but which is also part of the Alliance group in which major western airlines collaborated to fly passengers from different regions of the world depending on their areas of strength. Flight time, according to the pilot will be 7 hours. As we cruised over the Atlantic, and every inch of our progress displayed on TV screens directly facing you, it struck me that the carrier-or United-as it is simply and legendarily called-was in its hour of grief. It's planes were the ones hijacked by terrorists in the September 11, spectre.
Consequently, many employees had had to be sacked when revenue dwindled as a result of low patronage. Indeed special funds exist to assist United staff who lost their lives on September 11, and passengers could contribute on board, if they wished.
I did many things on board United. Dozed off time and time again. Read, ate, meticulously avoiding bacon. Drank tea, water. Chatted. And principally prayed to arrive safely on American soil which we did at 5:00pm Sunday. A bus car conveyed us to the arrival terminal to go through customs and immigration. Routine security checks here were really serious, and no one it seemed was exempted. But while my colleagues passed through without much of a hindrance, it would appear my earlier apprehension was coming to pass. At the immigration counter, I was asked to go in for more questioning. A Chinese-American or so packed my papers and led me in. A Ghanaian who said he's a fashion designer was there as I got in. So was a tall Asian-either a Palestinian or Afghan, certainly from the Middle East. His wife was with him. After about 15 minutes a female immigration official who was evidently pregnant announced my name asking me to come to her desk. My problem, she told me was why my visa was issued only a day before I travelled when the trip had been planned more than a month before. After simple explanations she stamped my passport permitting me entry. "Welcome to America," she said. "Routine checks."
When I claimed my luggage Mrs Lee Mitch Land, wife of Dr Land and coordinator of the APDF programme was on hand to welcome me, help me with my luggage and led me to where others were already waiting in a van for onward journey to our hotel. My colleagues bombarded me with questions, wanting to know what happened. I told them to keep their cool, that I will explain when I can. What I wanted the most at that moment was silence so I could sit back and watch my new environment-the drive from Dulles Airport to Washington suites Hotel, Virginia. And I did just that.
On the drive which took about 40 minutes, I concentrated my viewing on the well laid out roads-freeways they call it-our equivalent of expressways; and the way Americans drive, whether, for instance what we see in movies-dare-devil driving-was a reality or not. On the contrary, I realised that Americans obey traffic laws with a passion, especially traffic lights. It is also a must to fasten seat belts otherwise you are fined. We drove carefully like that to the hotel we were booked into many days, as we were told, before our arrival. Surprisingly though the hotel, Washington Suites is not situated in Washington DC where going by our programme we were supposed to visit historical sites for some days. Virginia, which is where the hotel is, is a next door neighbour to the capital of the United States and has a lesser cost of accommodation. Having settled in suite 508, which I shared with National Assembly's Lawal Usman, we all gathered at 6:00pm for a briefing by our coordinator, Dr Mitch Land. Aside the 12 fellows were two other officials of the University of North Texas-Dr Oppong who is a professor of geography and Mr Ben Williams a graduate student and special assistant to Dr Land. Lee Ann, Dr Land's genial wife was also present.
By the itenary given to each of us, eight of the fellows-the four journalists (including me) and four of the librarians will spend 10 days doing the rounds in DC, while the four internet connectivity fellows will spend only 4 days and then move to Denton, Texas ahead of us. Our feeding allowances we were told, would be $42 dollars per day while in DC and $30 dollars when we moved to Texas. The disparity, Dr Land explained was because DC was a more expensive place where an average meal costs $15 dollars and above. However, our first place of call, the following day, October 1-Nigeria's national day-will be to the US State Department. So we were issued with metro-underground train-tickets that would last 10 days, and some four days. Dr Land's wife also presented us each with a basket containing bread, biscuits, oranges, apples and a range of assorted chocolates. Afterwards we seemed to be all exhausted and everyone retired early.
Monday, October 1st we were driven in the Washington Suites bus service to Vandorn street metro station to embark on our first official engagement. 15 of us, we were a curious sight to many who saw us because we moved in a group. Some of us looking every inch Africans, who may have arrived as tourists, and some not, and then the sprinkling of two white Americans-Ben and Dr Land. However it appeared that we basked in the curiosity we were stirring. We took a metro "Blue Line" to foggy Bottom Gwu (George Washington University) station. Dr Land led us through 23rd Street, then crossed Virginia Avenue to 2201 st. where we came upon the Department of State. We were five minutes late for our 9:30 am appointment. It didn't appear to matter as we were thoroughly checked and guided upstairs to the African section where officials were waiting to receive us. Messrs Cox and Robinson, over tea and coffee explained to us for the first time the main purpose of the Azikiwe Fellowship, which our group was pioneering.
According to Robinson, the African section of the Department of State was given 15 minutes to come up with a programme that will expose and hone key Nigerian institutions that are central to the information super structure in maintaining democratic norms after Nigeria's return to democracy. It was, he said, at the wee hours of the Clinton administration and a few days to the then outgoing president's trip to Nigeria. They faced two dilemmas: One, the institutions to incorporate. Secondly, what to name the programme. Finally, the library, information technology and journalism were considered as key elements that could form a synergy. Thus four libraries, one each from the Nigerian Institute of International Affairs, National Universities Commission, Kano State Library, and the National Assembly were selected; and four internet connectivity personnel (one each from the National Assembly, University of Port Harcourt Nunet Centre, the Presidency, and the Kaduna State Judiciary); and four journalists (yours truly from the Weekly Trust newspaper in Kaduna, Punch newspaper, Lagos, Channels Television, Lagos and the head of communications, Liberty Bank, Lagos; the latter a rather strange mix). To give a name to the programme, Robinson said they unanimously agreed on the name of Nnamdi Azikiwe, Nigeria's first president and pioneer journalist and publisher of many independence struggle tabloids. According to them, the late Chief Nnamdi Azikiwe is among a few Nigerians accepted across Nigeria's ethnic and religious divides.
At this, all of us heaved a sigh of relief, agreeing they couldn't have done better. Robinson prayed we would enjoy our visit and professionally benefit from it as "everything possible had been done to give the distinguished fellows the right kind of contact with America's democratic institutions."
Since it was October 1st, Nigeria's independence, we asked to discuss our country vis-à-vis its 41st birthday. We all variously maintained that democracy was inviolable but some of us, Emeka Madunagu of the Punch and me, the newspaper minds on the team, posited that the current leadership in Nigeria is running a charade and had be told. Our colleagues, mostly government people were not happy with our opinion saying we were washing our dirty linen outside. We countered that we also 'wash' it inside. Nevertheless, it was clear that we were actually not speaking before 'outsiders' but with 'insiders' - people who were more aware of what is happening in Nigeria than some of us probably did. Robinson and Cox indeed displayed a keen knowledge of Nigeria, past and present.
Three hours later when we got through with the Department of State, a tour of the White House area which was on our programme was cancelled due to the September 11 attacks. The area was now a no-go zone but tourists could go round the fence but not into the lawns as was previously possible. So we went to visit the Vietnam Veterans Memorial and the Korean War Veterans Memorial both located at the West Potomac Park, which is a strolling distance from the Department of State. As we stood on 17th Street, facing us from the opposite were two of America's most important monuments: The Lincoln Memorial across the long reflecting pool of water and the Washington Monument which erects boldly into the sky: Even now, back in Nigeria, I see the Washington Monument as some unshakable structure with roots that go deep down; it tells me of a plant that will be there for all time. But Lincoln Memorial is as majestic as Abraham Lincoln's sitting position: posed solidly like the king of the world - perhaps of the 'democratic world?' By the time we went through the Franklin Delano Roosevelt and Thomas Jefferson Memorials around what is called the Tidal Basin I came off with the impression that Washington is indeed a monumental city.
The following day, October 2, the library of congress turned out to be a building like no other I have yet seen. It is a bewildering building that makes you wonder how it was constructed in the firs place. As for the 'insides' it is simply ethereal. The library collected materials in nearly every field of knowledge in a variety of formats. We were told that Thomas Jefferson, a war-time US president sold his personal library for $23,950 to the library of congress in 1815 following Britain's burning of the capital (where the library was initially located). He believed that "there is no subject to which a member of congress may not have occasion to refer." The statement is said to remain the guiding principle of the library's collecting policy.
Established in 1800, the library of Congress is an unparalleled world resource. The collection include more than 28 million catalogued books and other print materials in 460 languages. Indeed, offer me a roof in America and I will choose the library of congress where in the main reading room I'll daily look up to see contributors to humanity's progress. Islam, it said, contributed physics, Egyptian civilisation. There were more but another daily companion will be the sculpture of Herodotus representing history.
Later in the afternoon we were at the Voice of America. At the Hausa section of the Africa Service, we met for the first time since our arrival, our countrymen. Sunday Dare, new head of the Hausa Service and a former editor at the News magazine in Nigeria welcomed us. There were also Aliyu Mustapha, Kabir Isa Jikamshi, Aliyu Danladi, Hadiza Isa Wada, Shehu Kaura, all of the Hausa Service. There was also Shaka Ssale, the Ugandan and presenter of Straight Talk Africa, as well as Josephine Kamara. Four of us, Rilwan, Nasarawa, Lawan and myself were interviewed on the Hausa Service, after which we met with the legendary Donna Butler, producer of many a Hausa service programme and whose name is known to listeners across Nigeria.
The following day, October 4, we were before Professor Jibril Aminu, the Nigerian Ambassador to the US at the Embassy building in central DC. Dr Land, deploying his oratorical skills to the hilt briefed Ambassador Aminu on our mission after which questions and answers followed. A group photograph followed and we were treated to a refreshment. The Ambassador promised also to be present later in the evening at the reception in our honour by the office of Representative William Jefferson, a co-sponsor of the Africa Growth and Opportunities Act (AGOA).
The reception held at the Capitol Building Cannon 121 was interesting in many respects. First, we had a chance to meet with members of the US congress and their staff who shared ideas with us. Secondly, Nigeria was discussed with promises by the congressmen to work harder, legislatively for Nigeria's nay Africa's democratic and economic turn around.
The next day our tour included a visit to the Newseum and Freedom Centre, opposite the magnificent USA today building. The Newseum is indeed one of a kind. It's a modern museum documenting news and their organs, even archivally in print, audio and visuals. It is a really captivating 'world.' Ms Joan Mower, director Africa, Latin America programmes of the Freedom Forum received us with a rather sad news that the forum had just cancelled its programmes for Africa and that she was technically out of a job herself. While discussing the press in Nigeria, which she was sufficiently familiar with, Ms Mower spoke the minds of many that newspapers in Nigeria have not been able to detach from ethnic and religious passions in their reports. She said also that not only were they too verbose but they were pretentiously "national" whereas their true competence is only regional. She declared that there are actually no national newspapers yet in Nigeria. I couldn't agree more.
Next day at the Washington Times, we, Emeka of Punch, Stella Din of Channels television and Promise Okekwe sat through an editorial meeting at the paper. After which, finally, we thought, editorial meetings are the same all over the world. The Washington Times we were told is considered a conservative paper which gives open support to Republican regimes and that it is meant to rival the Washington Post as a dominant paper in the American capital. The paper's circulation is about 70,000 daily, and most subscribers reside in the D.C area.
Same day at the National Public Radio (NPR), we spent two hours discussing radio technology, and the concept of public radio which in the case of the NPR came by congressional legislation. The NPR is a non governmental public funded station which produces programmes for sale to hundreds of frequency modulated (FM) radio stations across the American nation. Unlike Nigeria's FRCN, the NPR is truly independent and broadcasts programmes in the interest of the American public, and not the government.
The rest of our stay in D.C involved making the rounds among the cluster of museums called the Smithsonian. From the American Museum of art to the Museum of American History, to the Air and Space Museum, it was clear that the U.S is a country steeped in the culture of preservation. To be part of history myself, I stood beside the (original!) Apollo 11 space shuttle with a robot-like Neil Armstrong in the cockpit. From the earliest Wright brothers planes to the latest in civilian, military and space shuttles, America documents them at the Air and Space Museum. And they are all real.
On Wednesday, October 10, we arrived Denton, Texas on a United Airlines flight. The air trip took about three hours to the DFW (Dallas Fort Worth) airport. Dr. Joseph Oppong, the Ghanaian lecturer who had earlier left with the four internet fellows to Texas, was on hand at the airport to receive us. Within one hour we were fully settled at our hotel, the comfort Suites which is close to the UNT.
Here, called the west, the weather was more akin to Nigeria's - bright and sunny - unlike Washington in the east which was occasionally cold.
During a short briefing by Dr Land, our programme here - we journalists that is - would include workshops and lectures at the university, and that the two print journalists were to begin working at the Denton Record Chronicle, a small-sized newspaper by American standards which serves the people of Denton county. We promptly showed up at the paper on Thursday, October 11, led by Dr. Land. The paper's editor, Jim Flansby handed us over to the city editor, unarguably a veteran journalist named Mike Trimble - who now took us round introducing us to each and every staff, including marketing and advertising and other technical operators at the press.
