Emeka and I were given seats with personal computers before us, programmed with our names and instructed on how operations run daily in the paper. Our work we were informed will begin in earnest on Monday, October 15.
Before then, however, we were - all 12 fellows - hosted to a reception on Friday by the Mayborne Graduate Institute of Journalism, after which we had a two-hour workshop on Front Page, a web design training.
As is traditional with the University of North Texas since its establishment 27 years ago, every 13th October is a homecoming day for former students and parents of students. So Saturday it was. Every faculty put up what is called a float, in which traditional dresses would be worn and in a convoy the university would be traversed on foot and in cars, with drums beating stridently. Azikiwe fellows in our various traditional wears (yours truly in Kaftan and a cap) rode on the journalism float.
After the rounds everyone proceeded to an open place close to the school's stadium where tents representing each faculty were put up. Here, drinks, foods, wears and souvenirs were sold. We got a chance - Emeka and I - to speak with members of the National Association for the Advancement of Coloured People (NAACP), UNT branch who talked to us as 'brothers' updating us on issues of racism and other forms of discrimination. To start with, they said, as a testimonial to the continuation of racism black music is not still played during Homecoming at UNT.
Later in the evening the UNT football (American soccer) team called - can you believe it? - the Green Eagles engaged in a match with another university. I went along with Ben Williams and his friend Brent to cheer the 'Mean Green Eagles' who were said to be a terrible side, always losing matches home and away. I told my friends, however, that Nigeria's national soccer team is called the Super Eagles, but initially Green Eagles were a strong team, perhaps the best in Africa and always win their matches. So with me there to cheer the Mean Green, I added, victory was assured. And so it happened as UNT walloped their opponents.
The following day the Pan African Students Association (PASA) of UNT honoured Azikiwe fellows with a reception in the night. The president of the association, a Nigerian called Ifeanyi, together with his colleagues cooked assorted Nigerian dishes from pounded yam and egusi to jollof rice. We also in turn donated some cash to the association to maintain their secretariat. Patron of the Association, Dr. Al Bavon, a Ghanaian assistant professor of economics commended members of PASA saying they were true representatives of Africa.
Among the several workshops and interactive sessions organised for journalists, the National Association of Black Journalists (NABJ) organised lecture, in conjunction with Model International Organisation (MIO), a student foreign affairs body, was significant. The topic, "Democracy in Nigeria" was discussed with professor Van Doepp of the political science department moderating.
The second time the NABJ held another interactive session with yours truly and Emeka Madunagu of Punch as discussants, Mr Gary Reaves a veteran TV reporter with WFAA TV - a member of the Belo Group which also owns the Denton Record Chronicle, TXCN TV, and the Dallas Morning News - moderating, the turnout was a full house. Next morning it was reported in the NT Daily, the university's daily tabloid with Emeka's picture and I, on the cover.
However, on Friday October 26, the biggest reception was held for all Azikiwe fellows at the home of the secretary of the Dallas Black Chamber of Commerce, Nigeria's Edward E. Okpa II - no doubt a successful businessman; an immigrant who has risen high in the Dallas business circle. Held beside the blue pool waters of Okpa's home in Dallas, it was at the reception we were issued with certificates where UNT president and other officials, including Dallas legislators and crème of society came together to give us a befitting American treat with a live Jazz band. It was our night, which rhymed with the perplexing neonlights of night time Dallas.
Two days later, Sunday October 28, we were to repeat a trip to Dallas, for the rodeo - a medley of cowboys, games including risky bull and horse riding competition. In fact, a day before at Fort Worth, a typical cowboys city we had gone, a few of us - led by Ben Williams and Brent - to see traditional cowboys setting, eat their kind of food, listen to their music and perhaps ride a bull - oh common, I mean an electric bull - which I did ride only for a few munites before I screamed that it should be put off. The thrill cost me $4. But it was real fun. However, the rodeo at the American Airlines centre building, an architectural masterpiece, was the ultimate human show as it was intensely competitive with podiums filled to the brim and large TV screens, hanging from top beaming every second of the thrill. We were given cowboy hats and interviewed on WFAA channel 8 on our impressions. Yes, I was on American TV, for the right reasons too.
The rest of the days, Emeka and I, visiting Nigerian journalists as we were called, concentrated on our work at the Denton Record Chronicle. While there we experienced three different editors. Jim Flansby resigned his appointment, as was said, from the shocks of September 11. In came Dawn Cobbs, the business editor who was immediately appointed in acting capacity. A week later she gave way to a substantive Editor, Mr. Barry Boesch who would talk to us daily on different aspects of journalism. But by far the person I learned more from was Mike Trimble, probably in his 50's who, to my mind was the engine room of the paper. Senior Reporters like Mike Zabbel and Mike Hall showed us around Denton and how they gather their own news - at the courts, the Commissioner's Court, the University of North Texas, the Texas Women's University and a host of other places.
A stone throw from the DRC office would be found one man I shall always remember. I usually take my lunch in his restaurant - Steve's BBQ. Steve Logan, a 55-year-old African/ American whose family has lived in Denton for 150 years can be described as a griot. Virtually everyone knows him in the city. According to him, his Dad was the first African American to own a business on the Denton Square, no small pride I gathered. We became friends. He would ask me questions about Africans - but his main worry was why "we" sold them out into slavery. A lover of hunting who pasted pictures of animals on his walls, one day, together with other African American friends/customers of his pointed at a picture of a wild animal asking, "Rabiu, what's that?". When I couldn't make out what it was, Steve shouted "That's a Wildebeest. Ha ha Rabiu, you aren't an African, you're a New Yorker. Go back to Manhattan". I couldn't help but laugh out loudly myself. I said, "Do you think every African lives in a forest with wild animals and which he must know"? He wouldn't even listen to me. The others kept on laughing as well, saying "This guy's a New Yorker, men."
On November 3, my state of mind was split. In one direction I was eager to get back home to Kaduna, Nigeria and to my place of work - where as I had started feeling - I have gathered in the US, more to offer to Weekly Trust. On the other hand I was going to kiss goodbye to a functional society I had started getting used to. Moreso, North Texas is a marvellous place for studying. Mike Trimble, a good teacher. Mitch land, Mr Efficiency. Ben Williams, a true friend. James Steve Logan, ever present.
November 4 when we arrived Lagos, I was indeed dozing and imagining myself under the marbled dome of the Library of Congress. The book I held reading - Song of Solomon by Toni Morrison - which I had bought at the Union Station in D.C. for the princely sum of $14, slipped from my grip. "You're welcome to Murtala Muhammed Airport, Lagos, said the German accent". Back home!

Headline: Nigeria: Modernisation of Agriculture
Body_Text:
In every state of the North, there are rivers and lakes that have not been utilized for effective farming. At this stage in our development, it is necessary to open up these rivers and lakes and channel their flow for irrigation purposes, in support of effective agriculture, especially during the dry season. Then the veterinarian should be deployed to improve animal breeding and other similar activities, as in developed and progressive countries.
Many wealthy countries at this time have attained their industrial success not from oil wealth alone but from their agricultural success. As a minor example, if we pay attention to the rapid increase in our population and the immigration into Nigeria by the people of our neighbouring countries, our need for improved agriculture and food supply would be better understood and taken care of. Another problem is the fact that many of the able-bodied men who are children of farmers and have been brought up in farming environments have left their villages for the cities and towns in search of their own share of the naira. They have left their old parents on the farms to take care of the farming business. Whatever the old people can produce is what they use for their feeding; what is left over is what they bring to the cities and towns for sale. It is therefore imperative that our leaders mobilise fully to attain modern agricultural process and food sufficiency for our people.
Mechanised farming
This is a very important way to improve agriculture. For example, one caterpillar (tantan) can do the cultivation of about 5,000 farmers. The engine used for seed removal can in one day remove gero, guinea corn, beans, groundnuts, and rice or wheat up to 500 bags.
If many ideas are not taken seriously, it may be possible at any time under the present conditions to see an able-bodied man relying completely on load carriage or other menial jobs just to survive. The money he earns in that way will not be enough for a measure of maize or a tuber of yam. In such condition which may be nationwide, all kinds of difficulties and conflicts may occur leading the country to instability.
The petrol money on which all attention has been put to the point of forgetting the need for proper development of agriculture sooner or later become exhaustible. Even if one tin of petrol can be sold for a hundred dollar, the Nigerian naira may not appreciate. It will depreciate as it is doing now until we go back to emphasize agriculture which will enable Nigerian industrialists to have all the raw materials they need. It will not be necessary to import them from abroad. Therefore, the Nigerian naira will stop fluctuating and start rising steadily in the world market.
My suggestion in this article is that Northern Nigeria should promote the adoption of modern techniques of farming and other agricultural activities by the farmers; not by the compulsory acquisition of farms from their owners. That is not the issue and it will be wrong to do so.
The late former governor of Kano State, Alhaji Audu Bako gave a good example and he was hundred per cent successful. He purchased from abroad all kinds of modern machines and farming equipment, cows and other animals necessary for farmers. Even though the government has a lot of farmlands, he offered to give all private farmers who needed such equipment and animals for temporary use on loan for a reasonable price. As a result of this policy, many took the advantage to have the equipment and animals for use in their farms. They were later able to buy their own equipment for their farming activities.
Alhaji Audu Bako also made available sufficient fertilizer and insecticide. Farm technicians in good number went round the villages, giving farmers advice and veterinarians went round all the herds, taking care of animal health. Even animal breeders were helped to cross-fertilize with eggs or species from abroad.
All these were not for profit making by the government. But the objective was to develop agriculture and increase food production for the country as well as guide the farmers in the right direction required by the government to attend sufficient food supply.
If this policy is imbibed nationwide, the federal government could stop the issuance of licence for the import of foodstuff, clothes and shoes in Nigeria. All those who import rice and wheat should be told that there are farmlands in Kaduna, Niger, Kwara, Plateau, Benue, Adamawa and other Northern states. They should go to any of these places to cultivate whatever they want to produce.
For this purpose, it is absolutely necessary for the state governments to establish good programmes for the people for the things that will benefit them and keep the country in peace.

Headline: Uganda: Organic Farming is Their Backbone
Body_Text:
Gabula Atudde women group aims at boosting household food production in peri-urban Mukono
At the crack of dawn, Sarah Katende sets off for her field. In the garden, next to her homestead, she hoes out the weeds and Katende mulches her garden. Around 8:00 am, she returns home to feed her cows and do domestic chores. Thereafter, Katende proceeds to her workplace - Mukono Town council - where she works as a town agent and a community development assistant.
The burly forty-year-old farmer lives in Bugujju village in Mukono district. She is a single mother. Katende caters for three children. The oldest is an undergraduate at Makerere University.
Two miles from Katende's garden, 37-year-old Ruth Kafeero is tending her crops too. She is busy digging and weeding in her garden. She has five children. Her husband is a mechanic.
The women are members of Gabula Atudde women group, established in 1992.
Gabula Atudde is a Luganda phrase which implies that if you do not work, you cannot get a better social position or status.
Gabula Atudde mainly aims at boosting household food production in peri-urban areas. The group encourages working together. This has facilitated the transfer of agricultural knowledge and farming skills from one member to another.
Katende talks with enthusiasm about the group: "Over the years, farm output has increased due to our efforts", she discloses with a smile.
Katende attributes their success in achieving self-sufficiency in food production to Peter Lusembo, the centre manager, Mukono Agricultural Research and Development Centre (ARDC).
The centre has designed one-day intensive courses to equip the women with farming knowledge. The courses include crop, animal and soil husbandry.
"We are trained in modern agriculture. The training is paying dividends through high yields," Katende stresses.
The women grow bananas, maize, oranges, yams, beans, cassava, and potatoes.
Mrs. Christine Luyombya, 75, the chairperson of the group says, "our objective among other things is to improve the welfare of women." Luyombya is a widow. She has eight children.
When the programme began, 10 years ago, the women were ignorant of organic gardening. Luyombya remembers the birth of the organisation.
"We began with only 10 members. The members were not fully conversant with organic farming".
Today, the women are armed with knowledge about organic agriculture. "We use cow dung, urine and decaying plants," Katende explains. "We also make compost bags. The bags contain a mixture of decaying plants, cow dung and soil. We live in a peri-urban area. The members have little land. Compost bags enable us to farm even on the veranda".
Organic gardening has proved a valuable contribution to food production. The crops fertilised by natural manure produce high yield and are free from pesticides. Now the women harvest enough food to feed their families. Sometimes they produce surplus food.
"We sell the surplus. The money got is kept in the bank", says Juliet Ochieng, 36. "When a member gets a problem, we provide moral and material support".
The project has enabled the women to improve on their nutrition. It also provides a lifeline for the families.
"We eat a balanced diet. It includes a variety of vegetables and fruits from our gardens," explains Kafeero. "The money I get from the sale of some produce is a welcome addition to the family income."
She talks about another benefit of the project - social solidarity.
"We hold gift days after every three months. Members exchange gifts on this occasion," reveals Kafeero. "This strengthens the bond of mutual affection."
The women contribute sh100 whenever they meet.
"We save the money and use it for buying craft materials," Katende says. "We meet once every week."
The group engages in other income generating activities, besides farming. Kafeero turns to the subject of craft making.
"We meet on Thursdays to learn craftmaking. We make mats, bags, baskets, and bedcovers. We also knit table cloths. We sell our crafts to people who want them."
In 1997, the women set up a bakery.
"We bake cakes and buns", says Luyombya. "They are often bought by the members."
The group also runs a brickmaking project in lower Kauga in Mukono.
Gabula Atudde has enhanced the status of the women. Their major problem, however, is lack of funds to run the organisation efficiently.
"We primarily depend on our savings and members' contributions. Membership fee is sh3,000 yearly," says Katende." Sometimes well-wishers donate money to the organisation. Nonetheless, our revenue is insufficient. There is a small market for our products".
The group liaises closely with other organisations such as Kulika Charitable Trust Horizone T3000 and Young women Christian Association (YWCA).
"YWCA teaches us how to grow vegetables and to cater for our husbands," Kafeero says.
Do their husbands like the group's activities?
"We are taught how to look after our husbands. We are better wives and mothers. Why should they complain?" asks Kafeero.
Her husband fully concurs with her.
"My wife is an excellent housekeeper," he says proudly. "Besides my family has two incomes now." His face shines with profound satisfaction.
In spite of money constraints, the women are resolute in their struggle for self-reliance.
"Our fight against poverty and food insecurity continues. We believe our efforts are paying off," concludes Luyombya optimistically.

Original_Search_Title: Onigbinde; The Eagles With A Wing
Headline: Nigeria: Onigbinde; The Eagles With A Wing
Body_Text:
Mention Festus Onigbinde and people would take you down memory lane
of how he picked a silver medal in the Nations Cup. Depending on
where you belong, they would easily tell you he believes in his
talismanic AMORC Society that he presents his team list to the gods
before the Eagles file out for the main show. For the critics, the
Modakeke man belongs to the old school. But for others, Onigbinde is
God sent. With a heavy heart from the scandalous outing of the Super
Eagles in the Nations Cup in Bamako, Mali, even a greenhorn at the
Nigeria Institute of Sports, NIS, would have made a perfect
replacement.For such people, anybody that is not Amodu is good for
the Eagles.
As for Onigbinde, he came to Mali like an orphan. At the Murtala
Muhammed International Airport, Lagos, not even the protocol officers
honoured him with their presence. He was left alone. Even in Mali, he
was confronted with questions by the NFA Chairman why he came so
early. After surviving the barrage of embarrassing questions,
Onigbinde ran into another stormy weather with the Minister who
insisted that the Technical Study Group(which he was a member) must
head back to Nigeria. After much ado, Onigbinde was spared. It
appeared from then that his stay in Bamako was cursed. He was to run
into another controversy. This time with the chief Coach of the
Eagles, Amodu Shaibu. Onigbinde s technical jottings were rejected
and torn to shreds.And like a man who had offended the gods,
Onigbinde continued to stir controversy.After walking down the dusty
road of his Colibris Hotel to Amadine, where he finds solace in
eating Nigerian food, Onigbinde looked ruffled. He was to inform me
that a Newspaper reported that he had been sent packing from Mali.
"Why worry when you are still in Mali", I advised.
After making mince-meat of the balls of pounded yam, Onigbinde
predicted that Amodu and his Eagles would fail if he fails to listen
to useful advice."I made it clear to him that I was not after his
job.But if you observe the team very well, you would see that they
don t have wings.There is no wing play. I m a strong believer in wing
play. It is one of the characteristics of Nigerian football. It has
worked for us.We have very fast players who run down the flanks and
cross for others to make hay. I ve passed these to Amodu and it is
left for them to adopt or reject"
Onigbinde also frowned at the attitude of the players."It s obvious
that these players are no longer enthusiastic about the game. I don t
blame them.But one sure way of cutting down on their over-bloated ego
is to introduce younger players from the league. That way, there
would be competition, and you would get the best. For now, our league
is in limbo because there is no avenue for the players to come to
limelight. The national teams which are their target have been made
an exclusive club for professionals.
Those who know Onigbinde think that he is too defense conscious.
"There is no law that forbids any player from scoring. When I was on
a redemption mission to Iwuanyanwu Nationale, I accused Mike
Onyemachara that there was a time he scored a goal and it was
over-ruled because he was not an attacker.The players were
surprised.It was to prove to them that any player could score goals.
No coach would want to lose a match. As you want to score goals, so
would you not want to conceed goals. Defenders are as important as
attackers. In all, however, it is a team game", Onigbinde answered.
Without a faint knowledge that he would mount the saddle, Onigbinde
from all his travails in Mali has become the rejected stone.
And unknown to many, Onigbinde s second coming to the Eagles is on a
different setting. He is coming as the first titled indigenous coach
to handle the Eagles. He is a Chief. And to complement his job from
the secretariat is another titled man, Chief Taiwo Ogunjobi.Together,
these chiefs , with their red caps would be either shouting
instructions from the sidelines or battling to pay players bonus in
the world cup in Korea/Japan World Cup.

Original_Search_Title: Blast Victims Turn Alausa to Relief Camp
Headline: Nigeria: Blast Victims Turn Alausa to Relief Camp
Body_Text:
Lagos — Following the distribution of relief materials by the Lagos State
Government to victims of the January 27 bomb blasts, more victims of
the disaster have continued to invade the Lagos State Secretariat
daily.
The victims who have almost turned the secretariat premises to
another relief camp daily converged at the secretariat seeking the
state government attention.
Some of the victims who spoke with P.M.News stated that they decided
to come to the secretariat to collect food items and other relief
materials since what they are getting from the relief camps are not
sufficient for them.
Some of them, mostly women, were seen by P.M.News been attended to by
officials of the ministry of special duties who distributed one bag
of rice, 1 bag of gari, two tubers of yam, two bags of semovita and a
loaf of bread to the victims.
Mrs. Josephine Osakwe of CBQ Flat 104 Ikeja Military Cantonment told
P.M.News that the assistance of the state government is still needed
to help the family get back to normal life.
While commending the Lagos State government for its efforts, Mrs.
Joke Fagbemi solicited the assistance of the state government in
re-building their damaged houses.
The victims, mostly from the Ikeja and Ipaja barracks were full of
praises for the state government for its concern and sensitivity to
their plights.

Original_Search_Title: Syrian Community Donates N10 Million To Bomb Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Syrian Community Donates N10 Million To Bomb Victims
Body_Text:
The Syrian Community in Nigeria has donated relief materials worth N10 million to the victims of the January 27 bomb explosions in Lagos.
The material comprised bags of rice, yams, matresses, bags of beans, sugar, flour, biscuits and milk.
The material were delivered to the Lagos State Government representatives at the Police college in Ikeja.
Leader of the delegation who is also the Honarary Consul of Syrian Arab Republic in Lagos, Mr. Ziad Saimua, said the community was ready to do more if the situation arises.
"The Syrian community has stayed over 100 years in Nigeria and we felt that anything that happens to Nigerians, happens to us. Nigeria is our second home," Saimua said.
He advises that the government should ensure that all the victims have a fair share of the relief materials being donated by every concerned individuals, institutions or groups.
"We hope that the relief materials would help alleviate the sufferings of the victims," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Commonwealth Aid for Accra to Boost Capacity Building
Headline: Ghana: Commonwealth Aid for Accra to Boost Capacity Building
Body_Text:
Ghana is to benefit from a 300,000 pounds sterling Commonwealth Local
Government Good Practice (CLGGP) for the period of five years, which
is intended to promote capacity building in human resources and
physical engineering.
The Good Practice Scheme is designed to ensure smooth succession plan
for the Local Government Ministry.
To this effect, the District Assemblies through MILGRD and NALAG
would apply the transfer of effective financial management, food and
preservation as well as better management and recycling of wastes.
These were made known at a Commonwealth Local Government (CLG) forum
at Brisbane, Australia recently.
The forum was to discuss the CLG audited accounts of the year
2000/2001 and the operational budget for the year 2001/2002.
In a chat with the Ghanaian Chronicle on his arrival, the Minister of
Local Government and Rural Development, Hon. Kwadwo Baah-Wiredu,
disclosed that the Indian Minister for Rural Government pledged to
support Ghana in food preservation such as plantain, yam and among
others.
According to the Minister, the Indian Local Government Minister has
agreed to help Ghana acquire the preservation technology that India
is noted for.
This, the minister said, will help sustain the nation's food security
programme.
"When this process becomes successful, the Rural Development Program
designed for food security and guaranteed economic prices for farmers
would be achieved," he pointed out.
"If this becomes a reality it will go a long way to help alleviate
poverty among farmers", Baah-Wiredu underscored.
Touching on India's experience on micro-financing, he hinted that the
country's Village Council selects hard working and trustworthy people
for micro-finance facilities.
Elaborating, he revealed that the intended purpose of the Poverty
Reduction Programme (PRP) has always been successful as the ¢16
billion allocation to the 16,000 Unit Committees is likely to give a
measurable impact.
Hon Baah-Wiredu presented a paper on 'Supporting Sustainable
Development Through Capacity Building for Technical Cooperation and
Local Government.
The paper indicated the 10 regions and 110 districts in Ghana.
Baah-Wiredu learnt that some waste managements in terms of domestic
wastes can be converted into electricity and others into energy.
Among the detailed discussions in Brisbane was the Korle-Bu and
Mallam wastes, which emit methane gas, that can be converted into
power.
Hon Baah-Wiredu as part of his visit met with the Ghanaian community,
and suggested that a Ghana Investment Bond should be issued for all
Ghanaians outside especially those in Australia, Canada, and France,
to be tied to specific infrastructure projects.
Furthermore, the Dual Citizenship Law was discussed, as the focus was
on when it would become operational and effective. Fifty Ghanaians
attended the meeting.

Headline: Nigeria: One Fire, Too Many
Body_Text:
Fire disaster is gradually becoming rampant in Abuja. Apart from the valuables that are being lost, such disasters pose serious threat to security of lives. Ikenna Emeka Okpani takes a hindlook into cases of fire outbreaks in this piece
When Mr. Kunle (not real name) woke up on the Valentine day last week, he may have given his wife a peck and instructed her on what to prepare for the family that day before leaving for his office.
In a flat he had lived for more than two years, he probably had no cause to ponder on any unimaginable occurrence that could render him homeless on a lovers' day.
He may have borrowed new films or video CD's to watch. Probably, he may have stocked his refrigerator with drinks for the evening. But unfortunately, before 10.30 a.m. that fateful day, he had lost everything in his flat to fire which gutted the top floor of one of the two three-storey buildings at plot 1954 Cotonou Crescent, Wuse, Zone 6, popularly called Nigerian Social Insurance Trust Fund (NSITF) Wuse estate.
At about 9.30 a.m., that fateful morning, a stream of thick smoke started coming out from one of the flats at the top floor. Then there was fire coming out of the roof. People began to gather outside especially at the express road overlooking the building. Cars passing by started parking to catch a glimpse of what the fire was all about.
The occupants of the building were running out and the onlookers were encouraging them not to look back.
"Come out! Forget bringing out anything! Your life first!" They were shouting and it seemed the escaping occupants heeded. Apparently shocked with great fear, the occupants ran out looking berserk.
At about 9.50 a.m., the First batch of Fire Service men arrived at the scene with a van with registration number FG 443 B20. The men quickly got down to work.
The National Electric Power Authority men arrived at about 9.55 a.m. and went straight to the mini transformer servicing the estate and disconnected the power supply to the estate.
The leader of the fire service team kept making frantic effort to contact the Garki station, NNPC fire fighting men and those of Julius Berger.
The Garki station responded and arrived at about 10.05 a.m. in a tank with registration number FG 438 B20. Just after some minutes that the new truck arrived, the first one ran short of water supply and had to leave to refill at about 10.13 a.m. to the chagrin of the onlookers.
"How can they come here without enough water?" one of the members of the crowd asked. By this time, over four thousand people had gathered round the building urging the fire service men to keep fighting the fire.
The fire fighting wing of the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) arrived in a giant tank with registration number AH 436 BWR at about 10.15 a.m. But the tank could not pass through the gate and had to be parked outside the gates where the firemen connected the tank through the hose to the scene of the fire.
But men of the Julius Berger added a different dimension to it. They came through the express and took position at the back of the building with a tank that had registration number XA 859 GWA and special Berger number B4671.
A long water tank serving as reservoir for the pressure tank stood beside it. The reserve tank had registration number XG 297 ABC and special Berger number B6126.
When the Berger men started work, the crowd applauded them. The quantity of water and the force with which it was leaving the nuzzle doused the fire by force. With the continued fuselage of water on both sides of the building, the fire was brought under control at about 10.45 a.m. almost an hour after batches of fire fighters arrived at the scene.
Just when people thought the fire was all over, one of the flats on the first floor caught fire again. The firemen returned and put out the fire finally.
The fire was the second in a week in Abuja. A day before the Valentine day, the new building that is to serve as the headquarters of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs under construction caught fire. The wooden scaffold and other wood works got burnt completely. But for the arrival of firemen at the scene, more harm would have been wrecked on the building.
Abuja Trust gathered that the fire was suspected to have originated from the kitchen of the labourers at the building who, once in a while, roast yams and eat same with real palm oil.
"It is a bad omen for a building that is still under construction to catch fire. That means that by the time it is fully completed, it might be prone to perennial fire outbreak," a civil servant told Abuja Trust.
Between December 2001 and February this year, there had been three major incidents of fire outbreaks.
It all stared early in December when the AIT/Raypower lost its Abuja centre to fire.
The office situated at the top floor of the Labour House was gutted by fire, which was said to have broken out as a result of fluctuation in power supply to the station.
The fire was said to have originated at the TV studio where the network of cables caught fire and extended to the whole of the office.
Modern broadcast equipment worth millions of naira was lost in the inferno.
In less than two weeks after the incident, the Wuse new market was gutted by fire, an incidence which is still shrouded in mystery.
According to a source, the fire broke out in a boutique where a seller was trying to put on a faulty generator. The attempt to bring the generating set to life resulted in a spark, which then ignited some of the materials on sale leading to spread of the fire.
The fire did not only render so many people who lived in the market homeless but their means of livelihood was completely lost to the inferno.
Those who could not contain the reality of the event were said to have jumped into the fire.
The new Wuse market fire was difficult to control because of non-existence of access roads in the market for fire fighting tanks to go in. Property worth millions of naira was lost in the inferno.
"The fear of fire is the beginning of wisdom in Abuja," a respondent told Abuja Trust. According to him, the new Wuse market burns at least twice in a year either in part or in whole.
"And no arrangements have been made by anybody to contain the outbreak. The market is not yet planned so the problem is the same all the time," he said.
It seems the fire service command in Abuja anticipated the current fire incidents in Abuja which may have prompted it to recruit over 100 firemen last march and sent them for training in Lagos.
The training ended in July last year. Were it not for the additional fire fighters, the story would have been different in most of the fire outbreaks in Abuja.
Abuja Trust gathered that the men of the fire service are always incapacitated in the event of fire outbreak because of either lack of equipment or the obsolete nature of available ones.
"When we go to fight fire you see onlookers applauding, "Berger!" because they often think that Julius Berger fire fighters are doing better than us but in most cases we are the people who make use of the equipment, one of the firemen who pleaded anonymity told Abuja Trust.
According to him, the service is greatly hampered by the unavailability of fire fighting equipment.
"Even at that, we are often the first to arrive on any scene of fire outbreak," the fire service man said and asked government to equip the fire service with modern fire fighting equipment.
An Igbo man chuckled when he saw people watching the firemen fighting the fire alone.
"In Onitsha you rarely see firemen fighting fire like this. Every person at the scene of the fire often turns to a fireman. I am surprised that despite the effort of the firemen here, no one is lending a helping hand," he told this reporter.
According to him, fire fighting should not be left to men of the fire service alone, it should be a combined effort of the service and the society.
But all said and done, Abuja residents need to be taught fire safety measures and sensitised on ways to avoid incessant fire outbreak. The harvest of fire incidents in the city makes this recommendation expedient going by the quantum of losses as a result of these outbreaks.

Headline: Nigeria: The Prostitutes of Ijora Badia
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ijora Badia, Lagos' notorious slum, has taken a dangerous plunge. Sex
trade thrives there at an alarming rate, leading to fear of widespread of
AIDS. There is also fear of imminent outbreak of epidemic. ANTHONY OKORO who
visited the place, reports.
It has a semblance of the defunct Maroko, Lagos most notorious slum. It
also has a striking similarity with the notorious Kaduna 'Yam' market where
no single yam is sold, except a high level ignominious sex business. It is,
in fact the exact opposite of a modern day abode.
Living in Ijora Badia, a swampy area of Apapa, Lagos, is a nightmare.
But then, it is a haven for maximum enjoyment and relaxation. One peculiar
feature of Ijora Badia is the mountains of refuse that 'decorate' the entire
area, constantly sending out offensive odour.
There is hardly any street in the area without heaps of refuse, the most
notorious being Fadaini Street. Nearly half of the entire street is blocked
by mountains of refuse, thereby obstructing both human and vehicular
traffic. Metrolink gathered that the street was tarred over 30 years ago and
is now full of dangerous potholes.
Apart from this, the drainage network in the entire area has completely
broken down. The gutters are full of all sorts of dirty things and stinking
all the time.
Similarly, all the houses in the area constitute an eyesore.  Metrolink
findings revealed that about 85 per cent of the houses at Ijora Badia are
built with planks. No wonder wood business booms in the area.
In areas like Akorede, Amusu, Oniyide streets, all the living rooms and
business premises are built with planks.
The situation is even pathetic at Amuda Lane where all the Pako houses
are built on swampy soil. When Metrolink visited the area, hundreds of water
pipes were seen lying on the muddy ground. Some have broken many years
ago. In all the streets, people have to meander through the stinking refuse
and muddy ground before they eventually get to their destinations.
The state of the roads in Ijora Badia is very deplorable. It is only
Obale Road which links the slum with Orile that is a bit motorable.
For residents of Ijora Badia, it is a taboo to provide toilet facilities
in houses. People just defecate into nylon bags and throw them into the
swamp. Some even make holes in their huts and defecate directly into the
swamp. One worrisome issue is that the swamp is stagnant. So, apart from the
refuse, stench also emanates from the swamp. The fear among the people is
that one day, an epidemic of immense magnitude may soon occur there.
However, the people of the area survive by the grace of God. This
explains why churches abound there, some with names that are
jaw-breakers.
Despite this, life at Ijora Badia is at its peak. The Ajeromi railway
line is the hub of activities. It is often described as the life-wire of
Ijora Badia. Both day and night, the railway line is always a beehive of
activities. Different categories of people, rich and poor, old and small,
throng there daily to have a taste of life.
The Ajeromi railway line is notorious for illicit sex trade.  Metrolink
investigations revealed that the line alone has over 200 brothels. No other
street or area in the whole of Lagos state has such a large number of
prostitutes homes.
Of all the brothels, only two have signposts. They are Chico Star Light
Hotel and Titilayo Hotel, situated at 55 and 52 Ajeromi Railway Line
respectively.
Others simply have street numbers to identify them. Such brothels are
located at 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 24, 25, 27, 28, 30, 42,
45, 55, 80, 86, 87, 90, 92, 94, 98, 197 Ajeromi Railway Line. And the list
is endless. The way and manner the brothels are so clustered indicates the
degree of competitiveness in the sex business here.  Apart from three, all
the brothels are built with planks. In some brothels, there are over 200
prostitutes while the smallest of them have about 10 call girls. Built very
close to the brothels are cinema houses. Statistics showed that there are
over 50 of such cinema houses, all built with planks. Some popular ones are
Love Film Centre and Base One. Operators of the cinema houses don't show any
other film, except pornographic films. They operate both day and night with
impunity. Outside the cinema houses are pornographic materials which are
well displayed to attract customers. Metrolink observed that some youths
after watching the blue films, become sexually stimulated and storm the
brothels to satisfy themselves.
The sex workers openly solicit for men. The sexual escapades at Ijora
Badia has reached such a crescendo that an elderly man who was just passing
by the Ajeromi Railway line murmured to himself: "The population in hell
fire would be great because I know that none of these people can smell
heaven".
At Ijora Badia, the dreaded Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS)
is a non issue. The only language they understand is enjoyment. This
explains why they go about their businesses without using condom.
"Condom or no condom, person must die when it is time. Na money I dey
find", a sex worker told Metrolink.
As expected, crime rate is high at Ijora Badia. There are numerous smoke
shacks along the railway line and these joints are highly patronized by
miscreants.
The situation was worsened in that for long, the place had been without
a police station.
However, a new police station has just been built along Fadaini Street
and is now waiting for commissioning. But would the police be able to
contain the excesses of dwellers of Ijora Badia?

Headline: Nigeria: Hausa Leader Disappears With N150, 000
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor Bola Ahmed Tinubu's efforts to alleviate the sufferings of
displaced victims of the 27 January bomb explosions at the Ikeja
Police College relief camp have been thwarted by the Seriki Hausawa
of Mushin, Babalosha, Alhaji Sani Lambo, who has reportedly
disappeared with N150,000 given the victims by the Lagos governor.
Governor Tinubu, who visited the camp last Friday shortly after the
Ju'ma'at service was said to have given the victims (about 20
families) N150,000 to share.
According to one of the displaced soldiers, Corporal Hassan, the
money was given to them so that they could travel to their states to
celebrate the Eid-El Kabir.
The money, P.M.News gathered, was given to Alhaji Lambo to share but
no sooner did he collect the money than he disappeared from the camp.
The displaced soldiers from the Ikeja Military Cantonment, who said
they have been looking for him since Friday vowed to do everything
possible to fish him out.
"You see, some of us have packed our loads, we were ready to travel
to our states to celebrate the Sallah, but there is no money. The
Seriki has disappeared with our money. This is wickedness. The
government should look for him and collect the money from him",
Hassan stated agitatedly.
P.M.News further gathered that Governor Tinubu specifically told the
victims to use the money to celebrate the Sallah holidays and that
those whose families are not in Lagos should use the money to travel
home to see their loved ones.
Apart from the N150,000 donated, the governor also gave each of the
families a bag of rice, semovita, carton of large size Bournvita,
tubers of yam, cartons of macaroni, groundnut oil, garri and other
foodstuffs.
Governor Tinubu told them that despite the fact that they are
soldiers and employed by the federal government, the fact that they
reside in Lagos which he governs makes it incumbent on him to take
care of their welfare. He urged them to be patient as the government
is making efforts to provide them temporary accommodation.
P.M.News observed that houses made of roofing sheets are being built
at the camp to accommodate the victims.

Headline: West Africa: The Fascinating World of Twins
Body_Text:
Statistics from across the globe indicate that the Yoruba of West Africa have the highest number of twins on earth! Meanwhile, twins are a perpetual source of great joy, and boundless adoration, wherever they are found. Shakespeare praised twins in his classical play, Twelfth Night, while communities all over the world hold twins in high esteem. Victor Akobundu, our reporter, though not a twin, recently stepped quite joyfully into this fascinating world.
Background
The world of twins is indeed a very fascinating and compelling one. Their identical traits, emotional attachment and mysterious way of communicating with each other, provide intriguing insights into human nature.
Thus, long ago, African Culture wasn't fair to twins, as a double birth then, was regarded as a bad omen. Consequently, bizarre stories had it that twins in the early days were thrown in the forest and left to die, or they were sacrificed.
In fact, a double birth was not seen as a natural phenomenon, but rather as a supernatural offence which required performance of certain rituals to appease the gods which may avert any doom that may befall the land. Therefore it was most odd for women to give birth to twins. One might even wonder why it is rare to see twins at a very ripe age in our society. Even though the practice of killing twins was discouraged early enough, it took quite a while to fizzle out.
Against the backdrop of contemporary civilisation, double births in our contemporary setting are viewed with much respect and pride, thereby making enviable the status of twins. In line with this, the striking identical features and attributes, in itself is an enigma, let alone how it comes about. The mechanisms behind the creation of twins reveal volumes on the creative ingenuity of the Almighty Father.
The biological process involved in the formation of twins in itself is very awesome. In this point of view, there are different kinds of twins peculiar to man, but the most common being the phenomenon of identical twins.
Science and twins
Therefore, identical twins are said to account for about one in 250 births, and they are created when a single egg is fertilised by one sperm. The eggs therefore split into halves, whereby each develops into a foetus with the same genetic composition.
The occurrence of fraternal twins therefore, arises when two eggs are released at once. If both eggs are fertilised by separate sperms, two foetus form genetically, and they are just ordinary siblings. However, the striking difference between these common type of twins is their degree of genetic similarity.
Twins can also have different fathers. If an ovum released after the previous month has begun developing into an embryo, it can be fertilised by the next act of sexual intercourse. These sort of twins in essence are step siblings for they have the same mother but different fathers.
It is with avid delight that most couples' desire twins in spite of the burden they may pose to mothers in terms of providing double satisfaction.
Twins: Biological origins and legends
However, no culture is known to have had an unprecedented experience with twins than the Yorubas of West Africa. Twins account for about three per cent of the tribes' birth rate compared with 17 per cent for other blacks, one per cent for white, while Asians account for five per cent.
Twins in Yorubaland are regarded as harbingers of good fortune. With the significant rate of this double blessing among the Yorubas, research suggests that this marvellous phenomenon largely has to do with yam, which the tribe considers as a staple diet. It is known to contain high amounts of a substance similar to the female hormone oestrogen, which in turn, may stimulate the production of other hormones called gonadotropins. However, one gonadotropin called follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) may trigger the release of more than one ovum (a female cell that develops into baby when fertilised) from the ovaries, thus paving way for the conception of fraternal twins. Although, the hormone connection remains unclear, there is indeed circumstantial evidence that these chemicals do influence the manifestation of twins. Recent research meanwhile reveals that Yoruba women have higher levels of FSH than Americans who have higher levels than the Japanese do. This fact therefore explains the pattern that matches the rate of twin births among these groups.
Furthermore, certain mysteries and myths have been woven around behaviours in twins. Twins, especially the identical type are known to have exceedingly high perceptive powers. This in fact, has been a concept that has proved quite difficult to unravel given certain coincidences, which revolve around major events like injury, birth and death.
Certain coincidences are known to occur around twins. For instance twins may have injuries at identical spots on their body. They may cut their first teeth at the same time, or even have the experiences of going bald at the same time.
A story was told of female twins who were separated from each other, shortly after birth however, and did not see each other for 34 years. When they were eventually reunited, each wore rings on seven fingers, and a bracelet on each wrist. More surprising perhaps was that the first name of the first child of each twin was the same. Such phenomena may sound strange but is part of the inexplicable circumstances that surround twins all over the globe.
Meanwhile, in as much as most couples earnestly desire for these double blessings, the experiences of raising such may not be rosy, especially for those who are worst hit by the ailing economy.
Daily Trust sought the views of Mrs. Remi Moses, a mother of female twins on her experiences.
According to her, having twins is a blessing from the Creator who therefore will certainly provide for their upbringing. She further stated that there is no way bringing up twins can b e as easy as raising a single child. "It is strenuous both physically and mentally, more especially when all you have to do for them is double. But seeing them grow mutually reveals a lot," she added.
Even singles are often green with envy at the radiance which twins normally exude and may fantasise about having a twin sometime. One of such is Philip Garba, a businessman who says he has a fancy for twins to the extent that he wished he was one. In his words: "My admiration for twins is due to the fact that they are like two beans in the same pod, perfect companions, a splendid confidante and soul mate, and they are so attached to each other that there is always this complete trust, which single persons can't even find in a friend."
Twins have identical traits, or identical items like clothing, attending the same school, and in fact receiving things that are identical. Consequently, mothers of twins have to contend with the joy of giving names to their twins and these are names which have to illuminate their status as twins. For instance, if a set of twins are both male and female they are usually given names that reflect this status.
In the same vein, since identical twins are of the same sex, names given to them are largely alliterative and rhyme in the first vowel.
Daily Trust encountered twin brothers, Olisa and Obum, both students, and sought their experiences as twins. They disclosed that being twins has afforded them the opportunity of facing certain challenges in life individually, saying even though they may have and share the same dreams however, circumstances may nip their efforts in the bud, thereby hindering the realisation of their collective dream. Obum asserted "we wanted to study medicine and that has been our ambition, but Olisa was fortunate to make the marks, therefore he studied pharmacy. So, in this respect we have divided dreams," he said

Headline: Nigeria: Bomb Scare Hits Benue
Body_Text:
Makurdi — The Ikeja cantonment bomb explosion has triggered off a new wave of
fears amongst Ugondo people in Benue State Ordinarily the huge
presence of the Airforce Base, Makurdi and the headquarters of the
Tactical Air Command in Ugondo land should make the community one of
the safest communities in Benue State. But since 1975 when government
took over their land for establishment of the military installations
the case of safety in Ugondo has been a study in disappointment.
When they were pushed away from their ancestral land, they thought
government was aware of the problem of economic and social insecurity
associated with such forceful dislocation. They hoped for
compensation at least for their land but they got nothing.
The matter became worse for people of Ugondo in 1979 when bomb blasts
chased them away from where they had resettled close to the Airforce
Base. Since then deafening bomb blast has been a regular recurrence.
Settlements like Mbakya, Mbaakwa, Mbagoho, Mbakpe and Mbagban
settlement are the worst affected areas of the blasts.
In 1985 series of bomb blasts in their area lasted for three days
from 23 December to 26. The community in a letter to the then chief
of general staff quoted the base commander as saying the explosion
was the Airforce exercise of destroying expired bombs.
Curiously what was supposed to be a peaceful and cautious exercise,
the community said "has caused loss of limbs, homes, property, farms,
crops and untold hardship to the entire Ugondo community." Another
devastating explosion occurred in April and June 1993 when the
villagers unable to find alternative place of residence had returned
to their settlements. A 13 year- old boy, Samuel Timbir Iornenge of
Community Secondary School, Apir died from injuries he sustained from
the bomb blast. Earlier in 1991 a middle aged man was reported to
have been killed in a bomb blast when he made fire near a bomb to
roast yam in his farm.
Following the April 1993 blasts, and the resultant sudden deaths in
the community, medical experts with the Makurdi based Polyclinic
Limited were engaged to conduct survey in the affected communities.
In their report dated June 3 1993, signed by the chief consultant of
the clinic, Dr. JA Adagba, the experts said "we confirm that since
the inception of the bomb blasts from April 14, 1993 there have been
three sudden deaths from what by description is reminiscent of heart
attack." They explained that the association of their death with the
explosions cannot be a mere coincidence.
This is because "eye inflammation, and injection, deafness, heart
attacks and irritating useless cough with chest pains assumed
epidemic dimension" in the area.
The report observed that although the bombs were not nuclear in
nature, "the potential chemical teratogenic effect on the yet unborn
baby is a matter which can only be determined retrospectively." A
total of three deaths and over four hundred medical ailments were
documented by the consultants as resulting from the blasts.
They remarked that "there must be considerable number of bombs spread
out in that region going from the blasts that one experience even
from the Makurdi township and the exercise is probably continuous
till every dangerous bomb is disposed of".
The consultants ended their response with the observation that "one
thing is clear, frequent blasts have left these people (Ugondo) in
constant fear and state of not being safe," recommending that "a
permanent solution would only be to properly resettle them." Several
years after, the community is yet to be resettled as they demanded in
their letters and have not been compensated for their land. Mr. J.D
Sule, a leader of the Ugondo community told TheNews in Makurdi that
"our fears has been rekindled by the last week explosion in Lagos."
He said the Ikeja cantonment incident was reminiscent of what the
Ugondo people have been going through without any form of serious
attention from government.
On 10 March , 2000, the community lawyer, Michael Gusa, wrote a
letter to the Base commander of the Nigerian Airforce Base, Makurdi
through the Benue State governor. The letter which was titled "Re:
Bomb Explosion in Ugondo community Makurdi Local Government Area of
Benue State: The Need for Compensation and Resettlement" chronicled
the travails of the villagers in the series of bomb blasts in the
area from the Airforce Base.
The community among other things demanded a compensation of N400
million, the resettlement of the residents in an alternative area and
intensification of efforts by the Airforce authorities to get the
bombs outside the Base diffused.
In 1991 it was reported that the authorities of the Nigerian Airforce
had awarded a contract for the disposal of the bombs to a Russian
company at a cost of N4.8 million. That contract, according to
sources, was controversial as an indigenous company, Enosei , owned
by one retired Airforce officer, Ft. Lt. E.N Onwuachi had earlier
been contacted for the job. The officer who was a bomb disposal
expert before his retirement was said to have been approached in 1986
by authorities of the Nigerian Airforce authorities to dispose the 50
kg and 500kg bombs, that littered the area inhabited by Ugondo
Community.
Onwuachi was said to have quoted N3 million which was reduced to N1.4
million. But he failed to impress the Airforce authorities which
claimed that Onwuachi cost was on the high side.
In a National Concord report of Thursday, 18 April 1991, the then Air
Officer commanding the Tactical Air Command, Air Commodore Akin Dada,
was quoted as saying a "contract has been signed with a Russian
company to clear and store the bombs at a cost of N10m". He
complained that the six bomb shelters will be constructed at a cost
of N30 million to house the explosives. The company's job also
included providing advice to military authorities on how to destroy
the dead bombs and housing the useful ones. Not much has been heard
of the contract and the disposal efforts since then.
The authorities of the Nigerian Airforce Base, Makurdi could not
provide answers to questions on the state of the bombs at the time of
this report. But the Lagos incident has awaken the fears of the
people of Ugondo and neighbouring communities of the Airforce Base
that there may be a recurrence.

Headline: Nigeria: Women Tasked On Abandoned Children
Body_Text:
Women in Nigeria have been called upon to always ensure that they are well prepared before going into child bearing to minimise recent increasing cases of abandoned children.
President and founder of Klub 20/36 Women Indigenes, Alhaja (Princess) Mulikat Olufunke Ajaga, who made the remark at the motherless babies home, Karu, Abuja, recently, enjoined women to be involved in concrete family planning where they were not ready for child bearing.
"If you are not ready for child bearing, protect yourself sexually or go to family planning outfit, or better still, avoid early or premature pregnancy", Alhaja Ajaga said.
While delivering some items to the babies' homes, Ajaga advised that children, as leaders of tomorrow, should always be handled with care.
"These children are leaders of tomorrow and great potential for this country. We should not allow our children to waste away like a piece of paper. As I know, a child is the joy of every mother. A woman, who cannot bear any, is nobody", she said.
Ajaga chided women, who abandon their children for no just cause, as they cause such children some unimaginable pain.
"My fellow women, the joy of every child is to belong to a particular family because a man with no root is nobody. It is painful to see children abandoned for no just cause and languishing in the agony of lack of parental care. There are some people out there praying and crying to God for a child, while some given on a platter of gold, instead of thanking God, abandon them to have their identity lost", she said.
Receiving the items, which included two packets of soap, slippers, tubers of yam and envelopes, Matron of the Home, Theresa Nya-Robert, and the children's Image Father, Alhaji Umaru Bawuro, thanked the Klub for coming to the children's aid and encouraged them to extend same to other motherless babies homes across the country.
Commenting on the recent bomb blasts in Lagos, Princess Ajaga thanked President Olusegun Obasanjo and the Lagos State Governor, Asiwaju Ahmed Bola Tinubu, for all the assistance they rendered to the victims during the unfortunate incident, and called on those affected by the blast to brace up, stating that God would see them through.
"In fact, we Klub 20/36 Women Indigenes of Lagos State of Nigeria appreciated their effort and support. May God continue to be with them and their families," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Nasfat, Eib, Syrian Community Donate to Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Nasfat, Eib, Syrian Community Donate to Victims
Body_Text:
More donations are still coming in for the victims of the Ikeja military Cantonment Explosion of January 27 2002. Eko International Bank plc (EIB) donated the sum of N2 million naira to the Lagos state Disaster relief Fund to assist the state government minimise the hardship of the victims of the bomb explosion. presenting a cheque to the governor of Lagos state, Governor Bola Ahmed Tinubu during a condolence visit at Alausa, the Chairman Dr. Akinola Ogunlewe, together with some board of Directors of the bank expressed deep concern for the plight of the victims. Ogulew also said the humanitarian gesture was informed by EIB's commitment to social responsibility.
He said, "EIB as socially responsible corporate citizen has come to demonstrate support for the laudable humanitarian gesture being accorded victims of this unmitigated disaster through a modest donation of N2 million." The chairman commended Governor Tinubu's exemplary leadership qualities which he said has ensured a steady flow of relief for the victims.
The governor also received one million naira cheque from members of the Nasrul-lahi-il-fathi society of Nigeria NAFSAT. The President of the association in his presentation stated that the amount is a token contribution of its members towards resettlement of the displaced persons as a result of the bomb explosion and the recent clashes at Idi-araba. The president also used the opportunity to pray for the repose of the soul of those who died in the two incidents.
Also the honorary consul of Syrian-Arab Republic in Lagos Mr. Zaid Saimua together with some of his consuls also made some donations at the Lagos State Government house. The presentations of relief materials which they said was valued at N10million naira, comprised of; rice, yams, beans, sugar flour, biscuits milk and mattress. Mr Saimua later advised that the government representative endeavour that all the victims have a fair share of the materials Saimua also stated that "the Syrian community has stayed over 100 years in Nigeria and we feel that anything that happens to Nigeria happens to us Nigeria is our second home, we hope that the relief materials will help alleviate the suffering of the victims."

Original_Search_Title: We'll Process Your Claims With FG Free of Charge - Tinubu Tells Victims
Headline: Nigeria: We'll Process Your Claims With FG Free of Charge - Tinubu Tells Victims
Body_Text:
Governor of Lagos State, Ashiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu has assured all the victims of the January 27 bomb explosions that his government would process all their claims for damages suffered with the Federal Government, "free of charge."
Governor Tinubu made this known while assessing the mode of distribution of relief materials at the premises of the Lagos State Television, Agidingbi, Ikeja.
According to him, "I want to assure you that your claims with the Federal Government will be processed free of charge. We will do our best to help you. I have seen your problems. I have moved round. I have seen it all. We'll attend to all your problems we will help you."
While explaining that they should be patient with his government over their quest for damages from Federal Government, Ashiwaju Tinubu stated: "we will process your claims with the Federal Government. You know it is difficult getting money from the Federal Government. It is like using a pair of pliers to remove an aching tooth."
Governor Tinubu nonetheless assured them that everything was being done to provide them with the necessary succour to enable the victims and their families outlive the trauma of the January 27 explosion.
Talking on how he has priotise the succour that would come their ways, Tinubu continued: "My priority for now has been how to give you food to quench your hunger. I have also made the prompt rehabilitation of several thousands of school children affected by the bomb blast back to school my priority, because we cannot afford to abandoned them. Then the priority is shifting to according the dead befitting burials tomorrow Saturday at Oke-Afa, after an Interdenominational service at the LTV 8 premises, Ikeja.
He added that the new priority Saturday's burial ceremony would be paying urgent attention to the settlement of their damage claims against the Federal Government.
Governor Tinubu also assured the victims that his administration would soon make some cash donations to those who had already registered but it will be such that would be aimed at assisting them pending the time their claims would be approved and released by the Federal Government.
He then disclosed that the various donations will go to the reconstruction of schools that were demolished while outstanding if any will be given to the victims.
Governor Tinubu also stated that the various state owned hospitals have been directed to treat any victims free of charge adding too that the state has ensuring that they get the best treatment free of charge.
At the venue a number of cases were also treated on which the Governor directed that prompt medical attention should be given to a boy who had a fractured skull on whom N30,000 had been spent and another with eyes affected by the explosion.
Governor also altered the original plan to limit relief being distributed to the 500 persons who had earlier registered, saying that relations, wives and children of military persons who claim they have not been covered by the exercise at the military cantonment will now be considered.
He directed that evidence of being relations should be brought to verify their claims.
The governor explained that though the state government had not, in it original plans, made a destruction between the military and civilian victims, the military and civilian victims, the action of the Ministry of Defence officials and the Federal Government have shown that their concern is with the military establishment, so the consideration for only the civilian victims.
He urged the victims to find solace with relations and made do with the little that they got by way of food stuff adding that he will only assist with repairs of houses and not major repairs.
Speaking earlier with media persons Information and Strategy Commissioner Mr. Dele Alake opined that though the state plans to have a proper funeral for the victims who lost their lives it will not be a mass of black water that is about 15kms. What can be done is to fence it to avoid people running into it.
On the issue of honouring the volunteer who brought out the bodies, Mr. Alake said while politicising such things in unfortunate that state government will still go ahead with it planned honouring of such people who had been given N10,000 daily while the operation lasted.
Also speaking the Chief Secretary of the Salvation Army, the government's partner in the distribution, Lt. Col. Rowland Sewell said there is a need for the Nigerian Government to put in place a disaster response agency which can act quickly and decisively in terms of emergency and prevent such fall out as this.
He said his organisation which preaches the gospel and undertake humanitarian services, is well placed to handle the distribution having done so in places like India Singapore, Ecuador and such other places.
In all about 500 persons were given such materials as rice, spaghetti, semovita, yam, bournvita and rubber shippers.

Headline: Nigeria: Nnamani And the Development Idea
Body_Text:
Since May 29,1999, when Nigeria was freed from the second colonialism imposed on it by the military, it has become self-evident that several years of military rule brought Nigeria to its knees in many spheres of national development. The education sector collapsed like a pack of cards. Roads dilapidated to dangerous levels. Insecurity of lives and property reached all time high.
One state where a lot of difference has been made in the lives of the people since 1999 is Enugu. When Dr Chimaroke Nnamani decided to leave his flourishing medical practice in the United States for politics in 1998, not a few people felt he was making a very big mistake. From the early 1980s, a plethora of professionals especially medical personnel had left the country to seek fulfillment abroad. Therefore, anyone firmly established abroad, and the United States for that matter, has no business coming back, so they thought. But in the last two years, the people of Enugu State have seen that there is a difference between a government elected by the people and one imposed on them.
In this dispensation, virtually all the states of the southeast have been experiencing one form of crisis or the other but Enugu State, despite the spirited efforts of some forces outside the state to cause havoc, has remained relatively peaceful. There have been detractors here and there but the state government, with the co-operation of the Enugu State House of Assembly, has remained focused in executing its laudable programmes.
The Nnamani Administration has made giant strides different sectors like roads, rural electrification, water, housing, healthcare, education and so many others.
Before the advent of this administration, passing through Enugu State was nightmarish. The roads were impassable. A journey that ordinarily should not take more than 15 minutes would take close to three hours and vehicles were always breaking down. But today, it is very possible, for example, for one to visit Nsukka, Awgu and Ndeaboh in one day and still have time for other engagements. Earlier, at the onset of the administration, he had announced a plan to connect all the local government areas through a road network. In a society where, for a long time, people had lost confidence in government and took every statement from government with a pinch of salt, not many gave him any chance of succeeding. In addition to all those roads, the following roads have been added to the list of completed roads: Amechi, Amodu road network, Oji River Express Link Road, Agu Obu Owa, Umumba Ndiuno, Obu Noffia Ugwuoba roads, Agbani Ugboka Nara road, Uda Ogurute Obollo Afor road, Agbani Akpugo Amagunze road, etc.
The people of the southeast have been complaining of marginalisation. This marginalisation has manifested in several ways: lack of federal presence in the region and non-appointment of easterners in key positions at the federal level. Therefore, when the military government of the late General Sani Abacha decided to decentralise the Nigerian Law School and situate one campus in the east, not a few easterners jumped up in celebration. Enugu was chosen as one of the campuses but it did not fully take off before the transition to democratic rule was completed by which time the government was at a loss as to the practicability of the multi-campus structure. But in order not to take chances, the Nnamani Administration went to work and in no time came up with a standard campus that can compete with anyone in the world. Today, the campus named after the late Justice of the Supreme Court, Augustine Nnamani, an illustrious son the state, is functioning very well. The state government spent over N300 million to build and equip the campus. The Federal Government is expected to refund the state government the money spent on the campus.
The governor goes round the state from time to time to feel the pulse of the governed and enlighten them on programmes and policies of the state government. The tours also serve as a medium for the people to interact with the governor and tell him what their problems are. Last December, just before Christmas, the governor visited Nsukka Local Government Area. During the visit, he pledged the determination of his government to address the problem of perennial water scarcity in the area. He expressed dismay that despite efforts of the state government to find a lasting solution to the problem, not much success had been recorded. He then told the people of the local government area that even if it entailed supplying water in tanks, his government would stop at nothing in ensuring that the situation was redressed.
In addition to various water projects already executed in the area, he said, the booster station at Ede-Oballa would be reactivated.
He assured them that work would soon begin on the Edem-Nrobbo-Abbi road, Ogurute-Ebte and Ofurute-Nsukka road while electricity transformers would be provided for the Alo Uno rural electrification project. The governor told them it was the determination of his government to ensure that every community in the state felt the presence of the state government.
And at the launching of a book on the rehabilitation of children with mental and learning disabilities at the Hotel Presidential, Enugu, the governor stated the preparedness of his government to provide a conducive learning environment for disabled people in the state. He pledged financial and logistical support to the Therapeutic Day Care Centre and Boarding School, Ugbo-Ezeji, Abakpa Nike, Enugu that organised the launching. He also called on the Federal Government to make the school a centre of excellence for the training of mentally retarded children.
The synergy of effort between the government and the private sector, which is seen as a sine qua non for development was given expression in October last year, when Nnamani laid the foundation stone of the multi-million naira Enugu International Market, Nsude. Apart from the market, the development programme will include a housing scheme for the business community.
On completion, the market will have 9000 stalls, a fire service station, police post, a bank, telephone service, public conveniences, water supply and warehouses. The market is strategically located as a gateway to the northern and western parts of the country.
It was also an opportunity for the governor to remind Igbo businessmen who are based outside the east of the need to also establish businesses in the Zone.
The Enugu State University Teaching Hospital has received unprecedented attention from the Nnamani administration in the state. Before now, the structures in the institution were not befitting of a teaching hospital. As a medical doctor, the governor knows the importance of a well-equipped teaching hospital. Any institution that produces half-baked doctors is inflicting serious injury on the society. This point is not lost on the governor and that is why he has deployed resources to ensure that the ESUT College of Medicine is a pride to all indigenes of the state.
But, by far, it is in intellectually enriching the polity that Nnamani has surprised Nigerians. Those who knew him very well before he became governor were not surprised but they are the privileged few. Last year, Nnamani went to different parts of the country, delivering lectures and speeches on what he called the Nigeria Idea. In a society that is used to intellectually bankrupt leaders, Nnamani's philosophical dissection of the Nigerian state and proposal for a way forward is reassuring.
In Kaduna he was a guest of the state council of the Nigerian Union of Journalists (NUJ) on August 18, 2001. He called his lecture "The Nigeria Idea... Can the Press Sustain the Nation's Interest?" In that lecture, Nnamani said, "Editors are at liberty to cast their headlines and use the stories of their reporters at headquarters and states. But isn't it essential that they realise that the new values they built could be brought to ruins in a few days if they fail to exercise restraint. The society they snatched from the military was a badly deformed one, which needs time to recuperate.
"Yes, editors may be right in screaming that all is not well. We all know that all was never well. They can even raise hell about the tendency of Nigerians to attempt to retain the vestiges of unaccountable and disorderly society of the military but they cannot remove the fact that they must have, and hold on to, their society before they can reform it.
"Of course, the editors cannot be mistaken if they urge a revisit of the social contract of which the elites have violated. They have the professional duty to scream but I believe that they ought to raise the issues of some blatant socials deviations, which I think have further widened the gulf between the rich and the poor. We now have two societies with a far apart dividing line. A society of pomp and pompous wealth marching side by side with one of squalid deprivations and lack of hope".
In analyzing the problems militating against true nationalism, Nnamani delved into history. The way in which independence was attained without much fight or turmoil made leaders to elevate self rather than the public. He noted that this "goes to strengthen the argument that Nigerian nationalism was abandoned in 1951 when the first offer of native participation in higher services was made. Unlike the Eastern and Southern Africa, nationalists easily got sucked into the colonial system and so abandoned the struggle. Elsewhere, nationalists had outrightly rejected offers of momentary job and so held on to the fight till the end. Some even abandoned normal life and taken to stiff opposition to the colonial order.... Actually, if you take into consideration the various stages of our nationalism, which were mainly three, you begin to wonder whether actual nationalism was long on this land".
He posited that if the nationalists of that time failed to lay proper foundation for nationhood, the military usurpers that held power for much of our years of independence reduced everything to individualism. "The abortion of true nationalism which, by extension, arrested the emerging Nigeria Idea did not entirely end with the crusaders turned ethicists. If those early fighters had abandoned the nation for their ethnic groups and provinces, the military that ruled for 30 out of 41 years had degenerated further from the ethnic consciousness to the elevation of the person and his greed, even though it had pretended that it served ethnic interest", he further said.
And in Jos, the Plateau State capital, towards the end of last year, Nnamani addressed the executives of the Correspondents Chapel of the state chapter of NUJ. The subject was democracy. He declared that all hands must be on deck for every segment of society to derive some benefits from democracy. Nnamani advised Nigerians not to bow to such pressures as ethnicity, moral decadence and other problems of the third world. He assured that with learning and determination, the country would overcome her problems.
After his session with the correspondents' chapel executives, Nnamani had gone ahead to deliver a lecture entitled: "The Press, the Faith and the State". It was after the unfortunate crisis in Jos during which there was so much destruction of lives and property. Until that episode, Jos was seen by not a few as safe haven, a neutral ground. In his lecture, Nnamani had raised posers.
