"...Following our unceasing interactions with the press, we must form our opinion but in the respect of analyzing the press alongside the other operative elements of this topic, I consider the institution along the track of the challenge posed by the faith and the state and in this respect, the measure of our national of our national possibilities in wading through the tough terrain of sentiment, the reality and other externalities which go a long way in influencing the forces at play at each level of national interaction.
"To this effect, isn't it imperative that we raise necessary questions about the journey of the Nigerian state riding the unavoidable media vehicle top the mind, the soul and the matters in national co-habitation and tolerance? This must ride alongside the track of national development and in comprehension of those matters, which rule the mind and cause the individual and group energy to obey the head rather than the minds.
"So, we may ask reasonably, is it then impossible that we seek to appreciate the sustaining values of that which cause so much waste of man and material with a view to addressing the grievances of our folk so as to enhance the confidence of the citizenry, the groups and the faith or is it healthier to let the regional or provincial tempo run its course and leave our dear people with whatever comes of it?"
The governor had earlier delivered a number of other media lectures. These included one organized by the National Association of Women Journalists (NAWOJ) and the maiden Post Express Annual Lecture in Lagos.
In Enugu, he had spoken at the maiden lecture of the Enugu Sports Club, during which he enunciated his vision of a regional economic integration of the eastern states comprising Abia, Anambra, Akwa Ibom, Cross River, Bayelsa, Ebonyi, Imo, Rivers and Delta.
According to him, the body should be called Eastern Nigeria Development Co-ordinating Commission as the planning, co-ordinating and implementing organ.
He said he necessary facilities have been established. He mentioned the existing road network from Agbor-Onitsha-Enugu-Abakaliki to Calabar-Uyo-Eket-Portharcourt-Yenogoa. He proposed the establishment of specific areas of entrepreneurial activities designating Portharcourt as the center of oil and gas; Nnewi for automobile, and Aba for textile and clothing.
"We can designate Enugu as the center for information technology, while in the area of agriculture, we can allocate the production of specific cash and food crops to particular centers such as Cross River, pineapple; Rivers, palm produce; and Ebonyi, rice and yam", he had said.
Other areas he suggested include the transformation of existing state universities into centers of excellence for specific areas of study with a view to developing a collegiate university system, he further elaborated
"We can only succeed in this development business if both the public and the private sectors understand their respective responsibilities and each group makes a serious commitment to discharge those responsibilities", the governor declared.
The people of Enugu State have not been unappreciative. This explains the series of endorsements for re-election in 2003, which Nnamani has been receiving from various communities, local governments and groups in the state.
Miss Ugwu, a member of the Peoples Democratic Party, writes from Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Fleeing Idi-Araba Residents Yet to Return One Week After Mayhem
Headline: Nigeria: Fleeing Idi-Araba Residents Yet to Return One Week After Mayhem
Body_Text:
Lagos — ROWS and rows of burnt houses, charred walls, shattered and fallen
roof-tops, burnt cars and buses, premises strewn with fallen walls
and torn corrugated iron-sheets, the sad and bitter faces of the
dispossessed and the bereaved this is Idi-Araba.
But it has not always been so, for this Lagos suburb which
accommodates the famed Lagos University Teaching Hospital (LUTH) has
just unwittingly hosted the consequences of the infamous ethnic
hostilities in the country.
Now in Idi-Araba, a man can stand at a street corner and point at
where his house once stood.
On Saturday, February 9, 2002, in Idi-Araba at about 12 noon, trouble
started when an alleged Hausa man defecated somewhere on the roadside
and was asked by alleged Yoruba to "pack the *****."
Reports had it that the man resisted all efforts to make him "pack
the ****."
According to Mr. Chris Thomas, a resident of No. 8 Fagbenro Street,
one of the affected streets, "they then asked the Hausa man to pay
N5.00 but he refused."
The Hausa man, according to Mr. Thomas demanded: "What about other
people who have been defecating in the same place? What are you
people going to do to them? "They told him they had warned people not
to defecate there.
Subsequently, the Hausa man was beaten up and a report said, to a
state of unconsciousness. There was a reprisal attack from the Hausa
community in the area and a major conflict ensued, leaving death and
destruction in its wake.
When Vanguard visited Idi-Araba yesterday it was no longer the
bustling Lagos suburb it once was. It was deserted with skeletal
activities here and there.
At the Ishaga/Fagbenro streets junction near the Idi-Araba Motor
Park, a police jeep with registration number NPF 2776 B is packed by
the roadside.
A few policemen holding on to their guns are sitting on a bench in
front of a house at the junction. Two or three other policemen, guns
hanging from their hands, are on the road-side checking the light
traffic.
Not very far from his colleague, another policeman also with a gun is
in the process of buying fried yam by the road-side.
The police men are relaxed. The fighting has stopped. The dead have
been taken away. The streets are almost deserted. Most of the shops
are locked up.
Down Fagbenro Street, some of the shops are locked. In front of house
No.
14, Fagbenro Street, a heap of rubbish is in flames, sending smoke to
the skies. The house, a burnt storey building stands there minus its
roof. Some metres away on the roadside is a burnt 505 Peugeot car and
beyond that, is a burnt Volkswagen bus.
At house No. 8 lives Mr. Chris Thomas, an elderly former staff of the
New Nigerian Newspapers. He stands alone in front of the house.
Except for his presence, the entire two-storey building is deserted.
Recounting his experience, Mr. Thomas said, "almost N50,000.00 was
taken from my flat. All these locked stores you are looking at, the
owners are afraid to come back.
Some have said that their customers are no more around to patronise
them.
Some of the men who fled are, however, making plans to come back.
They looted some of my properties."
In front of house No. 6, is a burnt 305 Peugeot with an Edo number
plate and registration number AA362-EKP.
At the intersection where Fagbenro Street meets Ajegunle Street,
stands a school, named Unique Junior Academy a nursery and primary
school. Other houses around the building flaunt diverse levels of
destruction. The proprietress of the school, Mrs. Chinyere
Aiyemonisan had a sad tale to tell.
According to her, "my pupils have all left. They have refused to come
back because their parents are threatening to take them to other
schools. I have 300 kids in the school. I thank God that the crisis
erupted on Saturday and not a school day, you can imagine what would
have happened to my school kids. But I thank God I did not lose any
pupil.
I paid the rent for this place in December and it was to expire in
December this year and none of my pupils have paid their fees, I have
spent huge sums of money. I have not recouped and this crisis
occurred. Nobody knows when life is going to return to this place and
I am about to lose my pupils to other schools. I would like to get a
new place. My school business has been disrupted by this crisis and I
am asking government for compensation.
As Mrs. Aiyemonisan spoke, her teaching and non-teaching staff, about
16 men and women sat on chairs and benches watching.
From the look of things, it will take quite a while for Idi-Araba to
be reconstructed. The destruction was on a large scale affecting
Taiwo, Morgan, Paul Okuntola, Ishaga, Hassan, Ajegunle, Gbolahon,
Bakare, Fagbenro and Ojerinde streets

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo Orders Withdrawal of Soldiers From Sankera
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo Orders Withdrawal of Soldiers From Sankera
Body_Text:
Benue State Governor, Mr. George Akume, has disclosed that President Olusegun Obasanjo has approved the final withdrawal of the men of the Nigerian Army from Sankera area in favour of the police.
Mr. Akume, who disclosed this on Tuesday in Sankera during a civil reception organised in his honour by the Ukum Local Government Council as part of his official visit to the area, noted that the recent withdrawal of soldiers from the area was part of the overall process of total withdrawal.
Governor Akume further assured the Ukum people of the total implementation of the Kuru retreat recommendations to ensure that absolute peace returns to Tivland and Ukum area in particular, adding that the state government would continue to demand that justice be done.
The governor commended those who have stood in defence of the interest of victimised Tiv people, particularly late General John Mark Inienger, adding that those who refused to speak out when they had the opportunity have no moral right to now claim interest in the welfare of the people.
The governor further assured the Ukum people of the massive implementation of projects in the area this year with the commencement of the year 2002 budget, noting particularly the expected completion of the Zaki-Biam-Gbeji road and the proposed General Hospital, more boreholes and rehabilitation of more schools, including the realisation of the proposed Zaki-Biam Yam Flour Mills among others.
While calling on the youths to be more dedicated to their callings in life with due respect to the elders and deep interest in education, Governor Akume urged the elders to also advise the youths, assuring that their request for new local governments would be given due consideration.
Earlier, the council chairman, Mr. James Tyoakosu, in his welcome address commended the governor for siting several projects in the area as well as appointing several sons of the area into key positions in his cabinet and for his astitute and matured handling of the communal crisis affecting Tivland and her neighbours.
The chairman, who appealed to the state government for resettlement assistance to set up new infrastructure destroyed by the soldiers, called for the speedy resolution of the crisis and withdrawal of the military from the zone.
Mr. Tyoakosu on behalf of the council assured the governor of the continued support and loyalty of the people of the area particularly his re-election in 2003.

Headline: Nigeria: Man, 27, Nabbed for Impersonation
Body_Text:
Makurdi — Police in Makurdi have arrested a 27 year old impersonator army corporal, who has been harassing and extorting money from businessmen and traders in the Wadata area of Makurdi, the Benue State capital.
The man identified as Mohammed Usman was arrested in an army uniform. According to the Benue State Police Public Relation Officer (PPRO), ASP Emmanuel Adesina, the suspect "got another person's identity card, belonging to a military officer, removed the officer's ID card and inserted his own."
Armed with the ID card and the uniform, the suspect, it was learnt, went about extorting money on a daily basis from traders who had to part with N50 and more daily depending on the amount of wares.
But unfortunately for Mohammed, who claimed he was from 72 Battalion, North Bank, Makurdi, some "soldiers came from the 72 Battalion and demanded for his ID card," which they suspected immediately to be fake.
According to Adesina, the "soldiers came to the police station and complained" on the basis of which the police effected the arrest.
"In the course of our investigation," according to the PPRO, "we discovered that his real name is Mohammed Usman."
A trader, Madam Adamu, told THISDAY that she could not remember the total amount of money extorted from her by the corporal already accused of impersonation, but stated that the man collected N60 from her daily.
Similar complaints came from yam sellers as well as other traders who in grains, vegetables and other produce, all of who said the "fake" corporal has been extorting various sums of money from them.
THISDAY also learnt that the action of the suspect did not end with petty traders; sometimes representatives of some companies often fell victims,." Perhaps given the mentality of the Nigerian public concerning the military, some of them keep giving him money each time he visited them," said a police source.

Headline: Nigeria: Witness to Bedlam
Body_Text:
Nigerians do not know how to behave when disaster, natural or man-made, strikes because we have never been taught what to do in situations like this. Hundreds of people died because they did not know what to do other than to run
It promised to be an interesting evening at Onigbongbo, a small high density settlement by the army cantonment, Ikeja,   Lagos  . Oscars was hosting the monthly Ikpilogwu family union meeting on Sunday, January 27. We had been served pounded yam with a particularly delicious ukpo/abasi soup. Obande, the president of the union called the meeting to order. Kola nuts were broken and served. The meeting was committed to God and to the gods - the latter being our ancestors - to make sure nothing went amiss. We settled down to business. Oscars read the minutes of the previous meeting. Contents debated and approved. The promise of the evening was about to be broken. We suspected nothing.
At about  5.15 p.m. we began to hear what sounded like an exchange of small arms fire. Not a strange sound in   Lagos  . One often hears that kind of sound when either armed robbers are operating or are engaged in a shoot out with the police. It was coming from around Oshodi, not far away from where we were. We dismissed the sound and got on with the meeting. The sound did not go away. It became louder and more diffused. Evidence, we thought, that the police and the armed robbers were probably evenly matched gun for gun and neither side was willing to surrender to the other. We were wrong. Proof of that came soon.
A loud blast. The kind of sound that gives you the momentary impression that your head has been blown off. We were jolted. From the room, we could see the cantonment. Skido pointed out that some women in the barracks were running. All of us crowded in the doorway. No one was running. Or more correctly, we did not yet see them. Some children and women with babies strapped to their backs were more or less strolling in the barracks. We went back to business.
Oche, a serving soldier, had a worried look on his face. Something told him we were wrong in our assumption. He was going to the barracks to put on his uniform, he said. He was not to be caught dead in mufti. We persuaded him that it certainly was not a military activity. He sat back, unwilling to argue further but clearly unconvinced by us.
Not quite three minutes after that, there was a particularly loud blast. Never heard anything like it before. The house shook to its foundation. No rain of bullets. Some luck. This was getting weird. We rushed down to the street. The narrow street was already crowded with people who had poured out from their houses. Questions were asked of no one in particular. Then we saw the thick black smoke billowing from what appeared from where we were to be the back of the army barracks. Trouble had come in black smoke. Soon the street was teaming with anxious men, women and children. Fear and confusion were about to take over, shattering the peace of the evening and ending the meeting. Think of all the cold beer we were about to miss. Ye gods.
No one knew what was happening. Everyone knew there was trouble. Eshi, a serving soldier, offered the view that the armoury had been bombed. That meant bigger trouble. Probably, enemies or dissident soldiers had taken on the military establishment. He quickly assessed the situation and concluded that Onigbongbo was no longer safe for us. It was evident. We could see hundreds of people running towards the gate of the army barracks. Skido offered to drive me home. As he fumbled with the security lock of his car, another loud bang shook the neighbourhood. We got in. He drove by his house in the neighbourhood. His wife, carrying their little daughter, ran to meet us on the road. Skido told her to run with the neighbours if they were running. Our mistake. We realised that rather late.
We drove to   Ikorodu Road  . Skido is a young man with some pluck. He decided he would take me home through Oshodi. I said it was dangerous. The blasts were coming from that direction. No problem, he said. Another big bang settled the argument. We headed for Ojota on the wrong side of the road. At the best of times, we, Lagosians, do not obey traffic rules. On this day, my driver, Isiaka, would have been shocked to find that even I did not remember that there were traffic rules left in the books.
Bedlam. Chaos. Not a pretty sight. Hundreds of people had already crowded every inch of the road. Some were running. Some commercial drivers, too shaken to hold the steering, did the most sensible thing. They simply jumped out of their vehicles, contributing to the madness and the traffic bottleneck.
An opportunity for a quick naira or two presented itself in the midst of the rather helpless situation. From nowhere, in my untutored view, some young men emerged with planks. I soon saw why. A motorist wishing to cross from one side of the road to the other needed the planks to help him climb the road divider. He paid the young men and was soon on his way across the road. Several motorists needed and used the services of the plank men.
At Ojota, the first wave of guilt hit me. Here was I being spirited to safety by Skido who had left his family behind. He groaned and said we made a mistake by not taking his family away with us. I agreed. We were helpless. We could not turn back. The road was clogged. It was getting progressively worse back where we came from. The bombs were now in full and frightful blast. We were stuck. No movement. Only panic and confusion. Women were running everywhere, shouting, weeping and asking to jump into any available vehicles. They took off their head scarves and their spare wrappers as if not wearing them would quicken their escape from the looming trouble. At the moment, these prized items of a beautiful Sunday outing had become liabilities. They had to be discarded.
Again, Skido remembered his family he had unwisely left behind at Onigbongbo. He groaned the groan of a man caught up in a bad and unforgivable mistake. I too groaned the groan of the guilty. Because of me he left them behind. What if .? Perish the thought.
People were simply running, well, helter skelter. They did not quite know where to. They only knew there was trouble. Instinct told all of us that if there was trouble, the most sensible thing to do was to run. Problem was, we did not know where the trouble really was and how widespread it was in the metropolis. No time to ask questions. Just run. If your shoes or slippers or sandals impeded your speed, discard them. Many did, littering the roads with evidence of panic. If your car was held in traffic and you could not move fast enough from trouble, abandon it. Many did. Evidence that the car can, sometimes, be a hindrance rather than help in certain circumstances. There comes a time when the feet earn the trust of their owners. Weep not, you who own no cars of your own. Unfortunately, some of those who abandoned their cars returned the next day to find their condition totally different from what they left behind. Thieves had cashed in on the situation. How awfully sad.
We tried to make contact with family and friends with the mobile phone. No use. Network failure was all the help we could get when we needed the wonders of the GSM mobile phone most. Some radio stations were off the air. Everyone was frantic. Some information just so our worst fears were not confirmed. None came. No one told anyone anything. Where were the police and military authorities? We wondered in vain. No information. No help. Nothing. Sometimes, help is tardy in its own funny way. We stewed in our panic and confusion. We just kept moving to wherever we felt was a long way from trouble.
Which was how Ekong made it to a white garment church in Otta. Quite a distance from his residence in Mafoluku. He did not know how he got there but once there, he trusted God to deliver him from whoever was making trouble for the country.
A loud blast. A man jumped out of his car. He left the engine running. Another blast. So close. No rain of bullets. We looked around to see who or what was hit. Nothing. The crowd changed direction at the command of no one; briefly faced where the smoking billowed to the sky and then changed direction again, facing Ketu.
We drove on to   Kudirat Abiola Way  , Oregun. Some volunteers, as they so often do in   Lagos  , had taken it upon themselves to bring some sanity to the traffic because no motorist was willing to concede an inch of space to the other. Ironical. Escape consists in movement, not in standing still. Wrong time to preach, wrong people to preach to.
We managed to get through and on to Agidingbi. Same story. A big blast. The houses in the neighbourhood rattled. More people poured to the street. No lone rangers in this confusion. Everyone simply followed where the mass of people flowed. The majority could not be wrong. Even in confusion, we instinctively practise democracy in   Nigeria  .
Something worth recalling. Despite the chaotic traffic situation, there was a surprising abundance of civility among the motorists. Lagosians usually leave their homes and offices fully armed each day with imprecations which they haul at one another at the slightest provocation. This evening, no curses, no swear words. Panic and the looming shadow of the unknown had engendered in us a new spirit of civility and even politeness. It raised the hope that on the day of Armageddon, no Lagosians would be caught dead with their daily ration of imprecations. It takes a crisis.
On to Ogba, Oba Akran,   Agege Motor Road  , local airport and then to the   International Airport Road  . The big blasts had subsided. It has been some one hour and forty-five minutes since they started. The rumbling continued. At the toll gate between the local airport and the international airport, children aged between five and 10, probably returning from a Sunday outing, spread themselves on the ground, tired from head to toe. The vehicles that brought them were parked by the roadside. Some adults stood by the vehicles. They looked vacant. They did not know what was happening. They thought it wise to simply stop and wait. They were wise.
Albert was at a naming ceremony at Otta. Everyone was blissfully unaware of what was happening in   Lagos  . Until a strange man ran into them, asking for cold water. They offered him a sachet of pure water. He drank some and poured the rest on his head. Everyone waited. He then told them that half of   Lagos  had been destroyed by bombs. He escaped all the way on foot to Otta. The long distance runner had won but he had misled the people as so many other people did in phone-in radio programmes this evening of all evenings.
Talk of an evening that promised to be interesting. Some evening!
In the panic and the confusion and the tragedy of January 27, two things were evident. The first is that we all appear to be sitting on the edge of our chairs, anticipating a military take over. That, indeed, was the conclusion of most people. Some radio stations reportedly, rather thoughtlessly that the cantonment  been bombed and that the alleged military action was spreading to other parts of the city pretty fast. Some foreign news media simply added two and two together and got their answer:   Nigeria  was up in political flames again.
We were stuck in the traffic on   Lateef Jakande Road  , Agidingbi, Ikeja. A beat up taxi, fully loaded beyond its capacity, drew level with us. Were it not on a day like this, he would have  paid a toll through his nose to the police or the traffic warden. I heard the driver say: "See now, dem say God no dey answer poor man him prayer. God don answer now. All dis Obasanjo dem jolly-jolly don finish." I found that shocking.
It was worse than cynical. None of us should find it funny. Even when Governor Bola Tinubu of   Lagos  State  appeared on television with the commander of the 9th brigade, not a few people immediately concluded the governor had been arrested by soldiers. One must commend Tinubu. His timely appearance on the tube reassured all of us that the rain of bombs was an accident.
The second point, which is no less worrisome, is that Nigerians do not know how to behave when disaster, natural or man-made, strikes because we have never been taught what to do in situations like this. Hundreds of people died because they did not know what to do other than to run. Running anywhere was better than standing still to be probably picked up like a sitting duck. But it was a grievous mistake and grievously hundreds of men, women and children paid for it. The federal government hardly needs to be persuaded that it is time to do something about this. The sooner, the better. But of course, when the enquiry by the military is over, nothing will change. Call it the curse of indifference.
Newswatch Volume 35 No 6, February 11, 2002

Original_Search_Title: Child Labour High On the Afram Plains
Headline: Ghana: Child Labour High On the Afram Plains
Body_Text:
Donkorkrom — High school dropouts, child labour and the misuse of the girl-child
for trading instead of being schooled at the Afram Plains remain
light in spite of critical press reports published in the recent
past.
The two little girls I the picture, aged between 2 and 5 years, were
captured on the Afram Plains by our camera man as the girls were
carrying cassava to the Donkorkrom market.
It was on a Thursday and during school hours when they were supposed
to be in the classroom being prepared for their future lives.
he girls who could not even express themselves clearly, showed that
they were not even from the town of Donkorkrom but had been sent from
a distant village on that market day to fetch a few cedis for the
up-keep of their family.
In a district of 145,000 population, an estimated 40% or 58,000 are
believed to be children of school going age.
But a check at the District Directorate of Education, Donkorkrom,
revealed that pupils and students in primary, junior and senior
secondary schools are less than 15,000.
The story of children being apprenticed to fishermen on the Volta and
Afram lakes; children eking out a living from yam and maize farming,
charcoal burning and the grazing of cattle, have already received
widespread publications.
Yet, remedial actions from local and central governments, NGOs and
the United Nations' appropriate agencies have achieved very little
success in this very rural and deprived district.

Original_Search_Title: The Effects of Petroleum Products Price Increase
Headline: Nigeria: The Effects of Petroleum Products Price Increase
Body_Text:
Despite the widespread unpopularity of increasing the prices of petroleum products, the federal government eventually did that on the beginning of the year. In the case of PMS (petrol) price per litre was increased from N22 to N26. This is an increase of 18.2%.
In the last four weeks, prices in the economy have increased significantly. For instance, motorcycle (Inaga) fares have increased from N20 to N30 per short drop, taxi fares from N10 to N20, and Imo Transport Company (ITC) increased cost of distances from N100 to N140. These represent an increase of 50%, 100% and 40% respectively. In the three cases above, it was more than proportionate to the rate of increase in the price of petrol.
The commonest food item around is garri. The price of garri has increased from N10 per cup to N100 for eight cups (i.e N14.3 per cup). This represents an increase of 43%.
Those who are not in a position to appreciate why increase in petrol prices is unpopular, perhaps because they are chaffaur driven by government fuelled vehicles or they live in Government Houses and feel free, can begin to have an idea into what most Nigerians are passing through. Only those who wear the shoe know where it pinches.
In a nutshell, an 18% increase in petrol prices has led to economy -wide increase in prices which range from 40% to 100% and this is based on basic consumer goods. Note that previous patterns of observations on price change patterns in Nigeria suggest that the worst is yet to come. From past experiences, food prices rise most between March and June, that is until the harvest season sets in. Since prices for instance are least in December - January, while last seasons harvest of yams are still in sufficient stock. If therefore such significant price increases are the case now, what will happen between March - June? Ordinary Nigerians are in for hard times.
Every government since the Babangida government has had to increase petrol prices. Some of them more than once. Even the short Shonekan government increased petrol prices, so did General Abdulsalami Abubakar. General Sanii Abacha did too, but has the credit of keeping prices stable for most of his tenure. President Olusegun Obasanjo has increased prices about three times in three years, that is at least once each year. In effect, increasing petrol prices is a common and preferred policy tool of successive Nigerian governments. Why is this so, and what has it achieved.
Government officials claim that increasing petrol prices is meant to reduce (eliminate) subsidy, reduce wasteful consumption, stop smuggling, boost supply, provide finds for maintenance of refineries, as well as for general infrastructure development of the country. All of these are simultaneously true and false. Subsidy in Nigeria petroleum sector is an implicit one. It only arises when petrol prices are related to the value of the Naira vis-a- viz some major foreign currencies. However, with any increase in domestic petrol price, inflation is generated, this leads to further devaluation of the Naira, and therefore the re- emergence of subsidy. It is like slowly walking-up a downward moving elevator.
It is a never fill grave. Without alternative means of public transport, the culture of using cars (and therefore huge petrol consumption) will persist. Wasteful energy (not only petrol) consumption persist. Nigeria needs better public transportation means. Smuggling of petroleum products will continue so long as our borders are porous, and there is lawlessness, corruption and collaboration by law enforcement agents, particularly as the Naira continues to loose value vis-a-viz other currencies. A proper liberalization of the petroleum sector must encompass formal export of petrol to Nigeria's neighbours. Petroleum product price increase, provides huge funds to the government. Most of these are frittered away on frivolous and indicated contracts. That is why infrastructure remains underdeveloped in Nigeria.
Over the years, these expected benefits of petroleum products price increases have remained a mirage what we however know for sure is the rising poverty profile of Nigerians. With inflation and devaluation, Nigerian governments proclaim annual budgets in millions of Naira, the people remain unemployed, domestic production drops, while reliance on imported goods increase. The consequence is poverty and other related social crises.

Original_Search_Title: How Diets Can Be Used to Manage Diseases
Headline: Nigeria: How Diets Can Be Used to Manage Diseases
Body_Text:
Dr. Kathleen Egbuna's book, Eating for Health is a compendium on everything about food from its calorific content to foods that best suit everybody and how food could be prepared so as not lose the nutritious value.
Egbuna, who obtained a Masters degree in nutritional biochemistry from the University of London and a Ph.D from the University of Benin, noted that in caring for the healthy person one should bear in mind that the body needs food that would provide all the necessary nutrients in the proper proportions.
But for a person suffering from certain diseases there may be need to abstain from some food and opt for therapeutic diet. Egbuna said notable are the impact of diets in the management of diabetes, peptic ulcers, obesity, hyperlipidermia, coronary disease and inborn errors of metabolism.
Also, she informed that for example in cancer, Riboflavin is known to retard the growth of tumours, nutrients such as selenium, vitamin A, vitamin E and vitamin C, are being explored as possible chemo preventive agents for cancer.
She said that a therapeutic diet is a modification of the normal diet, prepared with the aim of attaining and maintaining the perfect nutritional status.
"When a patient is being put on a special diet, one must design a regime to suit the normal food habits and the diets should be adjusted to the body's ability to metabolize nutrients and energy.
A person suffering from Diabetes Mellitus need the following daily quantity of nutrients: energy-1800 kcal, protein- 80 grams, Lipids- 80 grams, CHO- 180 grams. The person must not take sugar and artificial sweeteners.
The patient must be consistent in meal timing, and must religiously take vegetable and fruits.
A hypertensive patient should avoid salt. Quantity of nutrients for such person are sodium- +40mmol protein- 90 grams and energy 2000kcal.
Also, a person suffering from failure to digest and absorb fats which results in pancreatic disease must adhere to a low fat diet, fried foods not allowed , fats restricted in diet, quantity of some nutrients needed include lipids 50 grams, protein 120 grams and energy 2,800 kcal.
Those who need a very low fat or high carbohydrate are those who suffer from hepatitis. There should be restriction of iron in diet, no lard, cooking fat or oil allowed and restriction of cholesterol; quantity of nutrients needed include energy- 2,300kcal, lipids 25 grams, protein 90 grams and CO 400 grams.
Egbuna, however notes that for a healthy diet all the nutrients are inter-linked and lack of or an excess of one nutrient can affect the body reaction to other.....? eating variety of foods ensures the widest range of nutrients from diets and is very important because the body needs about forty different nutrients in order to function properly.
She further noted that a diet based on appropriate proportions of cereals, legumes, root tubers, fruits, vegetable oils and some quantity of fish and meat will take care of all the psychological needs of a person.
Giving general diet tips, Egbuna said regular small meals are better than starving and then gorging on large meals, because they get the metabolic rate burning off food more efficiently.
She explained that the hunger stimulus is controlled by part of the brain which contains centers known as the appestat that send out hunger signals when stimulated by either nervous, chemical or physical effects.
She said food such as cereals, legumes, tubers and fruits which contain natural sugar and starch, raise insulin level in the blood fairly quickly such that the feeling of satisfaction is felt sooner.
According to her, natural sugar is best all the time as refined sugar upset the blood-sugar balance by inducing insulin, which lowers its blood level and leaves the body craving extra sugar to top up quickly.
Dr. Egbuna also has advice for those who want to slim, the best slimming diet should be made of cereals in modest quantities, lean meats, fish, protein rich vegetables, legumes such as beans and peas and lots of fresh fruits.
She advises that a high intake of dietary fiber helps in weight control because it is filling with fewer calories and keeps away the feeling of hunger.
Slimming she said should be based on sensible eating habits and eating the right kind of foods. "The only way to lose weight permanently is to do it slowly. A diet based on an intake of about a thousand calories per day should cause a weight loss of about one kilogram in a week.
However she informed that a simple and flexible method f controlling calorie intake is by listing foodstuffs into three groups. The first list should include foods that someone watching calorie intake can eat as desired. Under this can be grouped lean meat, fish, green leafy vegetables, fresh fruit and fruit juices.
The second group would list foods that one should eat sparingly, they include beans, potatoes, yam, bread eggs, butter, margarine, corn , rice, plantains and bananas. The last group are foods that should generally be best avoided, they include, nuts alcoholic drinks, pastry of any kind ice cream, sugar, sugary foods, oil and fried foods.
Eating for Health also contains chapters such as impact of socio-cultural factors on food habits, impact of the psychological values of food, the need for food processing, destroying anti nutrients and intoxicants in foodstuffs, feeding the infant, feeding in pregnancy and lactation and feeding the elderly

Headline: Nigeria: Soldiers Sack Town, Raze 60 Houses
Body_Text:
Yenagoa — In what appears as a re-enactment of the raid on Odi town in November 1999, soldiers have sacked Liama town, also in Bayelsa State, leaving no fewer than 60 houses burnt down completely.
The attack on Liama in Brass Local Government Area, is the third in the state in recent times. The incident has left hundreds of the inhabitants homeless even as others have sent a passionate appeal to the Bayelsa State Government to come to their aid in form of relief materials.
Speaking in Yenagoa, a spokesman for Liama community, Chief Ikisa Dokubo Ikisa, said the soldiers came into the community with sophisticated weapons.
Ikisa also alleged that three youths of the community were shot dead by the soldiers. His account tallied with the figure of those earlier declared missing in the community by the state government.
The community spokesman who sobbed intermittently while narrating their ordeal, said that apart from houses destroyed, properties worth millions of naira were also vandalised.
Governor Diepreye Alamieyeseigha had already paid a visit to Liama to assess the level of damage in the community even as he called for the immediate withdrawal of troops from the area.
Alamieyeseigha also summoned the commandant of the military operations, the management of the contracting firm as well as Shell to a meeting in his office at Government House, Yenagoa.
The state Commissioner for Environment, Chief Lionel Jonathan-Omo in a chat with THISDAY confirmed that part of the community was actually razed down by military men attached to the Nigerian Agip Oil Company (NAOC) Limited's terminal at Brass following the seizure of a company boat by youths of Liama.
He blamed the incident on Shell for giving the contract to the oil service firm without any input from the state government.
The commissioner stated that the incident would have been avoided if the relevant government agencies were involved in the drafting of the memorandum of understanding (MOU) between Liama and the oil service firm.
He said most oil companies were operating in the state without the knowledge of the state government. He said that it was only when most of these companies ran into problems with their host communities that they run to the state government for help.
Efforts to speak with the authorities at the Nigeria Agip Oil Company (NAOC) Limited terminal at Brass proved abortive as at press time.
In 1999, Odi was also razed down by soldiers. Militant youths had killed some policemen, who were protesting perceived neglect.
Last year, soldiers sacked Tiv land kiling hundreds in retaliation of the kiling of 20 soldiers who were part of the peace-keeping mission in the area following the outbreak of violence between the Tiv and the Jukun.
Areas affected include Zaki Biam in Ukum Local Government which is widely acclaimed as the food basket of Benue State on account of having the largest yam market. Thousands of people had to take refuge in Daudu and Ukpam camps.
Buildings belonging to prominent Tiv citizens were razed including that of former Army Chief, Gen. Victor Malu by the vengeful soldiers. Three family members of Gen. Malu were also killed.
Last month, security agents also attacked Danja community in Katsina in protest of the killing of a policeman.

Headline: Nigeria: Bombs, Blasts And Smoking Corpses
Body_Text:
It was not exactly 11 September, 2001. But while it lasted, the
concurrent feeling of anxiety, confusion, panic and anguish must have
convinced Lagosians that last Sunday - 27 January - could not have
been more than a few streets away from the grisly episode in New
York.
And the day almost slipped into history as just another Sunday. Sadly
it did not. Lagosians knew where they were and what exactly they were
doing when the city started throbbing at about 5:15 pm on Sunday;
when explosions from the munition dump of the Ikeja Military
Cantonment started, killing hundreds and displacing a lot more.
David Eche, son of a Warrant Officer II (WO2) Michael Eche, was in
the cantonment's Catholic Church with other youths. David and his
peers were having a youth meeting when the bombs announced their
rage. Because the first explosions were milder, their minds never
went to the cantonment armoury, also known as Armunition Transit
Depot (ATD), as the source of the explosive sounds. "We initially
heard that the sounds were coming from expired bullets that were
being disposed," David told TEMPO.
Moments later, louder explosions followed. Before David and his
friends could work out what the matter was, a bomb seared the
church's roof, landing right in the midst of the congregating youths.
It exploded. And, the impact was wicked - a scary and fast-paced
confetti of asbestos shards from the collapsed ceiling. Shrapnel of
glass from the windows and fragment of concrete from the shuddering
walls mixed up with the smoke of bombs and fire. Hell, literally,
paid the church a visit. "There was no time to see if anyone was
injured. I just wanted to get out. Some people were injured but they
kept running with their injuries," David told TEMPO.
With the church in the process of incineration, the youths fled the
fire. But a hotter hell awaited them on the outside. By the time they
stepped out, the bombs had grown angrier, spewing, smoke, fires,
destruction and, naturally, death. The latter came in a huge dose.
Warrant Officer II (WO2) Peter Monday, returned from the Lagos State
Orientation Camp of the National Youth Service Corps (NYSC), where he
had been a platoon instructor for three weeks. Monday returned home
to a grotesque welcome of bomb blasts. His wife and eight children
fled when the bombs hit their home at Corporal and Bellow Quarters
(CBQ) 117, Flat 19 at the cantonment.
According to Monday, his family, like many others, fled the
cantonment when the bombs exploded hitting homes and killing
occupants as well as those fleeing. As at press time, Monday had
found his wife and two of the eight children. "I don't know where
they could have gone. I've served the army for 35 years and
everything I have is lost in the building," Monday moaned
regretfully. But he is not alone. An estimated one thousand people
are yet to return home.
They will return if they are alive and their homes are still standing
as many buildings, both within and outside the barracks crumbled like
cookiesfrom the serial explosions. Residents of buildings which
defied the blast were kept busy sweeping mounds of broken glasses
from their windows, ceilings and walls.
At the Senior Non-Commissioned Officer's (SNCO) quarters, Flats 6
-10, is a pasable imitation of the World Trade Centre scenario. The
roofs of the flats were licked by the hungry heads of the bombs,
flying off from the armoury.
The impact was the same as with everywhere at the cantonment. So
twisted was the ceiling fan in one room that it seemed it had been
made of Plasticine. The kitchen of the flat was strewn with various
food items which blended with pieces of the ceiling brought down when
the bomb exploded. Sitting, ominously, amidst the rubble, was an
unexploded bomb the size of an average yam tuber.
Thankfully, the occupants were not at home when the bombs visited.
The sister of the allottee who refused to give her name, told TEMPO
that she saw a boy pick a hand grenade that fell onto his path.
According to her, the boy attempted to throw off the grenade but he
wasn't lucky and the grenade exploded in his hands.
Scenes like that produced panic as homes and offices burnt without
hindrance.
Eyewitnesses said soldiers, with guns on their soldiers, fled,
scaling the high walls of the cantonment. In the melee, miscreants,
otherwise known as Area Boys cashed in on the situation, raping
fleeing soldiers wives and female children. Within the cantonment,
the explosions destroyed the armoury itself, three secondary schools
and numerous residential quarters among which were the 1,004 flats
occupied by middle-level military officers and the ICB, popularly
called Oshodi Barracks. Other buildings hit include CBQ55, 62, 64 and
68 and SSCO among others. On one road, a deep gash was burnt into the
tar by a bomb which exploded as it hit the road. Motorists abandoned
their vehicles as shops were looted and people robbed.
The bombs were all over. Residents fled in all directions. Parents
forgot their children and children, naturally, adopted a safety-first
approach abandoning parents too. But safety was in very short supply.
The fires grew with every explosion. Buildings quaked and fell within
the cantonment, fires flared so did panic, resulting in a massive
stampede through which hundreds met their deaths.
Sefiu Akojenu and Godwin Johnson, national and Lagos State Training
Officers of the Red Cross movement, told TEMPO that there was really
little to do by the way of rescue operations. The rescued persons
were those lucky to sustain only injuries in the stampede. "We took
victims to the hospital," they said. But the greatest worry is that
of displaced persons. As at press time, many parents were yet to
locate their children and other family members. To help, the Red
Cross, according to Akojenu and Johnson, has established a number of
centres in Lagos where displaced people are advised to go so that
their parents or guardians could trace them. Media houses are also
being used. The success rate however, has been low. Many of those who
fled, ran as far as Ikorodu, Epe and other towns.
Outside the cantonment, life was worse. At the neighbouring
Onigbongbo and other areas in nearby Maryland, panic walked tall. The
explosions rocked buildings, smashing windows, ceilings, burglary
proofs and in some cases bringing down buildings. At the Maryland end
of the Mobolaji Bank Anthony Street, the Maryland branch of the Wema
Bank had its sliding doors smashed. On Irawo, Oketa, Araromi,
Omolanke and other streets at Onigbongbo, residents fled in droves as
their homes heaved with ceaseless explosions, with a few returning on
Monday afternoon when the explosions had subsided considerably.
On the streets at Onigbongbo lie a large number of unexploded bombs.
However, residents are still not over the shock and the presence of
unexploded bombs makes returning unappealling. According to Jude
Ogbechie, a resident of Oketa Street, they have been warned not to
return to the neighbourhood until the unexploded bombs were removed.
Within the upscale Government Reservation Area (GRA) Ikeja, the
damage was equally massive. The chamber of the human rights activists
and lawyer, Femi Falana, was destroyed. Falana's office is on
Adekunle Fajuyi Street, beside the cantonment. When TEMPO visited the
office on Monday, it was deserted, with everything upturned. Numerous
spent shells were strewn all over the premises. On the same street,
the home of General Oladipo Diya, former Chief of General Staff, also
suffered extensive damage. The same fate befell the home of another
human rights lawyer, Chief Gani Fawehinmi, who had his ceilings blown
off.
Elsewhere in the city, the panic was huge. While those within the
vicinity of the cantonment knew the cause of the explosion, people in
other parts of the city had their heads whirling as homes in places
as far as Ikoyi, Idimu, Agege, Ogba and Ipaja throbbed relentlessly.
Motorists sped like demons as they tried to flee from what was
initially thought was a coup attempt. Buildings on Agege Motor Road
were not spared. The LSDPC estate was badly hit. Also hit were Wema
bank, Agege Motor Road branch, Ben Clinic and Maternity Centre, Omega
Community bank, Mandela Hospital and Christ Foundation Miracle
Centre.
In fact, the earliest explanation of the event suggested that a coup
was in progress. A television broadcast by the Lagos State governor,
Alhaji Bola Tinubu, however, dispelled this. Tinubu told Lagosians
that it was the armoury that had gone on fire. Tinubu appealed for
calm.
Calm, however, was uncommonly rare as the explosions continued to
rage late into Sunday night. At Oke Afa canal, Isolo, several
kilometres from the cantonment, the impact of the explosions drove an
estimated 200 persons - mostly children - to their death.
The victims are believed to have rushed into the canal because there
was no clear information on what was happening.
By Monday morning, hundreds of corpses were taken out of the swampy
canal where they mistakenly sought refuge but found death. Mr. J. A.
Moses, one of those who brought out the corpses told TEMPO that he
came to the canal to dig sand but found bodies floating. "So I
started removing them together with my son Gbenga. I have removed
about 150 corpses," Moses said. Monday Olaleye, a bystander at the
canal, told TEMPO that he saw about 2,000 corpses. "The victims were
coming from so many areas. They were trying to cross the canal but
they were deceived by the hyacinth in the water," Olaleye explained.
Among those who found their relatives in the canal was Mr. Jelili
Amuda. Amuda found the corpses of his elder brother and three of his
(the brother's children) floating. Also found, were people identified
as Babatunde Ogunsemowo, of 50, Olaiya Street, Mafoluku Oshodi.
Babatunde was said to have fled his home when the explosion began.
Mrs. Awawu Salami, 47, of Boladale Street, Oshodi, lost her son
Isiaka Salami to the canal. The list is endless.
On Monday, President Olusegun Obasanjo visited the cantonment. The
President, who arrived in the company of the Lagos State governor,
Alhaji Bola Tinubu, addressed a large crowd of displaced persons,
promising to relocate them. Obasanjo also promised relief materials
to the victims of the explosions. The President has also directed the
army authorities to commence inquiries into the tragedy.
According to TEMPO investigations, the tragedy could not have been of
lesser magnitude. TEMPO gathered that the affected armoury was built
by a Finnish Company, Panistama Nig. Limited (PIN) in 1974. Within
its now burnt bowels were an array of high calibre weapons. These
included thousands of 100mm bombs used by T55 armoured tanks, 75mm
artillery launchers and 95mm anti-aircraft bombs. Others were Olikum
bombs which are used for blasting bunkers and other concrete
structures. The Olikum bombs are believed to have been the largest
contributors to the explosion.
Military sources also told TEMPO that the armoury had numerous rocket
launchers, Brownie Magazine Guns (BMGS) made in Hungary and a slew of
British made hand grenades.
Sources told TEMPO that some of the ammunition were acquired during
the Civil War and moved to the cantonment when it was completed in
1974. This magazine learnt that the facility's cooling system,
numbering about 50 units, packed up a long time ago but the army
authorities refused to put them in order.
Successive commandants of the cantonment, we gathered, had complained
about faulty cooling systems, but such complaints did not stir the
desired actions from the appropriate authorities. A hint to this was
dropped by the commandant of the cantonment, Brig-Gen. Gabriel Emdin,
in a broadcast featuring Governor Tinubu, Emdin and Lagos State
Police Commissioner, Mr. Mike Okiro.
Meanwhile, the army authorities have cautioned that more explosions
could occur in the cantonment and nearby neigbourhood. These are
expected to come from yet unexploded bombs which may be set off by
excessive heat. There are many of these all around the cantonment. A
source also told TEMPO that another bunker behind the 1004 (magazine
depot) quarters, which contains low-calibre weapons, could go up in
flames because of lack of maintenance.
Though the bunker was built with the capacity to absorb shock and
pounding, it was razed because hand grenades and smaller bombs had
been exploding for hours before the extensive conflagration was set
off.
The cost of the explosions remains unknown. Military sources,
however, told TEMPO that the tragedy would affect the troops on
peace-keeping duties abroad. The collapsed bunker is said to be the
source of supplies for peace-keeping operations.

Original_Search_Title: The Flight From Bombs to Death
Headline: Nigeria: The Flight From Bombs to Death
Body_Text:
Godwin Ifijeh, who was part of the crowd, fleeing from the bomb explosions in Lagos recalls the journey that led to death for many
Mrs Tokunbo Adeniji, a resident of Ogundele, Mafoluku, Oshodi, a Lagos suburb, had just began preparing dinner that Sunday evening. Time was 5.55pm.
Now a routine, the family takes pounded yam for dinner every Sunday.
"My oga (husband) loves it. He eats it with vegetable soup every Sunday", Mrs. Adeniji, who had delivered a baby boy only a week earlier, began the story of her experience of the bomb explosions at the Ikeja Army Cantonment last Sunday, which not only destabilised the immediate neighbourhood completely but the entire Lagos.
Moment after she peeled the yam, put it on fire, the first bomb blast reverberated, shaking the entire community. Mrs. Adeniji did not take it seriously. She thought a Nepa transformer in the neighbourhood had exploded and went about what she was doing.Then, a second explosion of a proportional vibration came on,it then dawn on her that something was amiss.
Before she could come out to the balcony to take a look at the direction of the explosions, a third of a more severe dimension did not only explode, it came on, flamming all over the sky. It shook the house to its foundation, shattering slide doors and windows. More than ever before, she realises the seriousness of the situation. She rushed out of the house,abandoning her food on the cooker and her other children, playing next door ,jumped into her car to run for cover. The woman had gone over a kilometre before she recollected her self. She stopped,waited at the point speechless, watching the blazing explosions with fear. She eventually summoned courage,made a u-turn to race back home as the flames of the explosions burst with much more rapidity.
At home, the food was almost drying up on fire , she put off the cooker , raced out of the house to the direction the children were playing but like their mother, they had gone their own way to run from the explosions. Mrs Adeniji was aghast and confused but the explosions were no ones she could stand. Everywhere and every object around her was almost giving way from the quaking vibrations of the explosions.
"I stood there for over five minutes, contemplating what to do. I eventually raced all over the place in search of the children, and I eventually met them around the Airforce gate, running towards the airport. I shouted their names, picked them into the car and we sped away towards Ajao Estate. Vehiculer and human traffic was heavy.
"Resident of Oshodi, Mafoluku, Shogunle and to a large extent the exploding cantonment all headed in that direction, some holding out in the premises of the St. Jude Catholic Church, and Church of God Mission International both around the Murtala Muhammed Airport Road .
"We managed to find our way into Ajao Estate but before long we were trapped, human and vehiculer traffic were completely held down. We locked up our car, and abandoned it. The bombs were still flaming, flying all over as if the farther we go, the more they pursued us. There was stampede, people were climbing over themselves, scrambling to get past the area.
"With movement totally halted, motorists abandoned their vehicles and joined those walking to further compound the stampede," Mrs. Adeniji explained.
The story as told by Mrs. Adeniji adequately tells what the situation represented for residents of Oshodi/Mafoluku, Shogunle, Ladipo, Ilupeju, the Ikeja GRA, Maryland Onigbongbo, Anthony, Palmgrove, men, women, children and soldiers of the Ikeja Military Cantonment.
There was chaos all over. Women, children wailed, running as in war situations. Families mixed themselves with everybody including children for running in different directions as the earth and homes continued to quake from the explosions.
As residents of the immediate nieghbourhoods to the Army Cantonment moved away inward from the explosions, those of where they were fleeing were found to have abandoned their homes to move inward safety, equally shaken by the explosions. With vehicles completely out of the roads, people fled to as far as Badagry from Oshodi and suburbs, some others to Sango Ota, then of course, Ikotun to turn the Synagogue of All Nations to a refugee camp. It was in that attempt by some to cross from the inner most part of Ajao Estate into Ejigbo,the next community behind that ended in many dying in the stinking Oke-Afa Canal.
The Oke-Afa incident resulted from the fact that the more you moved away from the explosions, the more they appeared to be coming on. And with the explosions quaking the entire Lagos, no one in the suburbs was spared the flight bid into safety.
At Ajao Estate, while some of the panicking crowd gathered in groups to sing praises to God and pray for His intervention to save the situation, some others, children especially, chose to move deeply away across the swampy Oke-Afa canal if that would make them more prefected. But it turned into a flight from bombs to death.
The swampy grassy canal, an area, where a military jet, which claimed the lives of several officers some years back, crashed and sank, kept swallowing any one that stepped on it. And with the explosions continuing in the same proportions into the night, hundreds, thousands pushed towards the canal, attempting to cross the place in flight. But the hungry canal kept eating them up. No one stepped on it and crossed over.
While many slept in the open and in their cars that Sunday in Ikotun, Ejigbo and Ajao Estate till Monday morning, hundres had sunk to death in the Oke-Afa Canal

Headline: Nigeria: Ethnic Clash: 19 Killed, 19 Houses Burnt
Body_Text:
Tinubu orders security agents to deal with trouble makers
The ethnic clash between the Yoruba and the Hausa in Idi Araba, Mushin local council area of Lagos State, which erupted late Friday, continued yesterday with the death toll, rising to 19.
The clashes also left 19 houses completely razed.
Mike Okiro, Lagos State commissioner of police (CP), who told THISDAY on phone last night that the situation had normalised, confirmed that the clashes left 19 persons dead and 19 houses burnt.
"The situation is now under control. Casualties showed that 19 persons have been killed, and 19 houses burnt. "We have arrested 10 persons in the area with dangerous weapons, petrol and matches. All those injured have been taken to the General Hospital Ikeja, we cannot readily ascertain the figure of the injured right now," Okiro said.
The clashes, which began late Friday, had continued throughout Saturday, leaving many houses burnt and several persons killed. The two sides had used dangerous weapons, including guns, machetes and knives.
Residents of Okunola Street, the scene of the trouble in Idi Araba, Mushin local government area said the two sides engaged themselves in an all night shoot out through Saturday.
The clash, which initially involved Yoruba residents in the area, particularly Area Boys, and some Hausa, was taken over by members of the Oodua Peoples Congress (OPC), who reportedly carried guns and other dangerous weapons as against the Hausa, who were using machetes and knives.
The OPC were said to have come in after the Hausa youths attacked innocent Yoruba as against the Area Boys who were involved in the clash with them. The OPC were said to have been particularly touched by the killing of a three month old baby by the Hausa. The Hausa youths were said to have slashed off the head of the three-month old baby, a development that made residents to invite the OPC to come to their rescue.
Residents said the crisis began after a Hausa boy allegedly went to a public toilet in the area being manned by Area Boys to ease himself and thereafter refused to pay. The Area boys N5 from anyone who uses the toilet. In the process of forcing him to pay, the young man, who was being chased by the Area Boys, was said to have ran into an Hausa house.
The boys were said to have followed him to meet a strong resistance by the Hausa, who were there.
The Area Boys reportedly regrouped, mobilised and returned to the house to attack the Hausa, thereby triggering the crisis.
THISDAY however was privileged to meet the Hausa young man, Yam Idris from Kano, who reportedly used the public toilet and refused to pay in the area. He denied in smattering English that the toilet he used was a public one maintained by the Area Boys.
According to him, the toilet has been there for a long time and was always used by them, a reason he gave for refusing to pay as demanded by the Area Boys.
Meanwhile, speaking last night in a broadcast to the state, Governor Bola Tinubu ordered security forces to deal with all acts of public disorder.
"There is no where in the Holy Quran or the Holy Bible that says we must promote hatred and kill over minor disagreements. The various ethnic groups have lived together in peace in Lagos for several decades. Why are we now needlessly antagonising ourselves," the governor questioned.
Governor Tinubu therefore, expressed condolence to the families of the deceased saying: "I stand with you in this time of great sorrow."

Original_Search_Title: Government Agencies Worried About Environment Abuse
Headline: Uganda: Government Agencies Worried About Environment Abuse
Body_Text:
A wetland is a vegetated area of land that is flooded either permanently or seasonally. In Uganda, wetlands are normally referred to as swamps. The most common vegetation in Uganda's wetlands is papyrus but other wetlands include bogs, flood plains and swamp forests. Wetlands in Uganda occupy about 13 percent of the country's total area, mostly located in the central region of the country though are found in the West, Eastern and southern areas.
Conservation of wetlands is any assurance of income, health and safety of the people who live near them.
Wetlands store water as well as help in formation of conventional rainfall. Some wetlands maintain the flow of rivers throughout the year as well ensuring the constant flow of water from boreholes and wells. The wetlands act as water reserves.
Wetlands can be used to provide food to the population. Agriculture may be practiced along their banks, which are good sites for agriculture. They include fishing, material for manufacture of art and crafts, providing building materials, providing plants of different medicinal values, agriculture, brick making and many others.
The wetlands provide a habitat for several different species of plants and animals. Thus, their depletion will greatly affect in terms of population the different species including the Nile crocodile, the Hippopotamus and the monitor lizard. The plant species constitute of different types of palms and papyrus. Wetlands also provide a winter home and a resting-place to many millions of migratory birds as marsh harriers, northern ducks and European storks, and other species, which migrate to tropical African wetlands during the winter season in Europe. The Crested crane, Uganda's national bird breeds exclusively in seasonal grass wetlands.
If these wetlands are conserved, therefore, the country's tourism industry will grow since wetlands are a natural habitat for many different animals and plant species. This increases foreign exchange earnings.
However, wetlands in Uganda are under threat. This has already or is in the process of creating severe changes in climatic conditions now and in the near future. Brick making is one of the most serious threats to wetlands in Uganda today. Today, more and more people are getting involved in building houses and there is a drastic demand for bricks. These can be got only from the waterlogged area's many of them from the swamps.
The growth of industries has also resulted into increased amounts of waste being given off by industries. These wastes are being deposited into former wetlands as the garbage dumping areas in Kololo and Wakaliga near Natete.
Urbanisation has greatly been responsible for wetland depletion since the wetlands have been turned into economically viable areas. There has been rapid development in the country which has led to growth of towns, construction of infrastructure and migrations all of which need a great deal of land to be fully developed.
The wetlands are fertile and this has attracted many people to carry out agriculture. Crops such as yams, sugarcane, maize and sweet potatoes do well in wetlands. This results in over-cultivation of the wetlands due to desire for high yields, hence high rate of their depletion.
However, the wetlands in Kampala are continuously being depleted in-spite of the numerous calls by the different environmental bodies such as National Environmental Management Authority (NEMA), Ministry of Natural Resources and different wildlife clubs.
Initially for instance, the areas of Wakaliga (between Rubaga and Natete), the dumping grounds of Kololo were viable wetlands for different purposes. However, the Kampala City Council, licenced by Government, used these areas as dumping grounds for waste. This situation was worsened by the licencing of private firms into rubbish disposal industry, which used the same grounds for disposal of waste.
The area occupied by the Mukwano Industries, Lugogo trade show grounds, Kitante Golf Club, Nakawa industrial area, and Owino Market area, Luzira-Bugolobi and until recently Munyonyo recreation grounds were all wetlands.
Due to the high poverty level in the country, there has been a tremendous increase in slums. These slums such as Kibuye, Makerere-Kivulu and Kisenyi, once wetlands, are now being used for human settlement.
Wetland depletion has also been associated with floods. Wetlands are known to have been able to store water but since they have all been destroyed, there is no way water run-offs can be controlled. This has resulted into increased floods around the Clock-Tower area, Nsambya, Bugolobi, Jinja-road and Nakawa. These floods have greatly hindered movement within the city and around the affected areas.
Floods have also increased the number of people suffering from waterborne diseases. Areas mostly affected by these epidemics were formally wetlands, which were depleted. Examples of such epidemics include the Cholera epidemic, which killed a large number of people in areas of Kisenyi, Mulago, Kivulu, Kibuye and many others.
One solution to avoid wetland depletion is massive education and local sensitization campaigns. It's important to note too, that high demand for land for settlement in urban centers like Kampala has also tended to endanger the existing wetlands e.g. Nakawa, Industrial area, Nalukolongo and Bwaise. Education and campaign can be done through meetings with their local councils and through publication of different environmental sectors.
More so, alternative sources of income generation may be the best alternative instead of exploiting the wetlands. People are encouraged to take strong measures in their activities in order to avoid the natural casualties. Instead of displacing some of these animals, one can instead preserve and protect them thereby earning income through the tourist industry.
In addition to the above, environmental bodies should be formed like the National Environmental Management Authority so that these bodies aid in wetland conservation. Clubs like the Uganda Wildlife, which assist in the sensitization of the masses about conservation of both the flora and the fauna. The government has legislated against swamps and wetland reclamation. Swamps must be allowed to re-grow.

Headline: Uganda: Domestic Survival Depleting Wetlands
Body_Text:
Uganda is a blessed country with a proportionately well distributed network of water bodies and wetlands. It is difficult to imagine a situation where we can no longer talk about a green Uganda. However, if the trend continues unchecked, we shall invite the desert here faster than we could ever have anticipated.
Wetlands cover about 30,105 sq kms representing 13 percent of the total area of Uganda. There are two types of wetland in Uganda. The permanent wetlands currently cover an estimated 7,296 sq km and the seasonal wetlands cover 22,809 sq km. At least 69 percent of the total area under wetlands comprises impeded drainage, while swamps constitute at least 30 percent and swamp forest 1 percent. Uganda has mainly two categories of wetlands where one type is associated with lakes (lucustrine) and the other with rivers (riverine).
The lucustrine lakes include the Kyoga/Kwania complex, Lakes George, Edward and Albert.The riverine rivers include the Okele and Kafu systems.
For a very long time now wetlands in Uganda have been put under various uses. There were fishing and hunting activities, rice growing, grazing, brick making, and harvesting raw materials for building purposes. Other related primary roles include sediment, nutrient and toxin retention, stabilisation of the hydrological cycle and microclimate.
Wetlands have for long provided people with a way of livelihood. It is not easy to attach monetary value to all the activities that are carried out as a result of wetlands. Estimates put it at $200(appr. Shs 350,000) per family for those in the rural areas engaged in the harvesting of papyrus. As for the supply of water in rural areas, the estimate is at $ 25m (Shs 43.7bn) per year at commercial prices.
Despite the protection by law for our wetlands as per the National Environment Statute 1995, they continue to be reclaimed and degraded. Wetlands are now being drained for purposes of agriculture especially for the cultivation of sugarcanes, yams, sweet potatoes and eucalyptus. Pallisa and Iganga districts have experienced serious encroachment for agricultural usage.
Sand mining and clay extraction for brick making have resulted in the creation of pits which usually keep stagnant water in turn becoming a breeding ground for disease carrying vectors like mosquitoes (malaria) and snails (bilharzias). Such activities are closely linked to developments in the urban centres where there is an ever-increasing demand for bricks.
The unprincipled dumping of solid garbage a number of times next to water bodies poses a serious problem. Such is the case at the Gaba Market in Kampala and another near the Luzira Prisons. Masese swamp in Jinja and Walugogo valley in Iganga Town.
Degradation of the wetlands is also experienced through the deforestation of swamp forests. Such activities exist in Mukono, Mpigi, Masaka and Rakai districts.
Swamp fires common in the districts of Apac, Mpigi and Lira have a danger of affecting the biodiversity in the area. They are normally deliberately started by hunters or to encourage the regeneration of new papyrus.
The degradation of wetlands in places as Jinja district has had adverse effects on the environment. The reclamation of the Mutai swamp forest by Kakira Sugar Works for the purpose of growing sugar cane from where the river Kiko originates has led to the loss of wetland's capacity to clean wastewater from the sugar factory.
In Iganga, district, it is estimated that 64 percent of the seasonal wetlands have been reclaimed for rice growing. In Pallisa, 68 percent of the seasonal wetlands have also been reclaimed for the same purpose. The final impact of this level of degradation has been the reduction in number of permanent streams, disappearance of permanent springs and a low ground water yield in the wells. In some instances, the reclaimed lands have become unproductive and abandoned after two years.
It should be pointed out here that rice growing does not necessarily destroy the wetlands but it does change the natural form to a managed ecosystem. A case in point is in Iganga where because of the bad practise that went unchecked, there has now been a shift from the growing of perennial crops such as bananas to annual crops such as maize and rice.
Until recently, it was the official policy to drain wetlands. They were associated with diseases like malaria and schistosomiasis. Many swamps around urban areas in Uganda were drained in the 1950s as a malaria eradication measure.
The situation is worse in the Eastern part of the country where about 20 percent of the wetlands have been destroyed. It is also important to note that the fertility of wetlands for the growing of food crops is not a sustainable endeavour. Wetlands have a low natural fertility. Studies have revealed that over 72 percent of wetlands if drained, would provide poor soil fertility while only 3 percent would provide medium to high soil fertility. The remaining 25 percent would be in the low to nil soil productivity range.
Industrial pollution is also a major impediment to the conservation of wetlands. This kind of pollution is mostly in the mining sector, breweries and tanneries. There is usually nutrient enrichment originating from agricultural land, urban sewage and industries.
Wetland management is still in its infant stages in Uganda. The government in 1986 undertook to protect the country's wetlands by declaring a ban on large-scale drainage. A number of pieces of legislation have been passed with an aim of enhancing sustainable utilisation and management of wetlands in the country. More to that, a wetlands information system to provide relevant information to all stakeholders was inaugurated in July 1998. At the end of the day, it is the duty of each and every one of us to attach importance to our environmnent and let others know its importance in our lives and the generations to come.

Original_Search_Title: Government Agencies Worried About Environment Abuse
Headline: Uganda: Government Agencies Worried About Environment Abuse
Body_Text:
A wetland is a vegetated area of land that is flooded either permanently or seasonally. In Uganda, wetlands are normally referred to as swamps. The most common vegetation in Uganda's wetlands is papyrus but other wetlands include bogs, flood plains and swamp forests. Wetlands in Uganda occupy about 13 percent of the country's total area, mostly located in the central region of the country though are found in the West, Eastern and southern areas.
Conservation of wetlands is any assurance of income, health and safety of the people who live near them.
Wetlands store water as well as help in formation of conventional rainfall. Some wetlands maintain the flow of rivers throughout the year as well ensuring the constant flow of water from boreholes and wells. The wetlands act as water reserves.
Wetlands can be used to provide food to the population. Agriculture may be practiced along their banks, which are good sites for agriculture. They include fishing, material for manufacture of art and crafts, providing building materials, providing plants of different medicinal values, agriculture, brick making and many others.
The wetlands provide a habitat for several different species of plants and animals. Thus, their depletion will greatly affect in terms of population the different species including the Nile crocodile, the Hippopotamus and the monitor lizard. The plant species constitute of different types of palms and papyrus. Wetlands also provide a winter home and a resting-place to many millions of migratory birds as marsh harriers, northern ducks and European storks, and other species, which migrate to tropical African wetlands during the winter season in Europe. The Crested crane, Uganda's national bird breeds exclusively in seasonal grass wetlands.
If these wetlands are conserved, therefore, the country's tourism industry will grow since wetlands are a natural habitat for many different animals and plant species. This increases foreign exchange earnings.
However, wetlands in Uganda are under threat. This has already or is in the process of creating severe changes in climatic conditions now and in the near future. Brick making is one of the most serious threats to wetlands in Uganda today. Today, more and more people are getting involved in building houses and there is a drastic demand for bricks. These can be got only from the waterlogged area's many of them from the swamps.
The growth of industries has also resulted into increased amounts of waste being given off by industries. These wastes are being deposited into former wetlands as the garbage dumping areas in Kololo and Wakaliga near Natete.
Urbanisation has greatly been responsible for wetland depletion since the wetlands have been turned into economically viable areas. There has been rapid development in the country which has led to growth of towns, construction of infrastructure and migrations all of which need a great deal of land to be fully developed.
The wetlands are fertile and this has attracted many people to carry out agriculture. Crops such as yams, sugarcane, maize and sweet potatoes do well in wetlands. This results in over-cultivation of the wetlands due to desire for high yields, hence high rate of their depletion.
However, the wetlands in Kampala are continuously being depleted in-spite of the numerous calls by the different environmental bodies such as National Environmental Management Authority (NEMA), Ministry of Natural Resources and different wildlife clubs.
Initially for instance, the areas of Wakaliga (between Rubaga and Natete), the dumping grounds of Kololo were viable wetlands for different purposes. However, the Kampala City Council, licenced by Government, used these areas as dumping grounds for waste. This situation was worsened by the licencing of private firms into rubbish disposal industry, which used the same grounds for disposal of waste.
The area occupied by the Mukwano Industries, Lugogo trade show grounds, Kitante Golf Club, Nakawa industrial area, and Owino Market area, Luzira-Bugolobi and until recently Munyonyo recreation grounds were all wetlands.
Due to the high poverty level in the country, there has been a tremendous increase in slums. These slums such as Kibuye, Makerere-Kivulu and Kisenyi, once wetlands, are now being used for human settlement.
Wetland depletion has also been associated with floods. Wetlands are known to have been able to store water but since they have all been destroyed, there is no way water run-offs can be controlled. This has resulted into increased floods around the Clock-Tower area, Nsambya, Bugolobi, Jinja-road and Nakawa.
