These floods have greatly hindered movement within the city and around the affected areas.
Floods have also increased the number of people suffering from waterborne diseases. Areas mostly affected by these epidemics were formally wetlands, which were depleted. Examples of such epidemics include the Cholera epidemic, which killed a large number of people in areas of Kisenyi, Mulago, Kivulu, Kibuye and many others.
One solution to avoid wetland depletion is massive education and local sensitization campaigns. It's important to note too, that high demand for land for settlement in urban centers like Kampala has also tended to endanger the existing wetlands e.g. Nakawa, Industrial area, Nalukolongo and Bwaise. Education and campaign can be done through meetings with their local councils and through publication of different environmental sectors.
More so, alternative sources of income generation may be the best alternative instead of exploiting the wetlands. People are encouraged to take strong measures in their activities in order to avoid the natural casualties. Instead of displacing some of these animals, one can instead preserve and protect them thereby earning income through the tourist industry.
In addition to the above, environmental bodies should be formed like the National Environmental Management Authority so that these bodies aid in wetland conservation. Clubs like the Uganda Wildlife, which assist in the sensitization of the masses about conservation of both the flora and the fauna. The government has legislated against swamps and wetland reclamation. Swamps must be allowed to re-grow.

Original_Search_Title: FOS Records Mixed Price Movements in 4th Q 2001
Headline: Nigeria: FOS Records Mixed Price Movements in 4th Q 2001
Body_Text:
THE Composite Consumer Price Index which measures changes in the
average level of retail prices paid by consumers in both urban and
rural areas of the country was 4419.2 in November, 2001. This
represented a drop of 2.9 per cent below 4553.1 recorded in October
2001. The decline in the index resulted from decreases in the prices
of food items, kerosene and household goods. However, the prices of
drinks and transport fares rose in many centres. The urban and rural
indices fell by 1.4 and 3.3 per cent respectively.
In the twelve months to November, 2001, the average consumer price
level went up by 17.4 per cent. This was lower than 19.3 per cent
observed in the previous month. During the same period, the urban and
rural indices rose by 18.0 and 17.3 per cent respectively.
The percentage change in the average composite CPI for the twelve
month period ending November, 2001 over the average of the CPI for
the previous twelve month period was 18.7. This means that a fixed
market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with N100.00
during the twelve month period ending November, 2000 sold for N118.7
on the average for the corresponding period ending November, 2001.
The Composite Food Index dropped by 4.1 per cent in November, 2001.
This was largely caused by decreases in the prices of staples such as
yam tuber, plantain and beans. However, the prices of edible oil rose
in many centres.
Between November, 2000 and November 2001, the index rose by 31.5 per
cent.
The average annual rate of rise of the Composite Food Index for the
twelve month period ending November, 2001 was 26.3 per cent.
The "All Item Less Farm Produce" index which excludes the price of
agricultural produce fell by 1.0 per cent in November 2001. In the
twelve months to November, 2001, the index dropped by 1.0 per cent.
The average annual rate of rise of the index was 7.7 per cent for the
twelve month period ending November, 2001.

Headline: Nigeria: Grumblings In The Fold
Body_Text:
Complaints from millions of Nigerians who were registered as members
of Operation Farewell To Poverty, OFP, launched two years ago by
Chief (Mrs) Queen Ngozi Babalola, is capable of sparking off violence
of unimaginable proportions.
These days, Ibadan, the capital of Oyo state is fast gaining
notoriety as a city where distasteful and shattering news break.
Barely two weeks after the Attorney General of the federation and
Justice Minister, Chief Bola Ige was shot dead in his Bodija home,
Ibadan, another equally paralysing salvo was fired from the same
Bodija area of Ibadan to millions of Nigerians who have been battling
since 1999 to bid farewell to abject poverty through a
non-governmental organisation called Operation farewell To Poverty
OFP.
The bombshell came from no other person than Chief (Mrs) Queen Ngozi
Babalola, the initiator and national coordinator of OFP and who is
said to be a blood relation of the first lady, Mrs. Stella Obasanjo.
In a chat with TEMPO at the organisation's secretariat complex, old
Bodija Estate, Ibadan she disclosed that "for now there is no money
to give out as loans to members". Although the organisation is
expecting a whooping sum of N500 million which has allegedly been
approved by a federal government agency, Mrs. Babalola hinted that
not all duly registered members of OFP will benefit from the money.
"Loans will only be given to those who have viable projects which
will bring in profit for the loanee to be able to pay back the loan",
Babalola added. The director of Operations of OFP Chief Samson Funso
Abiri spoke in the same vein. " What we have discovered is that it is
not everybody that can manage money so those who cannot manage money,
who have not been into business, who have no skill, they will be
under one or two of their leaders who will be managing that business
and they will be sharing profit, while at the same time they will be
paying back the soft loan", he told TEMPO in an exclusive interview.
But, this arrangement, to millions of OFP members, smacks of fraud
and an ingenious means of making stupendous money at their expense by
Babalola, the national coordinator of OFP.
Their thinking is quite understandable. As millions of Nigerians live
below the poverty line, they jumped at the idea of registering as
members of OFP after Babalola promised to sell to them, at subsidized
rates, rice, sugar, milk and vegetable oil, and in addition grant to
each of them a loan of N100,000 which they would use for business
purpose, to enable them provide for themselves and their dependants.
Having served as a member of the Presidential Advisory Committee,
PAC, after President Olusegun Obasanjo came to power,thus convincing
many that her pet project, OFP enjoys the recognition and support of
the federal Government of Nigeria and some international
institutions, millions of Nigerians were falling over themselves to
obtain the organisation's registration forms. Initially, immediately
after OFP was launched on October 25, 1999, a membership form sold
for N100. It was later increased to N350 and was subsequently jerked
up to N1,000 per head. These poverty-stricken Nigerians had hardly
recovered from this initial payment when they were asked to pay N500
each for opening of bank accounts where their loans would be paid.
Payment is compulsory for anybody who hopes to take the loan.
TEMPO investigations reveal that both the registration fees and the
money paid by members for opening of bank accounts were remitted to
the purse of the national headquarters of OFP by state coordinators.
It was also learnt that collective accounts under the banner of OFP
were opened for members, rather than individual accounts which
members were made to pay for.
In Lagos state alone, OFP's members, TEMPO learnt, are well over
50,000. In Oyo state they number up to 500,000. The organisation has
thousands of members in all the 36 states of the federation,
including Abuja, the Federal Capital Territory. Going by the
numerical strength of OFP's membership, which Abiri says, is in
millions, a staggering sum of money has accrued to Babalola's purse.
These initial levies have ignited a grumbling among Babalola's flock,
and this is capable of sparking off violent backlash against the
leadership of OFP. Their patience, ostensibly, is fast running out.
For the past two years they had hoped, day after day to obtain the
loans. But it was not forthcoming. Yet many went through thick and
thin to cough out the compulsory fees. When words made the rounds in
Lagos that the loan would be paid unfailingly last October, Mrs.
Titilayo Ayorinde, a potential beneficiary headed for Abeokuta, Ogun
State to borrow N1,000 from one of her relations. The money was to be
contributed for opening a bank account.
Titilayo who was a distributor for UAC foods until she ran out of
money to run the business, was eager to get the loan and go back to
her business. But her dreams remain unfulfilled till today and she is
yet to refund the borrowed N1,000.
Mr. Michael Ladega another intending beneficiary banked so much on
the promised loan that he used the remaining N9,000 on him to take up
a shop where he would be selling planks. His expectation stretched
from days to weeks and from weeks to months until the one year rent
he paid expired without opening the shop for business. "My landlord
has given me notice to pay for another one year or forfeit my tenancy
of the shop. How can I pay again for a shop which I never opened for
business",he laments. The much expected financial assistance is also
far from coming to 55 year-old Ayinde Kazeem who would want to use
the loan to purchase a power generating plant, as constant power
outage renders his car washing venture dormant most of the time.
Although none of the 300 members who registered with OFP under the
aegis of Nigeria Abled Farmers Association NAFA Ile-Oluji, Ondo,
state has received the loan, their coordinator Chief Adeyeye Adebayo
sees OFP "as a laudable project which deserves to be assisted
financially by both states and federal government so that Mrs.
Babalola will be able to carry out her promise of alleviating the
poverty of the teeming masses".
The plan of Chief Abel Olusegun Badejo, 63, of investing his loan in
buying and selling of 'garri' and yams is evaporating by the day. "It
is unbelievable that up till now loans have not been disbursed to us.
As a Zonal coordinator of OFP, members under me are already casting
aspersions on me. Many of them believe that Mama (Mrs. Babalola) has
given me the cheque for their loan but that I kept it in my personal
account to yield interests.
When I refurbished my Mercedes Benz car recently, which I bought in
1993 when I was still working with National Oil and Chemical
Marketing PLC, many were saying that I purchased the car with their
loan", Badejo told TEMPO.
In spite of the complaints of unfair treatment from OFP's members
across the country, some members have smiled to the bank. Among the
lucky ones is Alhaja Safiu Omiyale from Ibadan who got a loan of
N100,000 for trading in June 2001. Mrs. Joyce Banigo and thirteen
other people also got a loan of N1.2 million on 5 November, 2001
which they shared. On November 28, 2001 Mrs. Philomena Pius went home
with a cheque of N100,000, while a sum of N200,000 came the way of
Mrs. R. A. Babatunde from Kwara State in September 2001. Mrs. Tokunbo
Fajuyitan was given N200,000 on November 2001, thus becoming one of
the few Lagosians who have benefited from the credit facilities.
Before the end of last year, a group of Ode-Omu community farmers was
given N1 million to cultivate pineapple for exporting. These are the
names made available to TEMPO although Abiri said there are many
other people who have benefited from the credit facility.
The idea of establishing Operation Farewell to Poverty, OFP, was
originated by Mrs. Queen Ngozi Babalola, an indigene of Owerri, Imo
state who married to a Yoruba man 35 years ago. Mrs. Babalola, who is
about 63 years old, allegedly made an inroad into the Nigerian
corridors of power through Mrs.
Stella Obasanjo with whom she is said to have blood relations. After
Gen.
Olusegun Obasanjo emerged as the president elect in 1999, a
Presidential Advisory Committee, PAC, was inaugurated and through the
first Lady, Stella, Babalola was appointed a member of the committee,
serving in the Poverty Eradication, Economic Empowerment and Rural
Development sub-committee.
The various suggestions and proposals put forward by this committee
enriched her knowledge of floating a non-governmental organisation
meant to engage in poverty alleviation through job creation, granting
of credit facilities, importation of food items for distribution at
affordable prices and promotion of cottage industries among others.
Duly registered and awarded a certificate of registration (RC 12737)
by the Corporate Affairs Commission of Nigeria, Babalola formally
launched OFP on October 25, 1999.
Today, OFP has grown into a gargantuan organisation, having offices
in all the state capitals of the federation with millions of card
carrying members.
At the head of the organisation is Mrs. Babalola, the national
coordinator and chief executive officer. She is assisted by the
national secretary and the director of operations in the day to day
running of the organisation.
Coordinators, who report to the national coordinator, are appointed
in all the states of the federation. For better coordination, each
state is divided into a number of zones, depending, on the size of
members and a zonal coordinator, who reports to the state
coordinator, is appointed for each zone.
To ensure a smooth operation of the organisation departments such as
Administration, Credit Management, Training and Inspectorate are made
to work hand-in-hand.
This organisational structure is seen by many as a product of a well
thought out arrangement. This however become relevant and useful if
it is used to empower the masses economically.

Original_Search_Title: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Headline: Nigeria: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Body_Text:
Questions why the police are holding on to the corpse six years after she was allegedly killed by a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi
She may never be able to put behind her for the rest of her life the gruesome death of her 25-year old daughter six years ago from the bullets of a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi (Force No 110279) at their village of Uokha, Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State.
Frail-looking Madam Olohigbe Jegede, 68, who has remained heavily weighed down by that unexpected sad loss of her fashion designer daughter, Miss Blessing Amore, in September 1996, said as she recalled the incident amidst tears to City Diary last weekend: "Police Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, without cause, shot and killed my daughter in company of three other policemen detailed to our village, Uokha, to investigate and arrest a lad who allegedly beat another to death while they were fighting.
"We were not living in the house where the incident happened. He shot my daughter, killed her, threw her corpse into their vehicle and drove away without even allowing me to see it. Till date, I have not set my eyes on her corpse. We were told that the corpse was deposited at the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, near Ekpoma, we visited Auchi, Afuze and Benin City several times, demanding for her corpse for proper burial, its six years now since she was killed, the police have refused to release her corpse to us. they are not giving any explanations for it either. But we have continued to ask that the police release the corpse to us so that we can give her a proper burial. The corpse is not seen. The policeman involved has long been transferred away from the Afuze Police Division he was when he committed the offence, so is the Divisional Police Officer (DPO) then, Mr. Kenene Onyire, a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP)".
"If only I had known that Blessing would not return home," the aging woman, who said she had separated from the late fashion designer's father, continued: "I would not have allowed her to go and fix her hair that day. We had just finished frying garri and she told me she was going to fix her hair. I told her to return quickly as I was going to put yam on fire for pounded yam.
"The next thing I heard were cries, and shouts of they have killed the girl, they have killed the girl. Of course, I never thought that the person in question was my daughter and I have no reason to think other wise, convinced that she was were she was fixing her hair until somebody ran to me to say that the person he saw on the ground looked like her. I threw away what I was doing, ran to the place but the policemen threw the corpse into their vehicle before I got there. I tried to catch a glimpse of the body but they would not allow me. They drove everybody away from the scene and sped off with the corpse and a bleeding young man they shot along with her.
"Then, we began running around their offices from Afuze to the Area Command Headquarters, Auchi, as well as the State Command Headquarters, Benin City. All through, they did not allow me to see her corpse, I am not even sure they carried out any autopsy on it.
"They also did not take any concrete action aganist the policeman who shot her. Each time we asked them what they were doing about the case, they would tell us that they were handling it their own way. The next thing was that they transferred the man and till date, we never heard anything about the matter again neither do we know where the culprit is or what has happened to him".
As the account had it, sergeant Nnamdi, who was attached to the Afuze Divisional Headquarters of the Edo State Police Command then, was detailed along with three other policemen to effect the arrest of a young boy who had beaten another child to death.
The two kids were said to have fought on September 13, 1996 and one of them collapsed in the course of the fight and eventually died on September 23.
The policemen arrived the town at about 4.00pm on that fateful day, moved to the house of the other kid, who allegedly fought with the dead one to effect his arrest but met the house deserted. They then embarked on indiscriminate arrest of people in the area, relying on what they termed magistrate courts rule 2, which empowers the police to arrest as many suspects as possible to a crime committed to make it possible for the real suspect to be arrested. The entire area was said to have been thown into confusion, with people fleeing in all direction to escape being arrested.
Amidst that confusion, the late Blessing Amore was said to be returning from where she went to fix her hair, unaware of what was going on.
Sergent Nnamdi reportedly halted her, pointed his service pistol at her while ordering her to go into their vehicle. The late Miss Amore was said to have quietly obeyed but the unexpected happened. As she was trying to climb into the police van, Nnamdi's gun went off and hit the lady at her side. The bullet was said to have tore through her, uprooted her breast before hitting and wounding a young man, Friday Ohikhena, who was already in the vehicle. Miss Amore fell and died instantly, soaked in her pool of blood to set the community in uproar. The policemen reportedly fired several shots into the air to scare away the gathering crowd, picked up the body, threw it into the van to join the injured young man and sped away with it.
On the advicof the then DPO of the Afuze Police Divisional Headquarters, Mr. Kanene Onyire, the policemen were said to have taken the the corpse and the injured Ohikhena to the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, for deposit in the mortuary and treatment respectively.

Headline: Nigeria: Tiv-Jukun Crises: Legal Matters Arising
Body_Text:
The following facts have become notorious in the TIV - JUKUN crises. First, the conflict has become endemic, with no immediate sign of cessation. Secondly, the war took a different dimension when, in October, 2001, "soldiers" allegedly on peace - keeping mission originally mandated to confine themselves to Taraba State, missed (?) their way into Benue State, got captured by suspected Tiv militia and also got murdered. Thirdly the Federal Government, led by President Olusegun Obasanjo, ordered soldiers to "track down" the killers of the "soldiers", which order saw the perpetration of genocide against the Tiv nation generally. Following all these are accusations, counter accusations, historical genealogies, and at times even abuses. The verbal cum paper combatants are the two warring tribes on one hand and the Federal Government versus either or both of the combatants on the other hand.
Several legal issues arise from this otherwise simple narration. The first legal matter arising is who is a citizen of Nigeria; and what makes him an indigene of a particular state? Secondly, who owns land in Nigeria? Thirdly, when, how and why does the Federal government need to deploy military personnel to quell internal crises? These and several other ancillary issues will be dealt with in this piece.
We shall proceed to analyse the issues one after the other. The first is who is a true citizen of Nigeria, and what makes him an indigene of a particular state? Chapter III of the l999 Constitution deals with the vexed issue of Nigerian citizenship. Section 25 provides thus:
"25 - (1) The following persons are citizens of Nigeria by birth, namely:
(a) every person born in Nigeria before the date of independence, either of whose parents or any of whose grand parents belongs or belonged to a community indigenous to Nigeria: Provided that a person shall not become a citizen of Nigeria by virtue of this section if neither of his parents nor any of his grand parents was born in Nigeria;
(b) every person born in Nigeria after the date of independence either of whose parents or any of whose grand parents is a citizen of Nigeria; and.
(c) every person born out side Nigeria either of whose parents is a citizen of Nigeria.
(2) In this section, "the date of independence" means the 1st day of October, 1960".
Section 31 of the same Constitution, which is also relevant here, provides thus:
"31. For the purpose of this chapter, a parent or grand parent of a person shall be deemed to be a citizen of Nigeria if at the time of the birth of that person such parent or grand parent. would have possessed that status by birth if he had been alive on the date of independence; and in this section, " the date of independence" has the meaning assigned to it in section 25(2) of this Constitution".
One would add straight away here that in defining who a Nigerian citizen is, the Constitution has only mentioned two generations - those of one's parents and grandparents. No mention of great grand parents is made. Therefore, even if by 1884- 1885 when the territory today known as Nigeria was handed over to Britain at the Berlin Conference, or in 1914 when the historical amalgamation took place, one's great grand parents were not Nigerians yet, once the grand (or ordinary) parents became Nigerians by birth, they would still enjoy or be enjoying equal citizenship rights with persons whose great, great, grand parents had been living in Nigeria before historical times. In addition, it is a notorious fact that all the tribes and ethnic nationalities that today make up Nigeria, apart from a negligible few migrated from somewhere to their present locations. With this legal cum historical background, it is safe to conclude that all Nigerians who profess to be citizens of Nigeria by birth are indeed true citizens, the times their parents and grandparents or great grand parents migrated into Nigeria not withstanding. I make this submission, especially in the light of the Jukun claiming to have reached the Benue Valley before the Tiv, which claim is highly disputable in any case.
The second sub-issue is who is an indigene of a State, properly so called? It is hereby submitted first, that for a person to be a citizen of Nigeria, he must first be deemed as belonging to a "community indigenous to Nigeria" See Shugaba Darman V. The Federal Minister of Internal Affairs & Ors. (1980) FNLR 302. Reference here is to "a community indigenous to Nigeria" and not "indigenous to a State". In Chediak V. Permanent Secretary, Federal Ministry of Internal Affairs & Ors. (1980) FNLR 143 at 145, Johnson, J, held, inter alia that "the claim of the applicant that he was born in Nigeria must be supported as required by evidence showing that either of his parents or grandparents belongs or belonged to a community indigenous to Nigeria. What in the context is the meaning of community indigenous to Nigeria ? The Concise Oxford Dictionary defines indigenous as follows - 'native, belonging naturally'." No reference is made to "community indigenous to State A or State B"; the reference is to "a community indigenous to Nigeria" as a whole. If the Constitution had intended otherwise, it would have so provided. On this construction, therefore, all Nigerians by birth are indigenes of all the states of the Federation. None are "settlers " in any State of the Federation they find themselves.
The framers of the various Constitutions of Nigeria, especially from the 1963 Republican Constitution, to the 1999 Constitution, have always had this in mind, hence the provisions relating to citizenship by birth have always remained fundamentally the same. And to further protect citizens and even residents of Nigeria, provisions guaranteeing fundamental human rights have always been made. For a proper appreciation of this point, judicial definition of the nagging phrase "fundamental human rights "will be hereby made. In Ransome Kuti V. Attorney - General of the Federation (1985) 2 NWLR ( Pt.6) 211, Eso, J.S.C, had this to say-
"But what is the nature of a fundamental right? It is a right, which stands above the ordinary laws of the land and which in fact is antecedent to the political society itself. It is a primary condition to a civilized existence and what has been done by our constitution, since independence is to have these rights enshrined in the constitution so that the rights could be "immutable" to the extent of the "non - immutability" of the constitution itself".
This means that what has been enshrined in Chapter IV of the 1999 Constitution "stands above the ordinary laws " of Nigeria; is antecedent to the project called Nigeria, hence survives all governments and is " a primary condition to a civilized existence". The right of every citizen to " reside in any part " of Nigeria is guaranteed under section 41 (1) of the 1999 Constitution, while section 42 of the Constitution prohibits discrimination against citizens based on circumstances of their birth or gender, political association or place of origin . Section 43 grants "every citizen of Nigeria " "the right to acquire and own immovable property any where in Nigeria ". All these are guaranteed as fundamental human rights, which are antecedent to the Nigerian "political society ". They do not seek to protect "indigenes" of a particular state, but citizens of Nigeria by birth. The issue of one being an indigene of a particular state as it relates to citizens by birth is, therefore, unconstitutional, null and void.
Another legal theory that pulls the rug off the feet of persons claiming to be "indigenes" of particular states is the effect of state creation in a federation like Nigeria. Section 318 (1) defines a state as being " one of the component parts of the federation " Also, under the Exclusive Legislative List found in the Second Schedule to the Constitution, the National assembly has the exclusive power to legislate on creation of states, citizenship, naturalization and aliens. So far, the National Assembly has not yet carved out states based on tribal or ethnic distributions. There is no "Hausa State" " Yoruba State", Igbo State" "Tiv State", "Ijaw State" "Jukun State" ,etc. That is why the Yoruba ethnic nationality is in 6 or 8 states, the Ibo nation is in 5 or 7 states, the Efiks in two states, the Hausa/Fulani in more than 10 states, etc. The Tiv are also in 4 or 5 states, the Jukun in about 3 states e.t.c. To single out the Tiv and label them as "settlers" in some states where they have existed for over 600 years is the biggest government backed joke of the 21st century. The Tiv were living in those states before the Nigerian political society evolved, hence their (that is Tiv's) fundamental rights are antecedent to Nigeria and ought to be adequately protected. Even sworn enemies of the Tiv nation would swallow this bitter truth.
Also in the case of Olawofoyeku V. Attorney General of Oyo State (1996) 12 SCNJ 347, the Supreme Court was called upon to determine the legal implications of the creation of states. The apex court held, inter-alia that all states of the federation are equal. It also held that all the assets being owned by an existing state belong jointly to both the old and the newly created states save where they have been shared. It is an incontestable fact that human beings are assets. To the best of my knowledge, there has been never a time that the governments of Benue, Nasarawa, Taraba or Plateau have shared out their citizens (human assets). Upon the creation of these states at different intervals, those human assets have been where they are today, hence any attempt to "share" them now will amount to on illegality. That is the stark reality we must all agree to embrace, for Nigeria to survive as a civilized society.
Related to the above is the effect of the present land tenure system on claims of tribal, communal or family ownership of land. Prior to the promulgation of the Land Use Act in 1978, there were different land tenure systems in Nigeria. There were, for instance, communal, family and individual ownership of land. But the Land Use Act came to radically change all these, making all Nigerians tenants in their fatherland. The Supreme Court had to painfully acknowledge this fact in the case of Savannah Bank (Nig.) Ltd. V Ajilo-(2001) FNWLR (Pt. 75) 513. Obaseki, J.S.C., had this to say at pages 545-546:
"While the interest vested in the Military Governor is unstated in the Act, the interest a Nigerian can lawfully acquire from the military governor is scaled down to statutory right of occupancy.... When therefore section 34(2) of the Act converted the interest held by an owner to a statutory right of occupancy, the Act reduced him to the position of a tenant subject to the control of the state through the governor. As a tenant he is bond by the implied and express terms of the tenancy".
Nnamani, J.S.C, at PP. 550 -551 not only agreed, but also stated that it is "the refusal to accept" this revolution in land ownership that has led to many wrong interpretations of the Act.
For a particular tribe to, therefore, claim that it is the owner of land while others are "settlers" amounts, in the face of the Land Use Act, to daydreaming and wishful thinking. This is moreso if the other tribe called a group of settlers is acknowledged by the so-called original settlers as having settled on the land in question for centuries. In law once title is proved to be by settlement, then it surpasses all other titles. See Alli V. Alesinloye (2000) 4 SCNJ 264; Dokubo V. Omoni (1999) 6 SCNJ 168 Aseimo V. Abraham (2001) 6 SCNJ 178 and Kode V. Yussuf (2001) 2 SCNJ 49 at 72. For a particular tribe to claim that it migrated earlier into Nigeria (or a part of it) than others (which claim is itself faulty), hence it owns all the lands that make up Nigeria or that particular region is legally and; factually impossible. If that is the case, then we will have to go all round history to know who first migrated to Nigeria and term the rest "settlers " or "visitors" who must be expelled by all means. The framers of the Nigerian federal constitution never intended that, the international community never intended that, and no sane society will tolerate that .
Finally on that sub-topic, it is hereby submitted that to resort to self-help, even in a landlord tenant relationship has been abhorred by courts of law. See Calabar East Co-operative Thrift and Credit Society Ltd. V. Ikot (l999) 12 SCNJ 321. Even if the Tiv are tenants to the likes of the Jukun, the Alago, the Hausa/Fulani, the Quan Pam, etc (but they are not, they are better landlords), to use force to throw them out is illegal, unconstitutional, null and void.
The second major issue that calls for attention in this discuss is the right of the President and Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces to deploy military personnel to flash points within the country. This is important in view of experiences from Odi, Choba, Benue/Taraba, Plateau, Kaduna, and most recently, Osogbo, following the cruel murder of Justice Minister and Federal Attorney General, Chief Bola Ige, S.A.N. Increasingly, the Nigerian civil society is being over charged and militarised. The future portends danger, but the authorities in Abuja seem not to care. Gradually, Nigeria is leading towards a constitutional interregnum, and unless urgent steps are taken by all stake holders, our nascent democracy may collapse. Section 217(2) (c) of the 1999 Constitution is to this effect:
"(2) The Federation shall, subject to on Act of the National Assembly made in that behalf equip and maintain the armed forces as may be considered adequate and effective for the purpose of
(c) Suppressing insurrection and acting in aid of civil authorities to restore order when called upon to do so by the President, but subject to such conditions as may be prescribed by an Act of the National Assembly".
I am not aware of any Act of the National Assembly that authorized the Odi, Choba, Benue/Taraba operations. Again, I am yet to get an explanation that whereas the Constitution stipulates adequate and effective maintenance of soldiers on a peace-keeping mission, those that were allegedly killed in Zaki-Ibiam wore sandals and light shoes, had no protective hamlets and actually strayed into "enemy" territory through a private, white- colour vehicle. Another worrisome aspect is the allegation that those army personnel actually exchanged fire with militiamen, mowed down people, etc. The other set of personnel sent on a revenge mission also set houses ablaze, killed over 500 innocent civilians, destroyed the Zaki-Ibiam yam market, and wraught sundry havoc. They even burnt town police stations and the Ukum Local Government headquarters at Sankera. Are all these behaviour patterns in accordance with the Army Act Cap. 294,1990, especially sections 32,33,34,36,49 and 51? Is the President's response that "kill a soldier, invite disaster" in tandem with his alleged powers to use the army to curb civil break down of the law? History and the law must one day answer all these questions, either in this generation or in others yet unborn!
I make bold to submit that the rule of law which is necessary for our nascent democracy has been murdered in what appears to be a state -organized pogrom against the Tiv nation. In Governor of Lagos State V. Ojukwu(1986) 1 NWLR (Pt.18) 621at 638, Obaseki J.S.C. held thus:- "The Nigerian Constitution is founded on the rule of law the primary meaning of which is that everything must be done according to law. Oputa, J.S.C. at pages 647-648 held inter alia that the rule of law presupposes:
"1. That the state is subject to the law
2. That the Governments should respect the rights of individual citizens. During world War II Lord Atkin was still able to say: 'In this country amid the clash of arms, the laws are not silent. They be changed but they speak the same language in war as in peace. It has always been one of the pillars of freedom, one of the principles of liberty....'
I can safely say here in Nigeria even under Military Government, the law is no respecter of persons, principalities, governments or powers......."
But my Lord, Hon. Justice Oputa, your sound legal doctrine is no more, even under a pseudo - democracy. While innocent citizens are being killed, raped and dehumanised on the Benue side of the divide by soldiers, the same soldiers are aiding the Jukun and the Fulani to flush out Tiv people from Taraba State. The person that saw your soundness in law and appointed you to head an ego booster called the Human Rights Violations Investigations Commission is the same person supervising the worst forms of human rights abuses against an ethnic nationality, a "community indigenous to Nigeria"!.
Should the Tiv consider themselves true citizens by birth of Nigeria or should they rethink Nigeria? Only time can tell.

Headline: Nigeria: Parade of Memories On January 15th
Body_Text:
Dates are symbolic and each date is significant to various people in different ways. January 15 generates a welter of emotions within the country, and Victor Akobundu, our reporter, spoke to old soldiers, school pupils and others, to determine the place of January, 15 in the national psyche. Here is going down memory lane!
Mankind has for long at-tached great importance to certain dates of the year ostensibly for cultural, social, economic, political, and even personal purposes.
The idea of setting aside or marking certain days to celebrate or commemorate an event or an incident, is an age old tradition which has stretched down to the present time.
The phenomenon cuts across every race, culture, nation etc.
To most people it has become a tradition to celebrate one's date of birth, or wedding date.
Even one's date of birth is fondly remembered by loved ones and more so, a date associated with a remarkable occasion, or incident of memorable significance. With regard to culture certain cultural celebrations owe their origin to stages in the evolution of the various communities. However, due to a consistency of annually celebrating such events, it has been able to stand the test of time. For instance it is common practice among many cultures in Nigeria as a matter of tradition, to set aside certain dates of the year to mark cultural events like the beginning of the farming season, fishing season, New yam festival etc. which have been age old cultural events.
In similar vein, dates play a significant role in the national consciousness. This largely explains why every nation has a national day, or specific dates to commemorate very vital events in their history, or even the date of birth of great personalities whose achievements and contributions towards the well being of their country are emblazoned in gold in the annals of the nation's history. Therefore it is imperative to sustain such legacy for posterity through dates set aside for such purposes.
Dates in Nigeria
In Nigeria, there are dates which are considered special. One characteristic of such dates is that the Federal Government gives importance to these dates by declaring public holidays, thereby making it a work free-day apparently to give the date the desired honour which it deserves.
Although public holidays are not declared in respect of dates, there is always a lofty aura of commemoration that courses through the nation on such dates.
Furthermore, Nigeria, like every other sovereign nation has a national day. To Nigerians every October 1st is marked as Independence Day. The particular day brings in its wake lots of expectations and reflections as to how the country has fared. This date again is a symbolism of liberation from the shackles of colonial rule, which makes it a very important date in the history of the country.
Apart from a date like October 1st, we have special dates recognised as national dates. Some of these dates cut across the political, religious and social spheres. Some of these dates include January 1st, which is marked as New Year day. May 1st is Workers or Labour Day, and Children's Day is marked on May 27.
Meanwhile, in Nigeria, January 15th of any year, is a very remarkable date in the nations history, though many might not fathom what the date signifies, perhaps because no public holiday is declared on that day. However, January 15th, other wise recognised as the Armed Forces Remembrance Day is significant to Nigerian Military personnel both serving and retired, because it is a moment to remember the fallen heroes who laid down their lives to ensure that peace and unity flourished.
The major attractions of January 15th, include the laying of wreaths at the tomb of the unknown soldier by the President, followed by the firing of guns shot in honour of the souls of the fallen heroes, and the release of Pigeons into the air, an act which symbolises freedom and peace.
Against this backdrop, Daily Trust explored the origin of January 15th in terms of its significance, and the experiences of retired military personnel, or members of the Nigerian Legion who are deeply involved in this annual event, as well as their expectations during the Armed Forces Remembrance Day.
The Armed Forces Remembrance Day is marked on January 15th of each year.
Initially, though, the Armed Forces Remembrance Day was, prior to the adoption of January 15th, known as "eleven eleven" meaning the eleventh day of the eleventh month (November) which is the precise date that marked the end of the 2nd World War, (11th November 1945).
Ideally, it is a tradition for countries to specify certain dates for the remembrance of their fallen heroes. To this end the Federal Government in its effort to reconcile the former date known as "eleven-eleven", and to immortalise the heroic feats of those who sacrificed their lives for the nation on January 15th, 1966, had to adopt the date January 15th, of each year, as the national Armed Forces Remembrance Day.
This occasion, meanwhile serves as a meeting or rallying point for members of Nigeria Legion who are soldiers who participated actively in major historical encounters like the First World War, the Second World War, the Congo War and the Nigerian Civil War. Daily Trust further gathered that with the adoption of January 15th certain changes in that respect followed suit. For example the Nigeria Ex-Service Men Welfare Association (NEMA) had to be changed to the Nigeria Legion, which is a body comprising of veterans of the various wars.
Daily Trust went round to speak with some of these members of the legions in respect of their feelings towards January 15th, the Armed Forces Remembrance Day, their experiences during the war, and expectations for the Nigeria Armed Forces.
Roland Okundaye, who identified himself as the Chairman of the medically unfit ex-servicemen, and who participated actively in the Nigeria Civil War, told Daily Trust that January 15th for the members of the Nigeria Legion is a moment that calls for sober reflection in memory of their colleagues who paid the supreme sacrifice of death for the sake of peace. Again it is an occasion that brings together war veterans for a moment of joyous re-union.
Roland adds, "January 15th is for us members of the Legion. It is our ceremony. We are remembering our late comrades. The government is involved simply because they want to appreciate what the fallen heroes had done for the country. So the exercise is for us."
On his wartime memories, Roland Okundaye said enlisting in the Nigerian Army was a decision made out of his free volition, but he sustained an injury on the head after he escaped an ambush at Onitsha during the Nigeria civil war. However he was relieved from military service in 1979 on grounds of being medically unfit.
In his words: "The Nigerian miliatry is a disciplined one. This is so because we imparted and left behind a legacy for the younger ones who took over from us."
Another legionnaire, Mr. Ilebosa Ige, refers to January 15th as a solemn moment which brings painful memories of the war front, but being an annual event which commemorates the nations fallen heroes. Ige, further disclosed that he was relieved from the army after a routine medical check-up in 1979, on grounds of being unfit, as a result of the injury he sustained on his left leg, when a bomb exploded during the civil war.
He speaks of the military thus "during our time, we were more disciplined, compared to what is obtainable at present." Meanwhile another legionnaire, Bello Alhaji who is presently a farmer, expressed regrets for joining the army, saying after the risks and sacrifices he paid in fighting for his fatherland and sustaining injuries in the process, he was relieved of duties on grounds of being medically unfit, and left without pension.
To the military, January 15th signifies a day set aside to commemorate the Armed Forces Day, but to some other persons it could mean something quite different as some other unique events or incidents might have prevailed on that date, therefore making that date remarkable in several ways. Mr. Vin Ezeora, a retired civil servant posits that he has never thought of January 15th as being the Armed Forces Remembrance Day, but opined that at first thought, the date coincides with the first coup in the country as well as the murder of the pre-eminent Sardauna of Sokoto Alhaji (Sir) Ahmadu Bello.
Surprisingly though, the date seems to strike a familiar chord with those who were of age at the time of the events, that signalled the adoption of the date.
Many pupils of secondary schools, however, could not respond to the significance of January 15th, in much the same manner as those mature persons who were alive during the events of January 15th 1966. Some pupils view the date as an occasion when they could go and view the various parades, and delight in them.
January 15th generates emotions of sadness, particularly in respect of the assassination of Alhaji (Sir) Ahmadu Bello, Sardauna of Sokoto and premier of the Northern region, alongside other eminent politicians. There are emotions of great joy, especially when the old soldiers and their contemporaries step forward in finely starched uniforms against a background of solemn and lofty music. With medals glittering in the morning sunshine, they salute their fallen comrades with all the strength they are capable of mustering in the circumstances.

Headline: Nigeria: Night Fire Guts Onitsha Market
Body_Text:
Onitsha — A large position of Ose-Ekwodu market one of the seven major markets in Onitsha the commercial nerve centre of Anambra State was on Thursday night gutted by fire.
The inferno which started at about 1.00 am affected mainly the yam tubers, live fowl, plantain and pumpkin seeds section of the market worth millions of naira.
At the scene on Friday morning, THISDAY checks reveal that men of the fire service could not control the inferno as their water hose was said to have cut into two shortly on arrival as they were about to commence the process of putting off the fire.
At the scene also the shop owners were seen trying to exhume their burnt goods such as roasted yam tubers and plantains, even as the live fowls had been burnt to ashes.
One of the traders who spoke to THISDAY, amidst tears, Mr. Mark Amadi said he had up to 150 pieces of live fowl, but that by the time he could rush to the scene at about 2.00 a.m he was only able to recover 20 fowls alive.
The Secretary to Onitsha North council authorities, Mr. C.J. Chinwuba said in his office that he received the news of the inferno with great shock. The state police public relations officer, Mr. Yemi Ajayi (ASP) confirmed the story in a telephone interview and idsclosed that investigations into the matter had just been opened.

Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Strike: An Interpretation (2)
Body_Text:
Lagos — We are coming back to this topic today. The reason we find the
interface interesting is that we view it as a contest for relevance
before the citizenry.
Government believes that as an elected regime it has a right to make
whatever policies it deems necessary to enable it govern as
effectively as possible. It wants more cash in government coffers and
therefore decides to raise the prices of these products on which
every Nigerian depends one way or the other. To justify the move, it
adopts the term: liberalisation or deregulation; a system of making
fuel a commodity whose prices are determined by market forces and not
by fiat.
The NLC, led by Comrade Adams Oshiomhole, argues that there is no
problem about all this. During earlier negotiations with government,
the union of Nigerian workers and by extension, the ordinary people,
argued that government should increase the prices marginally at the
rate of 7.5 per cent of the existing market prices of N22 for petrol,
for instance. In so doing, an increase at the beginning of 2002 would
not exceed N1.50k. Labour posited further that with N23.50 for a
litre of petrol, those involved in the business of buying and selling
fuel from any part of the world will make modest profits and the
common man can absorb it with minimal pain.
With this argument, Labour s position looked more concerned with the
plight of the people than government s, which is supposed to be an
offshoot of the people s mandate. Adding four Naira to a litre of
petrol and other petroleum products seemed arbitrary and an overkill.
Setting up the Petroleum Products Prices Regulating Committee
(PPPRC), headed by amiable Dr. Rasheed Gbadamosi to effect the price
increases when the Bill establishing the body is said to still be in
the National Assembly, seemed an illegal and unconstitutional act of
the Obasanjo government. And so, on Wednesday, January 17, 2002,
Labour called a national strike.
Our first part of this write-up on Thursday last week put on the
table Labour s two major reasons for embarking on this strike. Number
one, according to the NLC, it was aimed to "force" a reversal of the
increases or at least a negotiation to reduce them. The second was to
let the PDP Federal Government led by General Olusegun Obasanjo to
know that this is a democracy. Governance must not only reflect the
popular wishes of the citizenry, it must also be sympathetic and
humane. It must contain its impulsive propensity to raid the pockets
of the already impoverished common man from time to time in search of
cheap raw cash to throw around without even bothering to account for
how much was gotten and spent. It must, for once, do a bit of
management, a bit of creative thinking, which was the way of past
genuine political leaders, such as Dr. Michael Okpara and Chief
Obafemi Awolowo. These people knew how to create wealth and deploy
it. They did it so effectively that other newly-independent Asian
countries such as India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Pakistan, which have
now turned into economic "tigers", came here to copy our economic
models in the early 1960 s. What we seem to have now is a bunch of
neo-military cake-sharers and cash maniacs who grab the common man by
the throat, tear his pockets and collect the little pennies he
manages to get, and then go sharing among federal, state and local
councils.
Today, we wish to do a little post-mortem of that two-day war of
nerves.
This has become important in view of some of the things that happened
during those 48 hours and what some people have been saying. I have
been listening to various classes of people. To my dismay, traders
are among those who are the least informed on the finer things of
politics and civics. My people are mostly traders. Many of them are
graduates. Those who are not have been travelling round the world and
transacting business with people from all climes, and so their level
of exposure and experience far outstrip those of university graduates
who sit in offices and do the same crummy routines year in, year out.
These traders are the ones who are forever complaining about one
thing or the other that governments are doing to frustrate their
vocation. Little do they know that the moment they are able to
acquire political education and consciousness, they will understand
the power they hold in the downstream sector of the economy. They
will understand that apart from petroleum, commerce is the only
reason that the Nigerian economy has not yet collapsed. Armed with
this knowledge and knowing how to close ranks to manipulate this
power, they can make the government to concede to them a better
operating environment for their businesses.
The working class, by embarking on such measures as strikes and
work-to-rule actions, are only capitalising on the power at their
disposal.
Surely, sometimes this power is ill applied or abused or
over-dramatised.
The fact remains that you should understand the power your
circumstances put in your hands and apply it in pursuit of your
legitimate interests and within the purview of the law.
Questions have been raised. Labour went on strike to force a reversal
of the price hikes. Instead, the labour leaders found themselves
being arrested and arraigned in court for engaging in an illegal
strike and activities liable to cause a breach of the public peace.
The prices that government fixed are being subscribed to by the
people and willingly too.
What has Labour achieved with the strike? Can t it be validly said
that the strike failed? In fact, Professor Jerry Gana, the
Information and National Orientation Minister even said the strike
"collapsed". I think they all got it wrong.
The Minister s use of the word: collapse, is a misnomer and obviously
part of the sudden recourse to cheap propaganda by the Federal
Government. The strike did not collapse, just as it was not scored a
hundred per cent success. The fact on record is that the NLC called a
strike on Wednesday, 17th January 2002. The Federal Government,
through its Minister for Information, Professor Gana and his Labour
and Productivity counterpart, Alhaji Musa Gwadabe, told workers to
ignore the call. In fact, the Secretary to the Government of the
Federation, Chief Ufot Ekaette, threatened to sack those who failed
to report to their duty posts. What did happen? Whom did the people
listen to?
On Wednesday and Thursday last week, the country was at a standstill.
There was no work, no movement, and no trading activities. Labour
leaders were being arrested, re-arrested and put on trial in courts.
They were being abused over the FRCN and NTA, which suddenly resorted
to its old the-cap-fits-Abacha sycophancy journalism. But the moment
the labour leaders asked workers to return to work in compliance with
the ruling of an Abuja High Court, offices re-opened. Market
activities swung back to life.
The roads bubbled to life again. Life came back to normal. To me,
this does not suggest a "collapse". It suggests "success". It implies
"power", "effectiveness", and "popular support". During the strike,
Nigerians ignored the Federal Government. They listened to the NLC.
They took directives from the NLC. They believed the NLC officials
were suffering on their behalf. They knew that Adams Oshiomhole and
his colleagues were being subjected to police harassment, arrests,
re-arrests and legal terrorism for their sake. The independent media
reported it correctly. The world witnessed it. The only persons that
saw it and refused to admit it were those in the government media.
They reported only what they felt would please the government.
The only problem that the NLC had during the strike was its own lack
of readiness. They were caught flat-footed. Labour under-estimated
government s own preparedness and determination to fight back this
time. Labour was lulled into a slumber after it invaded Aso Rock and
the grounds of the National Assembly in the past two years and got
away without arrests. The carrots that the Federal Government was
believed to have dangled before some of its officials further
deceived them. These officials, including, as some speculated,
Comrade Oshiomhole, were lured into believing that they were now the
"friends" and confidants of Mr. President. All the while, government
officials, who have never forgiven Oshiomhole for his invasion of Aso
Rock, were perfecting strategies for striking back at him. They
dusted the state instruments of coercion the police and government
electronic media. They exploited the law, which the military made to
enhance government s capacity to burst strikes. They knew that the
National Assembly was on recess and in no position to mount their
usually feeble opposition.
To cap it all up, they noticed that Labour was in the lowest state of
alert since the restoration of civil rule. They had relaxed and taken
things for granted. They had gone palsy-walsying with government. It
is like the rat becoming friends with the cat, or the yam accepting
to be buddies with Mr.
goat. They only end up being eaten! They forgot that Labour s number
one job is "the struggle". The struggle is not meant to take over
governance but to make those in governance to do their jobs better.
It is not meant to derail democracy but to make democracy thrive. It
is not meant to sabotage the economy but to make the economy more
suitable for the citizens of the country rather than the foreign
masters of people in government. It is a struggle to bring governance
"down to earth", where the people reside.
One hopes that Labour has learnt its lessons.

Original_Search_Title: Woman Renews Demand for Slain Daughter's Corpse
Headline: Nigeria: Woman Renews Demand for Slain Daughter's Corpse
Body_Text:
Six years after her daughter was allegedly killed by Sgt Christopher Nnamdi, Madam Jegede is demanding that the killer cop be brought to justice writes Godwin Ifijeh
She may never be able to put behind her for the rest of her life the gruesome death of her 25-year old daughter six years ago from the bullets of Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi (Force No 110279) at their village of Uokha, Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State.
Frail-looking Madam Olohigbe Jegede, 68, has remained heavily weighed by the unexpected sad loss of her fashion designer daughter, Miss Blessing Amore, in September 1996. In tears, she recalled the incident to City Diary last weekend: "Police Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, without cause, shot and killed my daughter in company of three other policemen detailed to our village, Uokha, to investigate and arrest a lad who allegedly beat another to death while they were fighting.
"We were not living in the house where the incident happened. He shot my daughter, killed her, threw her corpse into their vehicle and drove away without even allowing me to see it. Till date, I have not set my eyes on her corpse. We were told that the corpse was deposited at the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, near Ekpoma. we visited Auchi, Afuze and Benin City several times, demanding for her corpse for proper burial. Its six years now since she was killed, the police have refused to release her corpse to us. they are not giving any explanations for it either. But we have continued to ask that the police release the corpse to us so that we can give her a proper burial. The corpse is not seen. The policeman involved has long been transferred away from the Afuze Police Division he when he committed the offence, so also the Divisional Police Officer (DPO) then, Mr. Kenene Onyire, a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP)".
"If only I had known that Blessing would not return home," the aging woman, who said she had separated from the late fashion designer's father, continued: "I would not have allowed her to go and fix her hair that day. We had just finished frying garri and she told me she was going to fix her hair. I told her to return quickly as I was going to put yam on fire for pounded yam.
"The next thing I heard were cries, and shouts of 'they have killed the girl', they have killed the girl. Of course, I never thought that the person in question was my daughter and I have no reason to think other wise, convinced that she was where she was fixing her hair until somebody ran to me to say that the person he saw on the ground looked like her. I threw away what I was doing, ran to the place but the policemen threw the corpse into their vehicle before I got there. I tried to catch a glimpse of the body but they would not allow me. They drove everybody away from the scene and sped off with the body and a bleeding young man they shot along with her.
"Then, we began running around their offices from Afuze to the Area Command Headquarters, Auchi, as well as the State Command Headquarters, Benin City. All through, they did not allow me to see her corpse, I am not even sure they carried out any autopsy on it.
"They also did not take any concrete action aganist the policeman who shot her. Each time we asked them what they were doing about the case, they would tell us that they were handling it their own way. The next thing was that they transferred the man and till date, we never heard anything about the matter again neither do we know where the culprit is or what has happened to him".
As the account had it, sergeant Nnamdi, who was attached to the Afuze Divisional Headquarters of the Edo State Police Command then, was detailed along with three other policemen to effect the arrest of a young boy who had beaten another child to death.
The two kids were said to have fought on September 13, 1996 and one of them collapsed in the course of the fight and eventually died on September 23.
The policemen arrived the town at about 4.00pm on that fateful day, moved to the house of the other kid, who allegedly fought with the dead one to effect his arrest but met the house deserted. They then embarked on indiscriminate arrest of people in the area, relying on what they termed magistrate courts rule 2, which empowers the police to arrest as many suspects as possible to a crime committed to make it possible for the real suspect to be arrested. The entire area was said to have been thown into confusion, with people fleeing in all direction to escape being arrested.
Amidst that confusion, the late Blessing Amore was said to be returning from where she went to fix her hair, unaware of what was going on.
Sergent Nnamdi reportedly halted her, pointed his service pistol at her while ordering her to go into their vehicle. The late Miss Amore was said to have quietly obeyed but the unexpected happened. As she was trying to climb into the police van, Nnamdi's gun went off and hit the lady at her side. The bullet tore through her, before hitting and wounding a young man, Friday Ohikhena, who was already in the vehicle. Miss Amore fell and died instantly, soaked in her pool of blood. The policemen reportedly fired several shots into the air to scare away the gathering crowd, picked up the body, threw it into the van to join the injured young man and sped away with it.
On the advice of the then DPO of the Afuze Police Divisional Headquarters, Mr. Kanene Onyire, the policemen were said to have taken the the corpse and the injured Ohikhena to the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, for deposit in the mortuary and treatment respectively.

Headline: Nigeria: Fuel Strike: An Interpretation
Body_Text:
Lagos — We are coming back to this topic today. The reason we find the
interface interesting is that we view it as a contest for relevance
before the citizenry.
Government believes that as an elected regime it has a right to make

effectively as possible. It wants more cash in government coffers and

adopts the term: liberalisation or deregulation; a system of making
fuel a commodity whose prices are determined by market forces and not
by fiat.
The NLC, led by Comrade Adams Oshiomhole, argues that there is no
problem about all this. During earlier negotiations with government,
the union of Nigerian workers and by extension, the ordinary people,

litre of petrol, those involved in the business of buying and selling
fuel from any part of the world will make modest profits and the
common man can absorb it with minimal pain.
with this argument, Labour s position looked more concerned with the
plight of the people than government s, which is supposed to be an
offshoot of the people s mandate. Adding four naira to a litre of
petrol and other petroleum products seemed arbitrary and an overkill.
Setting up the Petroleum Products Prices Regulating Committee
(PPPRC), headed by amiable Dr. Rasheed Gbadamosi to effect the price
increases when the Bill establishing the body is said to still be in
the National Assembly, seemed an illegal and unconstitutional act of
the Obasanjo government. And so, on Wednesday, January 17, 2002,
Labour called a national strike.
Our first part of this write-up on Thursday last week put on the
table Labour s two major reasons for embarking on this strike. Number

popular wishes of the citizenry, it must also be sympathetic and
humane. It must contain its impulsive propensity to raid the pockets
of the already impoverished common man from time to time in search of
cheap raw cash to throw around without even bothering to account for
how much was gotten and spent. It must, for once, do a bit of
management, a bit of creative thinking, which was the way of past
genuine political leaders, such as Dr. Michael Okpara and Chief
Obafemi Awolowo. These people knew how to create wealth and deploy
it. They did it so effectively that other newly-independent Asian
countries such as India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Pakistan, which have
now turned into economic "tigers", came here to copy our economic
models in the early 1960 s. What we seem to have now is a bunch of
neo-military cake-sharers and cash maniacs who grab the common man by
the throat, tear his pockets and collect the little pennies he
manages to get, and then go sharing among federal, state and local
councils.
Today, we wish to do a little post-mortem of that two-day war of
nerves.
This has become important in view of some of the things that happened

These traders are the ones who are forever complaining about one
thing or the other that governments are doing to frustrate their
vocation. Little do they know that the moment they are able to

Original_Search_Title: The Current Economic Situation And Elections 2003
Headline: Liberia: The Current Economic Situation And Elections 2003
Body_Text:
As the country prepares for another round of elections in less than two years, we must begin to demand from political parties and candidates, policy blueprints on the direction they wish to take the country. We must also begin to provoke debates on matters that are salient in forging a sustainable democracy in Liberia. It is necessary to do so because never in the history of our nation, do we need to unite on very crucial issues than now.
When we dialogue and exchange views, regarding matters that concern the building of a democratic culture, and elections 2003, the better we will be in galvanizing a formidable united force against anti democratic elements of our society. A debate of this kind and a subsequent one should particularly be of interest to the "seventeen presidential" aspirants who have directly and indirectly expressed their interest in contesting the coming general elections. It will be of interest to them because of two major reasons, (1) To begin challenging the candidates' own ability and mental readiness for the presidency, (2) The more we engage our aspirants, and leaders on critical issues that are fundamental to our social, political, economic development, the better we will understand their positions on key issues that are critical to the well-being of our people and the nation; (3) A healthy intellectual debate helps to prune most of those who are incompetent from the race of the presidency.
The process of elimination must begin with us now by scrutinizing these myriad presidential candidates through vigorous exercises in all areas. Only then will we be able to quietly remove those who think of the presidency as a place of retirement. In addition to testing the positions of various candidates on critical issues, it is equally important for those who are officials of government in waiting, to commence preparing and presenting blueprints of future government's modus operandi through a forum of this nature. Failure to craft new alternative policies, will lead us to inherit and adapt failed existing policies.
It is in this light, I wish to provoke a national debate by introducing one of those critical issues of our time. "The current economic situation and elections 2003 with the hope that we may fully discuss this topic prior to elections 2003, and beyond. There are several questions this topic suggests, however, a comprehensive analysis of this topic is impossible to exhaust or treat fairly in few pages. Therefore, I will limit my discussion to three basic areas.
1. The impact of the current economic situation on elections 2003.
2. The role Liberians must play to have the necessary economic environment that is required for an intelligent decision as voters.
3. Who is to benefit from elections 2003 when the current economic situation remains unalleviated by a minimum percentage?
This brings us to the Economy.
Economy
The Liberian economy is one that has adopted a heavily centralized fiscal policy, with a tradition of an incompetent rubber stamp legislature who dare question the chief executive on budgetary appropriations and expenditures. The checks and balances necessary for our fiscal and monetary policies provided by the constitution are completely lacking. This fiscal system does not allow for improvement in governance, and it allows wealth to be concentrated in the hands of the very few who rule the nation. It leads to excessive control of fiscal and monetary policies by the central government. In the end, it severs local government participation in the decision making process of the economy.
Most Liberians will agree that the current economic system, and all of the institutions responsible for administering this - are entirely controlled, and managed by Charles Taylor. Consequently, the economic situation in our country is defunct. It is an economy caught in a vicious downward spiral, surging unemployment to 87%. The poverty level is now at 80%. Illiteracy has increase to a little over 40%. The national per capita income is one of the lowest on the continent of Africa. The necessary infrastructures for economic development are non functional and in some cases non-existent. Only 40% of our school going-age has access to formal educational facilities. Over 80% of the nation's population, have no access to electricity or safe drinking water. Agricultural production is at its lowest ebb in history. Salaries to Civil servants are paid every nine months for one month. The financial institutions are in a state of fragility. Moreover, there is a high rate of capital out flow with no substantial inflow for investment, and the economy lacks coherent fiscal, monetary, and exchange rate policies.
With this general overview of the economy, let us examine both the rural and modern sectors.
Rural Economy
The rural economy of our nation is predominantly agriculture, and presently it is excessively underutilized. In some rural areas, farming and other agricultural activities no longer exist due to the ongoing war. Consequently, in those non-farming areas, poverty has reached to an unimaginable proportion. Our rural inhabitants are without health facilities, no access to electricity or any type of lights except that, which is provided by nature. There is no safe drinking water, and daily meals are not certain for most households. The mortality rate in the rural areas has increased to an alarming rate. Out of every five children, only one may see his or her first birthday.
The wild life of Liberia is fiercely competing over bush yams, wild fruits, and other uncultivated plants, which are not consume under normal conditions. The rural economy of the country is completely demarcated from the modern sector of the economy, because farmers are no longer producing for marketing purposes. In addition, their skills and other services do not match up with the modern sectors of our economy.
Modern Sector
The modern sector continued to be paralyzed by the wrong economic policy of the government. The government of Liberia like many governments of the world is the biggest buyer of goods and services of the economy. Therefore, government's policies have substantial impact on the entire economy. It is due to this correlation between government policies and the entire economy that makes government policies very essential for sustainable economic development. Therefore, the economic policies of the government must be productive, coherent, and responsible in order to have sustainable economic development.
Never in the history of our nation has there been a greater need for good governance and justice than now. Both good governance and justice are bedrocks for economic development. But most of Liberia's economic problems stemmed from the poor governance and the non-democratic nature of the Taylor government.
The global Economy
Today, the world has developed into the biggest market system one can ever imagine. In this market system, there are buyers and sellers. Nations perform both the buying and selling roles. When nations were the only players in this world market, the market was referred to as "international economy" but because there are other non-governmental participants in this world market, it is now referred to as global market. During the last quarter of the last century, the net outcome of all of the transactions among the many nations and other non-governmental participants, realized a growth by 3.5% per year. What this means is that on an average, every country that participated in this world market contributed to the goods and services consumed nations of the world. However, the pattern of growth has been rather uneven among countries and within countries. A significant number of developing countries did realize broad-based economic growth and thereby reduced poverty by an encouraging percentage. Why then Liberia continued to experience a downward trend in her economy? Did we not participate in this global market? We did - to a substantial level. As a matter of fact, we did cross borders to steal other nations' resources to add on to ours and sold them on the global market. With all the stealing from our neighbors, together with what we exported from our own territory, why is Liberia becoming poorer and poorer each day? In order to attempt to answer this paradoxical question, it is prudent to briefly view our participation in this global market during the last three years.
Liberia's Exports
In 1999, Liberia's own diamond exports were reported to be 8,500 carats, valued at $900, 000. The Minister of lands and mines and energy, of Liberia Mr. Jenkins Dunbar, of course refuted this report as a gross underestimate of what actually left Liberia. In the year 2000, it was reported that Liberia exported a little less than 500 million dollars diamonds. The steep increase in the export of diamonds from 1999 to 2000 could be attributed to the criminal activities of Mr. Taylor and his government in Sierra Leone. According to the Forest Development Authority Report, between January and June of 2001, round log production exported 679,253 cubic meters valued at $462 million dollars. Note however, that these figure are underestimation of the real export by 20% to 50% because of tax evasion by companies and wide spread corruption by government officials.
This is the minimum level at which Liberia participated in the global market for a three-year period.
Where is the source of our poverty problems?
The source of our poverty is Mr. Taylor's incompetence, couple with his criminal activities in and outside Liberia. His poor governance and mismanagement of the economy can be clearly stated as follows:
1. The major source of our poverty is Taylor's unquestionable power to expend revenue not within budgetary guidelines. Between 1999 and 2000, 60% of government revenue occurred outside the budgetary process. According to an IMF report, certain timber concessions, government public corporations, and revenue collection agencies undertook massive expenditure on behalf of government and recorded them as "non-cash" revenue with an offsetting outlay on goods and services. Most of the goods consisted of arms and ammunition.
2. Taylor and his partners' monopoly on essential goods such as rice, gas, and cement also serve as a major contributing factor for the downward spiral of the economy.
3. Lack of security for the rural farmers to carry out their farming activities without any molestation, looting of farms, and confiscation of farm produce by government's armed bandits is another contributing factor.
4. Failure on the part of the government to pay salaries and national debts owed the business community; the outstanding national debts amounting to over one hundred twenty million United States dollars is another, while the country's international debts is little over three billion dollars.
5. Certain government control Banking institutions failed to pay depositors funds. According to the 1999 Banking reports, approximately $450 million is yet to be paid to depositors by these non-functional Banking institutions.
6. Absence of democracy and other key factors are responsible for escalating the Liberian people's poverty. In the absence of democracy, the Liberian people are not allowed to freely choose their leaders and make free and intelligent decision. Without a free decision making process, the people can not fully utilize their potentials. As a result, skilled professionals leave the country for better opportunity elsewhere.
7. Reintroduction of the hut tax by the government is another policy in the wrong direction. In a country where there is practically no income for the rural inhabitants, the imposition of the hut tax is to take from them, what they do not have. This leads the government to garnishing future remittances from family and love ones. Consequently deepening their poverty.
8. Introduction of the Strategic Commodity Act by Taylor's government prohibits legislative approval of all concession's agreement between the government and foreign companies, and bestows plenary power to the President - to negotiate and signed all concession agreements, including the process by which tax and other financial obligations are paid by these companies.
9. Lack of incentives for foreign and local investors; the security problem in the country, purposely created by Taylor's government is a serious one. The government makes it difficult for foreign as well as Liberian nationals to invest in the country. The habit of requirimg investors to give a share in their business or pay up front a bribe before commencing operation is discouraging . Furthermore, there are too many armed bandits in the streets; too many checkpoints guarded by hungry armed youths; too many bureaucratic tips at the top, and armed robbery that are still of concern to potential investors.
10. Infrastructure and other services conducive for investment are completely lacking.
Election 2003
Having analyzed the prevailing economic situation in Liberia, I find it practically impossible for free and fair elections to be held in 2003. If election is to be held, it will not be free and fair, because the voters are infected by abject poverty. When voters are infected by poverty at the level that exists in Liberia, they tend to choose the one who provides for them the most during the day before election, ignoring five years of neglect, by the very candidate who now provides for them.
On the other hand, communication with the voters will be one-sided, since the one with the deepest pocket especially in a political system where there is no limit to campaign financing - Lies and misleading information will go unchecked. Critical issues will not be fully debated. In the absence of balanced information on critical issues through a healthy political debate, voters have the tendency to believe the person they hear, see and shake hands with. With a country that has no good roads, and poor means of transportation, the candidate with the deepest pocket is the only one to fly a helicopter to remote villages and towns.
